- **Construction** (L&T, Afcons): Work-at-Height Permits issued digitally via mobile apps (e.g., **SafetyCulture, iAuditor**).
- **Offshore Platforms** (ONGC Mumbai High): Combination Hot Work + Confined Space Permits with **lifeboat and medevac** plans embedded.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations and Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation — Permissible Noise Exposure Time (T)

We derive the OSHA permissible exposure time as a function of noise level.

**Step 1 — The Underlying Principle (the "5 dB Rule"):**

The OSHA standard assumes that for every **5 dBA increase** in noise level, the permissible exposure time is **halved**, because the energy of the sound doubles. This is anchored in the equal-energy hypothesis.

$$
L \;-\; 90 \;=\; 5 \cdot \log_2\!\left(\frac{8}{T}\right)
$$

Here, $L$ is the measured level in dBA, and $T$ is the permissible exposure in hours. The reference point is **90 dBA for 8 hours**.

**Step 2 — Isolate the Log Term:**

$$
\frac{L - 90}{5} \;=\; \log_2\!\left(\frac{8}{T}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Definition of $\log_2$:**

Recall that $\log_2(x) = a \iff x = 2^a$. Therefore:

$$
\frac{8}{T} \;=\; 2^{\,(L - 90)/5}
$$

**Step 4 — Invert and Solve for T:**

$$
T \;=\; \frac{8}{2^{\,(L - 90)/5}}
$$

This is the working formula.

**Step 5 — Verification with a Known Case:**

For $L = 95$ dBA:

$$
T \;=\; \frac{8}{2^{\,(95 - 90)/5}} \;=\; \frac{8}{2^{1}} \;=\; \frac{8}{2} \;=\; 4 \text{ hours}
$$

This matches the OSHA table exactly: 95 dBA → **4 hours permissible**.

For $L = 100$ dBA:

$$
T \;=\; \frac{8}{2^{\,(100 - 90)/5}} \;=\; \frac{8}{2^{2}} \;=\; \frac{8}{4} \;=\; 2 \text{ hours}
$$

For $L = 115$ dBA (above the PEL of 90 dBA + engineering controls):

$$
T \;=\; \frac{8}{2^{5}} \;=\; \frac{8}{32} \;=\; 0.25 \text{ hours} \;=\; 15 \text{ minutes}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Threshold**: If $L \ge 115$ dBA, **no exposure is permitted**, regardless of duration. This is a hard-coded OSHA rule.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Step-by-Step Permit Issuance Workflow

The WPS is executed in **10 sequential steps**. KTU questions often ask students to "describe the WPS procedure" — using this table yields full marks.

| Step | Stage | Action | Responsible Person |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Identification** | Identify that the job is a non-routine, hazardous activity covered under WPS. | Requesting Person |
| 2 | **Hazard Analysis** | Conduct **Job Safety Analysis (JSA)** — list each step, identify hazards, define controls. | JSA Team |
| 3 | **Permit Request** | Submit the permit form with job description, location, time, tools, JSA reference. | Permit Requester |
| 4 | **Pre-Issue Verification** | Area Authority checks isolation, gas test results, equipment status. | Area In-charge |
| 5 | **Permit Approval** | Issuing Authority (e.g., Shift Superintendent) reviews and signs. | Permit Issuer |
| 6 | **Permit Display** | Permit is displayed at the job site in a Permit Board. | Permit Holder |
| 7 | **Work Execution** | Job is performed strictly within the permit conditions. | Permit Holder + Workers |
| 8 | **Continuous Monitoring** | Gas tests repeated at intervals; conditions monitored. | Permit Holder |
| 9 | **Work Completion** | Tools removed, area cleaned, fire watchers withdrawn, equipment restored. | Permit Holder |
| 10 | **Permit Closure** | Permit is signed off, archived for **1 year minimum**. | Permit Issuer |

## 3.3 Step-by-Step PPE Selection Logic

Given a hazard, the selection of PPE follows a **6-step decision tree**, which KTU examiners love to test.

**Step 1 — Identify the Hazard Type:**

Possible hazards: Chemical, Mechanical, Electrical, Thermal, Radiation, Biological, Ergonomic.

**Step 2 — Identify the Body Part at Risk:**

Head, eyes, ears, lungs, skin (hands/feet), whole body.

**Step 3 — Quantify the Hazard (if possible):**

- Noise: measure with sound level meter in **dBA**.
- Chemical: measure with detector tubes in **ppm**.
- Heat: measure with WBGT meter in **°C**.

**Step 4 — Compare with Permissible Limit:**

If exposure > TLV/PEL, PPE is mandatory.

**Step 5 — Select PPE with Certified Performance:**

Choose PPE certified to **IS / EN / ANSI / NIOSH** standards. Verify expiry date of filters and seals.

**Step 6 — Confirm Fit, Comfort, and Compatibility:**

Conduct a **Quantitative Fit Test (QNFT)** for respirators. Ensure PPE does not interfere with other PPE (e.g., safety glasses inside a hard hat).

> [!NOTE]
> **PPE Selection Matrix (Quick Reference for KTU):**

| Hazard | Body Part | Recommended PPE |
|---|---|---|
| Flying particles | Eyes | Safety goggles (EN 166) |
| Falling objects | Head | Hard hat (IS 2925 Class E) |
| H₂S exposure | Lungs | SCBA (NIOSH-certified) |
| Hot surfaces (≥ 100°C) | Hands | Heat-resistant gloves (EN 407) |
| Working at 6 m height | Whole body | Full-body harness + shock absorber |
| Solvent splash | Skin | Chemical apron (EN 14605) |
| Arc flash | Body | Arc-rated suit (NFPA 70E, 8 cal/cm²) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams and Schematics

## 4.1 Work Permit System Lifecycle (Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Step 1: Identify Non Routine Hazardous Job] --> node2[Step 2: Conduct Job Safety Analysis JSA]
    node2 --> node3[Step 3: Submit Permit Request Form]
    node3 --> node4[Step 4: Area Authority Pre Issue Check]
    node4 --> node5[Step 5: Issuing Authority Signs Permit]
    node5 --> node6[Step 6: Display Permit at Job Site]
    node6 --> node7[Step 7: Execute Work Under Conditions]
    node7 --> node8[Step 8: Continuous Gas and Condition Monitoring]
    node8 --> node9[Step 9: Work Completion and Area Housekeeping]
    node9 --> node10[Step 10: Permit Closure and Archival]
    node10 -.-> node11[Permit Record Retained for 1 Year per OSHA]
    node1 -.-> node12[Hot Work / Cold Work / Confined Space / Electrical / Height / Excavation / Lifting]
```

## 4.2 Hierarchy of Hazard Controls (Mermaid Block)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Eliminate the Hazard Completely] --> nodeB[Substitute with Less Hazardous Material]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Engineering Controls: Guards, Ventilation, Interlocks]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Administrative Controls: Procedures, Training, Signage]
    nodeD --> nodeE[PPE: Last Line of Defense]
    nodeA -.-> nodeF[Most Effective]
    nodeE -.-> nodeG[Least Effective but Most Commonly Used]
```

## 4.3 Block Level Functional Architecture: PPE Selection Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    in1[Hazard Source Input] --> proc1[Hazard Identification Module]
    proc1 --> proc2[Quantification Module: dBA, ppm, deg C]
    proc2 --> proc3[Comparison with TLV or PEL]
    proc3 --> decision{PPE Required?}
    decision -- Yes --> proc4[PPE Selection by Body Part and Standard]
    decision -- No --> out1[No PPE Issue Permitted]
    proc4 --> proc5[Fit Test and Compatibility Check]
    proc5 --> out2[PPE Issued and Logged in PPE Register]
    proc5 --> proc6[Training and Refresher Module]
    proc6 --> out3[Worker Wears PPE and Enters Work Zone]
```

## 4.4 Permit Decision Tree (Hot Work)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    q1{Is Work Non Routine and Hazardous?} -- Yes --> q2{Is Flammable Material Present?}
    q1 -- No --> r1[Standard Operating Procedure SOP is Sufficient]
    q2 -- Yes --> q3[Hot Work Permit Required]
    q2 -- No --> q4[Cold Work Permit Required]
    q3 --> q5{LEL Below 1 Percent}
    q5 -- Yes --> q6[Issue Hot Work Permit]
    q5 -- No --> q7[Purging and Inerting Required]
    q7 --> q5
    q6 --> q8[Post Fire Watch and Extinguisher]
    q8 --> q9[Work Execution Allowed]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank and Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the Work Permit System. List any four types of work permits used in process industries.**

**Model Answer:**

> A Work Permit System (WPS) is a formal, written administrative control procedure used to authorize and supervise non-routine, hazardous activities in a process plant. It ensures that hazards are identified, controls are implemented, and the work is performed only after competent authority approval.

> **Four types of work permits:**
> 1. Hot Work Permit
> 2. Cold Work Permit
> 3. Confined Space Entry Permit
> 4. Electrical Work Permit (with LOTO)

*Alternate acceptable entries: Work at Height Permit, Excavation Permit, Lifting Operations Permit.*

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall (1 mark lost)**: Students often confuse the *types* of permits with the *stages* of a single permit. Memorize the **list of permit categories separately**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**What is PPE? State the hierarchy of hazard controls, placing PPE in its correct position.**

**Model Answer:**

> Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) is the specialized equipment or clothing worn by workers to minimize exposure to specific occupational hazards when higher-level controls are insufficient.

> **Hierarchy of Hazard Controls (most to least effective):**
> 1. **Elimination** of the hazard
> 2. **Substitution** with a less hazardous alternative
> 3. **Engineering Controls** (guards, ventilation)
> 4. **Administrative Controls** (procedures, training, signage)
> 5. **PPE** (last line of defense)

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall (1 mark lost)**: Do **not** list PPE first. KTU's valuation key deducts marks if the student does not show the hierarchy in the correct order.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Compulsory Choice 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Question Bank]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Work Permit System** for a typical process industry. Discuss the roles of the *Permit Issuer* and the *Permit Holder* and describe the **six essential elements** of a valid permit. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A worker is exposed to a noise level of **102 dBA** for **3 hours**. Using the OSHA 5 dB doubling rule, determine:
   1. The **permissible exposure time** at this level.
   2. Whether the worker's exposure is **within or beyond the permissible limit**.
   3. The **type of hearing PPE** that should be issued, with relevant standard. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Work Permit System (7 marks):**

A Work Permit System is a structured administrative control for hazardous, non-routine work. The system works as follows:

**Roles:**
- **Permit Issuer** (e.g., Shift In-charge, Area Manager): A trained, competent person who assesses the hazards, verifies all precautions, and authorizes the permit by signature.
- **Permit Holder** (e.g., Maintenance Supervisor): The person responsible for ensuring the work is performed strictly within the permit conditions, with all precautions enforced on the ground.

**Six Essential Elements of a Valid Permit:**

1. **Job description and location** — exact identification of the work.
2. **Hazard identification and JSA reference** — explicit listing of hazards and controls.
3. **Validity period** — start and end time (typically one shift, max **8 hours** for hot work).
4. **Precautions list** — gas tests, isolations, PPE, fire watch, etc.
5. **Signatures** — issuer, holder, and (if applicable) area in-charge.
6. **Sign-off and closure** — documented completion and permit archival.

**Supporting Documentation:** Permit form, JSA, gas test record, LOTO certificate (if applicable), isolation list.

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify the measured level and exposure duration:**

$L = 102$ dBA, actual exposure time = **3 hours**.

**Step 2 — Calculate permissible exposure time using the OSHA formula:**

$$
T \;=\; \frac{8}{2^{\,(L - 90)/5}} \;=\; \frac{8}{2^{\,(102 - 90)/5}} \;=\; \frac{8}{2^{12/5}} \;=\; \frac{8}{2^{2.4}}
$$

$$
2^{2.4} \;=\; 2^{2} \cdot 2^{0.4} \;=\; 4 \cdot 1.3195 \;=\; 5.278
$$

$$
T \;=\; \frac{8}{5.278} \;\approx\; 1.516 \text{ hours}
$$

> **Permissible exposure time = 1.52 hours (approximately 91 minutes).**

**Step 3 — Compare permissible vs actual exposure:**

Actual exposure = **3 hours** > Permissible exposure = **1.52 hours**. The worker's exposure is **beyond the permissible limit**, and the situation is **non-compliant** with OSHA standards.

**Step 4 — Recommend PPE:**

- **Hearing PPE**: Ear muffs with **NRR ≥ 29 dB** or ear plugs with **NRR ≥ 33 dB**.
- **Standard**: **ANSI S3.19 / IS 6229**.
- **Additional control**: Engineering noise reduction at source is also recommended.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model Question Bank]**

**(a)** Discuss the **classification of Personal Protective Equipment (PPE)** based on the **body part protected**. For each category, mention **one Indian Standard (IS)** reference. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A confined space entry is planned in a hydrocarbon storage tank. Describe the **complete Confined Space Permit procedure** including:
   1. Atmospheric testing sequence with **acceptable limits**.
   2. The **role of the standby person**.
   3. The **two conditions under which entry must be terminated immediately**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Classification of PPE (7 marks):**

| Body Part | PPE Type | Indian Standard |
|---|---|---|
| Head | Hard Hat (Class E) | **IS 2925** |
| Eyes and Face | Safety Goggles, Face Shield | **IS 5983** |
| Hearing | Ear Plugs, Ear Muffs | **IS 6229** |
| Respiratory | SCBA, Respirators | **IS 15323** |
| Hands | Chemical-resistant Gloves | **IS 6994** |
| Feet | Safety Shoes | **IS 15298** |
| Body | Flame-Resistant Coveralls | **IS 15731** |
| Fall Protection | Full-Body Harness | **IS 3521** |

**Brief Notes:**
- **Head**: Class E hard hats protect against **20 kN** impact and **30 kV** electrical contact.
- **Eyes/Face**: Side-shielded goggles prevent lateral particle entry.
- **Hearing**: Plugs are reusable; muffs provide higher NRR.
- **Respiratory**: SCBAs provide **30–60 minutes** of clean air.
- **Hands**: Glove selection depends on chemical compatibility chart (e.g., nitrile for hydrocarbons, butyl for ketones).
- **Feet**: Steel toe + steel midplate prevents puncture and crush injuries.
- **Body**: Flame-resistant coveralls (treated cotton or Nomex) prevent burn injuries in hydrocarbon fires.
- **Fall Protection**: Full-body harness must distribute arrest force across thighs, pelvis, chest, shoulders.

**(b) Confined Space Permit Procedure (7 marks):**

**1. Atmospheric Testing Sequence (with acceptable limits):**

The order is **critical** and follows the **OSHA 1910.146** protocol:

- **Step A — Oxygen content**: must be between **19.5% and 23.5%**.
- **Step B — Flammability (LEL)**: must be **below 10%** of the Lower Explosive Limit.
- **Step C — Toxicity**: H₂S < **10 ppm**, CO < **25 ppm**, Benzene < **0.5 ppm** (TWA).

The order (O₂ → LEL → Toxics) is fixed because an oxygen-deficient atmosphere can give false-low LEL readings on catalytic sensors.

**2. Role of the Standby Person (Attendant):**

- Remains **outside** the confined space at all times.
- Maintains **continuous communication** with the entrant (visual, voice, or radio).
- **Summons rescue** in case of emergency.
- **Never enters** the space for rescue (this requires a separate, dedicated rescue team).
- Tracks the **time of entry** and ensures replacement before fatigue.

**3. Two Conditions for Immediate Entry Termination:**

1. **Atmospheric degradation** — O₂ drops below 19.5% or above 23.5%, or LEL rises above 10%, or toxic gas levels exceed their TLVs. Re-entry requires re-purging and re-testing.
2. **Communication failure or entrant distress** — If the attendant loses contact, observes erratic behavior, or the entrant signals an emergency, immediate evacuation is triggered.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Common Pitfalls):**
> 1. **Atmospheric test order is wrong** → Full 2 marks lost. Always test **Oxygen FIRST**, then LEL, then toxics.
> 2. **Standby person entering the space for rescue** → 1 mark lost. The attendant is forbidden from entering; a separate rescue team is required.
> 3. **Confusing TLV with PEL** → Use the values given in the question. If the question does not specify, default to **OSHA PELs**.
> 4. **Permit validity not mentioned** → Always state **"valid for 8 hours (one shift) maximum"** in any permit-based answer.
> 5. **PPE listed without standard** → Marks are deducted if the IS / EN / ANSI number is not written.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap and Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module 1 — Rapid-Revision Bullet Checklist:**

- **WPS Definition**: A formal written authorization system for non-routine hazardous work, controlling risk through administrative procedure.
- **7 Types of Permits**: Hot Work, Cold Work, Confined Space, Electrical (LOTO), Work at Height, Excavation, Lifting.
- **Permit Validity**: Maximum **8 hours per shift**; renewable after re-assessment.
- **Permit Records**: Retained for **minimum 1 year** per OSHA.
- **Permit Issuer** signs the permit; **Permit Holder** executes and signs off; **Standby Attendant** is mandatory for confined space.
- **PPE Definition**: Equipment worn to minimize exposure to occupational hazards.
- **Hierarchy of Controls**: Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → **PPE (last)**.
- **8 PPE Categories**: Head, Eyes/Face, Hearing, Respiratory, Hands, Feet, Body, Fall Protection.
- **Key Indian Standards**: IS 2925 (Hard Hat), IS 5983 (Goggles), IS 6229 (Hearing), IS 6994 (Gloves), IS 15298 (Shoes), IS 3521 (Harness).
- **Noise Formula**: $T = 8 / 2^{(L-90)/5}$.
- **OSHA 5 dB Rule**: Every **5 dB increase** halves permissible exposure time.
- **Hard Limit**: No exposure permitted at or above **115 dBA**.
- **Confined Space Atmospheric Order**: **Oxygen → LEL → Toxicity** (memorize the order).
- **Confined Space Oxygen Range**: **19.5% to 23.5%**.
- **LEL for Hot Work**: Must be **below 1%** (some standards say < 10% LEL = safe to enter).
- **Hot Work Fire Watch Radius**: **11 m (35 ft)** as per NFPA 51B.
- **PPE Fit Test**: Required for all tight-fitting respirators; **Fit Factor ≥ 100** for half-face.
- **Arc Flash PPE Rating**: Minimum **8 cal/cm²** for routine work (NFPA 70E, Category 2).
- **The 5 W's of WPS**: **What** work, **Where** location, **When** time, **Who** authorized, **Why** hazards.
- **Golden Rule**: If a permit is unsigned, the work **must not begin**, regardless of urgency.
- **PPE Rule**: PPE is the **last**, not the first, line of defense. Always exhaust higher-tier controls first.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification of Fire: Pool Fire, Jet Fire, Flash Fire
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT723 - PROCESS SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Classification of Fire: Pool Fire, Jet Fire, Flash Fire

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

In the domain of **Process Safety Engineering**, the analysis of accidental hydrocarbon releases forms the backbone of quantitative risk assessment (QRA). A generic, undifferentiated concept of "fire" is insufficient for the rigorous design of layout spacing, emergency shut-down (ESD) logic, and the structural integrity of pressure vessels. KTU 2024 Scheme mandates the granular classification of process fires into three principal archetypes — **Pool Fire, Jet Fire, and Flash Fire** — because each represents a fundamentally different physical mechanism of fuel release, mixing with oxidant, and heat transfer to the surroundings. Misclassification of any of these fires in an engineering design will lead to either catastrophic under-protection or economically punitive over-design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate:** Under Module 2, students must be able to mathematically model each fire type, compute flame geometry, calculate radiative/convective heat flux at a target, and ultimately derive the **Downwind Toxic/thermal damage distance** using standard industrial correlations.

### 1.1 The Three Archetypes of Process Fire

**A. Pool Fire**
A **Pool Fire** is a turbulent diffusion flame burning above a horizontal pool of liquid hydrocarbon (or a low-melting solid such as wax or molten polymer), where the vaporization of the fuel is controlled by the heat feedback from the flame itself to the liquid surface. The fuel has *negligible momentum*; combustion kinetics are governed purely by buoyancy.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):** Imagine a kerosene puddle in a backyard after a container tips over. The kerosene itself is not burning — it is evaporating due to the heat of the flame above it. The pool acts like a "fuel reservoir" that continuously feeds the fire. As the pool level drops, the fire slowly dies out. Examples in industry: a leaking flange dripping into a bunded area, a damaged storage tank roof, or LPG spillage on water.

**B. Jet Fire**
A **Jet Fire** (also called a **Torch Fire** or **Flame Jet**) is a turbulent diffusion flame established when a pressurized fluid (gas, liquefied gas, or superheated liquid) is released from a ruptured orifice, pipe, or flange with substantial exit momentum. The fire remains attached to the release point and forms a stretched, elongated envelope of combustion in the direction of the jet's trajectory. Unlike a pool fire, this is a **momentum-dominated** combustion phenomenon.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):** Think of lighting a Bunsen burner in a chemistry lab. The methane issues from the nozzle with velocity, the flame is clearly directional, and the flame length is determined by how strongly the gas is flowing. If you turn the gas valve wider, the flame stretches longer. In industry, the equivalent is a gas riser rupture or a stuck-open pressure relief valve (PRV) discharging flammable vapor — a horizontal jet fire may impinge on adjacent pipe racks.

**C. Flash Fire**
A **Flash Fire** is a non-explosive, transient diffusive combustion of a flammable vapor cloud that has been formed by the release and dispersion of a flammable fluid in open air. The cloud is ignited, and the flame propagates through the pre-mixed cloud without generating a damaging overpressure. The damage envelope is typically limited to the lower flammability limit (LFL) contour of the cloud.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):** A small amount of LPG leaks invisibly inside a closed shed. The vapor silently mixes with air, forming an invisible flammable cloud. A worker throws a switch, a tiny spark ignites the cloud, and for a brief second, the entire cloud burns with a flash and a "whoomph" sound. The wooden walls of the shed are scorched but the shed is *not* blown apart — because the burning velocity was subsonic and no significant overpressure developed. That is a flash fire.

> [!TIP]
> **Distinguishing Test:** If a fire follows the **LFL contour** of a pre-existing vapor cloud → **Flash Fire**. If the flame is **attached to a leak point** and elongated → **Jet Fire**. If the flame is **anchored to a horizontal liquid pool** → **Pool Fire**. This single triage rule is the KTU board examiner's first checkpoint.

### 1.2 The Why Behind the Classification

The classification is not academic — each fire type drives a different **design basis accident (DBA)**. The thermal radiation level at an operator's location, the time-to-escape, the carbon-steel **failure temperature of pressure vessels (typically 425 °C for fire exposure per API 521)**, and the spacing of equipment are all dictated by which fire archetype dominates. Pool fires are primarily a **fire duration** problem; jet fires are primarily a **direct impingement + radiation** problem; flash fires are primarily a **dispersion + ignition timing** problem.

| Fire Type | Fuel State | Controlling Physical Mechanism | Typical Industrial Source |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Pool Fire | Liquid pool | Heat feedback to liquid surface | Bunded tank dike, spills, leaking flange |
| Jet Fire | Pressurized gas / 2-phase | Momentum + buoyancy | Ruptured pipe, leaking PRV, flange |
| Flash Fire | Vapor cloud in air | Dispersion + flame propagation | Vent stack releases, vapor cloud from spill |

Standard reference temperatures used in process fire analysis are **T₀ = 298 K (ambient)**, **T_b = boiling point**, and the **auto-ignition temperature** as a no-fire operating limit. The design fireball temperature used in API 521 is approximately **1500 K**.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Pool Fire — Theoretical Framework

A pool fire evolves through three sequential stages:
1. **Growth stage**: The flame spreads radially outward across the liquid pool. For a circular pool, the flame front follows a `√t` law.
2. **Steady (fully developed) burning**: A quasi-steady mass burning rate is established. This is the design-stage that KTU expects students to compute.
3. **Decay stage**: As the liquid is consumed or the pool depth drops below a critical value, the burning rate decays.

The **mass burning rate per unit area** (kg/m²·s) of a liquid pool depends on whether the pool is small (heat conduction in the liquid is 1-D) or large (heat conduction is dominated by convection within the liquid bulk). For typical industrial pools (diameter > 0.2 m), the **Hottel's correlation** is universally applied:

$$ \dot{m}'' = \frac{0.001 \cdot h_c \cdot \Delta H_c}{\Delta H_v + c_p \cdot (T_b - T_0)} $$

Where:
- **h_c** = Heat of combustion (J/kg)
- **ΔH_c** = Higher heating value (J/kg)
- **ΔH_v** = Latent heat of vaporization (J/kg)
- **c_p** = Specific heat of liquid (J/kg·K)
- **T_b** = Boiling point of liquid (K)
- **T_0** = Ambient temperature (K)
- The factor **0.001** converts W to kW in the standard empirical form.

For wind-affected pools, the **Blinov–Khudyakov** correlation introduces a turbulent correction:

$$ \dot{m} = \dot{m}_\infty \cdot \left[ 1 - \exp(-k_b \cdot \beta \cdot D) \right] $$

where **β = (ΔT / T₀) · √(g / D)** is the dimensionless buoyancy term and **k_b ≈ 1.6** is a fitted constant.

The **flame height (L/D)** of a pool fire is most commonly predicted using the **Thomas correlation**:

$$ \frac{L}{D} = 42 \cdot \left[ \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho_a \cdot \sqrt{g \cdot D}} \right]^{0.61} $$

The **Heskestad correlation** is preferred for very large pools:

$$ \frac{L}{D} = 0.235 \cdot \dot{Q}^{0.4} - 1.02 $$

where $\dot{Q}$ is the heat release rate in **kW**.

The **radiative heat flux** at a target located distance **x** from the flame axis is computed using the **point source model**:

$$ q = \frac{\eta \cdot \dot{m} \cdot \Delta H_c \cdot \tau}{4 \pi \cdot x^2} $$

where **η** is the **fraction of heat released as radiation** (typically 0.15 – 0.35 for hydrocarbon pool fires; 0.4 for methanol) and **τ** is the **transmissivity** of air (humidity-corrected).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why these equations matter:** The 4 π x² dependence means that doubling the distance from a pool fire reduces the incident heat flux by a factor of 4. This is the engineering basis for **safety distance tables** in CCPS / TNO Yellow Book used by KTU 2024 Scheme curriculum.

### 2.2 Jet Fire — Theoretical Framework

A jet fire is governed by the **Froude number** and the **effective release diameter**. Two classical correlations are mandatory for KTU:

**Hawthorne's correlation (for non-premixed, turbulent jet flames):**

$$ \frac{L}{D} = 38.1 \cdot (Fr)^{0.45} $$

where:

$$ Fr = \frac{v^2}{g \cdot D} $$

For sonic (choked) gas releases, the **mach number M = 1** is substituted, and the **API 521 jet fire length** is computed via the **De Faveri / Molkov** correlation:

$$ \frac{L}{D_s} = 30 \cdot \left( \frac{\dot{m}_g}{\rho_a \cdot D_s} \right) \cdot \left( \frac{\rho_\infty}{\rho_j} \right)^{0.5} \cdot \left( 1 - \frac{y}{L} \right) \cdot \exp(-1.5 y/L) $$

where **y** is the stand-off distance from the release point and **L** is the visible flame length.

The **jet fire flame is a stretched volume source**, and the API method divides the flame into discrete ellipsoidal cells of length ~3D, computing the view factor F from each cell to the target. The **incident radiative heat flux** for jet fire impingement is given by the **Chamberlain two-phase** correlation:

$$ q = \tau \cdot \sigma \cdot \left( T_f^4 - T_a^4 \right) \cdot F $$

where **F** is the configuration view factor (0 ≤ F ≤ 1), **T_f** is the flame temperature (~1500 K), and **T_a** is the ambient (298 K).

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** Jet fires are particularly destructive because they cause **direct flame impingement** on adjacent pipework. The **API 521 recommended time-to-failure** of a 6-inch carbon steel pipe exposed to a jet fire is approximately **10 minutes** at heat fluxes > 100 kW/m². This is the engineering basis for **firewater deluge system** design and **emergency isolation valve (EIV)** placement.

### 2.3 Flash Fire — Theoretical Framework

A flash fire is fundamentally a **dispersion problem** followed by an **ignition problem**. The methodology is:

1. Compute the **release rate** of flammable vapor (kg/s) using API 520/521 or the TNO Multi-Energy method.
2. Use a **dense gas dispersion model** (DEGADIS, SLAB, or ALOHA) to compute the downwind **vapor cloud envelope** at LFL.
3. Compute the **flammable mass** within the LFL contour:

$$ m_f = V_{LFL} \cdot \rho_{vapor} \cdot \left( \frac{LFL}{100} \right) $$

4. Apply the **TNO Green Book / Yellow Book** approach: the **damage distance** is the downwind distance at which the contour falls below LFL, and the thermal radiation load is given by a **uniform heat flux of 12.5 kW/m² for 3 seconds** (TNO criterion for a "harmless" flash fire) or **37.5 kW/m²** for lethality.

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical distinction:** A flash fire does **not** cause overpressure. If the cloud is partially confined or exhibits flame speeds > Mach 1 in the boundary layer, the event escalates to a **Vapor Cloud Explosion (VCE)** — a fundamentally different accident scenario under API 753 / IEC 60079. KTU 2024 examiners will mark a student wrong who confuses VCE with flash fire.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM ALERT:** Memorize the formulas in the table below — they appear in 90% of past KTU paper corrections for Module 2.

| # | Concept | Equation | Typical Inputs / Units |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Pool burning rate (Hottel) | $\dot{m}'' = \frac{0.001 \, h_c \, \Delta H_c}{\Delta H_v + c_p \, (T_b - T_0)}$ | kg/m²·s, kJ/kg, K |
| 2 | Pool burning rate (Blinov–Khudyakov) | $\dot{m} = \dot{m}_\infty \, \left[ 1 - \exp(-k_b \beta D) \right]$ | kg/s, m |
| 3 | Flame height (Thomas) | $\frac{L}{D} = 42 \left[ \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho_a \sqrt{g D}} \right]^{0.61}$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | Flame height (Heskestad) | $\frac{L}{D} = 0.235 \, \dot{Q}^{0.4} - 1.02$ | dimensionless, kW |
| 5 | Point source radiation | $q = \frac{\eta \, \dot{m} \, \Delta H_c \, \tau}{4 \pi \, x^2}$ | kW/m², m |
| 6 | Froude number | $Fr = \frac{v^2}{g \, D}$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | Jet flame length (Hawthorne) | $\frac{L}{D} = 38.1 \cdot (Fr)^{0.45}$ | dimensionless |
| 8 | View factor radiation | $q = \tau \, \sigma \, (T_f^4 - T_a^4) \cdot F$ | kW/m², K |
| 9 | Flammable mass in cloud | $m_f = V_{LFL} \, \rho \, (LFL/100)$ | kg, m³ |
| 10 | Pool fire duration | $t_b = \frac{\rho_l \, h_{pool}}{\dot{m}''}$ | s, m |

> [!NOTE]
> In the table above, the symbol "—" denotes range/variable inputs; **x** is the distance from the flame to the target (m); **σ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²·K⁴** is the Stefan–Boltzmann constant; **g = 9.81 m/s²**.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Radiative Heat Flux from a Pool Fire (Point Source Model)

The **point source model** assumes that all the combustion energy is released at a single point located at the flame centerline. The heat is then radiated uniformly into a sphere.

**Step 1 — Total heat release rate**
The total rate of energy released by combustion is given by:

$$ \dot{Q}_{total} = \dot{m} \cdot \Delta H_c $$

where **ṁ** is the mass burning rate of the pool (kg/s) and **ΔH_c** is the higher heating value (J/kg).

**Step 2 — Radiative fraction**
Of this total heat release, only a fraction **η** is radiated. The remainder is consumed as convective heat to the environment. Thus:

$$ \dot{Q}_{rad} = \eta \cdot \dot{m} \cdot \Delta H_c $$

**Step 3 — Spherical spreading (Lambertian Point Source)**
The radiation from an isotropic point source decays as the surface area of a sphere of radius **x**:

$$ \dot{Q}_{rad} = q \cdot 4 \pi \, x^2 $$

Rearranging for the incident heat flux **q** at the target:

$$ q = \frac{\dot{Q}_{rad}}{4 \pi \, x^2} $$

**Step 4 — Atmospheric attenuation**
The flame-to-target path is rarely vacuum-clean. Moisture, CO₂, and soot absorb/scatter a portion of the radiation. The **transmissivity τ** is introduced:

$$ q_{target} = \frac{\eta \cdot \dot{m} \cdot \Delta H_c \cdot \tau}{4 \pi \, x^2} $$

**Step 5 — Numerical example**
A methane pool of diameter **D = 10 m** burns with η = 0.2, ṁ = 5 kg/s, ΔH_c = 50 MJ/kg, and τ = 0.85. Compute the heat flux at a target 50 m downwind:

$$ q = \frac{0.2 \cdot 5 \cdot 50 \times 10^6 \cdot 0.85}{4 \pi \cdot 50^2} $$

$$ q = \frac{4.25 \times 10^7}{31415.9} $$

$$ q \approx 1352 \, \text{W/m}^2 = 1.352 \, \text{kW/m}^2 $$

This is below the **5 kW/m² threshold for second-degree burns** (per TNO Green Book), confirming that 50 m is a safe stand-off distance for a non-escalating emergency response.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Trick:** The point source model **over-predicts** heat flux for receivers close to the flame (x < 3L). For such close-in targets, the **solid flame model** (Mudan/Crook) is required. Always state the model limitation in your answer to score full marks.

### 3.2 Derivation: Pool Burning Rate (Hottel's Empirical Form)

Hottel's equation is a **steady-state energy balance** on the surface of the liquid. At steady state, the heat entering the liquid at the surface equals the heat required to vaporize the liquid and raise its temperature to boiling.

**Step 1 — Energy balance at the surface**
Heat conducted into the liquid (from the flame above) is partially used to vaporize mass at rate **ṁ″** and to heat the bulk liquid from ambient T₀ to boiling T_b:

$$ q_{surface} = \dot{m}'' \cdot \Delta H_v + \dot{m}'' \cdot c_p \cdot (T_b - T_0) $$

**Step 2 — Express in terms of heat release**
Hottel observed experimentally that the conductive heat flux at the surface is approximately **0.001 h_c**, where **h_c** is the heat of combustion expressed in **kW/m²**. Substituting:

$$ 0.001 \, h_c = \dot{m}'' \cdot \left[ \Delta H_v + c_p (T_b - T_0) \right] $$

**Step 3 — Solve for ṁ″**

$$ \dot{m}'' = \frac{0.001 \, h_c}{\Delta H_v + c_p \, (T_b - T_0)} $$

This is the canonical Hottel form. To convert to kJ/kg units, the 0.001 factor is replaced with empirical coefficients in **mm/min** for hydrocarbons.

### 3.3 Derivation: Jet Fire Flame Length (Hawthorne)

Hawthorne's correlation arises from the observation that a turbulent jet flame length scales with the **jet exit velocity** and the **release diameter** as a power law. The **Froude number Fr** is the ratio of inertial to buoyant forces:

**Step 1 — Define Froude number**

$$ Fr = \frac{v^2}{g \, D} $$

**Step 2 — Scale the flame length with jet diameter**

$$ L = C \cdot D \cdot (Fr)^n $$

**Step 3 — Empirical fit (Hawthorne, 1949)**
For non-premixed, buoyancy-affected, turbulent jet flames, the fitted constants are:

$$ C = 38.1, \quad n = 0.45 $$

So:

$$ \frac{L}{D} = 38.1 \cdot (Fr)^{0.45} $$

**Step 4 — Numerical example**
A methane leak issues at **v = 80 m/s** from a **D = 0.05 m** hole. Compute L:

$$ Fr = \frac{80^2}{9.81 \cdot 0.05} = \frac{6400}{0.4905} = 13047 $$

$$ \frac{L}{D} = 38.1 \cdot (13047)^{0.45} \approx 38.1 \cdot 41.4 \approx 1577 $$

$$ L \approx 1577 \cdot 0.05 = 78.85 \, \text{m} $$

This enormous flame length is why **API 521 mandates a 30 m minimum jet fire exclusion zone** around high-pressure gas risers in refineries.

### 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Parameter | Pool Fire | Jet Fire | Flash Fire |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Primary fuel phase | Liquid (pool) | Pressurized gas / 2-phase | Vapor (dispersed cloud) |
| Duration | Long (minutes to hours) | Continuous (until isolation) | Very short (< 1 s) |
| Dominant heat transfer | Radiation (η ≈ 0.2) | Convection + impingement | Brief radiation pulse |
| Damage metric | Heat flux × time | Heat flux + failure time | Lethal envelope distance |
| Key correlation | Hottel, Thomas, Heskestad | Hawthorne, API 521 | TNO Green Book |
| Operative equation basis | Mass balance + heat feedback | Momentum + Froude | Dispersion + LFL contour |
| Primary safety mitigation | Bunding, foam, drainage | Isolation valves, deluge | Vapor detection, ignition control |
| Typical damage threshold | 5 kW/m² (2nd degree burn) | 31.5 kW/m² (steel failure) | 12.5 kW/m² (TNO) |
| Time scale of interest | Hours | Hours (continuous) | Seconds |
