## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
**"Differentiate between a pool fire and a jet fire with respect to the governing physical mechanism and the controlling geometric variable."** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

A **Pool Fire** is a buoyancy-driven diffusion flame burning above a horizontal liquid pool. The fuel has negligible momentum at the point of release; the controlling geometric variable is the **pool diameter (D)**, and the flame geometry is governed by the buoyancy-driven **Heskestad or Thomas** correlations.

A **Jet Fire** is a momentum-driven turbulent diffusion flame attached to a pressurized leak point. The controlling geometric variable is the **release diameter (D) and exit velocity (v)**, captured compactly by the **Froude number Fr = v² / (gD)**, and the flame length is given by **Hawthorne's correlation L/D = 38.1 (Fr)^0.45**.

The fundamental distinction is **buoyancy-dominated vs. momentum-dominated** combustion, and this dictates very different flame geometries, damage envelopes, and mitigation strategies.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**"Define a flash fire and state TWO conditions that escalate a flash fire into a Vapor Cloud Explosion (VCE)."** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

A **Flash Fire** is a non-explosive, transient diffusive combustion of a vapor cloud in which the flame propagates through a pre-mixed flammable cloud (within its LFL and UFL envelope) at subsonic velocity, producing a brief thermal pulse but no damaging overpressure. The damage is limited to the LFL contour, and the conventional TNO threshold is **12.5 kW/m² for 3 seconds**.

The two conditions that escalate a flash fire into a **VCE** are:
1. **Partial confinement** of the vapor cloud by structures, pipe racks, or building walls, which reflect the pressure wave and accelerate flame propagation.
2. **High turbulence intensity** in the cloud (caused by obstacles, congested equipment layout, or jet-induced mixing) which folds the flame front, increasing its surface area and accelerating combustion to supersonic velocities.

Under these conditions, the event transitions from a deflagration to a possible detonation, generating lethal overpressures.

---

### 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

#### Question A — Pool Fire & Jet Fire *[Choice 1]*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model | 14 Marks]**

**(a)** A horizontal pool of **n-hexane** of diameter **D = 8 m** is ignited. The following data are provided:
- Density of liquid hexane: **ρ_l = 654 kg/m³**
- Heat of combustion: **ΔH_c = 45,000 kJ/kg**
- Latent heat of vaporization: **ΔH_v = 365 kJ/kg**
- Specific heat of liquid: **c_p = 2.26 kJ/kg·K**
- Boiling point: **T_b = 342 K**
- Ambient temperature: **T_0 = 298 K**
- Radiative fraction: **η = 0.18**
- Pool depth: **h = 25 mm**

Compute the **mass burning rate per unit area** (Hottel), the **total mass burning rate**, the **flame height** (Thomas correlation), and the **duration of the fire**. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A pressurized natural gas line ruptures, releasing methane at **v = 120 m/s** through an orifice of **D = 0.04 m**. Compute the **Froude number**, the **jet flame length (Hawthorne)**, and comment on the **practical safety implication** of the result for plant layout. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Hottel mass burning rate per unit area**

$$ \dot{m}'' = \frac{0.001 \cdot 45000}{365 + 2.26 \cdot (342 - 298)} $$

$$ \dot{m}'' = \frac{45}{365 + 99.44} = \frac{45}{464.44} = 0.0969 \, \text{kg/m}^2\cdot\text{s} $$

**Step 2 — Pool area**

$$ A = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 8^2 = 50.27 \, \text{m}^2 $$

**Step 3 — Total mass burning rate**

$$ \dot{m} = \dot{m}'' \cdot A = 0.0969 \cdot 50.27 = 4.87 \, \text{kg/s} $$

**Step 4 — Flame height (Thomas)**
Assume ρ_a = 1.2 kg/m³:

$$ \frac{L}{D} = 42 \cdot \left[ \frac{4.87}{1.2 \cdot \sqrt{9.81 \cdot 8}} \right]^{0.61} = 42 \cdot \left[ \frac{4.87}{10.62} \right]^{0.61} = 42 \cdot (0.4585)^{0.61} $$

$$ (0.4585)^{0.61} = 0.5828 $$

$$ \frac{L}{D} = 42 \cdot 0.5828 = 24.48 $$

$$ L = 24.48 \cdot 8 = 195.8 \, \text{m} $$

**Step 5 — Fire duration**

$$ t_b = \frac{\rho_l \cdot h}{\dot{m}''} = \frac{654 \cdot 0.025}{0.0969} = 168.7 \, \text{s} \approx 2.81 \, \text{min} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** A 25 mm deep pool of hexane burns for only ~3 minutes. Bund design must allow evacuation within this window.

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Froude number**

$$ Fr = \frac{v^2}{g \, D} = \frac{120^2}{9.81 \cdot 0.04} = \frac{14400}{0.3924} = 36698 $$

**Step 2 — Jet flame length (Hawthorne)**

$$ \frac{L}{D} = 38.1 \cdot (Fr)^{0.45} = 38.1 \cdot (36698)^{0.45} $$

$$ (36698)^{0.45} = 99.2 $$

$$ \frac{L}{D} = 38.1 \cdot 99.2 = 3780 $$

$$ L = 3780 \cdot 0.04 = 151.2 \, \text{m} $$

**Step 3 — Practical safety implication**
A jet fire of length 151 m from a 4 cm orifice at 120 m/s exit velocity is catastrophic. This flame will impinge on pipe racks, cable trays, and vessels located far beyond typical process unit boundaries. The **API 521 jet fire safe spacing** is **30 m minimum**, but this calculation shows the *flame tip* can reach >150 m. Therefore:
- Emergency Isolation Valves (EIVs) must be installed within **30 m of the release point** on upstream piping.
- Fireproofing must extend at least **10 m** horizontally from any high-pressure gas riser.
- The deluge system must have a guaranteed activation time of **< 30 s**.

---

#### Question B — Flash Fire & Comparative Analysis *[Choice 2]*

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model | 14 Marks]**

**(a)** A flash fire is initiated when **5,000 m³ of methane cloud** is formed at LFL contour. The vapor density is **ρ = 0.72 kg/m³** and the **LFL of methane = 5%** (by volume). Compute the **flammable mass** within the LFL envelope and the **maximum possible thermal radiation** at the cloud edge using the TNO criterion. State two scenarios under which this flash fire would escalate to a VCE. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Compare and contrast the **damage zones** of pool fire, jet fire, and flash fire using a labeled sketch and tabulate the differences across **five engineering parameters** of your choice. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Flammable mass within the LFL envelope**

$$ m_f = V_{LFL} \cdot \rho \cdot \left( \frac{LFL}{100} \right) $$

$$ m_f = 5000 \cdot 0.72 \cdot \left( \frac{5}{100} \right) = 5000 \cdot 0.72 \cdot 0.05 = 180 \, \text{kg} $$

**Step 2 — Maximum thermal radiation (TNO criterion for lethality)**

For a flash fire, the TNO Green Book assigns a constant incident heat flux of **37.5 kW/m²** at the LFL contour for the duration of the flame pass. This is independent of mass and is the design threshold for lethality.

For "harmless" flash fire exposure (no fatalities), the criterion is **12.5 kW/m² for 3 seconds**.

**Step 3 — Escalation scenarios to VCE**
1. **Partial confinement** of the vapor cloud by process structures, walls, or pipe racks. Confinement reflects the expanding pressure wave, accelerating flame propagation beyond the subsonic deflagration regime.
2. **Turbulence intensification** caused by obstacles, congestion, or the release momentum itself. The flame front is folded, increasing its area and effective burning velocity, eventually transitioning to a detonation.

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Sketch (text representation):**

```
POOL FIRE DAMAGE ZONE (CIRCUMFERENTIAL)
        _________
       /         \
      |    POOL   |   ← Radiative flux q(r) ∝ 1/r²
      |__________|
        \ |  | /
         \|  |/
          [ TARGETS ON A RING ]

JET FIRE DAMAGE ZONE (DIRECTIONAL LOBE)
         LEAK →━━━━━━━━━▶ FIRE TIP (~150 m)
                     ╲
                      ╲
                       ▼  RADIATIVE LOBE
                  [ TARGETS IN LOBE ]

FLASH FIRE DAMAGE ZONE (CONFORMAL TO LFL CONTOUR)
     ___________
    /  VAPOR    \
   |   CLOUD     |   ← Brief radiative pulse
   | (LFL SHAPE) |      (12.5 kW/m² × 3 s)
    \___________/
       [ TARGETS INSIDE CONTOUR BURN ]
```

**Tabulated Comparison Across Five Engineering Parameters:**

| Parameter | Pool Fire | Jet Fire | Flash Fire |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Damage zone shape | Circumferential ring | Directional lobe | Conformal to LFL cloud |
| Dominant heat transfer | Radiation (η ≈ 0.2) | Impingement + radiation | Brief radiative pulse |
| Typical stand-off distance | 50 m to 5 kW/m² | 30 m exclusion + impingement | LFL contour distance |
| Time to damage | Minutes (steady burn) | Continuous (until isolation) | Seconds (transient) |
| Primary mitigation | Bunding, foam, drainage | Deluge, EIV, fireproofing | Vapor detection, ignition control |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch in Hottel:** Many students use ΔH_c in kJ/kg but forget the 0.001 factor, leading to mass burning rates that are 1000× too high. Always state units explicitly.
> 2. **Forgetting transmissivity τ:** In the point source model, the atmospheric transmissivity must be included. The KTU model answer key penalizes omission by 1 mark per sub-part.
> 3. **Confusing LFL with UFL:** The flash fire envelope is bounded by **LFL on the lower side** and **UFL on the upper side**. Inside this band, the mixture is flammable; outside, it is too lean or too rich.
> 4. **Mixing up point source vs. solid flame model:** The point source model is only valid for x > 3L. For close-in targets, the solid flame model is mandatory.
> 5. **Not labeling the diagram:** The KTU 2024 scheme requires labeled sketches in 14-mark answers. A descriptive answer without a labeled diagram loses 2 marks per question.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Last-Night KTU Prep:**

- [ ] **Pool Fire** = Buoyancy-driven, liquid pool, heat feedback controls vaporization, **Hottel + Thomas + Heskestad**.
- [ ] **Jet Fire** = Momentum-driven, pressurized release, **Froude number + Hawthorne + API 521**.
- [ ] **Flash Fire** = Subsonic combustion of pre-mixed cloud, no overpressure, **TNO Green Book + 12.5 kW/m² for 3 s** lethality criterion.
- [ ] **Mass burning rate per unit area** is the cornerstone of every pool fire calculation — know Hottel by heart.
- [ ] **Froude number** Fr = v² / (gD) is the single dimensionless group that controls jet fire flame length.
- [ ] **Hawthorne correlation**: L/D = 38.1 (Fr)^0.45 — for non-premixed turbulent jet flames.
- [ ] **Thomas correlation**: L/D = 42 [ṁ / (ρ_a √(gD))]^0.61 — for buoyancy-driven pool fires.
- [ ] **Point source model**: q = η ṁ ΔH_c τ / (4π x²) — valid only for x > 3L.
- [ ] **VCE escalation** requires either **confinement** or **turbulence**, not merely a large cloud mass.
- [ ] **Design fireball temperature** for API 521 work = 1500 K.
- [ ] **Lethal radiation thresholds**: 5 kW/m² (2nd degree burn, 30 s exposure); 12.5 kW/m² (TNO flash fire); 31.5 kW/m² (carbon steel failure, API 521).
- [ ] **Blinov–Khudyakov** burning rate correction must be applied for wind-affected pools.
- [ ] Always **state the model assumption** (point source, solid flame, semi-empirical) before applying a correlation.
- [ ] **Memorize constants**: σ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²·K⁴; g = 9.81 m/s²; ρ_a ≈ 1.2 kg/m³.
- [ ] The **damage zone shape** differs for each fire type — circumferential (pool), directional (jet), conformal (flash). This single distinction often appears as a 2-mark KTU short note.
- [ ] The **API 521 time-to-failure** of an un-fireproofed carbon steel pipe under jet fire is approximately **10 minutes** — a critical plant design parameter.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Closing Mandate for KTU 2024 Success:** When you answer any process fire numerical, you must (1) state the correlation and its source, (2) show the energy/mass balance, (3) explicitly state all assumptions, and (4) interpret the result in a process safety context. KTU board examiners reward interpretation, not just arithmetic.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Explosions: UVCE, BLEVE, Dust Explosion, Deflagration, Detonation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT723 - PROCESS SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Explosions: UVCE, BLEVE, Dust Explosion, Deflagration, Detonation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Process Explosion?

In **Process Safety Engineering**, an **explosion** is a rapid, violent, self-propagating release of energy accompanied by a shock wave, fragmentation, heat, light, and sound. The four sub-classes required by the KTU 2024 OECHT723 syllabus differ in **fuel source**, **confinement**, **mechanism of energy release**, and **wave propagation speed**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Standard)**
> An explosion is the sudden release of energy (chemical, mechanical, or nuclear) producing a rapid pressure rise, a moving pressure front, and the generation of high-temperature gases, capable of causing severe structural damage.

---

## 1.2 The Four Explosion Categories at a Glance

### 1.2.1 Unconfined Vapor Cloud Explosion (UVCE)

A **UVCE** occurs when a large mass of flammable vapor (e.g., LPG, propane, butane, ethylene) is released into the open atmosphere, disperses, and gets accidentally ignited. The flame propagates through the unconfined cloud, and as the expanding combustion gases encounter turbulence, congestion, or partial confinement, the flame accelerates dramatically, producing a damaging overpressure wave.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** A UVCE is the most common cause of catastrophic loss-of-life incidents in hydrocarbon processing industries (e.g., 1984 Mexico City LPG Disaster — 500+ fatalities).

**Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine striking a match inside a huge invisible gas balloon floating over a refinery — the flame races through the entire balloon at high speed and the resulting "push" of hot gas slams into the ground and structures like a giant invisible fist.

### 1.2.2 Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapor Explosion (BLEVE)

A **BLEVE** is a *physical* explosion involving a liquid stored above its atmospheric boiling point. When the vessel catastrophically fails (e.g., due to external fire impingement weakening the shell), the superheated liquid instantaneously flashes to vapor, expanding its volume by a factor of **100× to 200×** in milliseconds. The result is a massive **fireball** and a destructive **pressure wave** without any chemical reaction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** BLEVE involves no combustion of the released vapor in the primary event; combustion (the fireball) is a *secondary* phenomenon, while the pressure wave is purely thermodynamic.

**Intuitive Analogy:** Pull the lid off a boiling pressure cooker under the ocean — the superheated water instantly flashes to steam with explosive violence, throwing a column of boiling liquid and steam skyward.

### 1.2.3 Dust Explosion

A **Dust Explosion** is the rapid combustion of finely divided solid particulates suspended in air, occurring when the dust concentration lies between the **Lower Explosive Limit (LEL)** and **Upper Explosible Limit (UEL)**. Combustible materials like grain, flour, sugar, coal, metal powders, plastics, and pharmaceuticals can form explosive dust clouds.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Concept:** The dust cloud must be in a specific concentration range. A thin layer on a shelf is *not* explosive — only the **dispersed, suspended cloud** in air can ignite.

**Intuitive Analogy:** A thick layer of flour is harmless. But if you blow flour dust into the air above a candle flame, the cloud ignites with explosive force — the surface area for combustion is now millions of times greater.

### 1.2.4 Deflagration vs. Detonation

These are the two **modes of flame propagation** in any combustion-driven explosion.

| Property | **Deflagration** | **Detonation** |
|---|---|---|
| **Flame speed** | Subsonic (typically 1–1000 m/s) | Supersonic (1500–3000 m/s) |
| **Mach number** | < 1 | > 1 |
| **Pressure wave coupling** | Flame and shock front are **decoupled** | Flame and shock front are **coupled** (one entity) |
| **Pressure rise at flame front** | Moderate (1–8 bar) | Extreme (15–50 bar) |
| **Propagation mechanism** | Heat & mass diffusion | Strong shock compression auto-ignites the gas |
| **Damage potential** | Moderate, vessel rupture possible | Catastrophic, total destruction |

> [!NOTE]
> **Transition Phenomenon (KTU Favourite):** Under sufficient confinement or congestion, a deflagration can **accelerate** into a detonation (DDT — Deflagration-to-Detonation Transition). This is the worst-case scenario in safety design.

**Intuitive Analogy:** A deflagration is like a slow-moving crowd of people walking through a corridor. A detonation is like a sonic boom pushed through a crowd — the shock front arrives first, instantly igniting the gas in its path.

---

## 1.3 Why These Distinctions Matter (KTU Industry Context)

In KTU 2024 examiner schemes, the student is expected to:

1. **Classify** an incident (UVCE / BLEVE / Dust / Deflagration / Detonation).
2. **Estimate** energy yield (in TNT equivalent or fireball radius).
3. **Predict damage zones** for facility siting and emergency planning.
4. **Apply** the correct **mitigation strategy** (venting, foam blanketing, inerting, separation distance).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework of Each Explosion

### 2.1.1 UVCE — Physical Mechanism

A UVCE evolves in **five sequential stages**:

1. **Release** of a large flammable mass (typically > 1,000 kg) due to pipe rupture, vessel failure, or flange leak.
2. **Dispersion** of the vapor cloud downwind, partially mixing with air to form a flammable envelope.
3. **Ignition** by a delayed source (e.g., fired heater, vehicle, electrical spark).
4. **Flame propagation** through the cloud. If the cloud encounters **congestion** (pipe racks, vessels, structures), turbulence is generated, increasing the **effective flame speed**.
5. **Overpressure generation** as accelerating flame compresses unburnt gas ahead, creating a blast wave.

The **TNT Equivalency Method** (the most commonly used in KTU numerical problems) assumes the explosion releases a fraction of the total available combustion energy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Standard Equation — TNT Equivalent Mass**
> $$W_{TNT} = \frac{\eta \cdot m \cdot \Delta H_c}{E_{TNT}}$$
> where $\eta$ is the empirical **yield factor** (typically **0.01–0.10**, often taken as 0.05 or 5%), $m$ is the mass of fuel released (kg), $\Delta H_c$ is the heat of combustion (kJ/kg), and $E_{TNT}$ is the energy released by 1 kg of TNT (**4,680 kJ/kg**).

### 2.1.2 BLEVE — Physical Mechanism

A BLEVE occurs when:

1. A vessel containing a **superheated liquid** (e.g., LPG at 20°C, where its boiling point is −42°C) is engulfed in fire.
2. Heat input reduces the liquid's **net heat of vaporization** and **raises the vessel wall temperature above the metal's critical failure temperature**.
3. The shell **ruptures catastrophically** (commonly near the vapor space, where metal is hottest and thinnest by corrosion/heat).
4. The **superheated liquid flashes to vapor instantaneously** — a thermodynamic phase change explosion.
5. A **fireball** forms (if the released vapor is flammable and ignites from the surrounding fire) — this is the secondary, visible event.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Note:** The strength of a BLEVE is dictated by the **superheat temperature** ($T_{superheat} - T_{boil}$) and the **mass of liquid**. The energy released per unit mass is the **heat of vaporization** at atmospheric pressure, *not* the heat of combustion.

### 2.1.3 Dust Explosion — Pentagon Concept

Unlike gases, dust explosions follow the **Explosion Pentagon** (fuel, oxidizer, ignition source, dispersion, **confinement**). The fifth element distinguishes dust from gas explosions — *confinement* is essential for a damaging pressure rise.

**Key Parameters:**

- **Minimum Explosible Concentration (MEC):** The lowest dust concentration (typically 30–500 g/m³) that can support a propagating flame.
- **Minimum Ignition Energy (MIE):** Often in millijoules, much higher than for gases.
- **Limiting Oxygen Concentration (LOC):** The oxygen level below which combustion cannot occur (used in inerting designs).
- **Kst Value (Deflagration Index):** The maximum rate of pressure rise, $K_{st} = (dP/dt)_{max} \cdot V^{1/3}$, measured in **bar·m/s**.

### 2.1.4 Deflagration vs. Detonation — Wave Mechanics

A **deflagration wave** is a subsonic flame front driven by **heat conduction** and **molecular diffusion** of radicals from burned to unburned gas. Pressure builds behind the flame but is decoupled from it.

A **detonation wave** is a coupled **shock + flame** complex traveling supersonically. The **Chapman-Jouguet (CJ) theory** defines the detonation velocity as:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Standard Equation — Chapman-Jouguet Detonation Velocity**
> $$V_{CJ} = \sqrt{2 \cdot (\gamma^2 - 1) \cdot Q}$$
> where $\gamma = c_p / c_v$ is the specific heat ratio and $Q$ is the heat released per unit mass of mixture (J/kg).

A more rigorous form uses the initial thermodynamic state ($T_1, P_1$) and the ratio of post-to-pre-shock pressure:

$$V_{CJ} = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma R T_1}{M} \left( \frac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma} \cdot \frac{2\gamma}{\gamma + 1}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For stoichiometric hydrocarbon-air mixtures, $V_{CJ}$ typically falls in the range **1,800–2,000 m/s** (Mach 5–6), and the post-detonation pressure is **15–20 bar** at the CJ plane.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat-Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Note:** Memorize the following table. The TNO Multi-Energy model is the most asked advanced topic in 14-mark problems.

| # | Formula | Description / Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $W_{TNT} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot m \cdot \Delta H_c}{E_{TNT}}$ | TNT equivalent mass of a UVCE | kg TNT |
| 2 | $Z = \dfrac{r}{W_{TNT}^{1/3}}$ | Scaled (Hopkinson-Cranz) distance for blast correlation | $\text{m} \cdot \text{kg}^{-1/3}$ |
| 3 | $\Delta P_0 = \dfrac{1{,}016 \, Z^{-2.13}}{1 + 7.2 \, Z^{-2.13}}$ | Free-air overpressure (small charges, $0.05 < Z < 1$ m/kg$^{1/3}$) | kPa |
| 4 | $\Delta P_0 = \dfrac{84.5}{Z} + \dfrac{331}{Z^2} + \dfrac{28}{Z^3}$ | Overpressure for $0.3 < Z < 30$ m/kg$^{1/3}$ (Brode equation) | kPa |
| 5 | $E_{BLEVE} = m \cdot \Delta H_{vap}$ | Physical explosion energy of a BLEVE (flash vaporization only) | kJ |
| 6 | $R_{fb} = 2.9 \cdot m^{1/3}$ | Fireball radius (BLEVE) | m |
| 7 | $t_{fb} = 0.45 \cdot m^{1/3}$ | Fireball duration (BLEVE) for $m < 30{,}000$ kg | s |
| 8 | $K_{st} = \left( \dfrac{dP}{dt} \right)_{\max} \cdot V^{1/3}$ | Deflagration index for dust explosion | bar·m/s |
| 9 | $P_{red, max} = P_{red,1} \cdot PIV$ | Maximum reduced explosion pressure (dust) | bar |
| 10 | $V_{CJ} = \sqrt{2(\gamma^2 - 1) Q}$ | Chapman-Jouguet detonation velocity | m/s |
| 11 | $M = \dfrac{V_{flame}}{a_0}$ | Mach number (flame speed / ambient speed of sound) | dimensionless |
| 12 | $E_{chem} = m \cdot \Delta H_c$ | Total chemical energy of fuel mass | kJ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Application:** The **Hopkinson-Cranz cube-root scaling law** (formula 2) is the most powerful tool for consequence analysis. It says: two charges of the *same* explosive type at distances $r_1$ and $r_2$ produce the *same* overpressure if $r_1 / W_1^{1/3} = r_2 / W_2^{1/3}$. This is heavily tested in KTU 14-mark questions.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

These formulas are used in:

- **Facility Siting Studies** (CCPS / API RP 752 / 753): calculating safe separation distances.
- **Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA):** estimating individual and societal risk.
- **Explosion Protection Design:** sizing **explosion vents**, **suppression systems**, and **isolation barriers**.
- **Emergency Planning:** defining evacuation zones (typically 1 psi overpressure boundary).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked-Out Problem (Model 14-Mark Style):**
> A 10,000 kg propane storage sphere is catastrophically ruptured. The entire mass is released as a vapor cloud and ignites 30 seconds later, producing a UVCE. Assume the explosion isotropically releases energy with a yield factor of $\eta = 0.05$. Propane heat of combustion = **46,000 kJ/kg**. TNT energy = **4,680 kJ/kg**.
>
> **Required:**
> 1. TNT equivalent mass of the explosion.
> 2. Scaled distance from a control room located **500 m** downwind.
> 3. Peak side-on overpressure at the control room using the Brode equation.
> 4. Classify the expected damage.

---

## 3.1 Step 1: Compute Total Combustion Energy Released

$$E_{chem} = m \cdot \Delta H_c$$

$$E_{chem} = 10{,}000 \, \text{kg} \times 46{,}000 \, \text{kJ/kg}$$

$$E_{chem} = 4.6 \times 10^{8} \, \text{kJ} = 460{,}000 \, \text{MJ}$$

**Conversion logic:** Multiplying fuel mass (kg) by specific heat of combustion (kJ/kg) gives total chemical energy available, *before* accounting for explosion efficiency.

---

## 3.2 Step 2: Compute Effective Explosion Energy

We use the empirical yield factor to obtain the energy that *actually contributes* to blast overpressure:

$$E_{eff} = \eta \cdot E_{chem}$$

$$E_{eff} = 0.05 \times 4.6 \times 10^{8} \, \text{kJ}$$

$$E_{eff} = 2.3 \times 10^{7} \, \text{kJ} = 2.3 \times 10^{10} \, \text{J}$$

**Conversion logic:** Real UVCEs are highly inefficient (only a small fraction of the total heat of combustion goes into the blast wave). A 5% yield factor is standard for partially confined hydrocarbon clouds.

---

## 3.3 Step 3: Compute TNT Equivalent Mass

$$W_{TNT} = \frac{E_{eff}}{E_{TNT}}$$

$$W_{TNT} = \frac{2.3 \times 10^{7} \, \text{kJ}}{4{,}680 \, \text{kJ/kg}}$$

$$W_{TNT} = 4{,}914.53 \, \text{kg TNT} \approx 4.91 \, \text{tonnes TNT}$$

**Conversion logic:** We express the explosion's blast energy in the conventional "kilograms of TNT" unit. The denominator $E_{TNT} = 4{,}680$ kJ/kg is a measured constant from the standardization of TNT as a reference explosive.

---

## 3.4 Step 4: Compute Scaled Distance (Hopkinson-Cranz)

The control room is at $r = 500$ m.

$$Z = \frac{r}{W_{TNT}^{1/3}}$$

**First, compute the cube root:**

$$W_{TNT}^{1/3} = (4{,}914.53)^{1/3} \, \text{kg}^{1/3}$$

**Use logarithm method:**

$$\ln(4{,}914.53) = 8.499$$

$$\frac{8.499}{3} = 2.833$$

$$e^{2.833} = 16.99 \approx 17.0 \, \text{kg}^{1/3}$$

**Alternatively, direct cube-root estimation:**

$$17^3 = 4{,}913 \approx 4{,}914.53 \checkmark$$

**Therefore:**

$$Z = \frac{500 \, \text{m}}{17.0 \, \text{kg}^{1/3}} = 29.41 \, \text{m/kg}^{1/3}$$

**Conversion logic:** $Z$ is a normalized distance that allows us to use the same blast curve regardless of charge size. This is the **Hopkinson-Cranz cube-root scaling law**.

> [!NOTE]
> Since $Z = 29.4$ lies in the range $0.3 < Z < 30$, the Brode equation is **directly applicable**.

---

## 3.5 Step 5: Apply the Brode Equation for Peak Overpressure

$$\Delta P_0 = \frac{84.5}{Z} + \frac{331}{Z^2} + \frac{28}{Z^3}$$

**Substitute $Z = 29.41$:**

**Term 1:**

$$\frac{84.5}{29.41} = 2.873 \, \text{kPa}$$

**Term 2:**

$$Z^2 = (29.41)^2 = 864.95$$

$$\frac{331}{864.95} = 0.3827 \, \text{kPa}$$

**Term 3:**

$$Z^3 = (29.41)^3 = 25{,}442.0$$

$$\frac{28}{25{,}442.0} = 1.101 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{kPa} \approx 0.001 \, \text{kPa}$$

**Sum the three terms:**

$$\Delta P_0 = 2.873 + 0.3827 + 0.001 = 3.257 \, \text{kPa}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta P_0 \approx 3.26 \, \text{kPa}}$$

**Conversion logic:** The Brode equation sums three physically meaningful contributions: (1) dominant at small $Z$ → strong shock, (2) intermediate, (3) far-field contribution (negligible beyond $Z \approx 5$). The answer is small because $Z = 29.4$ is in the far-field region.

---

## 3.6 Step 6: Classify Damage

Using the standard **damage overpressure table** (CCPS / Crowl & Louvar):

| Overpressure (kPa) | Damage Description |
|---|---|
| 0.5 | Loud noise — window rattle |
| 1.0 | Window breakage (10% probability) |
| 3.5 | **Minor structural damage** to houses |
| 7.0 | Significant damage to industrial buildings |
| 17.0 | Partial collapse of walls and roofs |
| 35.0 | Reinforced concrete damage, eardrum rupture |
| 70.0 | Complete demolition |

> At $\Delta P_0 = 3.26$ kPa, the control room will experience **window breakage and minor structural damage**. The control room should be relocated to at least **1.5 km** for the 1% fatality limit.

---

## 3.7 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Deflagration vs Detonation)

| Feature | Deflagration | Detonation |
|---|---|---|
| **$V_{flame}$** | 1 – 1000 m/s | 1,500 – 3,000 m/s |
| **Mach number (M)** | 0.001 – 3 (typically < 1) | 5 – 6 |
| **Pressure behind flame** | 1 – 8 bar (in confined vessels) | 15 – 50 bar |
| **Heat transfer mode** | Conduction, radiation, diffusion | Shock compression auto-ignition |
| **Equivalence** | $V_{flame} \propto \sqrt{\alpha \, t}$ (thermal) | $V_{CJ} = \sqrt{2(\gamma^2 - 1) Q}$ |
| **Damage severity** | Vessel rupture, throw fragments | Total fragmentation, demolition |
| **Mitigation** | Venting, suppression, isolation | Inerting, prevention only |

---

## 3.8 Energy Comparison: UVCE vs BLEVE vs Dust

$$\text{UVCE energy} = \eta \cdot m \cdot \Delta H_c \quad (\eta = 0.01 \text{ to } 0.10)$$

$$\text{BLEVE energy} = m \cdot \Delta H_{vap} \quad (\text{physical only})$$

$$\text{Dust energy} = m \cdot \Delta H_c \cdot \eta_{dust} \quad (\eta_{dust} = 0.10 \text{ to } 0.40)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** BLEVE energy is *orders of magnitude smaller* than UVCE for the same mass because $\Delta H_{vap} \ll \Delta H_c$. However, the BLEVE energy is released in milliseconds with **no chemical delay**, making its pressure wave very destructive locally.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric prefixed with letters. No markdown bold/italic inside quoted labels. All uppercase alphanumeric text used for clarity.

---

## 4.1 Explosion Classification Master Flowchart

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[START - Process Explosion Event] --> nodeB{Fuel Phase?}
    nodeB -- GAS OR VAPOR --> nodeC{Confined?}
    nodeB -- LIQUID SUPERHEATED --> nodeF[BLEVE - PHYSICAL]
    nodeB -- FINE PARTICULATE --> nodeG[DUST EXPLOSION]
    nodeC -- UNCONFINED IN OPEN --> nodeD[UVCE - UNCONFINED VAPOR CLOUD]
    nodeC -- FULLY CONFINED VESSEL --> nodeE[DEFLAGRATION OR DETONATION]
    nodeD --> nodeH[Flame Front Accelerates by Congestion]
    nodeE --> nodeI{Flame Speed?}
    nodeG --> nodeJ{Pentagon - Fuel Oxidizer Dispersion Confinement Ignition}
    nodeH --> nodeK[Blast Wave Generated - Overpressure]
    nodeF --> nodeL[Superheated Liquid Flashing to Vapor]
    nodeI -- SUBSONIC --> nodeM[DEFLAGRATION]
    nodeI -- SUPERSONIC --> nodeN[DETONATION]
    nodeJ --> nodeO[Use Kst and Pmax for Design]
    nodeK --> nodeP[TNT Equivalent Calculation]
    nodeL --> nodeQ[Fireball Radius and Duration]
    nodeM --> nodeR[Vent Sizing and Suppression]
    nodeN --> nodeS[Prevention by Inerting Only]
```

---

## 4.2 UVCE Consequence Analysis Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    inp1[FUEL MASS M KG] --> calc1[Total Combustion Energy E = M times Delta Hc]
    inp2[Yield Factor Eta 0.01 to 0.10] --> calc1
    calc1 --> calc2[Effective Blast Energy Eeff]
    inp3[TNT Energy 4680 kJ per kg] --> calc3[TNT Equivalent Mass WTNT]
    calc2 --> calc3
    calc3 --> calc4[Hopkinson Scaled Distance Z equals r over WTNT to the 1/3]
    inp4[Receiver Distance r meters] --> calc4
    calc4 --> calc5[Apply Brode or Sachs Equation]
    calc5 --> out1[Peak Overpressure Delta P in kPa]
    calc5 --> out2[Damage Classification Window Breakage to Demolition]
```

---

## 4.3 Deflagration to Detonation Transition DDT Timeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    st1[Stage 1 - Ignition - Small Flame Kernel] --> st2[Stage 2 - Laminar Flame Growth]
    st2 --> st3[Stage 3 - Onset of Turbulence in Congestion]
    st3 --> st4[Stage 4 - Flame Stretching and Acceleration]
    st4 --> st5[Stage 5 - Compression Wave Formation in Unburnt Gas]
    st5 --> st6[Stage 6 - Local Autoignition in Hot Spots]
    st6 --> st7[Stage 7 - Detonation Onset at Obstacle]
    st7 --> st8[Stage 8 - Steady Detonation Wave at CJ Velocity]
    st1 -- Velocity approx 1 to 10 m per s --> label1
    st4 -- Velocity approx 100 to 500 m per s --> label2
    st8 -- Velocity approx 1800 to 2000 m per s --> label3
```

---

## 4.4 BLEVE Fireball Damage Radius Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    inputA[Vessel Liquid Mass M kg] --> inputB[Superheat Temperature Above Boiling Point]
    inputB --> proc1[Catastrophic Vessel Failure at Vapor Space]
    proc1 --> proc2[Flash Vaporization of Superheated Liquid]
    proc2 --> out1[Fireball Radius Rfb equals 2.9 times M to the 1/3]
    proc2 --> out2[Fireball Duration Tfb equals 0.45 times M to the 1/3 for M less than 30000 kg]
    out1 --> final1[Radiation Heat Flux Zone]
    out2 --> final2[Total BLEVE Energy equals M times Delta Hvap]
    final1 --> final3[Determine Safe Separation Distance]
    final2 --> final3
```

---

## 4.5 Dust Explosion Protection Decision Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    start[Dust Material Available] --> test1[Determine Kst Value bar meter per second]
    test1 --> class{St Class?}
    class -- Kst less than 200 --> c1[St Class 1 - Low Hazard]
    class -- Kst between 200 and 300 --> c2[St Class 2 - Medium Hazard]
    class -- Kst greater than 300 --> c3[St Class 3 - Strong Hazard]
    c1 --> prot1[Use Standard Venting]
    c2 --> prot2[Use Suppression plus Venting]
    c3 --> prot3[Use Isolation plus Suppression plus Venting]
    prot1 --> end1[Acceptable Risk]
    prot2 --> end1
    prot3 --> end1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define UVCE. What are the conditions for a UVCE to occur? [KTU University Exam - May 2024, 3 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

A **Unconfined Vapor Cloud Explosion (UVCE)** is the combustion of a large flammable vapor cloud that is released into the open atmosphere (i.e., without any confinement) and subsequently ignited. For a UVCE to occur, the following conditions must be met simultaneously:

1. A **large release** of flammable vapor (typically > 1,000 kg) within a short time.
2. The vapor must **disperse** and partially mix with air to form a flammable mixture (between LEL and UEL).
3. **Delayed ignition** must occur (immediate ignition gives only a flash fire, not a UVCE).
4. The flame must encounter some **turbulence or partial confinement** (pipe racks, structures) to accelerate and generate damaging overpressure.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students wrongly state that UVCE occurs in *confined* vessels. By definition, a UVCE is **unconfined** — that is the "U" in the acronym. Confinement is *partial* (from obstacles) and not full (vessel walls).

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between Deflagration and Detonation. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, 3 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Deflagration | Detonation |
|---|---|---|
| Flame propagation speed | Subsonic (< 1 Mach) | Supersonic (> 1 Mach, ~1,800–2,000 m/s) |
| Pressure rise | 1–8 bar | 15–50 bar |
| Mechanism | Heat & diffusion driven | Shock compression auto-ignition |
| Damage severity | Moderate | Catastrophic |
| Mitigation possible | Yes — venting, suppression | No — prevention only by inerting |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Do not merely state that "deflagration is slow and detonation is fast." Examiners expect the *coupling/decoupling* of flame and shock front, the *Chapman-Jouguet theory* reference, and at least one numerical value (e.g., Mach number or pressure).

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2 Internal Choice A, 14 Marks]**

> *A 25,000 kg spherical propane storage tank is catastrophically ruptured. The vapor cloud ignites after 45 seconds. Compute the overpressure at 800 m, 1200 m, and 2000 m from the cloud center. Assume $\eta = 0.04$, $\Delta H_c$ for propane = 46,000 kJ/kg, and use the Brode equation. Also state the expected damage at each location. [14 Marks]*

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Compute TNT Equivalent and Overpressure at 800 m and 1200 m [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Total combustion energy.**

$$E_{chem} = m \cdot \Delta H_c = 25{,}000 \times 46{,}000 = 1.15 \times 10^9 \, \text{kJ}$$

**Step 2: Effective explosion energy.**

$$E_{eff} = \eta \cdot E_{chem} = 0.04 \times 1.15 \times 10^9 = 4.6 \times 10^7 \, \text{kJ}$$

**Step 3: TNT equivalent mass.**

$$W_{TNT} = \frac{4.6 \times 10^7}{4{,}680} = 9{,}829.06 \, \text{kg TNT}$$

**Step 4: Cube root.**

$$W_{TNT}^{1/3} = (9{,}829)^{1/3} \approx 21.4 \, \text{kg}^{1/3}$$

**Step 5: Scaled distances and overpressures.**

For $r = 800$ m:

$$Z_{800} = \frac{800}{21.4} = 37.38 \, \text{m/kg}^{1/3}$$

$$\Delta P_0 = \frac{84.5}{37.38} + \frac{331}{1397.3} + \frac{28}{52229.6} = 2.26 + 0.237 + 0.0005 = 2.50 \, \text{kPa}$$

For $r = 1200$ m:

$$Z_{1200} = \frac{1200}{21.4} = 56.07 \, \text{m/kg}^{1/3}$$

$$\Delta P_0 = \frac{84.5}{56.07} + \frac{331}{3143.8} + \frac{28}{176280} = 1.507 + 0.105 + 0.0002 = 1.61 \, \text{kPa}$$

#### Part (b) — Overpressure at 2000 m and Damage Classification [7 Marks]

For $r = 2000$ m:

$$Z_{2000} = \frac{2000}{21.4} = 93.46 \, \text{m/kg}^{1/3}$$

**Note:** The Brode equation is strictly valid for $0.3 < Z < 30$. Since $Z = 93.5$ is outside this range, we use the **far-field approximation**:

$$\Delta P_0 \approx \frac{84.5}{Z} = \frac{84.5}{93.46} = 0.904 \, \text{kPa}$$

**Damage Summary:**

| Distance | $\Delta P_0$ (kPa) | Damage |
|---|---|---|
| 800 m | 2.50 | Window breakage, minor structural |
| 1200 m | 1.61 | Window rattle, glass damage |
| 2000 m | 0.90 | Loud noise, no structural damage |

**Conclusion:** Personnel at 800 m are at risk; emergency response should be staged beyond **1,500 m**.

---

### QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2 Internal Choice B, 14 Marks]**

> *(a) Explain the phenomenon of BLEVE in detail. Derive the expressions for the fireball radius and the duration of the fireball. A BLEVE of 5,000 kg of LPG occurs. Calculate the fireball radius and duration. [7 Marks]*
> *(b) Differentiate between a deflagration and a detonation. A stoichiometric propane-air mixture is ignited in a tube. The heat of combustion is 46,000 kJ/kg. Compute the Chapman-Jouguet detonation velocity. Assume $\gamma = 1.4$. [7 Marks]*

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — BLEVE Explanation and Fireball Calculations [7 Marks]

**Phenomenon:** A **Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapor Explosion** is a physical (not chemical) explosion that occurs when a vessel containing a liquid stored above its atmospheric boiling point suffers catastrophic failure. The superheated liquid instantaneously flashes to vapor, expanding by 100–200×. The released vapor ignites from the surrounding fire, forming a fireball.

**Derivation of Fireball Radius (Roberts' correlation):**

Energy balance: the fireball energy equals the heat released by combustion of the vaporized fuel (or flash vaporization energy):

$$E_{fb} = m \cdot \Delta H_c \cdot \eta_{comb} \approx m \cdot \Delta H_c$$

The fireball is modeled as a hemispherical cloud. From dimensional analysis and experimental data (Roberts, 1982):

$$R_{fb} = k \cdot m^{1/3}$$

where the empirical constant $k$ is determined by fitting to fireball videos of actual BLEVEs (e.g., 1984 Mexico City). The standard form is:

$$R_{fb} = 2.9 \cdot m^{1/3} \quad \text{(m, with m in kg)}$$

**Derivation of Fireball Duration:**

The fireball lifetime is governed by the rate of fuel consumption within the lifted cloud. Experimentally:

$$t_{fb} = 0.45 \cdot m^{1/3} \quad \text{(s, valid for } m < 30{,}000 \text{ kg)}$$

**Calculation for 5,000 kg LPG:**

$$R_{fb} = 2.9 \times (5{,}000)^{1/3} = 2.9 \times 17.10 = 49.6 \, \text{m}$$

$$t_{fb} = 0.45 \times (5{,}000)^{1/3} = 0.45 \times 17.10 = 7.7 \, \text{s}$$

#### Part (b) — Detonation Velocity Calculation [7 Marks]

The **Chapman-Jouguet (CJ) detonation theory** assumes an ideal gas with a one-dimensional, steady-state, adiabatic flame front. The CJ detonation velocity for a perfect gas is:

$$V_{CJ} = \sqrt{2(\gamma^2 - 1) Q}$$

**Substitution:**

- $\gamma = 1.4$
- $\gamma^2 = 1.96$, $\gamma^2 - 1 = 0.96$
- $2(\gamma^2 - 1) = 1.92$
- $Q = \Delta H_c = 46{,}000 \, \text{kJ/kg} = 4.6 \times 10^7 \, \text{J/kg}$

**Compute:**

$$V_{CJ} = \sqrt{1.92 \times 4.6 \times 10^7}$$

$$1.92 \times 4.6 \times 10^7 = 8.832 \times 10^7 \, \text{m}^2/\text{s}^2$$

$$V_{CJ} = \sqrt{8.832 \times 10^7} \approx 9{,}399 \, \text{m/s}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Reality Check / Examiner's Caveat:** The computed $V_{CJ} = 9{,}399$ m/s is significantly higher than the **experimentally observed** detonation velocity in propane-air mixtures (~$1{,}800$ m/s). This discrepancy occurs because the simple CJ formula uses $Q$ as the *total* heat of combustion, but in real detonations, the effective $Q$ is much smaller due to incomplete reaction behind the leading shock. For KTU 14-mark answers, the value $V_{CJ} \approx 1{,}800$–$2{,}000$ m/s should be quoted for hydrocarbons. The formula above is acceptable when specifically asked to derive it.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Critical Pitfalls)

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Zones — Memorize Before Exam:**
>
> 1. **Yield Factor $\eta$:** Do not assume $\eta = 1$ (which is an *ideal* explosion). For UVCEs, $\eta$ is typically **0.01–0.10**. The default in the absence of data is **0.05**.
> 2. **TNT Energy Constant:** Always use $E_{TNT} = 4{,}680$ kJ/kg. Some students use 4,200 or 4,500 kJ/kg — instant loss of 1 mark.
> 3. **Brode vs. Sachs Equations:** The Brode equation is valid for $0.3 < Z < 30$ m/kg$^{1/3}$. Do not apply it for $Z > 30$; use the far-field approximation $\Delta P_0 \approx 84.5/Z$.
> 4. **BLEVE Energy:** Use $\Delta H_{vap}$ (heat of vaporization), **not** $\Delta H_c$ (heat of combustion). The 2 are different by an order of magnitude.
> 5. **Dust Kst Units:** Always write in **bar·m/s**. A unit of kPa·m/s will be marked wrong.
> 6. **DDT:** When asked to discuss *deflagration-to-detonation transition*, mention **congestion, obstacle density, and run-up distance** — these are the textbook triggers.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Print Before Exam):**

- **UVCE** = *unconfined* vapor cloud explosion. Yield factor $\eta$ = 0.01 to 0.10. TNT method is standard.
- **BLEVE** = *physical* explosion of superheated liquid. Fireball radius $R_{fb} = 2.9 \cdot m^{1/3}$. Duration $t_{fb} = 0.45 \cdot m^{1/3}$ (m in kg).
- **Dust Explosion Pentagon** = Fuel + Oxidizer + Ignition + **Dispersion** + **Confinement**.
- **Kst Classes:** St 1 (< 200), St 2 (200–300), St 3 (> 300) bar·m/s.
- **Deflagration** = subsonic (M < 1), diffusion-driven. **Detonation** = supersonic (M > 1), shock-driven. CJ velocity for hydrocarbons ≈ 1,800–2,000 m/s.
- **TNT Equivalency Equation:** $W_{TNT} = \eta \cdot m \cdot \Delta H_c / 4{,}680$.
- **Hopkinson-Cranz Scaled Distance:** $Z = r / W_{TNT}^{1/3}$.
- **Brode Equation:** $\Delta P_0 = 84.5/Z + 331/Z^2 + 28/Z^3$ (units: kPa, for $0.3 < Z < 30$).
- **DDT** is the worst-case explosion scenario and is triggered by congestion, partial confinement, and long run-up distances.
- **Damaging Overpressure Thresholds:** 7 kPa (building damage), 35 kPa (ear drum rupture), 70 kPa (demolition).
- **BLEVE Energy** = $m \cdot \Delta H_{vap}$ (NOT $\Delta H_c$).
- **CJ Detonation Velocity Formula:** $V_{CJ} = \sqrt{2(\gamma^2 - 1) Q}$.
- **Risk Mitigation:** Venting, suppression, isolation, inerting, separation distance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Mantra:** "UVCE is unconfined chemical; BLEVE is confined physical; Dust needs the Pentagon; Deflagration is subsonic; Detonation is supersonic." Memorize this line and the KTU examiner will award full marks for definitional accuracy.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Toxic Release and Runaway Reaction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT723 - PROCESS SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Toxic Release and Runaway Reaction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Toxic Release — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Toxic Release** is defined by KTU/CCPS (Center for Chemical Process Safety) as the *uncontrolled liberation of a chemical substance into the environment in a quantity, concentration, or rate that exceeds permissible exposure thresholds and poses acute or chronic health hazards to human populations, biota, or ecological receptors.*

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, a **toxic release** is an event where a hazardous chemical (gas, vapor, liquid aerosol, or fine particulate) escapes its containment boundary — whether due to vessel rupture, flange leak, valve failure, hose rupture, pipe break, or overfilling — and disperses into the workplace, plant boundary, or off-site community.

### 1.1.1 Key Toxicological Exposure Metrics (KTU High-Yield)

| Metric | Full Form | Meaning | Exposure Window |
|--------|-----------|---------|-----------------|
| **TLV-TWA** | Threshold Limit Value – Time Weighted Average | Average exposure over 8-hour workday | 8 h |
| **TLV-STEL** | Short-Term Exposure Limit | 15-min ceiling exposure not to exceed 4 times/day | 15 min |
| **TLV-C** | Threshold Limit Value – Ceiling | Absolute concentration not to be exceeded | Instantaneous |
| **PEL** | Permissible Exposure Limit (OSHA) | Legal workplace limit | 8 h TWA |
| **IDLH** | Immediately Dangerous to Life or Health | Max concentration from which one can escape within 30 min without irreversible effects | 30 min |
| **ERPG-1** | Emergency Response Planning Guideline-1 | Max airborne conc. below which nearly all individuals could be exposed for up to 1 h without experiencing more than mild, transient adverse health effects | 1 h |
| **ERPG-2** | Emergency Response Planning Guideline-2 | Max airborne conc. below which nearly all individuals could be exposed for up to 1 h without experiencing irreversible or other serious health effects | 1 h |
| **ERPG-3** | Emergency Response Planning Guideline-3 | Max airborne conc. below which nearly all individuals could be exposed for up to 1 h without experiencing or developing life-threatening health effects | 1 h |
| **AEGL-1/2/3** | Acute Exposure Guideline Levels | Federal (EPA) thresholds for general public exposure (10 min, 30 min, 1 h, 4 h, 8 h) | Multi-tier |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Specific Note:** When a question asks for "the most stringent" or "most conservative" toxic metric, ERPG-1 is generally **higher** (less conservative) than AEGL-1, which is **higher** than TLV-TWA. IDLH is typically **higher still** (more permissive for short exposure, but used differently — for escape only).

### 1.1.2 Intuitive Analogy — Toxic Release

Imagine a pressurized perfume canister rupturing in a closed bedroom. The scent (toxic vapor) immediately begins spreading (dispersion) in all directions. Initially, the concentration near the rupture is **very high** (potentially lethal), but as it travels across the room, it **dilutes** with air. People closest to the canister experience **higher dose** (concentration × time) — like the Bhopal 1984 disaster where methyl isocyanate (MIC) released and drifted into densely populated slums causing thousands of fatalities. Just as you would evacuate the bedroom and ventilate it, in industry we use **dispersion modeling + isolation distances + vapor barriers** to mitigate.

---

## 1.2 Runaway Reaction — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Runaway Reaction** is an exothermic (or autocatalytic) chemical reaction whose rate of heat generation exceeds the rate of heat removal available from the reactor, causing a self-accelerating rise in temperature and pressure that may culminate in vessel rupture, explosion, or toxic release.

KTU 2024 emphasizes this as one of the **top three causes** of catastrophic loss-of-containment events in chemical process industries, alongside static-electricity ignition and overpressure.

### 1.2.1 Why Runaway Reactions Occur — Triggering Events

1. **Loss of cooling** (cooling water pump failure, fouled heat exchanger)
2. **Agitator failure** → poor mixing, local hot spots
3. **Mischarge** of reactants (wrong ratio, double-charge)
4. **Contamination** (water in isocyanates, acids in epoxides)
5. **External fire** heating a vessel
6. **Loss of solvent** (evaporation removes heat sink)
7. **Catalyst over-dose**

### 1.2.2 Intuitive Analogy — Runaway Reaction

Consider a campfire in a tent: a small flame (reaction) generates heat. If you don't allow that heat to escape (no chimney), the tent fabric heats up, which **accelerates** the fire's burning rate, which generates more heat, which heats the fabric more — an **exponential feedback loop**. Within minutes, the entire tent is engulfed. The same thermal feedback occurs in a runaway reactor: rising T increases k (rate constant per Arrhenius), increasing r (reaction rate), increasing heat generation rate (Q_gen), which raises T further. This **positive feedback** continues until either the reactant is consumed or the vessel ruptures.

> [!NOTE]
> **Historic KTU-Favorite Case Study:** The **T2 Laboratories explosion (Jacksonville, Florida, 2007)** — a sodium methylate runaway reaction killed 4 workers. The reactor's cooling was lost; within minutes, the vessel exploded with a TNT-equivalent blast. This is the **canonical example** professors use.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Toxicology Fundamentals — Dose-Response Framework

Toxicity is fundamentally about **dose**, not just concentration:

$$\text{Dose} = C^{n} \cdot t$$

where:
- $C$ = concentration (typically ppm or mg/m³)
- $t$ = exposure duration
- $n$ = toxicological exponent (often ≈ 1 for systemic toxins, can be >1 for reactive irritants)

**KTU Important:** Many students wrongly equate **concentration** with toxicity. Always use **dose** (C·t or Cⁿ·t) when applying the Probit equation.

## 2.2 The Probit Equation (Probability Unit Method)

The Probit (Probability Unit) equation is the **standard tool** to translate toxic exposure into a probabilistic fraction of affected population:

$$Y = a + b \cdot \ln(C^{n} \cdot t)$$

where:
- $Y$ = probit variable (a transformed normal deviate)
- $a$, $b$ = empirical constants specific to the toxicant (from animal/cumulative data)
- $C$ = concentration (ppm or mg/m³)
- $t$ = exposure time (minutes)
- $n$ = toxicological exponent

The probit value $Y$ is then converted into a **percentage affected** using:

$$P = 50 \left[ 1 + \text{erf}\!\left(\frac{Y - 5}{\sqrt{2}}\right) \right]$$

For **fatality percentage** specifically:

$$P_{\text{fatality}} \approx \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \int_{-\infty}^{Y-5} e^{-u^{2}/2} \, du$$

### 2.2.1 Common KTU-Reported Probit Constants

| Toxicant | a | b | n | Source |
|----------|---|---|---|--------|
| **Chlorine (Cl₂)** | –5.30 | 0.5 | 1 | Withers & Lees (1985) |
| **Ammonia (NH₃)** | –9.82 | 1.0 | 2 | Withers & Lees |
| **Methyl Isocyanate (MIC)** | –1.75 | 1.0 | 1 | TNO Green Book |
| **Hydrogen Sulfide (H₂S)** | –11.5 | 1.0 | 1.43 | TNO Green Book |
| **Phosgene (COCl₂)** | –19.27 | 3.686 | 1 | TNO Green Book |
| **Acrylonitrile** | –8.65 | 0.78 | 1 | TNO |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Always include **units** when plugging C into the Probit equation (typically ppm for gases). Different references use mg/m³ — never mix.

## 2.3 Dispersion Models — Quick Survey

The trajectory of a toxic cloud is governed by **meteorology** (wind speed, atmospheric stability) and **buoyancy** (heavier-than-air or neutrally buoyant).

### 2.3.1 Pasquill–Gifford Gaussian Plume Model (Neutrally Buoyant)

For a continuous point release at ground level, ground-level centerline concentration is:

$$C(x, 0, 0) = \frac{Q}{\pi \cdot \sigma_y \cdot \sigma_z \cdot u}$$

where:
- $Q$ = emission rate (mg/s or g/s)
- $\sigma_y$, $\sigma_z$ = horizontal and vertical dispersion coefficients (functions of downwind distance $x$ and stability class)
- $u$ = wind speed at release height (m/s)

### 2.3.2 Dense Gas Dispersion (Britter & McQuaid Model)

For gases **denser than air** (e.g., chlorine, LPG, MIC), gravity-driven slumping dominates. The **critical Richardson number** distinguishes dense from passive:

$$Ri = \frac{g \cdot (\rho_g - \rho_a) \cdot H}{u^{2} \cdot \rho_a}$$

When $Ri \gg 1$, the cloud spreads laterally under gravity. For $Ri \lesssim 1$, behaves like passive dispersion.

## 2.4 Runaway Reaction Theory — Semenov Approach

The classic **Semenov thermal explosion theory** balances heat generation vs. heat loss.

### 2.4.1 Energy Balance on a Batch Reactor

$$\underbrace{m \cdot C_p \cdot \frac{dT}{dt}}_{\text{Accumulation}} = \underbrace{-\Delta H \cdot r}_{\text{Generation}} - \underbrace{U \cdot A \cdot (T - T_0)}_{\text{Loss}}$$

For zero-order reaction (reactant in large excess):

$$r = A \cdot e^{-E/RT}$$

### 2.4.2 The Semenov Plot

On a graph of $T$ vs. $T$:
- **Heat generation** $q_g = (-\Delta H) \cdot A \cdot e^{-E/RT}$ → exponential curve
- **Heat removal** $q_l = U \cdot A \cdot (T - T_s)$ → straight line (jacket temperature $T_s$)

The intersection of $q_g$ and $q_l$ gives steady-state operating points. When $q_g$ is tangent to $q_l$ (slopes equal), the system has reached the **critical condition** — beyond this, no intersection exists and runaway occurs.

### 2.4.3 Critical Tangency Condition

$$\left.\frac{dq_g}{dT}\right|_{T_c} = \left.\frac{dq_l}{dT}\right|_{T_c}$$

This yields the **Semenov criterion**:

$$T_c - T_s = \frac{RT_c^{2}}{E}$$

This elegant result says the critical temperature rise is approximately $RT_c^{2}/E$, independent of reaction order (zero-order case).

## 2.5 Self-Accelerating Decomposition Temperature (SADT)

SADT is the lowest ambient temperature at which a self-reactive substance decomposes self-acceleratingly in a specified commercial package (typically 50 kg). It is **measured experimentally** (UN Test H.4 / H.5) but **estimated theoretically** by:

$$\text{SADT} \approx T_{NR} - \frac{R T_{NR}^{2}}{E}$$

where $T_{NR}$ = temperature of No Return (highest temperature at which no runaway occurs).

## 2.6 Time to Maximum Rate (TMR / TMRad)

TMR is the **time** it takes an adiabatic system to reach the runaway maximum rate after a step-temperature increase. Frank-Kamenetskii approximation:

$$\text{TMRad} = \frac{C_p \cdot R T^{2}}{q \cdot E}$$

where $q$ = specific heat generation rate (W/kg). Logarithmic form (more commonly tabulated):

$$\ln(\text{TMRad}) = \frac{E}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_{D24}} \right) + \ln\!\left(\frac{C_p R T_{D24}^{2}}{q_{D24} E}\right)$$

where $T_{D24}$ = temperature at which TMR = 24 hours.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Favorite Rule:** The rule of thumb often cited is **"10°C rule"** — reaction rate doubles for every 10 °C rise (true only at certain T ranges).

## 2.7 The Phi-Factor (Thermal Inertia)

Real vessels are not pure reactant. Walls, internals, and solvent absorb heat. The **phi-factor** quantifies this:

$$\phi = \frac{m_{\text{total}} \cdot C_{p,\text{total}}}{m_{\text{reactant}} \cdot C_{p,\text{reactant}}}$$

Then the **adiabatic temperature rise** in a real vessel is:

$$\Delta T_{\text{ad,real}} = \frac{\Delta T_{\text{ad,ideal}}}{\phi}$$

A higher $\phi$ means more thermal inertia (safer); $\phi = 1$ is pure reactant.

---

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet — Consolidated Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $Y = a + b \ln(C^{n} t)$ | Probit variable | $C$ in ppm, $t$ in min |
| 2 | $P = 50\!\left[1 + \text{erf}\!\left(\frac{Y-5}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right]$ | % population affected | Use Standard Normal Table |
| 3 | $\text{Dose} = C^{n} t$ | Toxic load | ppmⁿ·min |
| 4 | $C(x,0,0) = \dfrac{Q}{\pi \sigma_y \sigma_z u}$ | Gaussian plume (centerline, ground) | Pasquill–Gifford |
| 5 | $\Delta T_{\text{ad}} = \dfrac{-\Delta H}{\rho C_p}$ | Idealized adiabatic rise | K or °C |
| 6 | $\phi = \dfrac{m_{\text{total}} C_{p,\text{total}}}{m_{\text{reactant}} C_{p,\text{reactant}}}$ | Phi-factor (thermal inertia) | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $\Delta T_{\text{ad,real}} = \Delta T_{\text{ad}}/\phi$ | Real vessel adiabatic rise | K or °C |
| 8 | $T_c - T_s = \dfrac{RT_c^{2}}{E}$ | Semenov critical rise | K |
| 9 | $\text{SADT} \approx T_{\text{NR}} - \dfrac{RT_{\text{NR}}^{2}}{E}$ | SADT estimate | K |
| 10 | $\ln(\text{TMRad}) = \dfrac{E}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T} - \dfrac{1}{T_{D24}}\right) + \text{const.}$ | Time to Max Rate | seconds |
| 11 | $k = A e^{-E/RT}$ | Arrhenius rate constant | 1/s or L/(mol·s) |
| 12 | $r = k C_{A}^{\alpha}$ | Reaction rate law | mol/(L·s) |
| 13 | $q_g = (-\Delta H) k C^{n}$ | Volumetric heat generation | W/L |
| 14 | $q_l = U A (T - T_s) / V$ | Volumetric heat removal | W/L |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Mantra:** Memorize the Probit equation, the Semenov tangency criterion, the Phi-factor, and the adiabatic temperature rise formula. Together they cover **>70%** of Module 2 numerical problems.

---

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Plant layout & siting:** Toxic release ERPG-3 contours dictate **minimum separation distances** between units and to plant boundary.
- **Vent sizing (DIERS):** Runaway reactions drive the **DIERS** methodology for emergency relief design (two-phase flow venting).
- **Hazard studies (HAZOP, LOPA):** Toxic release and runaway are two of the **top "nodes"** where deviation guides are written.
- **Storage & transport:** SADT determines **storage temperature limits** for organic peroxides, self-reactive monomers (e.g., styrene, acrylates).
- **Inherently Safer Design (ISD):** Minimize inventories, substitute less-toxic materials, attenuate to avoid both categories.

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Adiabatic Temperature Rise ($\Delta T_{\text{ad}}$)

We begin with an **adiabatic closed system** (no heat exchange with surroundings). Starting from the **steady-state energy balance on a batch reactor**:

$$m C_p \frac{dT}{dt} = (-\Delta H) \, r V$$

For an adiabatic (no heat loss) reaction converting a stoichiometric reactant $A$ → products, after **complete conversion**, the entire enthalpy of reaction appears as sensible heat of the mixture:

$$Q_{\text{released}} = (-\Delta H) \, n_{A,0} = (-\Delta H) \, n_{A,0}$$

where $n_{A,0}$ is the initial moles of limiting reactant.

This heat raises the temperature of the total reacting mass:

$$Q_{\text{absorbed}} = m_{\text{total}} \cdot C_{p,\text{total}} \cdot \Delta T_{\text{ad}}$$

Setting released = absorbed (energy conservation):

$$(-\Delta H) \, n_{A,0} = m_{\text{total}} \, C_{p,\text{total}} \, \Delta T_{\text{ad}}$$

Dividing both sides by $m_{\text{total}} C_{p,\text{total}}$:

$$\Delta T_{\text{ad}} = \frac{(-\Delta H) \, n_{A,0}}{m_{\text{total}} \, C_{p,\text{total}}}$$

For a single-component system (pure reactant, $m_{\text{total}} = m_A$, $n_{A,0} = m_A / M_A$, $C_{p,\text{total}} = C_{p,A}$):

$$\Delta T_{\text{ad}} = \frac{(-\Delta H)}{M_A \, C_{p,A}}$$

For a mixture (e.g., reactant + solvent), the **volumetric** form is more convenient. Using solution density $\rho$ and molar concentration $[A]_0$:

$$\Delta T_{\text{ad}} = \frac{(-\Delta H) \, [A]_0}{\rho \, C_p}$$

This is the **canonical formula** you will see in every KTU problem.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Probit Percentage Conversion (Inverse Standard Normal)

Given a probit value $Y$ from the dose-response equation, we want the **fraction of population affected**.

The probit transformation defines:

$$Y = 5 + \frac{1}{\sigma}\,(x - \mu)$$

for some response variable $x$ with mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$. The cumulative distribution function (CDF) of the **standard normal** is:

$$\Phi(z) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \int_{-\infty}^{z} e^{-u^{2}/2}\, du$$

The fraction affected corresponds to the upper-tail probability above threshold $z = Y - 5$:

$$P_{\text{affected}} = \Phi(Y - 5) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \int_{-\infty}^{Y-5} e^{-u^{2}/2}\, du$$

Using the **error function** definition $\text{erf}(z) = \frac{2}{\sqrt{\pi}}\int_0^z e^{-u^2}\,du$, and noting $\Phi(z) = \frac{1}{2}\left[1 + \text{erf}\!\left(\frac{z}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right]$:

$$P_{\text{affected}} = \frac{1}{2}\left[1 + \text{erf}\!\left(\frac{Y - 5}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right]$$

Equivalently, multiplying by 100 to express as **percentage**:

$$\boxed{\,\%_{\text{affected}} = 50\left[1 + \text{erf}\!\left(\frac{Y - 5}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right]\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering shortcut:** Use Standard Normal Tables (Z-tables). If $Z = Y - 5$, look up $\Phi(Z)$ in the table → multiply by 100 → that's the % fatality.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Semenov Critical Tangency Criterion

Start with heat generation (zero-order, constant reactant):

$$q_g(T) = Q_0 \, k(T) = Q_0 \, A \, e^{-E/RT}$$

and heat loss (linear in T):

$$q_l(T) = U A \, (T - T_s)$$

**Steady-state operating points** require $q_g = q_l$:

$$Q_0 \, A \, e^{-E/RT} = U A \, (T - T_s)$$

**Stability** requires that any temperature perturbation decays. Linearizing $q_g$ around operating point $T^*$:

$$\frac{dq_g}{dT}\Big|_{T^*} = Q_0 A \cdot \frac{E}{RT^{*2}} \, e^{-E/RT^*} = \frac{E}{RT^{*2}} \, q_g(T^*)$$

For stability, the slope of $q_g$ must be **less** than the slope of $q_l$ at the operating point:

$$\frac{dq_g}{dT}\Big|_{T^*} < \frac{dq_l}{dT}\Big|_{T^*}$$

Substituting:

$$\frac{E}{RT^{*2}} \, q_g(T^*) < U A$$

At the **critical** (tangent) condition, the slopes are **equal**:

$$\frac{E}{RT_c^{2}} \, q_g(T_c) = U A$$

Combined with the tangency equation $q_g(T_c) = U A (T_c - T_s)$:

$$\frac{E}{RT_c^{2}} \cdot U A \, (T_c - T_s) = U A$$

Cancel $U A$:

$$\frac{E (T_c - T_s)}{R T_c^{2}} = 1$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\,T_c - T_s = \frac{R T_c^{2}}{E}\,}$$

This remarkable result shows the **critical temperature rise** depends only on $T_c$, $R$, and $E$ — **not** on reactant concentration or heat transfer coefficient. Highly exam-relevant.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Phi-Factor and the Time to Maximum Rate

Consider a well-mixed batch reactor with reacting mixture + vessel walls + internals. Heat balance:

$$\underbrace{(m_r C_{p,r} + m_w C_{p,w} + m_s C_{p,s})}_{\text{total thermal mass}} \frac{dT}{dt} = (-\Delta H) \, r \, V$$

Define:

$$\phi = \frac{m_r C_{p,r} + m_w C_{p,w} + m_s C_{p,s}}{m_r C_{p,r}}$$

Re-write the energy balance:

$$m_r C_{p,r} \, \phi \, \frac{dT}{dt} = (-\Delta H) \, A \, e^{-E/RT} \, C^{n} \, V$$

Now, an **idealized** system (just reactant, $\phi = 1$) has:

$$m_r C_{p,r} \, \frac{dT}{dt} = (-\Delta H) \, A \, e^{-E/RT} \, C^{n} \, V$$

Therefore **real system temperature rise** is slower by factor $\phi$:

$$\frac{dT}{dt}\Big|_{\text{real}} = \frac{1}{\phi} \frac{dT}{dt}\Big|_{\text{ideal}}$$

For the adiabatic case, after full conversion:

$$\Delta T_{\text{ad,real}} = \frac{\Delta T_{\text{ad,ideal}}}{\phi}$$

For **TMRad**, the integral solution (Townsend & Tou) is:

$$\text{TMRad} = \frac{\phi \, C_p \, R T^{2}}{q \, E}$$

In logarithmic form (often used when plotting $\ln(\text{TMRad})$ vs. $1/T$):

$$\ln(\text{TMRad}) = \frac{E}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T} + \text{const.}$$

This linearizes on Arrhenius axes — the slope is $E/R$, giving a powerful experimental diagnostic.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — KTU Problem-Solving Decision Tree

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  PROBLEM: Toxic Release OR Runaway Reaction?                   │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
              │
              ▼
   ┌──────────────────────┐         ┌──────────────────────────┐
   │ Toxic Release given? │         │ Runaway Reaction given?  │
   └──────────────────────┘         └──────────────────────────┘
              │                                  │
              ▼                                  ▼
   Step 1: Compute Dose                 Step 1: Compute ΔT_ad
   Dose = Cⁿ × t                        ΔT_ad = -ΔH / (ρ·Cp)
              │                                  │
              ▼                                  ▼
   Step 2: Plug into Probit              Step 2: Compute φ-factor
   Y = a + b·ln(Dose)                    φ = m_tot·Cp_tot/(m_r·Cp_r)
              │                                  │
              ▼                                  ▼
   Step 3: Z = Y − 5                     Step 3: Compute critical T_c
   Lookup Φ(Z) → % affected              T_c − T_s = R·T_c²/E
              │                                  │
              ▼                                  ▼
   Step 4: Compare to ERPG/AEGL          Step 4: Compute TMRad
   Decide mitigation / siting             ln(TMR) = (E/R)(1/T) + c
                                          │
                                          ▼
                                  Step 5: Vent sizing via DIERS
```

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example — Probit for Chlorine Release

**Problem:** 200 kg of chlorine is released instantaneously from a ruptured tank in an open plant. Downwind at 500 m, the concentration (averaged over the 30-second cloud passage) is 60 ppm. Using the Probit constants for Cl₂ ($a = -5.30$, $b = 0.5$, $n = 1$), calculate the **percentage fatality** expected.

**Step 1: Compute the toxic dose.**

$$\text{Dose} = C^{n} \cdot t = (60)^{1} \cdot (30) = 1800 \text{ ppm·min}$$

**Step 2: Plug into Probit.**

$$Y = a + b \ln(\text{Dose}) = -5.30 + 0.5 \cdot \ln(1800)$$

$$\ln(1800) = 7.4955$$

$$Y = -5.30 + 0.5 \cdot 7.4955 = -5.30 + 3.748 = -1.552$$

**Step 3: Convert to Z-score.**

$$Z = Y - 5 = -1.552 - 5 = -6.552$$

**Step 4: Lookup Standard Normal Table.**

$$\Phi(-6.552) \approx 0 \text{ (essentially negligible)}$$

$$\boxed{\,\% \text{ fatality} \approx 0\,\%\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** Notice that even at 60 ppm for 30 seconds (a relatively mild exposure), fatality is essentially zero. Toxic releases kill primarily when concentrations are **very high** OR exposure times are **long**.

---

## 3.7 Worked Numerical Example — Adiabatic Temperature Rise

**Problem:** Methyl ethyl ketone peroxide (MEKPO) is a reactive initiator with $\Delta H = -1500$ kJ/kg, $C_p = 2.0$ kJ/(kg·K), and a 30% solution density of 1100 kg/m³ in dimethyl phthalate. Compute the adiabatic temperature rise of (a) the pure MEKPO, and (b) the commercial solution.

**Step 1: Pure MEKPO.**

$$\Delta T_{\text{ad}} = \frac{-\Delta H}{C_p} = \frac{1500 \text{ kJ/kg}}{2.0 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}} = 750 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2: Commercial solution.** Need phi-factor. With 30 wt% MEKPO + 70 wt% solvent:

- $C_{p,\text{solvent}} \approx 1.5$ kJ/(kg·K)
- $m_r C_{p,r}$ per kg of solution = $0.30 \times 2.0 = 0.60$ kJ/K
- $m_s C_{p,s}$ per kg of solution = $0.70 \times 1.5 = 1.05$ kJ/K
- $m_{\text{total}} C_{p,\text{total}} = 0.60 + 1.05 = 1.65$ kJ/K per kg

$$\phi = \frac{1.65}{0.60} = 2.75$$

$$\Delta T_{\text{ad,real}} = \frac{750}{2.75} = 272.7 \text{ K}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The dilution reduces runaway severity from 750 K to 273 K — a **2.75× safety factor** purely from formulation (a textbook Inherently Safer Design example).

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 2-Mark Short Answer Questions (Concept Recall)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define the term "Runaway Reaction" and list any four common causes of runaway reactions in chemical reactors.**

**Model Answer:**
A runaway reaction is an exothermic chemical reaction whose rate of heat generation exceeds the rate of heat removal from the reactor, leading to uncontrolled self-accelerating rise in temperature and pressure.
Causes:
1. Loss of cooling water supply
2. Agitator failure leading to local hot spots
3. Mischarge of reactants (incorrect stoichiometric ratio)
4. Contamination (e.g., water in isocyanates)
5. External fire engulfing the vessel
6. Catalyst over-dose

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between ERPG-1, ERPG-2, and ERPG-3 with their health-effect thresholds.**

**Model Answer:**
- **ERPG-1:** Maximum airborne concentration below which nearly all individuals could be exposed for up to 1 hour **without experiencing more than mild, transient adverse health effects**.
- **ERPG-2:** Maximum airborne concentration below which nearly all individuals could be exposed for up to 1 hour **without experiencing irreversible or other serious health effects**.
- **ERPG-3:** Maximum airborne concentration below which nearly all individuals could be exposed for up to 1 hour **without experiencing life-threatening health effects**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Descriptive / Numerical Questions

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Read before attempting.**
> 1. **Always show unit conversions** (ppm vs mg/m³, °C vs K) — failure to do so costs 2 marks.
> 2. **Probit constants must be cited with source** (TNO Green Book / Withers & Lees). No source = 1 mark penalty.
> 3. **Semenov derivation** — students often forget the *zero-order* assumption. State it explicitly.
> 4. **Phi-factor** is **NOT** 1 in real systems. Marks lost when students compute ΔT_ad without it.
> 5. **Z-table lookup** must be shown with the Z value. Do not write only the answer.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Module 2 Choice]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, explain the **Semenov thermal explosion theory** and derive the **critical temperature rise** criterion. State the assumptions clearly.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An exothermic liquid-phase reaction has $E = 80$ kJ/mol, $A = 10^{13}$ min⁻¹, $\Delta H = -200$ kJ/mol, and the jacket temperature is maintained at $T_s = 350$ K. Compute the **critical temperature $T_c$** at which runaway just begins. (Use $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K.)

#### Model Solution (a):

**Assumptions of Semenov Theory:**
1. Reactor is a single lumped system (uniform T throughout)
2. Reaction is **zero-order** (concentration assumed constant — valid for high initial concentration / large excess)
3. Heat loss follows Newton's law of cooling: $q_l = UA(T - T_s)$
4. Heat generation follows Arrhenius: $q_g = Q_0 A e^{-E/RT}$
5. No temperature gradient within vessel

**Energy balance:**

$$m C_p \frac{dT}{dt} = (-\Delta H) A e^{-E/RT} V - U A (T - T_s)$$

**Sketch description (Semenov Plot):**
- X-axis: Temperature $T$
- Y-axis: Heat rate $q$
- $q_g$ curve: exponential, increasing steeply
- $q_l$ line: straight line with slope $UA$, intercept $-U A T_s$
- **Subcritical case:** line cuts curve at two stable points (1 and 3), unstable at 2.
- **Critical case:** line tangent to curve at $T_c$ — single point of contact.
- **Supercritical case:** line lies below curve everywhere → runaway.

**Derivation:** At tangency,
$$q_g(T_c) = q_l(T_c)$$
$$\frac{dq_g}{dT}\Big|_{T_c} = \frac{dq_l}{dT}\Big|_{T_c}$$

Computing derivatives:

$$\frac{dq_g}{dT} = q_g \cdot \frac{E}{RT^{2}} \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{dq_l}{dT} = U A$$

At tangency:

$$q_g \cdot \frac{E}{RT_c^{2}} = U A$$

Substituting $q_g = U A (T_c - T_s)$:

$$U A (T_c - T_s) \cdot \frac{E}{RT_c^{2}} = U A$$

$$\boxed{\,T_c - T_s = \frac{RT_c^{2}}{E}\,}$$

#### Model Solution (b):

Given:
- $E = 80{,}000$ J/mol
- $T_s = 350$ K
- $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)

Substitute into the Semenov equation:

$$T_c - 350 = \frac{8.314 \times T_c^{2}}{80{,}000}$$

$$T_c - 350 = 1.03925 \times 10^{-4} \cdot T_c^{2}$$

Rearrange:

$$1.03925 \times 10^{-4} T_c^{2} - T_c + 350 = 0$$

Solve the quadratic:

$$a = 1.03925 \times 10^{-4}, \quad b = -1, \quad c = 350$$

Discriminant:

$$D = b^{2} - 4ac = 1 - 4(1.03925 \times 10^{-4})(350)$$
$$D = 1 - 0.14550 = 0.8545$$
$$\sqrt{D} = 0.9244$$

$$T_c = \frac{1 \pm 0.9244}{2 \times 1.03925 \times 10^{-4}}$$

Taking the lower root (physically meaningful — below $T_c$ system is safe):

$$T_c = \frac{1 - 0.9244}{2.0785 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{0.0756}{2.0785 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\boxed{\,T_c \approx 363.7 \text{ K}\,}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Module 2 Choice]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Probit equation** in detail. Define each term. How is the **percentage of affected population** calculated from the probit value $Y$? Using the probit constants for chlorine ($a = -5.30$, $b = 0.5$, $n = 1$), calculate the percentage fatality when the toxic dose is $5 \times 10^{6}$ ppm·min.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A runaway reactor contains 1000 kg of reaction mixture. The pure reactant has $C_p = 2.5$ kJ/(kg·K), $\Delta H = -150$ kJ/mol, $M = 100$ g/mol. The vessel + internals + solvent contribute an effective mass of 400 kg with $C_p = 3.0$ kJ/(kg·K). Compute (i) the phi-factor, (ii) the adiabatic temperature rise for the system, and (iii) the final adiabatic temperature if the initial temperature is 50 °C.

#### Model Solution (a):

**Probit Equation:**
$$Y = a + b \ln(C^{n} t)$$

Definitions:
- $Y$ = probit variable (probability unit)
- $a$ = empirical constant (intercept)
- $b$ = empirical constant (slope)
- $C$ = concentration (ppm)
- $t$ = exposure time (min)
- $n$ = toxicological exponent

**% Affected from Y:**

$$\%_{\text{affected}} = 50 \left[1 + \text{erf}\!\left(\frac{Y - 5}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right]$$

For chlorine at Dose = $5 \times 10^{6}$ ppm·min:

$$Y = -5.30 + 0.5 \cdot \ln(5 \times 10^{6})$$

$$\ln(5 \times 10^{6}) = \ln(5) + \ln(10^{6}) = 1.6094 + 13.8155 = 15.425$$

$$Y = -5.30 + 0.5 \times 15.425 = -5.30 + 7.7125 = 2.4125$$

Compute Z:

$$Z = Y - 5 = 2.4125 - 5 = -2.5875$$

From Standard Normal Table:
$$\Phi(-2.59) \approx 0.0048 = 0.48\%$$

$$\boxed{\,\% \text{ fatality} \approx 0.48\%\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Even with an enormous dose of $5 \times 10^{6}$ ppm·min, chlorine's relatively gentle probit slope ($b = 0.5$) keeps fatality low. Highly reactive toxins (phosgene, $b \approx 3.7$) yield dramatically different numbers.

---

#### Model Solution (b):

**Given:**
- $m_{\text{reactant}} = 1000$ kg, $C_{p,r} = 2.5$ kJ/(kg·K)
- $m_{\text{inerts}} = 400$ kg, $C_{p,\text{inerts}} = 3.0$ kJ/(kg·K)
- $\Delta H = -150{,}000$ J/mol, $M = 0.1$ kg/mol

**(i) Phi-factor:**

$$\phi = \frac{m_r C_{p,r} + m_{\text{inerts}} C_{p,\text{inerts}}}{m_r C_{p,r}}$$

$$\phi = \frac{(1000)(2.5) + (400)(3.0)}{(1000)(2.5)} = \frac{2500 + 1200}{2500} = \frac{3700}{2500} = 1.48$$

$$\boxed{\,\phi = 1.48\,}$$

**(ii) Adiabatic temperature rise (idealized):**

$$\Delta T_{\text{ad,ideal}} = \frac{-\Delta H}{M \cdot C_{p,r}} = \frac{150{,}000 \text{ J/mol}}{0.1 \text{ kg/mol} \times 2500 \text{ J/(kg·K)}}$$

$$= \frac{150{,}000}{250} = 600 \text{ K}$$

Real system:

$$\Delta T_{\text{ad,real}} = \frac{\Delta T_{\text{ad,ideal}}}{\phi} = \frac{600}{1.48} = 405.4 \text{ K}$$

$$\boxed{\,\Delta T_{\text{ad,real}} \approx 405 \text{ K}\,}$$

**(iii) Final adiabatic temperature:**

$$T_f = T_i + \Delta T_{\text{ad,real}} = 50 + 405 = 455 \text{ °C}$$

$$\boxed{\,T_f \approx 455 \text{ °C}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical Implication:** A final adiabatic temperature of 455 °C is well above the vapor pressure limit of most solvents — catastrophic pressure rise is virtually guaranteed. Relief venting (DIERS) or reaction inhibition must be in place.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Toxic Release** = uncontrolled release of harmful chemical; mitigated via dispersion modeling, isolation distances, ERPG/AEGL.
- [x] **Dose = Cⁿ × t** (not just concentration).
- [x] **Probit equation** $Y = a + b \ln(C^n t)$; convert to % via $\% = 50[1 + \text{erf}((Y-5)/\sqrt{2})]$ or Z-table.
- [x] **Toxic load constants** differ by toxicant — **always cite source**.
- [x] **ERPG-3** is the life-threatening threshold; **AEGL-3** is the lethal threshold (federal, general public).
- [x] **IDLH** = 30-min escape limit (not a routine exposure metric).
- [x] **Runaway Reaction** = positive feedback between T and rate; triggers: cooling loss, agitator failure, mischarge, contamination.
- [x] **Semenov criterion** $T_c - T_s = RT_c^2/E$ — derived from tangency of generation and loss curves.
- [x] **Adiabatic temperature rise** $\Delta T_{\text{ad}} = -\Delta H / (\rho C_p)$ for the ideal case.
- [x] **Phi-factor** $\phi = (m_{\text{total}} C_{p,\text{total}}) / (m_r C_{p,r})$ — corrects for vessel thermal inertia.
- [x] **Real-world** $\Delta T_{\text{ad,real}} = \Delta T_{\text{ad,ideal}} / \phi$.
- [x] **SADT** = lowest ambient T causing self-accelerating decomposition (UN test H.4/H.5).
- [x] **TMRad** = time to reach maximum rate under adiabatic conditions; decreases exponentially with T (Arrhenius).
- [x] **DIERS** methodology = standard for emergency relief sizing of runaway-reaction vessels.
- [x] **Case Studies:** Bhopal (toxic), T2 Laboratories (runaway explosion), Texas City (ammonium nitrate).
- [x] **Inherently Safer Design (ISD)** reduces both toxic and runaway risks via inventory minimization, substitution, attenuation, and moderation.
- [x] **Mnemonic for exposure hierarchy:** **AEGL-1 < ERPG-1 < TLV-TWA < ERPG-2 < AEGL-2 < ERPG-3 < IDLH < AEGL-3** (most → least conservative).

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute KTU Board Tip:** When a numerical question provides probit constants **without citing the source**, default to **TNO Green Book** or **Withers & Lees (1985)** values. Showing the source earns at least one mark in valuation.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fire Pyramid
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECHT723 - PROCESS SAFETY ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Fire Pyramid # 🔥 MODULE 2: FIRE PYRAMID — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **FIRE PYRAMID (Combustion Tetrahedron):** > The **Fire Pyramid** is a three-dimensional geometric representation of the four simultaneous, interdependent elements required to initiate and sustain combustion in a chemical process. It evolves from the classical **Fire Triangle** by introducing the fourth element — the **Uninhibited Chemical Chain Reaction** — thereby transforming the flat triangular model into a volumetric tetrahedron (pyramid).
