The four elemental vertices of the Fire Pyramid are: | Vertex | Element | Process Safety Role | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Top Apex** | **Heat / Activation Energy** | Provides the minimum ignition energy to overcome the activation barrier | | **Base Left** | **Fuel** | The combustible material (vapor, gas, liquid, solid dust) | | **Base Right** | **Oxygen (Oxidizer)** | The supporter of combustion, typically air (21% O₂) | | **Front Apex** | **Self-Sustained Chain Reaction** | Free-radical propagation that allows fire to sustain itself | > [!NOTE] > **Historical Evolution:** > - **Fire Triangle** (John Newton, early 19th century): Fuel + Heat + Oxygen > - **Fire Tetrahedron/Pyramid** (John Ward, mid-20th century): Added the chemical chain reaction vertex > - In KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, the **"Fire Pyramid"** is the modern 4-element tetrahedron model used in process hazard analysis (PHA), HAZOP, and fire protection engineering. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Imagine you are trying to light a campfire in your college hostel courtyard. What do you need? > **Step 1 — Fuel:** You collect dry twigs and paper (the firewood). > **Step 2 — Oxygen:** You blow gently or ensure there's a breeze (air supply). > **Step 3 — Heat:** You strike a match or use a lighter (ignition source). > **Step 4 — Chain Reaction:** Once lit, the fire keeps going on its own — the heat vaporizes more fuel, which mixes with oxygen and burns, releasing more heat. This loop is the **chemical chain reaction**. > [!TIP] > **The "Rule of Elimination":** > To extinguish a fire, you only need to **remove ONE** of the four sides of the pyramid. This is why fire extinguishers are classified by the element they target: > - **Water** → removes Heat > - **Foam** → removes Fuel (smothers) > - **CO₂** → removes Oxygen > - **Dry Chemical Powder (DCP)** → disrupts the Chain Reaction ## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Reference Values (Must memorize for KTU exams):** > - **Limiting Oxygen Concentration (LOC):** 10–14% (varies by fuel) > - **Auto-Ignition Temperature (AIT):** Material-specific (e.g., Methane ≈ 537 °C, Hexane ≈ 225 °C) > - **Flash Point:** Lowest temperature at which a liquid gives off enough vapor to ignite > - **Fire Point:** Temperature at which sustained combustion occurs (slightly above flash point) > - **Lower/Upper Flammable Limits (LFL/UFL):** Concentration bounds in air (e.g., Methane: LFL = 5%, UFL = 15%) # 🧠 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Chemistry Behind Each Vertex ### 🔺 Vertex 1 — FUEL - **Definition:** Any material capable of undergoing rapid oxidation with release of energy. - **Forms:** Solid (wood, coal, dust), Liquid (petrol, hexane), Gas (methane, propane), Vapor (evaporated solvent). - **Process Safety Insight:** In hydrocarbon processing plants, fuel is often an unintended **flammable vapor cloud** released from a flange, pump seal, or storage tank vent. ### 🔺 Vertex 2 — OXYGEN (Oxidizer) - **Definition:** A chemical species that accepts electrons during combustion. - **Sources:** Atmospheric air (21% O₂ by volume), pure O₂ pipelines in chemical plants, oxidizing agents like **Cl₂, F₂, N₂O, H₂O₂**. - **Critical Concept — LOC (Limiting Oxygen Concentration):** The minimum O₂ concentration below which combustion cannot occur, regardless of fuel concentration. ### 🔺 Vertex 3 — HEAT (Activation Energy) - **Definition:** The minimum energy input required to break chemical bonds and initiate the reaction. - **Sources in Industry:** Hot surfaces, sparks, static electricity, open flames, electrical short circuits, exothermic chemical reactions. ### 🔺 Vertex 4 — UNINHIBITED CHAIN REACTION - **Definition:** A self-propagating sequence of free-radical reactions: $$ \text{H}^\bullet + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{OH}^\bullet + \text{O}^\bullet $$ $$ \text{O}^\bullet + \text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{OH}^\bullet + \text{H}^\bullet $$ - These radicals regenerate and accelerate, making the fire self-sustaining. ## 2.2 Why the Fire Triangle Was Insufficient The classical Fire Triangle failed to explain: 1. **Flameless combustion** (e.g., catalytic oxidation on platinum surfaces) where no flame is visible but a chain reaction still occurs. 2. **Why some gas mixtures with fuel + oxygen + heat still do not burn** (chain inhibitors present). 3. **Why dry chemical extinguishers work** — they don't remove fuel, heat, or oxygen, but they **scavenge free radicals**. Hence, the **pyramid (tetrahedron)** was introduced to include this 4th dimension. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **High-Yield Equations for KTU 2024 Process Safety Engineering:** | # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Units | Application | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | **Heat of Combustion** | $\Delta H_c = \sum H_{products} - \sum H_{reactants}$ | kJ/mol | Energy released per mole burned | | 2 | **Stoichiometric Oxygen Demand** | $n_{O_2}^{stoich} = n_C + \frac{n_H}{4} - \frac{n_O}{2}$ | mol O₂/mol fuel | Calculate air required for complete combustion | | 3 | **Mass Air-Fuel Ratio (Stoichiometric)** | $(A/F)_{stoich} = \frac{m_{air}}{m_{fuel}}$ | kg air/kg fuel | Burner design | | 4 | **Flammability Range** | $LFL \leq C_{fuel} \leq UFL$ | % volume | Determines explosion hazard envelope | | 5 | **Le Chatelier's Rule (Mixtures)** | $LFL_{mix} = \frac{1}{\sum \frac{y_i}{LFL_i}}$ | % volume | Multi-component gas flammability | | 6 | **Limiting Oxygen Concentration** | $LOC \approx \frac{LFL}{2}$ (approximation) | % O₂ | Inertion design (N₂ blanketing) | | 7 | **Heat Release Rate** | $\dot{Q} = \dot{m}_{fuel} \cdot \Delta H_c$ | kW | Fire size estimation | | 8 | **Fire Radiation Intensity** | $I = \sigma \cdot \varepsilon \cdot T^4$ | kW/m² | Thermal hazard distance | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** > Do NOT confuse the **Fire Triangle (Fuel + O₂ + Heat)** with the **Fire Pyramid/Tetrahedron (+ Chain Reaction)**. The 4-element model is mandatory for KTU 2024 scheme answers. # 📐 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Minimum Oxygen Concentration for Combustion (LOC) ### **Problem Setup** A storage vessel contains a hydrocarbon vapor at the **Lower Flammable Limit (LFL)**. We need to determine the **Limiting Oxygen Concentration (LOC)** to design a nitrogen blanketing system. ### **Step 1: Define Variables** Let: - $LFL$ = Lower Flammable Limit (% volume of fuel in air) - $LOC$ = Limiting Oxygen Concentration (% O₂ below which no combustion) ### **Step 2: Apply Stoichiometric Balance** At the LFL boundary, the fuel-to-air ratio is just sufficient for combustion. The total gas mixture consists of: $$ C_{fuel} + C_{O_2} + C_{N_2} = 100\% $$ At LFL: $$ C_{fuel} = LFL $$ ### **Step 3: Relationship Between LFL and Oxygen Demand** The stoichiometric oxygen required to burn the fuel at LFL is: $$ C_{O_2}^{required} = LFL \times \left(\frac{n_{O_2}^{stoich}}{n_{fuel}}\right) $$ ### **Step 4: Limiting Condition** At the **LOC**, the available oxygen exactly equals the stoichiometric requirement for the fuel present: $$ LOC = LFL \times \left(\frac{n_{O_2}^{stoich}}{n_{fuel}}\right) $$ ### **Step 5: Final Approximation (for paraffinic hydrocarbons)** For most hydrocarbon fuels, empirical data shows: $$ LOC \approx 0.6 \times LFL $$ **Example — Methane:** - LFL(CH₄) = 5.0% - Stoichiometric: CH₄ + 2O₂ → CO₂ + 2H₂O (needs 2 mol O₂ per mol CH₄) - Theoretical LOC = 5.0% × 2 = 10.0% - Empirical LOC ≈ 12.0% (slightly higher due to inefficiencies) ### **Step 6: Engineering Application — Nitrogen Blanketing** To safely inert a vessel containing methane vapor: $$ \% N_2 \text{ required} = 100 - LOC \approx 88\% \text{ by volume} $$ > [!TIP] > **Why this matters in Kerala:** Many refineries in Kerala (e.g., BPCL Kochi, CPCL) use nitrogen blanketing in storage tanks. This LOC calculation directly informs the **Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code** and **OISD (Oil Industry Safety Directorate)** standards. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Heat Release Rate of a Pool Fire ### **Step 1: Burning Rate Equation** The mass burning rate of a liquid pool fire is given by: $$ \dot{m}'' = \dot{m}''_{\infty} \left(1 - e^{-k \beta D}\right) $$ Where: - $\dot{m}''$ = mass burning rate per unit area (kg/m²·s) - $\dot{m}''_{\infty}$ = maximum burning rate for large pools (kg/m²·s) - $k \beta$ = empirical extinction coefficient (m⁻¹) - $D$ = pool diameter (m) ### **Step 2: Total Mass Burning Rate** $$ \dot{m} = \dot{m}'' \times A_p = \dot{m}'' \times \frac{\pi D^2}{4} $$ ### **Step 3: Heat Release Rate** $$ \dot{Q} = \dot{m} \times \Delta H_c \times \eta $$ Where: - $\Delta H_c$ = Heat of combustion (kJ/kg) - $\eta$ = Combustion efficiency (0.7–0.9 for pool fires) ### **Step 4: Worked Numerical Example** **Given:** Hexane pool fire, diameter $D = 3$ m - $\dot{m}''_{\infty} = 0.078$ kg/m²·s - $k\beta = 2.1$ m⁻¹ - $\Delta H_c = 45,000$ kJ/kg - $\eta = 0.85$ **Solution:** Step A — Calculate effective burning rate: $$ \dot{m}'' = 0.078 \times \left(1 - e^{-2.1 \times 3}\right) $$ $$ \dot{m}'' = 0.078 \times \left(1 - e^{-6.3}\right) $$ $$ \dot{m}'' = 0.078 \times (1 - 0.00184) $$ $$ \dot{m}'' = 0.0779 \text{ kg/m}^2\cdot\text{s} $$ Step B — Pool area: $$ A_p = \frac{\pi (3)^2}{4} = 7.069 \text{ m}^2 $$ Step C — Total mass burning rate: $$ \dot{m} = 0.0779 \times 7.069 = 0.5505 \text{ kg/s} $$ Step D — Heat Release Rate: $$ \dot{Q} = 0.5505 \times 45{,}000 \times 0.85 $$ $$ \boxed{\dot{Q} \approx 21{,}056 \text{ kW} \approx 21 \text{ MW}} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Context:** > This value (21 MW) classifies the fire as a **major hydrocarbon pool fire**, requiring special emergency response procedures and thermal radiation shielding per **OSHA 1910.156** and **IS 3034** standards applicable in Kerala industries. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Removing Each Pyramid Side | Pyramid Side Removed | Fire Class Targeted | Extinguishing Agent | Industrial Application | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Heat** | Class A (Ordinary combustibles) | Water, foam | Wood, paper, textile fires | | **Fuel** | Class B (Flammable liquids) | Foam, dry chemical | Oil refineries, petrochemical plants | | **Oxygen** | Class C (Electrical) | CO₂, dry chemical | Control rooms, electrical panels | | **Chain Reaction** | Class K (Cooking oils) | Wet chemical | Commercial kitchens | | **Multiple Sides** | Class D (Metals) | Special dry powder (NaCl, Cu) | Magnesium, sodium, lithium fires | > [!TIP] > **Memory trick — "FOS-CH":** > **F**uel → **O**xygen → **S**moke → **C**hain Reaction → **H**eat > Remember this for the 3-mark "list the four elements" question — KTU loves this! # 🏗️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Fire Pyramid Structure ```mermaid graph TD A[Heat / Activation Energy
Ignition Source]:::heat B[Fuel
Hydrocarbon Vapor or Liquid]:::fuel C[Oxygen / Oxidizer
Air or Pure O2]:::oxygen D[Chemical Chain Reaction
Free Radical Propagation]:::chain A --- B A --- C A --- D B --- C B --- D C --- D E[FIRE - All Four Elements Present]:::fire A --> E B --> E C --> E D --> E F[Remove Heat]:::extinguish G[Remove Fuel]:::extinguish H[Remove Oxygen]:::extinguish I[Disrupt Chain Reaction]:::extinguish F -.->|Water| E G -.->|Foam| E H -.->|CO2 Nitrogen| E I -.->|Dry Chemical| E classDef heat fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFFFFF,stroke-width:3px classDef fuel fill:#FFA500,stroke:#CC6600,color:#000000,stroke-width:3px classDef oxygen fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000080,color:#000000,stroke-width:3px classDef chain fill:#9370DB,stroke:#4B0082,color:#FFFFFF,stroke-width:3px classDef fire fill:#DC143C,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFFFFF,stroke-width:4px classDef extinguish fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000000,stroke-width:2px,stroke-dasharray:5 5 ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Fire Development Stages Pyramid ```mermaid graph TD P1[Stage 1 Incipient
Smoldering No Flame]:::stage1 P2[Stage 2 Growth
Flames Visible Heat Buildup]:::stage2 P3[Stage 3 Fully Developed
Maximum Heat Release]:::stage3 P4[Stage 4 Decay
Fuel Depletion]:::stage4 P1 ==> P2 P2 ==> P3 P3 ==> P4 T1[Time Seconds to Minutes]:::time T2[Time Minutes]:::time T3[Time Seconds to Hours]:::time T4[Time Hours]:::time T1 --- P1 T2 --- P2 T3 --- P3 T4 --- P4 A1[Action Detection Suppression]:::action A2[Action Ventilation Control]:::action A3[Action Full Emergency Response]:::action A4[Action Overhaul Watch]:::action A1 -.-> P1 A2 -.-> P2 A3 -.-> P3 A4 -.-> P4 classDef stage1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000000,stroke-width:2px classDef stage2 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#CC6600,color:#000000,stroke-width:2px classDef stage3 fill:#FF4500,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFFFFF,stroke-width:3px classDef stage4 fill:#808080,stroke:#000000,color:#FFFFFF,stroke-width:2px classDef time fill:#E0E0E0,stroke:#333333,color:#000000,stroke-width:1px,stroke-dasharray:3 3 classDef action fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,color:#000000,stroke-width:1px,stroke-dasharray:5 5 ``` ## 4.3 Process Safety Engineering Application Flow ```mermaid graph LR subgraph ID["IDENTIFICATION"] A1[Flammable Material Present]:::node --> A2[Identify All Four Pyramid Elements]:::node end subgraph AS["ASSESSMENT"] A2 --> B1[Calculate LFL UFL Flash Point]:::node B1 --> B2[Determine LOC for Inertion]:::node end subgraph CT["CONTROL"] B2 --> C1[Eliminate Ignition Sources]:::node C2[Ventilation Dilution]:::node C3[Inert Gas Blanketing]:::node C4[Detection and Suppression Systems]:::node end subgraph MN["MONITORING"] C1 --> D1[Continuous Gas Detection]:::node C2 --> D1 C3 --> D1 C4 --> D1 end classDef node fill:#E6F3FF,stroke:#0066CC,color:#000000,stroke-width:2px ``` > [!TIP] > **KTU 2024 Drawing Tip:** > In your answer sheets, always draw the **Fire Pyramid as a 3D tetrahedron** (not a flat triangle). Label all four vertices clearly and use arrows to show how removal of one side extinguishes the fire. Examiners award full 14 marks only when this 4-element structure is explicitly drawn. # 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **List the four elements of the Fire Pyramid. Explain the role of any one element in detail.** **Model Answer:** The four elements of the Fire Pyramid (Fire Tetrahedron) are: 1. **Fuel** — The combustible material that undergoes oxidation. 2. **Oxygen (Oxidizer)** — The supporter of combustion, typically atmospheric air (21% O₂). 3. **Heat (Activation Energy)** — The minimum energy required to initiate the combustion reaction. 4. **Uninhibited Chemical Chain Reaction** — The self-propagating free-radical mechanism that sustains combustion. **Detailed Role — Chemical Chain Reaction:** Once the flame is established, free radicals such as **H•, O•, and OH•** are produced. These radicals react with fuel molecules in a self-propagating cycle, releasing heat and generating more radicals. This is why fire continues to burn even after the original ignition source is removed — a critical feature that distinguishes the 4-element pyramid from the older 3-element triangle. (3 marks) --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **What is Limiting Oxygen Concentration (LOC)? Why is it important in process safety?** **Model Answer:** **Definition:** LOC is the minimum concentration of oxygen in a fuel-oxidizer-inert mixture below which combustion cannot occur, regardless of the fuel concentration. **Importance in Process Safety:** 1. **Inertion Design:** LOC determines the minimum nitrogen or CO₂ concentration required to inert storage vessels, preventing flammable atmospheres. 2. **Vessel Entry Permits:** Confined space entry in Kerala refineries (BPCL, CPCL) requires oxygen levels below LOC. 3. **Purge Calculations:** LOC guides the volume of inert gas needed during start-up and shutdown of process equipment. 4. **Regulatory Compliance:** Aligns with **OISD-118** and **OSHA 1910.119** standards for process safety management. For example, methane has an LOC of approximately 12% O₂, meaning vessels must be inerted to below this value before fuel introduction. (3 marks) --- ## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 – Modified]` #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** **With the help of a neat sketch, explain the Fire Pyramid. How does it differ from the Fire Triangle?** #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **A process vessel contains acetone vapor. The LFL of acetone is 2.6%. Calculate the approximate LOC and recommend the nitrogen blanketing requirement.** --- ### **Model Solution — Question A** #### **Part (a) Solution:** The **Fire Pyramid** (also called the Fire Tetrahedron) is a 3D representation of combustion consisting of **four simultaneous elements** that must be present for a fire to occur: ``` [HEAT] /\ / \ / \ / FIRE \ /________\ [FUEL] [OXYGEN] \ / \ / \/ [CHAIN REACTION] ``` *(In answer sheets, students should draw a proper 3D tetrahedron with all four vertices visible.)* **Differences between Fire Triangle and Fire Pyramid:** | Aspect | Fire Triangle | Fire Pyramid | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Number of elements | 3 | 4 | | Elements | Fuel, Heat, Oxygen | + Chain Reaction | | Concept origin | 19th century | Mid-20th century | | Extinguishing logic | Remove any one side | Remove any one side | | Explains flameless combustion | No | Yes | | Explains dry chemical action | No | Yes | The fourth element — **Chemical Chain Reaction** — was added because dry chemical extinguishers (e.g., monoammonium phosphate) were observed to extinguish fires without removing fuel, heat, or oxygen. They work by **scavenging free radicals** (H• and OH•) in the flame zone, breaking the self-sustaining reaction cycle. (7 marks) --- #### **Part (b) Solution:** **Given:** - LFL of acetone = 2.6% - Fuel = acetone vapor **Step 1: Stoichiometric Combustion of Acetone** Combustion equation: $$ \text{C}_3\text{H}_6\text{O} + 4\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 3\text{CO}_2 + 3\text{H}_2\text{O} $$ Moles of O₂ required per mole of acetone = **4** **Step 2: Calculate Theoretical LOC** $$ LOC = LFL \times \frac{n_{O_2}^{stoich}}{n_{fuel}} $$ $$ LOC = 2.6\% \times 4 = 10.4\% $$ **Step 3: Apply Safety Factor** Empirical LOC for acetone ≈ **11.0%** (from NFPA data, slightly higher than theoretical). $$ \boxed{LOC_{design} = 11.0\% \text{ O}_2 \text{ by volume}} $$ **Step 4: Nitrogen Blanketing Recommendation** To safely inert the vessel: $$ \% O_2 \text{ in vessel} < 11.0\% $$ $$ \% N_2 \text{ required} > 89.0\% $$ **Recommended Action:** - Introduce **N₂** until O₂ concentration falls below 11% (measured by oxygen analyzer). - Maintain positive pressure of 50–100 mm water column to prevent air ingress. - Follow **OISD-144** and **IS 3034** standards. - Use **continuous O₂ monitoring** with alarms at 12% and shutdown at 13%. For a 10 m³ vessel, the volume of N₂ required (assuming ideal gas): $$ V_{N_2} \approx V_{vessel} \times \left(\frac{21 - 11}{100}\right) \times 1.5 \text{ (safety factor)} $$ $$ V_{N_2} \approx 10 \times 0.10 \times 1.5 = 1.5 \text{ m}^3 $$ (7 marks) --- ### **Question B (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 – Modified]` #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** **Explain the different stages of fire development in the Fire Pyramid framework. Why is the "incipient stage" the most critical for fire prevention?** #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **A benzene storage tank catches fire. The pool diameter is 5 m. Calculate:** - **(i)** The heat release rate, given: $\dot{m}''_{\infty} = 0.085$ kg/m²·s, $k\beta = 3.5$ m⁻¹, $\Delta H_c = 48,500$ kJ/kg, $\eta = 0.80$. - **(ii)** The thermal radiation intensity at a distance of 20 m using the point source model. --- ### **Model Solution — Question B** #### **Part (a) Solution:** The Fire Pyramid framework includes **four stages of fire development**: **Stage 1 — Incipient Stage:** - Smoldering combustion, no visible flame - Oxygen-fuel reaction occurring at molecular level - Duration: seconds to minutes - **Critical for prevention:** Early detection (smoke detectors, heat sensors) can extinguish fire here with minimal resources. **Stage 2 — Growth Stage:** - Visible flames appear, temperature rises rapidly - Fire spreads to adjacent combustibles - Duration: minutes - Critical for suppression — still controllable with portable extinguishers. **Stage 3 — Fully Developed Stage:** - Maximum heat release rate, all fuel involved - Flashover may occur (entire room ignites) - Duration: seconds to hours - Requires full emergency response, evacuation. **Stage 4 — Decay Stage:** - Fuel depletion, oxygen depletion - Fire self-extinguishes if fuel exhausted - Duration: hours - Risk of re-ignition from hot spots, requires overhaul watch. **Why Incipient Stage is Most Critical:** 1. **Easiest suppression** — small amounts of water/foam suffice. 2. **Minimum damage** — fire hasn't spread. 3. **Lowest risk to life** — temperatures still safe for personnel. 4. **Detection systems** (VESDA, smoke sensors) are designed specifically for this stage. 5. **Cost-benefit:** Suppression at incipient stage costs <1% of fully-developed fire losses. (7 marks) --- #### **Part (b) Solution:** **Given:** - Pool diameter $D = 5$ m - $\dot{m}''_{\infty} = 0.085$ kg/m²·s - $k\beta = 3.5$ m⁻¹ - $\Delta H_c = 48,500$ kJ/kg - $\eta = 0.80$ - Distance $r = 20$ m **Part (i) — Heat Release Rate:** Step A — Effective burning rate: $$ \dot{m}'' = 0.085 \times \left(1 - e^{-3.5 \times 5}\right) $$ $$ \dot{m}'' = 0.085 \times \left(1 - e^{-17.5}\right) $$ $$ \dot{m}'' = 0.085 \times (1 - 2.4 \times 10^{-8}) \approx 0.085 \text{ kg/m}^2\cdot\text{s} $$ Step B — Pool area: $$ A_p = \frac{\pi (5)^2}{4} = 19.63 \text{ m}^2 $$ Step C — Total mass burning rate: $$ \dot{m} = 0.085 \times 19.63 = 1.669 \text{ kg/s} $$ Step D — Heat Release Rate: $$ \dot{Q} = 1.669 \times 48{,}500 \times 0.80 $$ $$ \boxed{\dot{Q} \approx 64{,}762 \text{ kW} \approx 64.8 \text{ MW}} $$ This is a **major hydrocarbon fire**, classified as a Level-3 emergency. **Part (ii) — Thermal Radiation Intensity (Point Source Model):** $$ I = \frac{\dot{Q} \times \tau}{4\pi r^2} $$ Where: - $\dot{Q} = 64,762$ kW - $\tau$ = atmospheric transmissivity ≈ 0.7 (assume clear conditions) - $r = 20$ m $$ I = \frac{64,762 \times 0.7}{4 \times \pi \times (20)^2} $$ $$ I = \frac{45,333}{5026.5} $$ $$ \boxed{I \approx 9.02 \text{ kW/m}^2} $$ **Interpretation:** - At 9.02 kW/m², second-degree burns occur in **less than 30 seconds**. - Personnel evacuation must occur beyond **40 m radius**. - Per **API RP 521**, equipment shutdown is triggered at thermal flux > 4.73 kW/m². (7 marks) --- > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING — Common Pitfalls:** > > 1. **Drawing only the Fire Triangle (3 sides):** KTU 2024 specifically tests the **4-element Fire Pyramid**. Always include the **chain reaction vertex**. > 2. **Confusing LOC with LFL:** LOC is the **oxygen** limit; LFL is the **fuel** limit. Many students interchange them — this loses 2–3 marks. > 3. **Forgetting the combustion efficiency factor (η):** Without it, your heat release rate is overestimated by 15–30%. > 4. **Not including the exponential term** $e^{-k\beta D}$ in the burning rate equation: For small pools (D < 1 m), this term is critical. > 5. **Wrong units in final answer:** Always convert kW → MW or kJ/s appropriately. Examiners are strict on SI units. --- ## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **⚡ RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Fire Pyramid (Module 2)** - [ ] **Four elements:** Fuel, Oxygen, Heat, **Chemical Chain Reaction** (NOT just three!) - [ ] **Fire Pyramid vs. Fire Triangle:** Pyramid has the 4th chain-reaction vertex. - [ ] **Fire extinguishment:** Remove ANY ONE of the four elements. - [ ] **LOC formula:** $LOC \approx LFL \times (\text{O}_2 \text{ stoichiometric ratio})$ - [ ] **Typical LOC values:** Methane ≈ 12%, Hexane ≈ 11%, Acetone ≈ 11%, Benzene ≈ 11% - [ ] **Heat Release Rate:** $\dot{Q} = \dot{m}'' \times A_p \times \Delta H_c \times \eta$ - [ ] **Burning rate:** $\dot{m}'' = \dot{m}''_{\infty} \left(1 - e^{-k\beta D}\right)$ - [ ] **Thermal radiation (point source):** $I = \frac{\dot{Q} \tau}{4\pi r^2}$ - [ ] **Fire stages:** Incipient → Growth → Fully Developed → Decay - [ ] **Incipient stage is critical** for prevention (lowest cost, highest control). - [ ] **Memory mnemonic — FOS-CH:** Fuel → Oxygen → Smoke → Chain reaction → Heat - [ ] **Free radicals in chain reaction:** H•, O•, OH• are the key propagators. - [ ] **Dry Chemical Powder (DCP)** extinguishers work by disrupting the chain reaction. - [ ] **Nitrogen blanketing** keeps O₂ below LOC to prevent fire in storage vessels. - [ ] **KTU 2024 standards referenced:** OISD-118, OISD-144, IS 3034, OSHA 1910.119, API RP 521, NFPA - [ ] **Always draw a 3D tetrahedron**, never a flat triangle, in 14-mark answers. > [!IMPORTANT] > **🎓 Final Exam Tip:** > For any question on the Fire Pyramid, your answer should: > 1. **Draw the 3D tetrahedron** with all four labeled vertices (1 mark reserved). > 2. **Explain each element** with one process-industry example each (4 marks). > 3. **Provide formulas** — at least two from the formula sheet above (4 marks). > 4. **Solve a numerical problem** (LOC, HRR, or radiation intensity) (4 marks). > 5. **Conclude with safety recommendations** relevant to Kerala process industries (1 mark). > > This structure guarantees **full 14 marks** in KTU 2024 ESE!

topics.title: Types of Fire Extinguishers and Their Handling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT723 - PROCESS SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Types of Fire Extinguishers and Their Handling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

A **fire extinguisher** is a portable or wheeled active fire protection device, charged with a fire-suppressing agent, designed to discharge that agent onto a small, incipient-stage fire. As per **IS 15683 (Indian Standard)** and **NFPA 10 (Standard for Portable Fire Extinguishers)**, an extinguisher is classified as a *"first-aid"* fire-fighting appliance, intended for use by trained personnel during the early minutes of an uncontrolled combustion event, prior to the arrival of professional fire services.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Phrasing Expected in Exams):**
> "A fire extinguisher is a pressurized vessel containing an extinguishing medium that can be discharged to suppress or extinguish a fire by removing one or more elements of the fire tetrahedron (Fuel, Oxygen, Heat, Uninhibited Chain Reaction)."

### 1.2 The Fire Tetrahedron — The "Why" Behind Extinguisher Selection

Every fire extinguisher works by attacking at least one vertex of the **fire tetrahedron**. Understanding this is critical to selection:

$$\text{Fire} = f(\text{Fuel},\ \text{Oxygen},\ \text{Heat},\ \text{Chain Reaction})$$

| Tetrahedron Element | Extinguishing Mechanism | Example Agent |
|---|---|---|
| **Heat Removal** (Cooling) | Endothermic phase change | Water, Foam |
| **Oxygen Exclusion** (Smothering) | Blanket formation | Foam, CO₂, Dry Powder |
| **Fuel Removal** (Starvation) | Physical separation | Foam, Dry Chemical |
| **Chain Reaction Inhibition** | Negative catalysis | Dry Chemical (Monoammonium Phosphate) |

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Fire Extinguisher Toolbox"

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Analogy for First-Time Learners:**
> Think of fire extinguishers as a **doctor's medical kit**. Just as you wouldn't use cough syrup to treat a broken bone, you cannot use water on an electrical fire or a foam extinguisher on a sodium metal fire. Each extinguisher is a **specialized tool** designed for a specific "ailment" (fire class). Using the wrong one can make the situation catastrophic — for example, using water on an oil fire causes a **BLEVE (Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapor Explosion)**.

### 1.4 Classification of Fires (The Universal System)

Before discussing extinguishers, the student must internalize the **5 internationally accepted fire classes** because extinguisher selection is *strictly* mapped to these classes:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fire Classes — Memorization Required for KTU Exams:**
> - **Class A** — Ordinary combustibles (wood, paper, cloth, rubber, plastics)
> - **Class B** — Flammable liquids & gases (petrol, diesel, LPG, solvents)
> - **Class C** — Flammable gases under pressure (methane, propane, acetylene)
> - **Class D** — Combustible metals (magnesium, sodium, titanium, aluminum powder)
> - **Class K** (or Class F in EU) — Cooking oils & fats (kitchen fires)
> - **Class E** (Australian/Old Indian system, often reclassified as energized electrical)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Misconception:** In India, **Class E (Electrical) is NOT a true fire class** in the modern NFPA system. Electrical fires are categorized based on the surrounding fuel (Class A, B, or C), and the extinguisher must be **non-conductive** (e.g., CO₂ or Dry Powder). The "E" is a *hazard classification*, not a fuel class.

---

### 1.5 Color Coding Standards (IS 15683 / BIS)

> [!NOTE]
> **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) Color Code — Mandatory for Exam:**
> All fire extinguishers in India must follow **IS 15683** color codes. The **entire body** of the extinguisher is painted **signal red (R-3000)** as a base, with a **color-coded band/label** identifying the extinguishing medium.

| Extinguisher Type | Color Band | BIS Code Reference |
|---|---|---|
| Water | Signal Red only (no band) | IS 15683:2018 |
| Foam | Cream (Cream Yellow) | IS 15683:2018 |
| Dry Powder (ABC) | Blue | IS 15683:2018 |
| Dry Powder (BC) | Blue | IS 15683:2018 |
| CO₂ | Black | IS 15683:2018 |
| Wet Chemical | Canary Yellow | IS 15683:2018 |
| Clean Agent (FM-200/Novec) | Mint Green / Fluorescent | IS 15683:2018 |
| Class D Powder | Red with White band | IS 15683:2018 |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Classification of Fire Extinguishers

The student must understand the **operational mechanism**, **ideal application**, and **limitations** of each extinguisher type. The following matrix is structured for direct exam reproduction.

#### 2.1.1 Water-Type Fire Extinguishers

**Operational Principle:** Cooling (Endothermic heat absorption). Water has a high specific heat capacity and latent heat of vaporization, making it the most effective cooling agent for Class A fires.

$$\text{Heat absorbed per kg of water vaporized} = c_p \Delta T + L_v$$

where $c_p = 4.186 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$ (specific heat), $\Delta T$ is the temperature rise, and $L_v = 2260 \text{ kJ/kg}$ (latent heat of vaporization at 100°C).

**Variants:**
- **Stored Pressure Water (SPW):** Pressurized with N₂ to ~10–12 bar.
- **Soda Acid Type:** Reacts NaHCO₃ + H₂SO₄ → Na₂SO₄ + H₂O + CO₂ (obsolete, but still appears in KTU theory papers).
- **Gas Cartridge Water:** CO₂ cartridge used for expellant.

**Limitations:** Cannot be used on Class B (water sinks below oil), Class C, Class D, Class K, or electrical fires (electrical conductivity hazard).

#### 2.1.2 Foam-Type Fire Extinguishers

**Operational Principle:** Dual mechanism — (1) cooling by water content, (2) smothering by forming a thick aqueous film (AFFF — Aqueous Film Forming Foam) over the fuel surface.

The foam blanket exhibits the following properties critical for performance:
- **Expansion ratio:** $\text{ER} = V_{\text{foam}} / V_{\text{solution}}$ (typically 6–10 for AFFF)
- **Drainage time (25%):** 2–4 minutes (BIS requirement)
- **Film formation rate:** Spreads at ~0.3 m/s over hydrocarbon fuel

**Mechanism Equation (Film Spread):**

$$\text{Spreading Coefficient} = \gamma_{\text{fuel/water}} - \gamma_{\text{foam/water}} - \gamma_{\text{foam/fuel}}$$

> A positive spreading coefficient ensures the foam film propagates across the fuel surface, sealing it from oxygen.

**Application:** Class A and Class B fires (NOT for polar solvents like acetone, methanol — use Alcohol-Resistant AFFF / AR-AFFF).

#### 2.1.3 Dry Powder (Chemical) Extinguishers

**The workhorse of process industries.** Two principal sub-types:

**a) ABC Dry Powder (Monoammonium Phosphate — NH₄H₂PO₄):**
- Multi-purpose, attacks **all four** vertices of the fire tetrahedron
- Primary mechanism: **Chain reaction inhibition** via free radical capture
- Reacts with combustion radicals: $\text{H}^\cdot + \text{PO}^\cdot \rightarrow \text{HPO}$ (terminates chain carriers)
- Yellow powder, suitable for **Class A, B, C** fires

**b) BC Dry Powder (Sodium Bicarbonate — NaHCO₃ or Potassium Bicarbonate):**
- Mechanism: Thermal decomposition releases CO₂: $2\text{NaHCO}_3 \rightarrow \text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{CO}_2$
- Suitable for **Class B, C** fires (NOT for Class A — leaves no cooling residue)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip — ABC vs BC Distinction:** ABC powder leaves a corrosive, hygroscopic residue that damages electronics. BC powder is preferred for electrical switchgear rooms.

#### 2.1.4 CO₂ (Carbon Dioxide) Extinguishers

**Operational Principle:** Smothering + cooling (expansion cooling of gas). CO₂ stored as liquid at ~58–70 bar at 21°C.

**Discharge characteristics:**
- Discharge time: 8–30 seconds (depending on cylinder size)
- Effective range: 1.5–2.5 meters
- Discharge temperature: **−78°C** (forms dry ice snow at nozzle)
- Leaves **zero residue** — ideal for laboratories, server rooms, precision machinery

**Critical Limitation:** CO₂ is an **asphyxiant**. Confined-space use requires SCBA (Self-Contained Breathing Apparatus). OSHA permissible exposure limit: **0.5% (5000 ppm)** for short-term exposure.

#### 2.1.5 Wet Chemical Extinguishers

**Operational Principle:** **Saponification** — the potassium acetate/carbonate solution reacts with burning cooking oil to form a soapy foam layer:

$$\text{Cooking Oil (Triglyceride)} + \text{K}_2\text{CO}_3 \rightarrow \text{Soap (Soap-like foam)} + \text{Glycerol}$$

The soap layer has a high heat capacity and is non-volatile, providing long-lasting cooling and oxygen exclusion. **Mandatory in commercial kitchens** per NFPA 96 and IS 15683.

#### 2.1.6 Class D (Metal Fire) Extinguishers

**Specialty dry powders** for combustible metal fires:
- **NaCl (Sodium Chloride):** For Na, Mg, Al, Ti fires
- **Cu powder (Graphite-based):** For Li fires (lithium reacts violently with water)
- **Na₂CO₃ (Soda Ash):** For sodium and potassium fires

> [!WARNING]
> **Lithium-Ion Battery Fires:** A modern KTU-favorite topic. Standard ABC powder is **insufficient** for large Li-ion battery fires. Special **Class D / Li-ion specific** extinguishers (e.g., copper-based) or large volumes of water (per NFPA 18 / NFPA 855) are required.

#### 2.1.7 Clean Agent Extinguishers

**Modern replacements for Halon 1301 (banned under Montreal Protocol, 1987) for ozone depletion.**
- **FM-200 (HFC-227ea):** Chemically suppresses fire, zero residue
- **Novec 1230 (FK-5-1-12):** Fluoroketone, atmospheric lifetime < 2 weeks
- **Inert Gas Systems (IG-541, IG-55):** Nitrogen/Argon/Carbon Dioxide blends, safe for occupied spaces

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Concept | Formula / Standard | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Foam expansion ratio | $\text{ER} = V_{\text{foam}} \div V_{\text{concentrate solution}}$ | dimensionless | AFFF: 6–10; Protein: 5–7 |
| 2 | Discharge time (CO₂) | $t_d = V_{\text{cylinder}} \div Q_{\text{discharge}}$ | seconds | BIS min: 8 s for 2 kg |
| 3 | Effective range | $R = \sqrt{\dfrac{P_{\text{gauge}} \cdot d^2}{k \cdot \rho_{\text{air}}}}$ | meters | Empirical; $k$ is nozzle constant |
| 4 | Agent quantity (Class A) | $W = 2 A$ | liters | $A$ = floor area in m² (per IS 15683) |
| 5 | Agent quantity (Class B) | $W = 0.25 Q$ | liters | $Q$ = liquid fuel quantity in L (large fires use different formula) |
| 6 | Spreading coefficient (foam) | $S = \gamma_{\text{f/w}} - \gamma_{\text{foam/w}} - \gamma_{\text{foam/f}}$ | N/m | Must be $> 0$ for film propagation |
| 7 | CO₂ toxicity threshold | $0.5\%$ = 5000 ppm | OSHA TWA-STEL | Requires SCBA above this |
| 8 | Soda acid reaction | $\text{NaHCO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow \text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{CO}_2$ | — | Obsolete type, theoretical only |
| 9 | Saponification (K-class) | Fat + K₂CO₃ → Soap + Glycerol | — | Wet chemical mechanism |
| 10 | Extinguisher inspection | Every 6 months (visual), 1 year (full service), hydrostatic test every 5–12 years | — | As per IS 15683 / NFPA 10 |
| 11 | Spacing of extinguishers | Max 15 m travel distance (Class A), 10 m (Class B) | meters | Per IS 15683 |
| 12 | Minimum discharge duration | $\geq 13$ s (Class A, $W \le 9$ L) | seconds | BIS performance requirement |

### 2.3 Engineering Utility in Process Industries

In a refinery, petrochemical, or pharmaceutical plant, extinguisher selection follows the **Hazard Identification and Risk Assessment (HIRA)** outcomes:

1. **Distillation columns (Class B, C hazard):** Foam + Dry Powder + CO₂ network
2. **Control rooms (Class A, C/E hazard):** Clean Agent (FM-200/Novec) + CO₂
3. **Warehouses (Class A hazard):** Water + ABC Powder combination
4. **Sodium/potassium handling areas:** Dedicated **Class D** extinguishers
5. **Lube oil storage (Class B):** Foam pourers + ABC powder

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Expected Answer Framework:** A complete exam answer must always tie the extinguisher type to (i) mechanism of suppression, (ii) applicable fire class, (iii) color code, and (iv) limitation/where NOT to use.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 The PASS Technique — Standard Operating Procedure for Handling

The **PASS technique** is the universally taught fire extinguisher operating procedure, mandated by **NFPA 10** and **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.157(g)**. Every KTU question on "handling" tests the student's knowledge of this sequence.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PASS Mnemonic — High-Yield for KTU Short Answers:**
> - **P** — Pull the safety pin
> - **A** — Aim at the base of the fire
> - **S** — Squeeze the handle
> - **S** — Sweep side to side

#### Detailed Step-by-Step Operational Sequence:

**Step 1 — Recognition and Decision (Pre-Deployment)**

The operator must first verify three preconditions:
- Fire is **small and incipient** (extinguishable with one extinguisher)
- The **type of extinguisher matches the fire class** (cross-check color band)
- A **clear evacuation path** exists behind the operator
- The fire is **NOT a Class D or 3D fire** (large three-dimensional liquid spill)

If any precondition fails → **evacuate immediately** and activate the fire alarm.

**Step 2 — Retrieval and Inspection**

$$\text{Retrieval time limit} = T_{\text{alarm}} - T_{\text{ignition}} \leq 30 \text{ seconds (rule of thumb)}$$

The operator confirms:
- Pressure gauge pointer is in the **green band**
- Safety pin and tamper seal are intact
- Nozzle/hose is unobstructed
- Cylinder body shows no visible damage or corrosion

**Step 3 — Deployment — The PASS Sequence**

**P — Pull the Pin:**

The safety pin prevents accidental discharge. Pulling breaks the tamper seal and unlocks the operating lever. Some extinguishers use a **twist-pin** mechanism requiring a 90° rotation. The pin's mechanical shear force is typically **2–4 kgf** — sufficient to deter tampering but accessible under stress.

**A — Aim at the Base:**

The extinguishing agent must be directed at the **fuel source**, not the flames. The visible flame is a *symptom*; the burning fuel is the *cause*. Aiming at the flame wastes agent and may not extinguish the fire. The recommended standoff distance varies by extinguisher type:

$$\text{Standoff distance} = \begin{cases} 2.5 \text{ to } 3.5 \text{ m} & \text{(Water)} \\ 2.0 \text{ to } 3.0 \text{ m} & \text{(Foam)} \\ 1.5 \text{ to } 2.5 \text{ m} & \text{(CO}_2\text{)} \\ 1.5 \text{ to } 2.5 \text{ m} & \text{(Dry Powder)} \\ 1.0 \text{ to } 1.5 \text{ m} & \text{(Wet Chemical)} \end{cases}$$

**S — Squeeze the Handle:**

Compression of the lever opens the discharge valve. For **stored-pressure extinguishers**, this allows the propellant (N₂ at 10–12 bar) to drive the agent out. For **gas-cartridge extinguishers**, the lever pierces the CO₂ cartridge. Discharge flow rate:

$$Q_{\text{discharge}} = C_d \cdot A_{\text{nozzle}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho_{\text{agent}}}}$$

where $C_d$ = discharge coefficient ($\approx 0.85$–$0.95$), $A_{\text{nozzle}}$ = nozzle cross-section, $\Delta P$ = pressure differential, $\rho_{\text{agent}}$ = agent density.

**S — Sweep Side to Side:**

Sweeping the discharge in a **horizontal arc** at the base of the fire ensures complete coverage of the fuel surface. The sweep rate is critical:

$$\text{Sweep rate} \approx 0.3 \text{ to } 0.5 \text{ m/s}$$

After the visible flame is suppressed, **watch the area for 10–15 minutes** for re-ignition. If the extinguisher empties before the fire is out, **evacuate immediately**.

### 3.2 Selection Logic — Analytical Decision Matrix

The following **decision-tree logic** is the KTU-recommended approach for selecting an extinguisher:

```
START: A fire is detected
  │
  ▼
  [Step 1] Identify energized electrical sources? ──── YES ──→ Use CO₂ or Clean Agent only
  │ NO                                                               (NON-CONDUCTIVE)
  ▼
  [Step 2] Identify burning metal? ──── YES ──→ Use Class D specific powder
  │ NO
  ▼
  [Step 3] Identify cooking oil/fat? ──── YES ──→ Use Wet Chemical (K-class)
  │ NO
  ▼
  [Step 4] Identify flammable liquid? ──── YES ──→ Use Foam (AFFF) or ABC Powder
  │ NO
  ▼
  [Step 5] Identify flammable gas? ──── YES ──→ Shut off source + Dry Powder (BC)
  │ NO
  ▼
  [Step 6] Ordinary solid combustibles ──→ Use Water or ABC Powder
END
```

### 3.3 Quantitative Analysis — Sizing a Foam Extinguisher for a Process Plant

**Given:** A 200 m² diesel storage shed in a process plant. Design a foam extinguisher system.

**Step 1: Determine fire class.**
Diesel = flammable liquid → **Class B fire**.

**Step 2: Calculate required foam solution volume (per IS 15683).**

$$W = 0.25 \cdot Q_{\text{fuel}}$$

For a small incipient-stage fire (extinguisher-grade), $Q$ = fuel quantity in liters. Assuming 1000 L diesel capacity:
$$W = 0.25 \cdot 1000 = 250 \text{ L}$$

**Step 3: Apply safety factor (1.5x) for application time.**
$$W_{\text{design}} = 1.5 \cdot 250 = 375 \text{ L}$$

**Step 4: Select extinguisher.**
Two wheeled foam extinguishers of **200 L** each, OR multiple portable units of **9 L** AFFF (would need 42 units — impractical, hence wheeled type recommended).

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Pattern Recognition:** KTU examiners often test this exact quantitative analysis. Memorize the 0.25·Q formula and the safety factor of 1.5.

### 3.4 Maintenance and Inspection Schedule (IS 15683 / NFPA 10)

| Frequency | Action | Performed By |
|---|---|---|
| **Monthly** | Visual inspection: pressure gauge, accessibility, physical condition | Plant safety personnel |
| **6 Months** | Visual + functional check, date stamping, record in register | Trained technician |
| **1 Year** | Full internal inspection, discharge test on sample units, replace seals | Certified fire service company |
| **5 Years** | Hydrostatic pressure test for CO₂ cylinders (visual for others) | Approved pressure vessel agency |
| **12 Years** | Hydrostatic test for stored-pressure cylinders (water, foam) | Approved agency |

$$\text{Hydrostatic test pressure} = 1.5 \times \text{Maximum working pressure}$$

The cylinder is held at this pressure for **at least 60 seconds** and inspected for permanent deformation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Fire Classification and Extinguisher Selection Matrix (Block Architecture)

The following block-level functional architecture diagram maps each fire class to its appropriate extinguisher, color code, and suppression mechanism.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Fire Class A: Ordinary Combustibles - Wood, Paper, Cloth]
    startB[Fire Class B: Flammable Liquids - Petrol, Oil, Solvents]
    startC[Fire Class C: Flammable Gases - LPG, Methane]
    startD[Fire Class D: Combustible Metals - Na, Mg, Al, Ti]
    startK[Fire Class K/F: Cooking Oils and Fats]
    startE[Electrical Fire Hazard - Energized Equipment]

    nodeA1[Water Extinguisher]
    nodeA2[ABC Dry Powder Extinguisher]
    nodeA3[SIGNAL RED body, no band]

    nodeB1[FOAM AFFF Extinguisher]
    nodeB2[ABC Dry Powder]
    nodeB3[CO2 Extinguisher]
    nodeB4[CREAM band]
    nodeB5[BLUE band]
    nodeB6[BLACK band]

    nodeC1[BC Dry Powder]
    nodeC2[BLUE band]

    nodeD1[Class D Specific Powder - NaCl or Cu based]
    nodeD2[RED body with WHITE band]

    nodeK1[Wet Chemical Extinguisher]
    nodeK2[CANARY YELLOW band]

    nodeE1[CO2 Extinguisher - NON CONDUCTIVE]
    nodeE2[Clean Agent FM-200 or Novec]
    nodeE3[BLACK or MINT GREEN band]

    startA --> nodeA1
    startA --> nodeA2
    nodeA1 -.-> nodeA3
    nodeA2 -.-> nodeB5
    startB --> nodeB1
    startB --> nodeB2
    startB --> nodeB3
    nodeB1 -.-> nodeB4
    nodeB2 -.-> nodeB5
    nodeB3 -.-> nodeB6
    startC --> nodeC1
    nodeC1 -.-> nodeC2
    startD --> nodeD1
    nodeD1 -.-> nodeD2
    startK --> nodeK1
    nodeK1 -.-> nodeK2
    startE --> nodeE1
    startE --> nodeE2
    nodeE1 -.-> nodeB6
    nodeE2 -.-> nodeE3

    style startA fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333
    style startB fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333
    style startC fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333
    style startD fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333
    style startK fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333
    style startE fill:#B0C4DE,stroke:#333
```

### 4.2 The PASS Handling Sequence — Operational Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeStepP1[Start: Fire Detected]
    nodeStepP2[Verify Fire is Small and Incipient]
    nodeStepP3[Verify Clear Evacuation Path]
    nodeStepP4[Cross Check Extinguisher Type]
    nodeStepP5[Letter P: Pull the Safety Pin]
    nodeStepP6[Letter A: Aim at the Base of Fire]
    nodeStepP7[Letter S: Squeeze the Handle]
    nodeStepP8[Letter S: Sweep Side to Side]
    nodeStepP9[Watch Area for 10 to 15 Minutes]
    nodeStepP10[Reignition Detected]
    nodeStepP11[Evacuate and Activate Alarm]

    nodeStepP1 --> nodeStepP2
    nodeStepP2 --> nodeStepP3
    nodeStepP3 --> nodeStepP4
    nodeStepP4 --> nodeStepP5
    nodeStepP5 --> nodeStepP6
    nodeStepP6 --> nodeStepP7
    nodeStepP7 --> nodeStepP8
    nodeStepP8 --> nodeStepP9
    nodeStepP9 --> nodeStepP10
    nodeStepP10 --> nodeStepP11
    nodeStepP11 --> nodeStepP3

    style nodeStepP1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333
    style nodeStepP5 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333
    style nodeStepP6 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333
    style nodeStepP7 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333
    style nodeStepP8 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333
    style nodeStepP11 fill:#DC143C,stroke:#333,color:#fff
```

### 4.3 Cylinder Architecture of a Stored-Pressure Dry Powder Extinguisher

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodePart1[Carrying Handle - Top]
    nodePart2[Safety Pin and Tamper Seal]
    nodePart3[Pressure Gauge]
    nodePart4[Discharge Lever Handle]
    nodePart5[Discharge Hose]
    nodePart6[Nozzle Assembly]
    nodePart7[Steel Cylinder Body]
    nodePart8[Pressurizing Gas N2 at 10 to 12 bar]
    nodePart9[Dry Powder Agent ABC Monoammonium Phosphate]
    nodePart10[Discharge Valve]
    nodePart11[Siphon Tube]

    nodePart1 --> nodePart2
    nodePart2 --> nodePart3
    nodePart3 --> nodePart4
    nodePart4 --> nodePart5
    nodePart5 --> nodePart6
    nodePart6 --> nodePart7
    nodePart7 --> nodePart8
    nodePart7 --> nodePart9
    nodePart7 --> nodePart11
    nodePart9 --> nodePart10
    nodePart11 --> nodePart10
    nodePart10 --> nodePart5

    style nodePart7 fill:#4682B4,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    style nodePart9 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333
    style nodePart8 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
    style nodePart10 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Maintenance & Inspection Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeMaint1[Extinguisher in Service]
    nodeMaint2[Monthly Visual Check]
    nodeMaint3[Pressure Gauge in Green Band?]
    nodeMaint4[6 Month Functional Inspection]
    nodeMaint5[12 Month Internal Inspection]
    nodeMaint6[5 to 12 Year Hydrostatic Test]
    nodeMaint7[Replace or Recharge Immediately]
    nodeMaint8[Continue in Service]
    nodeMaint9[Decommission and Test]

    nodeMaint1 --> nodeMaint2
    nodeMaint2 --> nodeMaint3
    nodeMaint3 --> nodeMaint7
    nodeMaint3 --> nodeMaint4
    nodeMaint4 --> nodeMaint5
    nodeMaint5 --> nodeMaint6
    nodeMaint6 --> nodeMaint9
    nodeMaint4 --> nodeMaint8
    nodeMaint5 --> nodeMaint8

    style nodeMaint1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
    style nodeMaint7 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    style nodeMaint8 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333
    style nodeMaint9 fill:#DC143C,stroke:#333,color:#fff
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Classify fire extinguishers based on the extinguishing medium used. Give at least three types with their color code and one application each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Examiner's Mark Distribution):**

**(1 Mark — Classification Principle):** Fire extinguishers are classified based on the **extinguishing medium** they contain and the **fire class** they target. The Bureau of Indian Standards (IS 15683) mandates color coding where the body is always signal red and a colored band identifies the agent.

**(2 Marks — Three Examples with Color + Application):**

| Type | Color Band | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Water Type** | Signal Red only (no band) | Class A fires (wood, paper, cloth). NOT for electrical or oil fires. |
| **Foam Type (AFFF)** | Cream / Cream Yellow | Class A and Class B (flammable liquid) fires. Creates aqueous film over fuel. |
| **CO₂ Type** | Black | Class B and electrical fires. Leaves no residue, suitable for switchgear. |

**(1 Mark — Conclusion):** Selection is strictly governed by fire class compatibility; misapplication (e.g., water on oil) can cause catastrophic outcomes such as BLEVE.

**Examiner's Marking Note:** Award **full 3 marks** only if all three extinguishers include both color code and application. Omitting color code = cap at 2 marks.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Expected]**
*List any four fire classes and indicate the appropriate fire extinguisher for each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **Class A** — Ordinary combustibles → **Water** or **ABC Dry Powder** extinguisher.
2. **Class B** — Flammable liquids → **Foam (AFFF)** or **ABC Dry Powder** or **CO₂** extinguisher.
3. **Class C** — Flammable gases → **BC Dry Powder** (after shutting off gas source).
4. **Class D** — Combustible metals → **Class D specific powder** (NaCl, Cu-based, or graphite).
5. **Class K (or F)** — Cooking oils → **Wet Chemical** (potassium acetate) extinguisher.

**(1 Mark – four classes identified; 1 Mark – four extinguishers; 1 Mark – correct class–extinguisher mapping)**

---

### 5.2 PART B — Extended Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Examination pattern**, every Part B question carries **14 marks** with sub-parts (a) and (b) of **7 marks each**, and a **Module-level internal choice** is provided. Two complete alternative questions are given below.

---

#### **QUESTION A — [14 Marks, KTU Board Exam Style, May 2024 Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe with neat sketches the construction and working of a **stored-pressure ABC dry powder fire extinguisher**. Explain the principle of operation and the chemistry of suppression.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (i) — Construction (3 Marks):**

A stored-pressure ABC dry powder extinguisher consists of the following components:
1. **Cylinder body** — Seamless steel or aluminum alloy, hydrostatically tested to **1.5 × working pressure** (≈ 17 bar for 12 bar working).
2. **Pressurizing gas** — Dry **nitrogen (N₂)** filled at the top of the cylinder at 10–12 bar to drive the powder.
3. **ABC Dry Powder** — Monoammonium phosphate (NH₄H₂PO₄), particle size 10–75 μm, filled to 50–75% cylinder volume.
4. **Siphon tube** — Reaches near the cylinder bottom; transfers powder upward.
5. **Valve assembly** — Includes a pressure gauge, safety pin, and discharge lever.
6. **Discharge hose and nozzle** — Convergent–divergent nozzle for fine dispersion.

**Part (ii) — Working (2 Marks):**

On squeezing the lever, N₂ pressure forces the powder up the siphon tube. The powder is discharged in a **high-velocity cloud** that smothers the fire. The discharge time is **8–15 seconds**, range is **1.5–2.5 m**.

**Part (iii) — Chemistry of Suppression (2 Marks):**

The monoammonium phosphate decomposes under heat:
$$\text{NH}_4\text{H}_2\text{PO}_4 \xrightarrow{\Delta} \text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 + \text{NH}_3$$

The phosphoric acid further dehydrates to form polyphosphates that **trap free radicals** ($\text{H}^\cdot$, $\text{OH}^\cdot$, $\text{O}^\cdot$) in the flame, terminating the chain-branching reactions. The mechanism is **negative catalysis (inhibition)** — the most effective form of fire suppression for hydrocarbon flames.

**Conclusion:** The ABC extinguisher is a multi-class agent suitable for **Class A, B, and C** fires, making it the most versatile extinguisher in process industries.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **fire tetrahedron** and discuss how each type of extinguisher attacks one or more vertices. List the **limitations** of water-type extinguishers in process industries.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (i) — Fire Tetrahedron (3 Marks):**

The fire tetrahedron represents the four essential elements required for sustained combustion:
1. **Fuel** — The combustible material
2. **Oxygen** — Oxidizer (typically air, ≥ 16% O₂ for most fires)
3. **Heat** — Activation energy and sustained energy release
4. **Uninhibited Chain Reaction** — Free-radical propagation ($\text{H}^\cdot + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{OH}^\cdot + \text{O}^\cdot$)

**Remove any one vertex → fire extinguishes.**

**Part (ii) — Mapping Extinguishers to Tetrahedron (2 Marks):**

| Extinguisher | Vertex Attacked | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| Water | Heat | Cooling via endothermic vaporization (2260 kJ/kg) |
| Foam | Heat + Oxygen | AFFF film cools and excludes O₂ |
| ABC Powder | Chain Reaction | Radical capture (most potent) |
| CO₂ | Oxygen | Displacement + slight cooling |
| Wet Chemical | Heat + Oxygen | Saponification forms cooling soap layer |
| Class D Powder | Oxygen + Heat | Forms crust, excludes O₂ |

**Part (iii) — Limitations of Water Extinguishers in Process Industries (2 Marks):**

1. **Cannot be used on flammable liquids (Class B)** — Water sinks below oil, may cause splashing and fire spread.
2. **Cannot be used on electrical fires** — Conducts electricity, may cause electrocution or short circuit.
3. **Cannot be used on metal fires (Class D)** — Reacts violently with sodium (produces H₂), magnesium, and lithium.
4. **Cannot be used on cooking oil fires (Class K)** — Causes splatter and may flash-ignite vapor.
5. **Freezing risk in cold climates** — Inert additives like ethylene glycol required.
6. **Corrosion damage** to equipment in chemical plants.

---

#### **QUESTION B — [14 Marks — Alternative Choice, KTU 2024 Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **PASS technique** for handling a fire extinguisher. Add a note on the inspection and maintenance schedule as per **IS 15683**.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (i) — PASS Technique Explained (4 Marks):**

The PASS technique is the standard operating procedure taught globally (NFPA / OSHA / IS 15683). It consists of four sequential steps:

- **P — Pull the Pin:** Breaks the tamper seal; unlocks the operating lever. The pin is designed to shear at 2–4 kgf force, allowing rapid deployment.
- **A — Aim at the Base:** The extinguishing agent must contact the **fuel source**, not the visible flame. Stand 1.5–3 m away depending on extinguisher type.
- **S — Squeeze the Handle:** Opens the discharge valve. For stored-pressure types, propellant N₂ drives the agent. For cartridge types, the lever pierces a CO₂ cartridge.
- **S — Sweep Side to Side:** A horizontal arc motion at the fuel base ensures uniform coverage. Sweep rate ≈ 0.3–0.5 m/s. Watch for **re-ignition for 10–15 minutes** after the flame is suppressed.

**Preconditions before using PASS:**
- Fire must be small (extinguishable with one unit)
- Correct extinguisher for the fire class
- Clear evacuation path behind operator

**Part (ii) — Inspection & Maintenance Schedule (3 Marks):**

| Frequency | Activity | Performed By |
|---|---|---|
| **Monthly** | Visual check of pressure gauge, seal, accessibility | Plant safety staff |
| **6 Months** | Functional check; record in logbook | Trained technician |
| **1 Year** | Internal inspection, discharge test, seal replacement | Certified service company |
| **5 Years** | Hydrostatic test for CO₂ cylinders | Approved agency |
| **12 Years** | Hydrostatic test for stored-pressure cylinders | Approved agency |

$$\text{Hydrostatic test pressure} = 1.5 \times P_{\text{working}}$$

The cylinder is held at test pressure for **60 seconds** minimum; permanent deformation or leakage leads to immediate condemnation.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A process plant stores **5,000 L of diesel** in a bulk storage shed. **(i)** Identify the fire class, **(ii)** select a suitable extinguisher with justification, and **(iii)** calculate the minimum quantity of foam concentrate required.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (i) — Fire Class Identification (2 Marks):**
Diesel is a **flammable hydrocarbon liquid** with a flash point of 52–96°C (Class II combustible liquid as per NFPA 30). At normal storage temperatures in tropical Kerala, diesel vapors can be ignited. → **Fire Class: B**

**Part (ii) — Selection of Extinguisher (2 Marks):**
**Selection: Foam (AFFF) extinguisher** is the most suitable because:
- AFFF forms an **aqueous film** that spreads over the diesel surface
- It provides **dual suppression** (cooling + smothering)
- It is the BIS-preferred agent for bulk hydrocarbon storage
- The cream-colored band on a red body makes it instantly identifiable

Alternative: **ABC Dry Powder** can be used for incipient spills, but foam is preferred for bulk storage due to film-forming persistence.

**Part (iii) — Quantity Calculation (3 Marks):**

Using the **IS 15683 formula** for Class B fires with foam:

$$W = 0.25 \cdot Q_{\text{fuel}}$$

$$W = 0.25 \times 5000 = 1250 \text{ L}$$

Apply a **safety factor of 1.5** (per industry practice for design margin and re-application time):

$$W_{\text{design}} = 1.5 \times 1250 = 1875 \text{ L}$$

**Recommended Configuration:** **2 × 1000 L wheeled AFFF foam extinguishers** (per NFPA 11), positioned at the entrance of the storage shed on the windward side.

**Additional Recommendation:** Install **fixed foam pourers** on top of the bulk storage tank per NFPA 16 for large-scale fires beyond extinguisher capability.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks on This Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing Class C and Class E (Electrical):** Class E is NOT a fuel class. Electrical fires are addressed based on surrounding fuel + non-conductive agent (CO₂/Clean Agent). Writing "Class E fire" loses marks.
> 2. **Recommending water on oil or electrical fires:** This is a fatal engineering error in process safety. KTU examiners specifically mark down for this.
> 3. **Missing the color band in answers:** IS 15683 color codes are frequently asked. Always pair extinguisher type with the color band (e.g., "Foam — Cream band").
> 4. **Ignoring the mechanism in chemistry questions:** For ABC powder, the examiner expects the radical-capture mechanism, not just "it puts out fire."
> 5. **Forgetting the "do not use" column:** Every extinguisher answer should state where it CANNOT be used. A complete answer lists both applicability and limitations.
> 6. **Confusing foam with foam pourers:** Foam extinguishers are portable; foam pourers are part of fixed systems. Don't interchange them in design questions.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — High-Density Recall Points for KTU 2024:**

- [x] **5 Fire Classes:** A (solids), B (liquids), C (gases), D (metals), K (cooking oils)
- [x] **Fire Tetrahedron:** Fuel + Oxygen + Heat + Chain Reaction — extinguish by removing ONE
- [x] **Color Code (BIS):** Water (no band) / Foam (Cream) / Dry Powder (Blue) / CO₂ (Black) / Wet Chemical (Canary Yellow) / Clean Agent (Mint Green)
- [x] **PASS Technique:** Pull, Aim, Squeeze, Sweep
- [x] **ABC Powder:** Monoammonium phosphate, attacks chain reaction, yellow powder, multi-class
- [x] **CO₂:** Asphyxiant, zero residue, ideal for electrical, OSHA limit 5000 ppm
- [x] **Foam (AFFF):** Aqueous film, ideal for hydrocarbon fires, NOT for polar solvents
- [x] **Water Limitations:** Cannot be used on B, C, D, K, or electrical fires
- [x] **Class D:** NaCl for Na/Mg, Cu-based for Li, Soda ash for K
- [x] **Wet Chemical:** Saponification mechanism for cooking oil fires
- [x] **Formula:** Class B foam quantity $W = 0.25 \cdot Q$ with 1.5× safety factor
- [x] **Hydrostatic Test:** $P_{\text{test}} = 1.5 \cdot P_{\text{working}}$, held for 60 s
- [x] **Inspection:** Monthly visual, 6-month functional, 1-year internal, 5–12 year hydrostatic
- [x] **Travel Distance:** ≤ 15 m (Class A), ≤ 10 m (Class B) per IS 15683
- [x] **Li-ion Battery Fires:** Standard ABC is INSUFFICIENT; use Class D / large water
- [x] **Saponification Equation:** Fat + K₂CO₃ → Soap + Glycerol (Wet Chemical mechanism)
- [x] **Halon 1301:** Banned under Montreal Protocol (1987); replaced by FM-200 / Novec 1230
- [x] **Discharge time minimum:** ≥ 13 s for Class A extinguishers
- [x] **NFPA 10 / IS 15683:** Governing standards for portable extinguishers
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fixed Fire Protection Systems
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT723 - PROCESS SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fixed Fire Protection Systems

# Fixed Fire Protection Systems (FFPS)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):**
> A *Fixed Fire Protection System (FFPS)* is a permanently installed, engineered network of detection, alarm, control, and suppression components that is integrated into a process facility's infrastructure to automatically detect, control, confine, and extinguish fires at the incipient stage, thereby protecting personnel, equipment, and continuity of operations.

In the language of process safety engineering (PSE), FFPS forms the third tier of the **hierarchy of loss prevention controls** (after inherent safer design and engineering controls), sitting in the *mitigation* layer of the safety barrier framework. KTU examiners specifically expect students to recognize that FFPS is a **passive-to-active engineered barrier** — passive in its installed nature but active in its response upon fire detection.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **"The 24×7 Watchman Inside the Plant" Analogy**
> Imagine your house has a watchman stationed at every room who never sleeps. The moment he sees a spark, he doesn't just shout "fire!" — he immediately pulls a hidden lever that floods the room with water, foam, or gas to kill the flame. That watchman is your **detector**, the lever is your **control valve**, and the flood is your **suppression medium**. Together, this triad constitutes a *Fixed Fire Protection System*. Unlike a portable extinguisher that needs a trained human to grab it and aim it, the FFPS reacts automatically within seconds, often before humans can react.

### Classification Overview of FFPS

Fixed fire protection systems are broadly divided into **two master categories**:

| Category | Primary Function | Detection Required? | Examples |
|----------|------------------|--------------------|----------|
| **Fire Detection & Alarm Systems** | Sense fire, alert personnel | Yes | Smoke, heat, flame, gas detectors |
| **Fire Suppression Systems** | Extinguish or control fire | May be automatic or manual | Sprinklers, deluge, foam, CO₂, dry chemical |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Metric**
> - **NFPA 72** governs detection and alarm
> - **NFPA 11** governs low/medium/high expansion foam
> - **NFPA 13** governs sprinkler systems
> - **NFPA 12** governs CO₂ systems
> - **NFPA 17** governs dry chemical systems
> - **NFPA 15** governs water spray fixed systems
> - **NFPA 16** governs foam-water sprinkler systems

The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically lists **water spray, foam, dry chemical, and gas-based systems** under the FFPS module. Let us now dig deeper.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Fixed Fire Protection Systems — The Master Tree

A clean mental model (which examiners love):

```
FIXED FIRE PROTECTION SYSTEMS
│
├── 1. WATER-BASED SYSTEMS
│   ├── (a) Wet Pipe Sprinkler
│   ├── (b) Dry Pipe Sprinkler
│   ├── (c) Deluge Sprinkler
│   ├── (d) Pre-Action Sprinkler
│   ├── (e) Water Spray (Fixed Spray Nozzles)
│   └── (f) Water Mist
│
├── 2. FOAM-BASED SYSTEMS
│   ├── (a) Low-Expansion Foam (ER ≈ 2–20)
│   ├── (b) Medium-Expansion Foam (ER ≈ 20–200)
│   └── (c) High-Expansion Foam (ER ≈ 200–1000+)
│
├── 3. GAS-BASED SUPPRESSION SYSTEMS
│   ├── (a) CO₂ (High & Low Pressure)
│   ├── (b) Halon & Clean Agents (FM-200, Novec 1230, Inergen)
│   └── (c) Inert Gases (Argon, Nitrogen, IG-541)
│
├── 4. DRY CHEMICAL SYSTEMS
│   ├── (a) ABC (Monoammonium Phosphate)
│   ├── (b) BC (Sodium Bicarbonate / Potassium Bicarbonate)
│   └── (c) Purple-K (PKP)
│
└── 5. SPECIAL HAZARD SYSTEMS
    ├── (a) Powder systems
    └── (b) Condensed aerosol systems
```

## 2.2 Working Principle of Each Sub-System

### (a) Wet Pipe Sprinkler System

- Pipes are **permanently filled with water under pressure**.
- Each sprinkler head has a **heat-sensitive fusible link or glass bulb** (typically rated 68 °C, 79 °C, 93 °C).
- When fire heat ruptures the link, the head opens and water discharges immediately.
- **Advantage**: Fastest response time (~3 min).
- **Limitation**: Cannot be used in **freezing environments** (water would freeze) or where water damage is unacceptable.

### (b) Dry Pipe Sprinkler System

- Pipes are filled with **pressurized air or nitrogen** above the water.
- A *dry pipe valve* holds back the water; when a head opens, air escapes, pressure drops, and water floods the system.
- **Advantage**: Used in unheated warehouses, cold storage.
- **Disadvantage**: 1-minute time lag; requires air compressor.

### (c) Deluge System

- All sprinkler heads are **open (no fusible link)** — they always discharge when activated.
- A *deluge valve* (pneumatically, hydraulically, or electrically actuated) releases water simultaneously from all heads upon signal from a separate detection system.
- **Use case**: High-hazard areas like transformer yards, LPG storage, dikes around tanks.

### (d) Pre-Action System

- Hybrid: pipes are dry but detection is required to fill them with water (single interlock) or detection + head opening (double interlock).
- Prevents accidental water discharge due to mechanical damage.

### (e) Water Spray (Fixed) System

- Uses **open spray nozzles** (not sprinklers) designed for **specific flow density** (typically 10.2 L/min/m² for hydrocarbon pool fires).
- Used for cooling of vessels, fire exposure protection of tanks, and protection of structural steel.
- Operates by **deluge valve activation**.

### (f) Water Mist System

- Uses high pressure (40–200 bar) to produce fine droplets (< 1 mm).
- Works by **oxygen displacement, radiant heat blocking, and cooling**.
- Ideal where water damage must be minimized (museums, electronics).

## 2.3 Foam-Based Systems

| Foam Type | Expansion Ratio (ER) | Application |
|-----------|----------------------|-------------|
| **Low Expansion** | 2 : 1 to 20 : 1 | Spill fires, tank bunds |
| **Medium Expansion** | 20 : 1 to 200 : 1 | LNG, marine, warehouses |
| **High Expansion** | 200 : 1 to 1000+ : 1 | Enclosed spaces (engine rooms) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mechanism of Foam Extinguishment:**
> 1. **Smothering** – Foam blanket cuts off oxygen.
> 2. **Cooling** – Water content cools the fuel surface.
> 3. **Vapor Suppression** – Prevents fuel vapor release.
> 4. **Radiant Heat Reflection** – Foam blocks heat feedback.

**Foam Proportioning:** Typically **1%, 3%, or 6%** concentrate is mixed with water via proportioners (bladder tanks, in-line venturi, or around-the-pump).

## 2.4 Gas-Based Systems (CO₂, Clean Agents, Inerts)

### CO₂ System
- Mechanism: **Oxygen dilution** + slight cooling.
- For Class B fires: design concentration **34–50%** (normal air O₂ = 21%, fires need ≥ 14% to sustain).
- Hazards: CO₂ is **asphyxiant** — systems must have pre-discharge alarm + time delay.
- Cannot be used on metal fires (Na, Mg, Al, Ti) — reacts violently.

### Clean Agents (FM-200, Novec 1230, Halon 1301)
- **FM-200 (HFC-227ea)** – No ozone depletion, safe for occupied spaces, design concentration ~7%.
- **Novec 1230** (C₆F₁₂O, FK-5-1-12) – Zero ODP, lowest GWP, used for data centers.
- **Inergen / IG-541** (52% N₂, 40% Ar, 8% CO₂) – Lowers O₂ to ~12% but CO₂ stimulates human breathing reflex.

### Design Concentration for Clean Agents
The agent must achieve the **Minimum Design Concentration (MDC)** calculated using:

$$\text{MDC} = \text{FC} \times \text{SF}$$

Where:
- **FC** = Flammable Concentration (cup-burner value)
- **SF** = Safety Factor (typically 1.2–1.4)

## 2.5 Dry Chemical Systems

- Use **fine powder** (sodium bicarbonate, monoammonium phosphate, Purple-K).
- Stored pressurized with **N₂ gas** at ~14 bar.
- Extinguishing mechanism: **Chain reaction interruption** (negative catalysis of free radicals).
- Effective on **Class B (flammable liquids) and Class C (electrical)** fires.
- Typical application: 1–5 seconds total discharge.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Formula Table for FFPS — Every formula here is examinable**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $q = \dfrac{Q}{A}$ | Water application density | L/min·m² |
| 2 | $N = \dfrac{A}{a_n}$ | Number of nozzles | dimensionless |
| 3 | $Q_{tot} = N \cdot q_n$ | Total system flow rate | L/min |
| 4 | $Q_{water} = Q_{sol} \cdot (1 - p)$ | Water flow in foam solution | L/min |
| 5 | $Q_{conc} = Q_{sol} \cdot p$ | Foam concentrate flow | L/min |
| 6 | $V_{foam} = V_{sol} \cdot \text{ER}$ | Expanded foam volume | m³ |
| 7 | $m_{CO_2} = V_{room} \cdot k \cdot \rho$ | CO₂ mass required | kg |
| 8 | $h_f = \dfrac{4.52 \cdot Q^{1.85}}{C^{1.85} \cdot D^{4.87}} \cdot L$ | Hazen-Williams head loss | m |
| 9 | $t_d = \dfrac{V_{agent}}{Q_{disch}}$ | Discharge time | s |
| 10 | $t_{detect} + t_{act} + t_{flow} \le T_{resp}$ | Total response time | s |
| 11 | $\text{MDC} = \text{FC} \times \text{SF}$ | Clean agent MDC | % vol |
| 12 | $A_{cov} = \pi R^2 \cdot \cos\theta$ | Spray nozzle coverage | m² |
| 13 | $P_{req} = P_{del} + P_{loss}$ | Required pump pressure | bar |
| 14 | $\text{Flow Rate} = K \sqrt{P}$ | Nozzle discharge equation | L/min |

Where:
- $q$ = application density, $Q$ = flow, $A$ = protected area
- $p$ = proportioning percentage (0.01, 0.03, or 0.06)
- ER = expansion ratio
- $k$ = CO₂ application factor (kg/m³); $k \approx 0.7$–$1.0$ depending on hazard
- $C$ = Hazen-Williams coefficient ($C = 120$ for new steel, $C = 100$ for old)
- $D$ = pipe internal diameter (m)
- $K$ = nozzle flow coefficient
- $\theta$ = nozzle spray angle from vertical

### Real-World Utility

These formulas appear daily in:
- **Fire protection engineering consultancies** (Designing plant layouts)
- **EPC companies** (L&T, Tata Projects, Bechtel)
- **Insurance risk engineering** (Factory Mutual, UL)
- **Oil & Gas majors** (Reliance, IOCL, BPCL for refinery FFPS)
- **Process safety audits** (PSM audits under OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Exhaustive derivation policy enforced.** Every algebraic step, every constant, every unit conversion is shown explicitly. No shortcuts, no truncated steps.

## 3.1 Derivation of Spray Nozzle Coverage Area

The fundamental requirement for designing any water spray or sprinkler system is to determine the **area each nozzle can effectively cover** and the **total water flow** required.

### Step 1: Geometric Setup

Consider a **solid-cone spray nozzle** mounted at a vertical height $H$ above the fire hazard, with the cone inclined at angle $\theta$ from the vertical axis. The nozzle produces a circular footprint on the horizontal plane.

The **radius of coverage** $R$ at the protected surface is given by:

$$R = H \cdot \tan\theta$$

### Step 2: Coverage Area of a Single Nozzle

The protected area per nozzle is a circle of radius $R$:

$$a_n = \pi R^2 = \pi (H \tan\theta)^2$$

$$\boxed{a_n = \pi H^2 \tan^2\theta}$$

### Step 3: Number of Nozzles Required

For a rectangular hazard of length $L$ and width $W$:

$$A_{total} = L \cdot W$$

Total nozzles (rounded up to next integer):

$$N = \left\lceil \dfrac{A_{total}}{a_n} \right\rceil$$

### Step 4: Total Water Flow Rate

If each nozzle has flow rate $q_n$:

$$Q_{tot} = N \cdot q_n$$

The application density must then satisfy:

$$q = \dfrac{Q_{tot}}{A_{total}} \geq q_{design}$$

where $q_{design}$ is the NFPA-specified minimum spray density:
- **Hydrocarbon pool fire**: $q_{design} = 10.2$ L/min·m²
- **Transformer cooling**: $q_{design} = 6.1$ L/min·m²
- **LNG spill**: $q_{design} = 20.4$ L/min·m²

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineer's Note:** For overlapping coverage (recommended in NFPA 15), the spacing $S$ between nozzles should be **≤ R**, not equal to R. This ensures every square meter receives overlapping spray.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Foam Solution and Concentrate Flow Rates

### Step 1: Definitions

- $Q_{sol}$ = Foam solution flow rate (L/min) — water + concentrate mixture
- $Q_{water}$ = Pure water flow rate
- $Q_{conc}$ = Foam concentrate flow rate
- $p$ = Proportioning percentage (e.g., 0.03 for 3% foam)

### Step 2: Mass Balance on Foam Solution

By definition of proportioning ratio:

$$Q_{conc} = p \cdot Q_{sol}$$

The remaining fraction is water:

$$Q_{water} = Q_{sol} - Q_{conc} = Q_{sol} - p \cdot Q_{sol}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{water} = Q_{sol}(1 - p)}$$

### Step 3: Expanded Foam Volume

The foam concentrate + water mix, once aerated, expands to a much larger volume:

$$V_{foam} = \text{ER} \cdot V_{sol}$$

where:
- $V_{sol} = Q_{sol} \cdot t$ (total solution volume over time $t$)
- ER = Expansion Ratio (e.g., 8 for low expansion, 250 for high expansion)

### Step 4: Total Foam Application Rate

The NFPA 11 specifies minimum foam application rate in **L/min/m² of fire area**:

$$q_{foam} = \dfrac{Q_{sol}}{A_{fire}} \geq q_{NFPA}$$

For hydrocarbon pool fires with AFFF 3%: $q_{NFPA} = 4.1$ L/min·m²
For hydrocarbon pool fires with AFFF 6%: $q_{NFPA} = 6.5$ L/min·m²

### Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** Design a foam system for a 30 m × 20 m bunded tank farm area. Use 3% AFFF, expansion ratio = 8, design application rate = 4.1 L/min·m². Calculate the foam concentrate quantity needed for **30 minutes** of operation.

**Solution:**

Step 1: Calculate area
$$A = 30 \times 20 = 600 \text{ m}^2$$

Step 2: Foam solution flow rate
$$Q_{sol} = q_{NFPA} \times A = 4.1 \times 600 = 2460 \text{ L/min}$$

Step 3: Foam concentrate flow rate (3%)
$$Q_{conc} = p \times Q_{sol} = 0.03 \times 2460 = 73.8 \text{ L/min}$$

Step 4: Foam concentrate for 30 minutes
$$V_{conc} = Q_{conc} \times t = 73.8 \times 30 = 2214 \text{ L} = 2.214 \text{ m}^3$$

Step 5: Verify water flow
$$Q_{water} = Q_{sol}(1 - p) = 2460 \times 0.97 = 2386.2 \text{ L/min}$$

Step 6: Total expanded foam volume
$$V_{foam} = \text{ER} \times V_{sol} = 8 \times 2460 \times 30 = 590400 \text{ L} = 590.4 \text{ m}^3$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer Summary:**
> - Concentrate storage: **2.214 m³** (round up to 2.5 m³ for safety)
> - Water required: **71,586 L** over 30 min
> - Expanded foam: **590.4 m³**

---

## 3.3 Derivation of CO₂ Quantity for Total Flooding

For total flooding of an enclosure of volume $V$:

### Step 1: Determine Design Concentration

For Class B (flammable liquid) fires, NFPA 12 specifies minimum CO₂ concentration of **50% by volume** for heptane fires.

### Step 2: Application Factor (Quantity per Unit Volume)

From NFPA 12 Table:

$$m_{CO_2} = V \cdot k_{CO_2}$$

For Class B fires: $k_{CO_2} = 0.79$ kg/m³ at 50% concentration.

### Step 3: Account for Pressure, Temperature, and Leakage

Real installations add a **safety factor** for door openings, ventilation, and unburnt losses:

$$m_{actual} = m_{CO_2} \cdot (1 + SF)$$

where $SF = 0.2$ (20%) for well-sealed enclosures, up to $0.5$ for less sealed.

### Step 4: Liquid CO₂ Storage Volume

CO₂ is stored as liquid at high pressure (~57 bar at 20 °C). Liquid density of CO₂ ≈ 0.77 kg/L.

$$V_{storage} = \dfrac{m_{actual}}{\rho_{liquid}} = \dfrac{m_{actual}}{770} \text{ (in L)}$$

### Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** Total-flood a battery charging room of dimensions 10 m × 6 m × 3 m using CO₂. Design concentration 50%, safety factor 30%. Calculate storage volume.

**Solution:**

Step 1: Enclosure volume
$$V = 10 \times 6 \times 3 = 180 \text{ m}^3$$

Step 2: Required CO₂ mass
$$m_{CO_2} = V \times k = 180 \times 0.79 = 142.2 \text{ kg}$$

Step 3: With safety factor
$$m_{actual} = 142.2 \times 1.30 = 184.86 \text{ kg}$$

Step 4: Storage volume
$$V_{storage} = \dfrac{184.86}{770} = 0.2401 \text{ m}^3 = 240.1 \text{ L}$$

Step 5: Number of standard cylinders
Each 68 L cylinder holds ~50 kg of CO₂:
$$N_{cyl} = \dfrac{184.86}{50} = 3.70 \rightarrow 4 \text{ cylinders}$$

---

## 3.4 Hazen-Williams Head Loss Equation (Hydraulic Network)

For sizing water mains in FFPS:

$$\boxed{h_f = \dfrac{4.52 \cdot L \cdot Q^{1.85}}{C^{1.85} \cdot D^{4.87}}}$$

Where:
- $h_f$ = head loss in **meters** of water column
- $L$ = pipe length (m)
- $Q$ = flow rate (m³/s)
- $C$ = Hazen-Williams roughness coefficient (steel = 120, cast iron = 100)
- $D$ = pipe internal diameter (m)

**Derivation Context:** Hazen-Williams is empirical, not from first principles. It assumes fully turbulent flow with Reynolds number independent friction factor. For precise engineering, Darcy-Weisbach may be used, but Hazen-Williams is the industry standard for fire protection hydraulics.

$$h_f = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{v^2}{2g}$$

where $f$ is from Moody chart or Colebrook-White equation.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: When to Use What System

| Hazard Type | Fuel Class | Recommended FFPS | Rationale |
|-------------|------------|------------------|-----------|
| Office building | Class A | Wet pipe sprinkler | Fast, reliable, low cost |
| Cold storage warehouse | Class A | Dry pipe sprinkler | Anti-freeze |
| Refinery process unit | Class B (HC liquids) | Deluge + Foam | Rapid full-area discharge |
| LPG bullet tank | Class B (gas) | Water spray (cooling) | Prevents BLEVE |
