| Diesel generator room | Class B | CO₂ / clean agent | No water damage to equipment |
| Transformer yard | Class C (electrical) | Water spray (deluge) | Effective cooling, non-conductive water spray |
| Data center | Class C | Novec 1230 / FM-200 | No residue, safe for electronics |
| Warehouse (high-rack) | Class A | ESFR sprinkler | Early suppression fast response |
| Aircraft hangar | Class B | Foam + water spray | Large area, high fuel load |
| Pharmaceutical plant | Class B | Clean agent (Inergen) | No contamination |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Architecture of a Complete Fixed Fire Protection System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Hazard["Protected Hazard e.g. Process Vessel or Tank"]
    Detector["Detection Layer Smoke, Heat, Flame, Gas Detectors"]
    Panel["Fire Alarm Control Panel FACP"]
    Valve["Deluge Valve or Solenoid Valve"]
    Supply["Water Supply Pumps, Tank, City Main"]
    Media["Suppression Media Water, Foam, CO2, Dry Chemical"]
    Nozzle["Distribution Network Pipes, Sprinklers, Nozzles"]
    Alarm["Occupant Alarm Hooters, Strobes"]
    Out["Suppression Discharge on Hazard"]

    Hazard -- "Fire initiates" --> Detector
    Detector -- "4-20 mA signal" --> Panel
    Panel -- "Logic decision" --> Valve
    Panel -- "Activate" --> Alarm
    Supply -- "Pressurized water" --> Valve
    Valve -- "Open command" --> Nozzle
    Media -- "Proportioned mix" --> Nozzle
    Nozzle -- "Discharge" --> Out
    Out -- "Extinguish or control" --> Hazard
```

## 4.2 Foam System Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    WT["Water Tank or City Main"]
    FCT["Foam Concentate Storage Tank"]
    PROP["Proportioner 1 percent or 3 percent or 6 percent"]
    MIX["Foam Solution Mixing Point"]
    DELUGE["Deluge Valve"]
    PIPE["Distribution Piping"]
    GEN["Foam Generators or Pourers"]
    TANK["Bunded Tank or Spill Area"]

    WT --> PROP
    FCT --> PROP
    PROP --> MIX
    MIX --> DELUGE
    DELUGE --> PIPE
    PIPE --> GEN
    GEN --> TANK
```

## 4.3 Detection → Suppression Decision Logic (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1["Smoke Detector Ionization or Photoelectric"]
    H1["Heat Detector Fixed Temperature or Rate of Rise"]
    F1["Flame Detector UV or IR"]
    G1["Gas Detector Flammable or Toxic"]

    OR1["OR Logic Any one signal"]
    CONF["Confirmation Logic 2 out of N voting"]
    DELAY["Time Delay 0 to 30 seconds adjustable"]
    REL["Relay Output"]

    ABORT["Manual Abort Switch"]
    DISCH["Discharge Signal to Suppression"]

    S1 --> OR1
    H1 --> OR1
    F1 --> OR1
    G1 --> OR1
    OR1 --> CONF
    CONF --> DELAY
    DELAY --> REL
    REL --> DISCH
    ABORT -.-> DELAY
```

## 4.4 Comparison Flow: Gas vs Foam vs Water Spray

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Q["What is the protected hazard?"]
    Q --> Q1{"Fuel class and risk profile?"}

    Q1 -- "Class A Solid combustibles" --> A["Use Water based Sprinkler"]
    Q1 -- "Class B Flammable liquids" --> B["Use Foam or Dry Chemical"]
    Q1 -- "Class C Electrical" --> C["Use CO2 or Clean Agent"]
    Q1 -- "Class D Metals" --> D["Use Special Dry Powder"]

    A --> A1["Wet pipe if heated space, Dry pipe if cold"]
    B --> B1["Low exp foam for tanks, High exp for enclosures"]
    C --> C1["CO2 if unoccupied, Clean agent if occupied"]
    D --> D1["Sodium chloride or Graphite based powder"]
```

## 4.5 Functional Block: Foam Concentrate Storage and Proportioning

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["Foam Concentrate Storage Bladder Tank or Atmospheric Tank"]
    A2["Concentrate Line 1 inch to 2 inch"]
    A3["Proportioner Venturi or Balanced Pressure"]
    A4["Foam Solution 97 percent water + 3 percent concentrate"]
    A5["Foam Generator or Foam Pourer"]
    A6["Expanded Foam over Fire"]

    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> A3
    A3 --> A4
    A4 --> A5
    A5 --> A6
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

> Define the term **"Fixed Fire Protection System"**. List any **four** major categories of fixed fire protection systems used in the process industry.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Fixed Fire Protection System (FFPS) is a permanently installed engineered assembly of detection, alarm, control, and suppression devices designed to automatically detect, control, confine, and extinguish fires within a process facility, providing continuous round-the-clock protection without requiring operator intervention. *(1 mark)*

The four major categories are: *(2 marks — ½ mark each)*
1. **Water-based systems** — wet pipe sprinklers, dry pipe sprinklers, deluge, water spray
2. **Foam-based systems** — low, medium, and high expansion foam
3. **Gas-based systems** — CO₂, clean agents (FM-200, Novec 1230), inert gases (Inergen)
4. **Dry chemical systems** — ABC, BC, Purple-K powders

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**

> Differentiate between **Wet Pipe Sprinkler System** and **Deluge Sprinkler System**. Mention one industrial application of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Wet Pipe Sprinkler | Deluge Sprinkler |
|---------|-------------------|------------------|
| Pipe content | Water under pressure | Air, water held by deluge valve |
| Heads | Closed (fusible link/glass bulb) | Open (no link) |
| Detection | In-head (thermal) | Separate detection system |
| Discharge | Only opened head discharges | All heads discharge simultaneously |
| Response time | Fast (≈ 3 min) | Fast (≈ 1 min) |
| Hazard | Low/ordinary hazard | High hazard |

Application: Wet pipe — office buildings, shopping malls *(½ mark)*. Deluge — LPG tank farm, transformer yard, hydrocarbon storage *(½ mark)*.

---

## PART B — 14 Mark Questions

### Question A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

> **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **components and working of a Deluge Sprinkler System**. Mention any **two advantages** and **two limitations** of the system. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A foam protection system is to be designed for a hydrocarbon tank farm bund of **25 m × 18 m**. Using **3% AFFF foam** with expansion ratio of **8** and design application rate of **4.1 L/min/m²**. Calculate:
> (i) Foam solution flow rate
> (ii) Foam concentrate flow rate
> (iii) Foam concentrate quantity required for **45 minutes** of operation
> (iv) Total expanded foam volume produced in 45 minutes **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to (a) — Deluge Sprinkler System

**Components (5 marks — list & describe):**

1. **Water Supply Source** — City main, elevated tank, fire pump, or dedicated reservoir.
2. **Deluge Valve** — A quick-release valve (pneumatic, hydraulic, or solenoid actuated) that when triggered releases water to the entire system.
3. **Open Sprinkler Heads** — All heads are permanently open (no thermal element).
4. **Detection System** — A *separate* fire detection system (smoke, heat, flame, or linear heat detection cable) triggers the deluge valve.
5. **Fire Alarm Control Panel (FACP)** — Receives signal from detector, energizes the solenoid/pneumatic actuator on the deluge valve.
6. **Auxiliary Components** — Pressure gauges, alarm bells, motor gong, water motor alarm, test valves, drain valves.
7. **Trim Piping** — Small-bore piping for valve actuation and supervision.

**Working Principle (2 marks):**
When fire breaks out, the detection system (heat/smoke/flame sensor) sends a signal to the FACP. The FACP activates the deluge valve's solenoid, which releases the holding water pressure. The main water supply then floods the entire pipe network, and **all open sprinkler heads discharge simultaneously** onto the protected hazard. The fire alarm bell also sounds. The discharge continues until the valve is manually reset.

**Sketch:**

```
   Detection   ──signal──>   FACP  ──signal──>  Deluge Valve
                                                  │
   Water Supply ──pressurized─>  Deluge Valve    │
                                        │         │
                              ┌─────────┴────────┴──────┐
                              │  Open Sprinkler Network   │
                              │  ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ ○     │
                              └────────────┬──────────────┘
                                           ▼
                              Water discharged on hazard
```

**Advantages (1 mark — ½ each):**
1. Provides **simultaneous coverage** of the entire hazard area.
2. Ideal for **high-hazard, fast-spreading fires** (e.g., hydrocarbon, LPG).

**Limitations (1 mark — ½ each):**
1. Requires **separate detection system** — added cost and complexity.
2. Accidental activation causes **complete water damage** — not suitable for low-hazard areas.

---

#### Solution to (b) — Foam System Calculation

**Given:**
- Bund area = 25 m × 18 m
- Foam type = 3% AFFF
- Expansion ratio = 8
- Application rate = 4.1 L/min/m²
- Operating time = 45 minutes

**Step 1: Calculate protected area** *(½ mark)*
$$A = 25 \times 18 = 450 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2: Foam solution flow rate** *(2 marks)*
$$Q_{sol} = q_{design} \times A = 4.1 \times 450 = 1845 \text{ L/min}$$

**Step 3: Foam concentrate flow rate** *(2 marks)*
$$Q_{conc} = p \times Q_{sol} = 0.03 \times 1845 = 55.35 \text{ L/min}$$

**Step 4: Foam concentrate quantity for 45 min** *(1 mark)*
$$V_{conc} = Q_{conc} \times t = 55.35 \times 45 = 2490.75 \text{ L} \approx 2.49 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 5: Expanded foam volume in 45 min** *(1 mark)*
$$V_{sol,total} = 1845 \times 45 = 83{,}025 \text{ L} = 83.025 \text{ m}^3$$
$$V_{foam} = \text{ER} \times V_{sol,total} = 8 \times 83.025 = 664.2 \text{ m}^3$$

**Summary of Answers (½ mark):**
- (i) $Q_{sol}$ = **1845 L/min**
- (ii) $Q_{conc}$ = **55.35 L/min**
- (iii) $V_{conc}$ = **2490.75 L ≈ 2.49 m³**
- (iv) $V_{foam}$ = **664.2 m³**

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023 Model Question]**

> **(a)** Explain the **principle, design concentration, advantages and disadvantages of CO₂ total flooding system**. What are the safety precautions required while using CO₂ for occupied spaces? **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A water spray cooling system is to be designed for protection of an LPG storage vessel of **6 m diameter and 12 m length**. The design spray density is **10.2 L/min/m²**. If the recommended number of spray nozzles is **24**, calculate:
> (i) Surface area to be protected
> (ii) Required flow rate per nozzle
> (iii) Total water flow rate
> (iv) Minimum water storage for **60 minutes** of operation **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to (a) — CO₂ Total Flooding System

**Principle (2 marks):**
CO₂ extinguishes fire by **oxygen dilution**. Normal air contains 21% O₂; CO₂ displaces it so that the O₂ concentration falls below the level needed to support combustion (typically < 14% for most fires). For Class B fires, NFPA 12 specifies minimum design concentration of **50% CO₂** in the protected enclosure. There is also a small cooling effect from the expanding gas.

**Design Concentration (1 mark):**
- Class A surface fires: minimum **34% CO₂**
- Class B (flammable liquids): minimum **50% CO₂**
- Deep-seated Class A: minimum **65% CO₂**

**Application Factor (1 mark):**
$$m_{CO_2} = V_{room} \times k$$
where $k$ depends on design concentration (e.g., $k = 0.79$ kg/m³ at 50%).

**Advantages (1 mark — ½ each):**
1. **No residue** — ideal for protecting electronic equipment, machinery.
2. **Non-conductive** — safe for electrical fires.
3. **Economical** for permanent installations.

**Disadvantages (1 mark — ½ each):**
1. **Asphyxiant** — cannot be discharged in occupied spaces without delay.
2. **Cannot be used on metal fires** (Na, Mg, Al, Ti) — reacts violently.
3. High-pressure storage required (57 bar).

**Safety Precautions for Occupied Spaces (1 mark — ½ each):**
1. **Pre-discharge alarm** with minimum **30-second delay** to allow evacuation.
2. **Lock-out / manual abort switch** at entry door to prevent accidental discharge on occupants.
3. **Adequate ventilation** system for post-discharge purging.
4. **Self-contained breathing apparatus (SCBA)** required for entry after discharge.

---

#### Solution to (b) — Water Spray Cooling Calculation

**Given:**
- Vessel diameter $D = 6$ m, length $L = 12$ m
- Spray density $q = 10.2$ L/min/m²
- Number of nozzles $N = 24$
- Operating time $t = 60$ min

**Step 1: Surface area of cylindrical vessel** *(2 marks)*
For a horizontal cylinder, the lateral (curved) surface area is:
$$A = \pi \times D \times L = \pi \times 6 \times 12 = 72\pi = 226.19 \text{ m}^2$$

Including end caps (optional — depending on whether nozzles cover ends):
$$A_{ends} = 2 \times \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} = 2 \times \dfrac{\pi \times 36}{4} = 18\pi = 56.55 \text{ m}^2$$
$$A_{total} = 226.19 + 56.55 = 282.74 \text{ m}^2$$

(Use **$A = 282.74$ m²** including ends for full coverage)

**Step 2: Total water flow rate** *(1.5 marks)*
$$Q_{tot} = q \times A = 10.2 \times 282.74 = 2884.0 \text{ L/min}$$

**Step 3: Flow rate per nozzle** *(1.5 marks)*
$$q_n = \dfrac{Q_{tot}}{N} = \dfrac{2884.0}{24} = 120.17 \text{ L/min per nozzle}$$

**Step 4: Water storage for 60 min** *(1.5 marks)*
$$V_{water} = Q_{tot} \times t = 2884.0 \times 60 = 173{,}040 \text{ L} = 173.04 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 5: Convert to storage tank size** *(½ mark)*
With 10% reserve margin:
$$V_{tank} = 173.04 \times 1.10 = 190.34 \text{ m}^3$$

**Summary of Answers (½ mark):**
- (i) Area = **282.74 m²** (with ends)
- (ii) Flow per nozzle = **120.17 L/min**
- (iii) Total flow = **2884 L/min**
- (iv) Storage = **173.04 m³** (or **190.34 m³** with 10% margin)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units in foam calculation** — Always write L/min and L clearly. Marks are deducted for unit-less numbers.
> 2. **Confusing proportioning rate** — 3% means $p = 0.03$, not 3. Many students write $Q_{conc} = 3 \times Q_{sol}$.
> 3. **Mixing up ER with proportioning** — Expansion ratio applies to aeration, not concentrate ratio.
> 4. **Ignoring safety factors in CO₂** — Real systems always include 20–30% safety factor. Skipping this loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Missing the time delay in CO₂ systems** — Always mention 30-second pre-discharge alarm.
> 6. **No mention of standards** — Reference NFPA 11/12/13/15/17 for full credit.
> 7. **Skipping the sketch in Part B** — Without a labeled sketch, you lose 1–2 marks even if the description is correct.
> 8. **Not mentioning detection layer separately for deluge** — A common error is assuming deluge heads have thermal links.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these bullets within 60 seconds before entering the exam hall:**

- 🔹 **Definition:** FFPS = permanently installed, automatic, integrated fire detection + suppression system.
- 🔹 **Hierarchy in Process Safety:** Inherent → Engineering → Procedural → PPE → FFPS (mitigation layer).
- 🔹 **Detection triggers suppression** — Detection is the *brain*; suppression is the *muscle*.
- 🔹 **Wet pipe sprinkler:** Water-filled pipes; heads open individually on heat.
- 🔹 **Dry pipe sprinkler:** Air-filled; for cold storage and unheated spaces.
- 🔹 **Deluge system:** All heads open; separate detection triggers valve — used for high hazard.
- 🔹 **Pre-action system:** Hybrid — used where accidental discharge must be avoided.
- 🔹 **Water spray:** Open nozzles, fixed deluge valve; used for vessel cooling, transformer protection.
- 🔹 **Water mist:** High pressure, fine droplets; low water damage.
- 🔹 **Low-expansion foam:** ER 2–20; tanks and bunds.
- 🔹 **Medium-expansion foam:** ER 20–200; marine, LNG.
- 🔹 **High-expansion foam:** ER 200–1000; enclosed spaces.
- 🔹 **Foam mechanisms:** Smothering + cooling + vapor suppression + heat blocking.
- 🔹 **CO₂ mechanism:** Oxygen dilution; minimum 50% for Class B.
- 🔹 **CO₂ danger:** Asphyxiant; cannot be used in occupied areas without delay/alarm.
- 🔹 **CO₂ incompatible with:** Na, Mg, Al, Ti (Class D metal fires).
- 🔹 **FM-200 (HFC-227ea):** Clean agent, ~7% design concentration, safe for occupied spaces.
- 🔹 **Novec 1230:** C₆F₁₂O fluid, zero ODP, lowest GWP among clean agents.
- 🔹 **Inergen (IG-541):** 52% N₂ + 40% Ar + 8% CO₂; lowers O₂ to ~12% but human can breathe.
- 🔹 **Dry chemical:** Chain-reaction interruption; Class B & C; Purple-K for high-performance.
- 🔹 **Application density** $q = Q/A$ is the master formula.
- 🔹 **Foam concentrate** $Q_{conc} = p \cdot Q_{sol}$, with $p = 0.01$ / $0.03$ / $0.06$.
- 🔹 **Foam volume** $V_{foam} = \text{ER} \times V_{sol}$.
- 🔹 **CO₂ mass** $m = V_{room} \times k \times \text{SF}$.
- 🔹 **Hazen-Williams** for pipe friction loss in water network.
- 🔹 **Standards:** NFPA 11 (foam), 12 (CO₂), 13 (sprinkler), 15 (water spray), 17 (dry chemical), 72 (alarm).
- 🔹 **Pump sizing:** $P_{req} = P_{del} + P_{loss} + P_{elevation}$.
- 🔹 **Discharge equation:** $Q = K\sqrt{P}$ (per nozzle).
- 🔹 **Response time total:** $t_{detect} + t_{act} + t_{flow} \le T_{allowed}$.
- 🔹 **Always include safety factor of 10–30%** in any sizing calculation.
- 🔹 **Sketch is mandatory** in 7-mark questions for any system description.
- 🔹 **Standards citation earns easy marks** — quote NFPA / IS / OISD numbers whenever relevant.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flammability Diagram: Construction and Application
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT723 - PROCESS SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Flammability Diagram: Construction and Application**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Flammability Diagram** (also known as the **Coward–Jones Diagram** or **Explosion Triangle / Fire Triangle Plot**) is a fundamental graphical tool in process safety engineering used to characterize the combustion behavior of a **ternary gas mixture** consisting of a **fuel**, an **oxidizer** (typically air or pure oxygen), and an **inert gas** (typically nitrogen, CO₂, or steam).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A flammability diagram is a two-dimensional Cartesian plot in which the **fuel concentration (vol %)** is plotted on the **vertical (Y) axis** and the **oxidizer concentration (vol %)** is plotted on the **horizontal (X) axis**, with the constraint that:
> 
> $$ \%\,\text{Fuel} + \%\,\text{Oxidizer} + \%\,\text{Inert} = 100\% $$
> 
> The diagram delineates the **flammable envelope** within which combustion can propagate, surrounded by regions of non-flammable compositions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are a **firefighter** trying to control a forest fire. You know that fire needs three things to burn: **fuel (trees)**, **oxygen (air)**, and **heat**. If you take away any one of these, the fire goes out. Now, translate this into a chemistry lab where you have a closed vessel containing a flammable vapor, air, and nitrogen.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Fire Triangle" Translated to Gas Concentrations**
> 
> Think of the flammability diagram as a **map of dangerous and safe zones** for gas mixtures — similar to a weather map showing hurricane paths.
> - The **x-axis** shows how much *air (oxygen)* is present.
> - The **y-axis** shows how much *fuel gas* is present.
> - The remaining space (which always adds to 100%) is **nitrogen / inert gas** — the fire's "enemy."
> - The **enclosed curved region** is the "fire zone" — anywhere inside it, an ignition source will cause a fire or explosion.
> - **Outside the envelope** = safe; **Inside the envelope** = explosive.
> 
> It's like a coastal map: the land is "safe ground" (non-flammable), the ocean is "danger" (flammable), and the **shoreline** is the **flammability envelope**.

## 1.3 Critical Standard Metrics (Industry-Standard Constants)

The following physical/chemical constants are commonly marked on every flammability diagram:

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Value (Methane in Air) | Units |
|:------:|:---------|:------------------------------:|:-----:|
| **LFL** | Lower Flammability Limit | **5.0** | vol % |
| **UFL** | Upper Flammability Limit | **15.0** | vol % |
| **LOC/MOC** | Limiting (Minimum) Oxygen Concentration | **12.0** | vol % O₂ |
| **x** | Stoichiometric Oxygen Requirement | **≈ 17.8** | vol % O₂ |
| **y** | Stoichiometric Fuel Concentration | **≈ 9.5** | vol % Fuel |
| **Air Composition** | Oxygen in atmospheric air | **21.0** | vol % O₂ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For the KTU examination, the values of **LFL, UFL, LOC, and stoichiometric concentrations** will be supplied in the question. **Memorize the construction method**, not the numerical values.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Ternary System — What Is Being Plotted?

A flammability diagram deals with a **closed system** of three gases summing to 100 %:

$$ N_F + N_O + N_I = 100 $$

where:
- $N_F$ = Mole percent of **Fuel** (vertical axis, Y)
- $N_O$ = Mole percent of **Oxidizer / O₂** (horizontal axis, X)
- $N_I$ = Mole percent of **Inert gas** (N₂, CO₂, steam, etc.)

Because there are only **two independent variables**, a 2-D Cartesian plot is sufficient.

## 2.2 The Five Critical Geometric Features of a Flammability Diagram

The diagram is defined by **five key points** and **three reference lines**:

### 🔺 The Five Key Points

1. **Origin (0, 0):** Represents **100 % Inert** (no fuel, no oxygen — impossible to ignite).
2. **Pure Fuel Point (100, 0):** Y-axis intercept at 100 % fuel, 0 % O₂ — too rich, cannot ignite.
3. **Pure Oxygen Point (0, 100):** X-axis intercept at 100 % O₂, 0 % fuel — no fuel, cannot ignite.
4. **LFL Point (0, LFL):** The lowest fuel concentration that supports combustion.
5. **UFL Point (0, UFL):** The highest fuel concentration that supports combustion.

### 📐 The Three Reference Lines

| Line | Definition | Slope |
|:----:|:-----------|:-----:|
| **Air Line** | A vertical line drawn at $N_O = 21\%$ (for atmospheric air). All mixtures using air lie on this line. | Vertical (slope = ∞) |
| **Stoichiometric Line** | A straight line joining the **stoichiometric fuel concentration (y)** to the **stoichiometric oxygen concentration (x)** through the **stoichiometric mixture point**. | $-y/x$ |
| **Tie Lines (Isopleths)** | Straight lines drawn from a point on the y-axis (pure fuel) to a point on the x-axis (pure O₂), representing all fuel-oxidizer mixtures having a **fixed ratio**. | Variable |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations appear in **almost every KTU board question** on flammability diagrams. Master these.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|:-:|:-------:|:-----------|:-----:|
| 1 | $N_F + N_O + N_I = 100$ | Ternary mass balance constraint | vol % |
| 2 | $\dfrac{N_F}{N_O} = \dfrac{y}{x} = \text{constant}$ | Tie-line equation (constant fuel/oxygen ratio) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $N_O^{\text{stoich}} = \dfrac{\text{O atoms needed}}{\text{O atoms in oxidizer}}$ | Stoichiometric oxygen for complete combustion | vol % |
| 4 | $N_F^{\text{stoich}} = \dfrac{1}{1 + \dfrac{x}{y}} \cdot 100$ | Stoichiometric fuel concentration | vol % |
| 5 | $N_I^{\text{stoich}} = 100 - x - y$ | Inert at stoichiometric point | vol % |
| 6 | $\text{Mixture point} = \dfrac{(P_1 \cdot C_1) + (P_2 \cdot C_2)}{P_1 + P_2}$ | Weighted average for mixing two streams | vol % |
| 7 | $\text{Air-to-Fuel ratio (vol)} = \dfrac{0.21 \cdot V_{\text{air}}}{V_{\text{fuel}}}$ | For air as oxidizer | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\text{Required inert for safety} = \dfrac{LFL - M_F}{LFL} \cdot N_I^{\text{air}}$ | Inerting formula using LFL | vol % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL EXAM TIP:** In every mixing/purging problem, the **final mixture point** is found as a **weighted linear average** of the initial and added streams along a **tie line**. Always identify the two endpoint compositions first, then interpolate.

## 2.4 The Role of Each Region in Process Safety

| Region | Composition | Engineering Action |
|:-------|:------------|:------------------|
| **Below LFL** | Too lean (insufficient fuel) | Safe to vent / open vessel |
| **Above UFL** | Too rich (insufficient O₂) | Safe; used for vessel "padding" |
| **Inside Envelope, Left of LOC** | Fuel + O₂ above LOC, no inert | Highly explosive — purge immediately |
| **Inside Envelope, Right of LOC** | O₂ concentration < LOC | Safe; this is the **inerting region** |
| **On Air Line (0–LFL)** | Pure fuel-air mixture below LFL | Safe to operate (e.g., gasoline storage tank vapor space) |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Storage Tank Design:** Maintain vapor space in storage tanks *above UFL* by floating-roof design or rich-gas padding.
- **Inerting Procedure for Confined Spaces:** Calculate how much nitrogen to inject to push a vessel from the flammable region into the **LOC-shielded** non-flammable region.
- **Purging Operations:** Determine whether a vessel must be purged with **fuel-rich** (above UFL) or **oxygen-lean** (below LOC) gas.
- **Emergency Venting:** Decide whether releasing a leaking gas cloud will create a flammable atmosphere.
- **Hazard & Operability (HAZOP) Studies:** Evaluate consequences of deviations in feed compositions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Stoichiometric Mixture Point

Consider a hydrocarbon fuel $\text{C}_m\text{H}_n$ burning in oxygen:

$$ \text{C}_m\text{H}_n + \left(m + \dfrac{n}{4}\right)\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow m\,\text{CO}_2 + \dfrac{n}{2}\,\text{H}_2\text{O} $$

### Step 1: Total Moles on Right-Hand Side (Oxidizer Side)

$$ n_{\text{O}_2}^{\text{stoich}} = m + \dfrac{n}{4} \quad \text{[moles of O}_2 \text{ required per mole of fuel]} $$

### Step 2: Stoichiometric Oxygen Concentration (vol %)

$$ x = N_O^{\text{stoich}} = \dfrac{n_{\text{O}_2}^{\text{stoich}}}{1 + n_{\text{O}_2}^{\text{stoich}}} \times 100 = \dfrac{m + n/4}{1 + m + n/4} \times 100 $$

### Step 3: Stoichiometric Fuel Concentration (vol %)

$$ y = N_F^{\text{stoich}} = \dfrac{1}{1 + n_{\text{O}_2}^{\text{stoich}}} \times 100 = \dfrac{1}{1 + m + n/4} \times 100 $$

### Step 4: Verify the Inert Concentration

$$ N_I^{\text{stoich}} = 100 - x - y = \dfrac{n_{\text{inert}}}{1 + n_{\text{O}_2}^{\text{stoich}}} \times 100 $$

> For methane ($m=1, n=4$): $n_{\text{O}_2}^{\text{stoich}} = 1 + 1 = 2$
> - $x = \dfrac{2}{1+2} \times 100 = 66.67\%$
> - $y = \dfrac{1}{1+2} \times 100 = 33.33\%$

## 3.2 Derivation of the Tie-Line Equation

A **tie line** represents all mixtures having the **same fuel-to-oxygen ratio**. For any point $(N_O, N_F)$ on a tie line connecting $(0, N_F^a)$ to $(N_O^b, 0)$:

$$ \dfrac{N_F}{N_O} = \dfrac{N_F^a}{N_O^b} = \text{slope inverse} $$

Rearranging in **slope-intercept form** $N_F = m \cdot N_O + c$:

$$ N_F = -\left(\dfrac{N_F^a}{N_O^b}\right) N_O + N_F^a $$

The **negative slope** indicates the line decreases as we move from the pure-fuel axis toward the pure-oxygen axis.

## 3.3 Derivation: Final Mixture Composition from Two Streams

Let:
- **Stream A** of mass/volume $P_1$ contain fuel $F_1$, oxygen $O_1$, inert $I_1$ (all in vol %)
- **Stream B** of mass/volume $P_2$ contain fuel $F_2$, oxygen $O_2$, inert $I_2$ (all in vol %)

### Step-by-Step Mass Balance

$$ N_F^{\text{final}} = \dfrac{P_1 F_1 + P_2 F_2}{P_1 + P_2} $$

$$ N_O^{\text{final}} = \dfrac{P_1 O_1 + P_2 O_2}{P_1 + P_2} $$

$$ N_I^{\text{final}} = 100 - N_F^{\text{final}} - N_O^{\text{final}} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Geometric Interpretation:** The final point lies on the **straight line connecting Stream A and Stream B** in the flammability diagram, and its position is the **weighted average** of the two endpoint positions.

## 3.4 Worked-Out Analytical Problem (KTU 2024 Style)

> **Q:** A vessel initially contains air at 1 atm and 25 °C. Methane is added until the fuel concentration reaches 8 vol %. Using LFL = 5 %, UFL = 15 %, LOC = 12 %:
> (a) Plot the initial and final mixture points.
> (b) Is the final mixture flammable? 
> (c) If not flammable, how much nitrogen must be added to make it inert? If flammable, what is the safe direction of dilution?

### Solution

**Step 1: Initial Composition (Stream A — Air)**

$$ N_F^{(A)} = 0\%,\quad N_O^{(A)} = 21\%,\quad N_I^{(A)} = 79\% $$

**Step 2: Pure Methane (Stream B — Fuel)**

$$ N_F^{(B)} = 100\%,\quad N_O^{(B)} = 0\%,\quad N_I^{(B)} = 0\% $$

**Step 3: Apply Weighted Average Formula**

Let 1 mole of air + $r$ moles of methane.

$$ N_F^{\text{final}} = \dfrac{1(0) + r(100)}{1 + r} = \dfrac{100 r}{1 + r} = 8\% $$

Solving:

$$ 100 r = 8(1 + r) \implies 100 r = 8 + 8 r \implies 92 r = 8 \implies r = 0.0870 $$

$$ N_O^{\text{final}} = \dfrac{1(21) + 0.0870(0)}{1 + 0.0870} = \dfrac{21}{1.0870} = 19.32\% $$

$$ N_I^{\text{final}} = 100 - 8 - 19.32 = 72.68\% $$

**Step 4: Check Flammability**

Since $LFL = 5 < N_F = 8 < UFL = 15$, the fuel is within the flammable range. The mixture is **FLAMMABLE**.

**Step 5: Determine Safe Direction**

To escape the envelope, we must move **below LFL** (dilute with air/inert to reduce fuel) or **above UFL** (add more fuel) or **below LOC** (add inert to reduce O₂).

**Decision logic:**
- The current point (8 %, 19.32 %) lies inside the envelope.
- Adding pure nitrogen moves the point **leftward and downward** along a tie line toward the inert corner, which can drop $N_O$ below the LOC (12 %).

**Step 6: Calculate Nitrogen Needed to Reach LOC**

We need $N_O^{\text{final}} = 12\%$. Using a tie line through the current point:

$$ \dfrac{N_F}{N_O} = \dfrac{8}{19.32} = 0.414 $$

At the LOC boundary: $N_F = 0.414 \times 12 = 4.97\% \approx LFL$

> The LOC point lies essentially on the LFL corner. So adding nitrogen moves the point along the tie line, and it touches the **LFL point** (0 %, 5 %) on the flammability envelope.

The fraction of nitrogen required:

$$ N_I^{\text{required}} = 100 - 4.97 - 12 = 83.03\% $$

$$ \Delta N_I = 83.03 - 72.68 = 10.35\% $$

We must add **10.35 vol % nitrogen** (relative to total mixture) to make the system inert.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Flammability Diagram (Coward–Jones Plot)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Pure Fuel 100 percent at origin of Y axis] --> B[LFL Lower Flammability Limit]
    B --> C[Flammable Envelope Curved Boundary]
    C --> D[UFL Upper Flammability Limit]
    D --> E[Pure Oxidizer 100 percent O2 on X axis]
    E --> F[Air Line at 21 percent O2 vertical]
    F --> G[Original Mixture Point]
    G --> H[Stoichiometric Mixture Point]
    H --> I[Inert Corner 100 percent N2 at origin 0,0]
    I --> A
    C --> I
    G -.->|dilute with N2| I
    G -.->|add air| F
    H -.->|along stoich line| A
```

## 4.2 Detailed Functional Block Architecture of the Flammability Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Axes[Coordinate System]
        XAxis["X axis = Oxidizer O2 in vol percent"]
        YAxis["Y axis = Fuel in vol percent"]
    end
    subgraph KeyPts[Five Key Points]
        Origin["Origin 0,0 = 100 percent Inert"]
        LFLpt["LFL point on Y axis at 5 percent fuel"]
        UFLpt["UFL point on Y axis at 15 percent fuel"]
        AirPt["Air point on X axis at 21 percent O2"]
        StoichPt["Stoichiometric Point x,y"]
    end
    subgraph RefLines[Three Reference Lines]
        AirLine["Air Line vertical at 21 percent O2"]
        StoichLine["Stoichiometric Line slope = minus y over x"]
        TieLine["Tie Line connecting isocomposition points"]
    end
    subgraph Regions[Five Regions]
        R1["Region 1 Below LFL Lean Safe"]
        R2["Region 2 Above UFL Rich Safe"]
        R3["Region 3 Inside Envelope Flammable"]
        R4["Region 4 Left of LOC below 12 percent O2 Safe"]
        R5["Region 5 Right of LOC above LOC within fuel range"]
    end
    XAxis --> R3
    YAxis --> R3
    Origin --> R4
    LFLpt --> R3
    UFLpt --> R3
    AirPt --> AirLine
    StoichPt --> StoichLine
    R3 --> TieLine
```

## 4.3 Process Flow: How a Mixture Moves on the Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start: Vessel contains initial gas mixture]) --> Identify[Identify initial N_F and N_O from problem]
    Identify --> AddFuel{Need to add fuel?}
    AddFuel -- Yes --> MoveUp[Mixture moves UP along a tie line]
    AddFuel -- No --> AddInert{Need to add inert gas?}
    AddInert -- Yes --> MoveLeft[Mixture moves LEFT toward inert corner]
    AddInert -- No --> AddAir{Need to add air?}
    AddAir -- Yes --> MoveDown[Mixture moves DOWN along air line]
    AddAir -- No --> CheckPos[Check if inside envelope]
    MoveUp --> CheckPos
    MoveLeft --> CheckPos
    MoveDown --> CheckPos
    CheckPos --> Decision{Inside envelope?}
    Decision -- Yes --> Dangerous[STOP Dangerous Apply mitigation]
    Decision -- No --> Safe[SAFE Continue operation]
    Dangerous --> InertPurge[Purge with N2 to push below LOC]
    InertPurge --> Recheck[Recheck on diagram]
    Recheck --> CheckPos
```

## 4.4 Tie-Line Construction Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Take pure fuel point on Y axis 100 percent] --> B[Take pure O2 point on X axis 100 percent]
    B --> C[Draw straight line connecting them]
    C --> D[Mark intermediate points at 20 40 60 80 percent]
    D --> E[Each point shows fuel to O2 ratio for that tie line]
    E --> F[All mixtures on this line have same F to O2 ratio]
    F --> G[When diluting with inert mixture slides along tie line toward origin]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The flammable envelope is **lemon-shaped** for most hydrocarbons, narrow at the top (LFL corner) and bottom (UFL corner), and bulges outward in the middle. The **apex of the bulge** generally lies near the **stoichiometric mixture point**.

## 4.5 Decision Matrix for Process Safety Operations

| Process Operation | Direction of Movement on Diagram | Safety Status |
|:------------------|:-------------------------------:|:-------------:|
| Dilution with air | Downward (↓) along air line | Depends on position |
| Inerting with N₂ | Leftward (←) toward origin | Usually safe |
| Enrichment with fuel | Upward (↑) along tie line | Risk of UFL crossing |
| Adding O₂ | Rightward (→) toward pure O₂ | High risk |
| Mixing two flammable streams | Straight line between them | Must check if line crosses envelope |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define a flammability diagram. State the three reference lines drawn on it.**

**Model Answer:**

A flammability diagram is a two-dimensional Cartesian plot showing the **fuel concentration (Y-axis, vol %)** versus the **oxidizer concentration (X-axis, vol %)** for a ternary gas mixture of fuel, oxidizer, and inert, with the constraint that the three percentages sum to 100 %.

The three reference lines are:
1. **Air Line** — a vertical line at $N_O = 21\%$ representing all mixtures with air as the oxidizer.
2. **Stoichiometric Line** — a straight line joining the pure-fuel axis to the pure-oxygen axis, passing through the stoichiometric mixture point $(x, y)$.
3. **Tie Lines (Isopleths)** — straight lines of constant fuel-to-oxygen ratio, used to track composition changes during mixing or dilution.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is the Limiting Oxygen Concentration (LOC)? How is it used in process safety?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Limiting Oxygen Concentration (LOC)** — also called the **Minimum Oxygen Concentration (MOC)** — is the **minimum concentration of oxygen** in a fuel-oxidizer-inert mixture below which combustion **cannot occur**, regardless of the fuel concentration.

In process safety, the LOC is used to:
- **Design inerting procedures:** By maintaining $N_O < LOC$ (typically achieved with nitrogen purging), any fuel concentration can be made non-flammable.
- **Establish safe storage conditions:** Vapor spaces of flammable-liquid storage tanks are designed to operate on the **fuel-rich side** of the air line where $N_O < LOC$.
- **Emergency response:** Indicates the target oxygen level for fire prevention through blanketing/inertion.

---

## 📕 PART B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** Per KTU 2024 scheme regulations, the following are **two alternative questions** (Module Internal Choice). Attempt **either A or B**.

---

### 🔹 QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**A vessel of volume 10 m³ initially contains air at atmospheric pressure and 25 °C. Methane is added until the methane concentration reaches 7 vol %. Given LFL = 5 %, UFL = 15 %, LOC = 12 %, and stoichiometric methane = 9.5 vol %:**

**(a)** **(7 Marks)** Plot the initial and final mixture points on a flammability diagram and comment on the flammability of the final mixture.

**(b)** **(7 Marks)** Calculate the minimum volume of nitrogen (at the same T and P) that must be added to render the mixture non-flammable.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

*Initial state (Stream A — Air):*
- $N_F^{(A)} = 0\%$, $N_O^{(A)} = 21\%$, $N_I^{(A)} = 79\%$
- Point A: $(N_O, N_F) = (21, 0)$ → lies on the **air line at the X-axis intercept**.

*Final state (Stream B — Methane):*
- Let $V_a$ = volume of air, $V_m$ = volume of methane added.
- $N_F = \dfrac{100 V_m}{V_a + V_m} = 7$
- $V_m = \dfrac{7 V_a}{93} = 0.0753\, V_a$
- $N_O^{\text{final}} = \dfrac{21 \cdot V_a}{V_a + 0.0753 V_a} = \dfrac{21}{1.0753} = 19.53\%$

**Final Point B:** $(N_O, N_F) = (19.53, 7)$.

*Flammability check:*
- $LFL = 5 < 7 = N_F < 15 = UFL$ → **Mixture is FLAMMABLE** (inside envelope).
- $N_O = 19.53 > LOC = 12$ → oxygen is sufficient for combustion.

*Plot:* Point A at (21, 0); Point B at (19.53, 7). The line from A to B is the **air-line dilution path** moving upward. Point B lies inside the **lemon-shaped envelope**.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

To make the mixture non-flammable, we must push it **below the LOC (12 % O₂)** or **below the LFL (5 % fuel)** by adding N₂.

Adding pure nitrogen corresponds to moving the point along a **tie line through the current composition**, toward the **inert corner (0, 0)**.

The current fuel-to-oxygen ratio is:

$$ \dfrac{N_F}{N_O} = \dfrac{7}{19.53} = 0.3584 $$

Along the tie line:

$$ N_F = 0.3584 \cdot N_O $$

The tie line must intersect the **envelope boundary** at the LFL corner (0 %, 5 %) and at the LOC corner (12 %, $N_F = 0.3584 \times 12 = 4.30\%$). Since the LOC corner point (12, 4.30) lies **below the LFL** of 5 % at the same oxygen level, the **binding constraint is the LFL at 5 %** when we reach $N_O = 12\%$.

Solving for the safe $N_O$ at LFL = 5 %:

$$ 5 = 0.3584 \cdot N_O \implies N_O = 13.95\% $$

Since $13.95\% > 12\% = LOC$, we can safely reach the **envelope boundary** at the **stoichiometric-fuel intersection with LFL**. Beyond this, the mixture is non-flammable.

*Required total inert:* $N_I = 100 - N_F - N_O = 100 - 5 - 13.95 = 81.05\%$.

*Current inert:* $N_I = 100 - 7 - 19.53 = 73.47\%$.

*Additional nitrogen required (in vol % of total):* $81.05 - 73.47 = 7.58\%$.

*Volume of nitrogen to add:*

$$ V_{N_2} = 0.0758 \times 10 \text{ m}^3 = 0.758 \text{ m}^3 $$

$$\boxed{V_{N_2} \approx 0.76 \text{ m}^3 \text{ at 25 °C, 1 atm}}$$

---

### 🔹 QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** **(7 Marks)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **construction of a flammability diagram** for a fuel–air–nitrogen system. Label all five key points and three reference lines.

**(b)** **(7 Marks)** A storage tank contains hexane vapor at 3 vol % in air. Given LFL(hexane) = 1.1 %, UFL(hexane) = 7.5 %, LOC(hexane) = 11.5 %. Calculate the percentage of nitrogen that must be added to make the vapor space non-flammable.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Construction Steps:**

1. **Draw the axes:** X-axis = O₂ concentration (0 to 100 %), Y-axis = Fuel concentration (0 to 100 %). Mark the **origin (0, 0) = 100 % inert**.

2. **Mark the five key points:**
   - Origin (0, 0) — 100 % Inert
   - Pure fuel (0, 100) — 100 % Fuel
   - Pure O₂ (100, 0) — 100 % Oxidizer
   - LFL on Y-axis at (0, LFL)
   - UFL on Y-axis at (0, UFL)

3. **Draw the air line:** Vertical at $N_O = 21\%$.

4. **Draw the stoichiometric line:** From pure-fuel point (0, 100) to pure-oxygen point (100, 0), passing through the stoichiometric point $(x, y)$.

5. **Draw tie lines:** Multiple straight lines from the Y-axis to the X-axis at various ratios.

6. **Mark LFL and UFL points** at (0, LFL) and (0, UFL). Join them to the LOC point on the air line to form the **lemon-shaped flammability envelope**.

7. **Mark the LOC point** on the air line at (LOC, $N_F$ corresponding to LFL) and draw the envelope enclosing the flammable region.

**The flammable region is a closed envelope bounded by:**
- The LFL point (lower-left corner near origin)
- The UFL point (upper-right area)
- The LOC point on the air line (lower-right corner)

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

*Initial composition:*
- $N_F = 3\%$, $N_O = 21 \times \dfrac{97}{100} = 20.37\%$ (assuming hexane displaces air).

*Step 1: Check current flammability*
- $1.1 < 3 < 7.5$ → **FLAMMABLE** (inside envelope).

*Step 2: Apply tie-line equation*
- Current fuel-to-oxygen ratio: $N_F/N_O = 3/20.37 = 0.1473$.

*Step 3: Find intersection with envelope boundary (LFL corner)*
- At LFL = 1.1 %: $N_O = 1.1/0.1473 = 7.47\%$
- Since $7.47\% < LOC = 11.5\%$, the **tie line exits through the LOC corner** (vertical boundary).

*Step 4: Required composition for safety*
- Move along tie line until $N_O = LOC = 11.5\%$
- $N_F = 0.1473 \times 11.5 = 1.69\%$
- $N_I = 100 - 1.69 - 11.5 = 86.81\%$

*Step 5: Original inert content*
- $N_I^{\text{initial}} = 100 - 3 - 20.37 = 76.63\%$

*Step 6: Nitrogen to add*
- $\Delta N_I = 86.81 - 76.63 = 10.18\%$

$$\boxed{\text{Approximately 10.2 vol \% nitrogen must be added (per total mixture)}}$$

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the constraint $N_F + N_O + N_I = 100$.** Many students compute fuel and oxygen but forget the inert gas calculation, leading to inconsistent compositions. **Always check your arithmetic sums to 100.**
> 
> 2. **Confusing tie lines with the stoichiometric line.** The stoichiometric line has a **fixed** slope of $-y/x$ for a given fuel. Tie lines have **variable slopes** depending on the fuel-to-oxygen ratio of the mixture.
> 
> 3. **Mixing up "vol %" with "mol %".** In a flammability diagram, all concentrations are in **vol % (or equivalently, mol %)** — never in mass %.
> 
> 4. **Assuming dilution with air is safe.** Adding more air moves the point *down along the air line* — this can **still be inside the envelope** if the fuel is in the LFL–UFL range. Always re-check.
> 
> 5. **Not labelling the LOC point on the air line.** This is the single most-skipped diagram label. Examiners explicitly look for it.
> 
> 6. **Wrong unit conversions.** When using ideal gas law, $V = nRT/P$, ensure $T$ is in **Kelvin** and $P$ in consistent units.

---
