> **High-Density Summary — Read This 5 Minutes Before the Exam**

- [x] **Flammability Diagram** = Plot of $N_F$ (Y) vs. $N_O$ (X) for a ternary fuel-oxidizer-inert system.
- [x] **Constraint equation:** $N_F + N_O + N_I = 100\%$ (always verify!).
- [x] **Five Key Points:** Origin (inert corner), Pure fuel (top of Y-axis), Pure O₂ (right of X-axis), LFL point (on Y-axis at LFL %), UFL point (on Y-axis at UFL %).
- [x] **Three Reference Lines:** Air line (vertical at 21 % O₂), Stoichiometric line (slope $-y/x$), Tie lines (any constant $N_F/N_O$ ratio).
- [x] **The Flammable Envelope** is a **lemon-shaped closed curve** bounded by LFL, UFL, and LOC.
- [x] **LOC (Limiting Oxygen Concentration)** is the **anchor on the air line** — below this, NO mixture is flammable.
- [x] **Tie lines move toward the inert corner** (origin) when diluting with N₂; **down the air line** when diluting with air; **up the Y-axis** when adding more fuel.
- [x] **Mixing formula:** $C_{\text{final}} = (P_1 C_1 + P_2 C_2)/(P_1 + P_2)$ — weighted average of endpoint compositions.
- [x] **Inerting criterion:** Move composition to where $N_O < LOC$ **or** $N_F < LFL$ **or** $N_F > UFL$.
- [x] **Real-world uses:** Storage tank design, confined-space purging, HAZOP studies, emergency venting, inert gas blanketing.
- [x] **Default values to remember:** Air = 21 % O₂; typical hydrocarbon LOC ≈ 10–12 %.
- [x] **Always plot** the original and final points, draw the connecting line (tie line or air line), and **check if it crosses the envelope**.

---

✅ **END OF NOTES — KTU 2024 SCHEME | OECHT723 | MODULE 2 | FLAMMABILITY DIAGRAM**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hazards in Process Industry: Classification, Chemical, Physical, Electrical, Mechanical, and Environmental Hazards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT723 - PROCESS SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Hazards in Process Industry: Classification, Chemical, Physical, Electrical, Mechanical, and Environmental Hazards

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Hazard (KTU 2024 Definition):** A *hazard* is any physical, chemical, biological, or environmental condition that has the potential to cause harm to people, property, environment, or production. In process industries, a hazard is an intrinsic property of a material, equipment, or process that can result in an undesired event (accident, injury, fatality, or asset loss).

In process safety engineering, hazard identification is the **first pillar** of the *Risk Management Hierarchy*: **Identify → Assess → Control → Monitor → Review**. The classical OSHA-NIOSH framework defines a hazard as a *source of danger*, while **risk** is the *probability × consequence* combination of that hazard materializing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hazard vs. Risk (Key Distinction):**
> - **Hazard** = "Can it cause harm?" (inherent property)
> - **Risk** = "How likely is harm and how severe?" (probability-driven)
>
> Example: **Toxicity of chlorine** is a *hazard*. **Releasing 1 ton of chlorine near a populated area** is a *risk scenario*.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Kitchen is a Mini Process Plant":**
> Imagine your kitchen as a small process industry. You have:
> - *LPG cylinder* → pressurized flammable gas (Physical + Fire hazard)
> - *Knife* → sharp object (Mechanical hazard)
> - *Wet floor near stove* → slip + electric shock hazard (Electrical + Mechanical)
> - *Burning toast smoke* → air quality hazard (Environmental + Toxic)
> - *Cleaning acid (phenol-based)* → corrosive hazard (Chemical)
>
> Just as a chef must know **all** hazards in a kitchen, a process safety engineer must systematically classify **every** potential source of harm in a chemical plant.

The major categories, as per **CCPS (Center for Chemical Process Safety)** guidelines adopted by KTU 2024, are:

1. **Chemical Hazards**
2. **Physical Hazards**
3. **Electrical Hazards**
4. **Mechanical Hazards**
5. **Environmental Hazards**
6. **Fire & Explosion Hazards** (cross-cutting)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Metric (used globally in industry):**
> - **NFPA Hazard Rating Diamond** is the universal industry standard for hazard communication.
> - **GHS (Globally Harmonized System)** classifies chemicals by physical, health, and environmental hazards.
> - **OSHA 29 CFR 1910** (US), **IEC 61511** (International) and **IS 14489** (Indian Standard) form the legal-regulatory framework.

The standard metric for acute toxicity is the **LC₅₀ (Lethal Concentration 50)** measured in **ppm or mg/m³**, and for fire/explosion, the **Lower Explosive Limit (LEL)** in **% v/v** is the governing metric.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Systematic Classification of Hazards

### 2.1.1 Chemical Hazards
Chemical hazards arise from the **toxic, corrosive, reactive, or biologically harmful nature** of substances handled in the plant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sub-classes of Chemical Hazards:**
> 1. **Toxicity** (acute / chronic / systemic)
> 2. **Corrosivity** (acids, bases, oxidizers)
> 3. **Reactivity** (unstable, water-reactive, pyrophoric)
> 4. **Carcinogenicity / Mutagenicity / Reproductive toxicity (CMR)**
> 5. **Asphyxiation** (O₂ displacement)
> 6. **Sensitization & Irritation**

**Common Examples in Process Industry:**
| Chemical | Hazard Type | Industry Exposure |
|---|---|---|
| H₂S (Hydrogen Sulfide) | Toxic + Flammable | Oil & Gas, Refineries |
| Cl₂ (Chlorine) | Toxic + Corrosive | Water Treatment, PVC |
| NH₃ (Ammonia) | Toxic + Corrosive | Fertilizer, Refrigeration |
| Benzene | Carcinogen + Flammable | Petrochemical |
| HF (Hydrofluoric Acid) | Highly Corrosive + Toxic | Refineries, Etching |
| HNO₃ (Nitric Acid) | Oxidizer + Corrosive | Explosives, Fertilizer |

**Key Toxicological Metrics:**
- **TLV-TWA** (Time-Weighted Average, 8-hr exposure)
- **TLV-STEL** (Short-Term Exposure Limit, 15-min)
- **TLV-C** (Ceiling limit — never to exceed)
- **IDLH** (Immediately Dangerous to Life or Health) — **30-minute** escape window
- **LC₅₀ / LD₅₀** — lethal concentration/dose for 50% mortality in test population

### 2.1.2 Physical Hazards
Physical hazards originate from **environmental conditions or material states** that cause harm without chemical reaction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sub-classes of Physical Hazards:**
> 1. **Fire** (Pool, Jet, Flash, Smoldering)
> 2. **Explosion** (VCE, BLEVE, Dust, Confined)
> 3. **Pressure** (Overpressure, Vacuum collapse)
> 4. **Temperature** (Cryogenic burns, Thermal radiation)
> 5. **Noise & Vibration** (>85 dBA OSHA limit)
> 6. **Radiation** (Ionizing: α, β, γ; Non-ionizing: UV, IR, RF)
> 7. **Ergonomic / Slip-Trip-Fall**

**Critical Fire/Explosion Parameters:**
- **Flash Point (FP)** — lowest temperature at which liquid emits vapor that *ignites briefly* on application of ignition source.
- **Fire Point (FP+1°C to 5°C)** — temperature at which vapor burns **sustainably** ≥5 seconds.
- **Auto-Ignition Temperature (AIT)** — self-ignition without external source.
- **Lower/Upper Explosive Limits (LEL/UEL)** — fuel concentration range in air for ignition.
- **Limiting Oxygen Concentration (LOC)** — minimum O₂ to sustain combustion.
- **Minimum Ignition Energy (MIE)** — minimum spark energy required (methane ≈ 0.28 mJ).

### 2.1.3 Electrical Hazards
Caused by **current flow, arcing, static buildup, or induced voltages**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Major Electrical Hazards:**
> 1. **Electric Shock / Electrocution** (> 100 mA can be fatal at 50 Hz AC)
> 2. **Arc Flash** (up to **35,000 °F** / 19,400 °C — copper vaporizes)
> 3. **Arc Blast** (pressure wave + shrapnel, up to 100 kPa overpressure)
> 4. **Static Electricity** (discharge during transfer of flammables)
> 5. **Lightning Strike** (especially on storage tanks)
> 6. **Short Circuits & Ground Faults** (fire ignition source)
> 7. **Stray Currents** (corrosion + sparking in tanks)

### 2.1.4 Mechanical Hazards
Originate from **physical equipment motion, energy storage, or mechanical failure**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Major Mechanical Hazards:**
> 1. **Rotating Equipment** (pumps, compressors, mixers — entanglement)
> 2. **Moving Parts** (conveyors, belts, gears)
> 3. **Pressurized Systems** (vessels, pipelines — stored strain energy)
> 4. **Lifting Operations** (cranes, hoists — dropped loads)
> 5. **Confined Space Entry** (oxygen-deficient atmospheres)
> 6. **Falling Objects / Height Work**
> 7. **Sharp Edges, Pinch Points, Crush Points**

### 2.1.5 Environmental Hazards
Hazards released **beyond the plant boundary** affecting air, water, soil, ecology, and community.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Major Environmental Hazards:**
> 1. **Air Pollution** (SO₂, NOₓ, VOCs, PM₂.₅, CO₂ — climate)
> 2. **Water Pollution** (oils, phenols, heavy metals, BOD load)
> 3. **Soil & Groundwater Contamination** (underground tank leaks)
> 4. **Hazardous Waste Accumulation**
> 5. **Thermal Pollution** (warm cooling-water discharge)
> 6. **Noise Pollution** (> 55 dB at residential boundary)
> 7. **Natural Disasters** (floods, earthquakes, cyclones — external hazards)

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

### 2.2.1 Fire & Explosion Parameters

| Parameter | Formula / Relation | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Lower Explosive Limit (LEL) | Empirical (Le Chatelier for mixtures) | % v/v | Below LEL → too lean |
| Upper Explosive Limit (UEL) | Empirical | % v/v | Above UEL → too rich |
| Le Chatelier Rule | $LFL_{mix}=\dfrac{100}{\sum_{i}\dfrac{y_i}{LFL_i}}$ | % v/v | $y_i$ = mole fraction of component i |
| Flash Point ↔ Fire Point | $T_{fire} \approx T_{flash} + (1 \text{ to } 5)^{\circ}C$ | °C | KTU standard value |
| Auto-Ignition Trend | Decreases with ↑ pressure, ↑ surface area | °C | AIT of gasoline ≈ 280 °C |
| LOC (Limiting O₂) | $LOC = LFL \times \dfrac{\text{Molar O}_2/\text{Fuel at LFL}}{100}$ | % v/v | Typically 8–12% for hydrocarbons |
| MIE (Minimum Ignition Energy) | $MIE \approx \dfrac{E_{spark}}{E_{loss}}$ | mJ | Hydrocarbons: 0.1–1 mJ |

### 2.2.2 Explosion Energy / TNT Equivalent

| Formula | Expression | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| TNT Equivalent Mass | $m_{TNT}=\dfrac{m_{fuel}\cdot \Delta H_{c, fuel}}{\Delta H_{TNT}}$ | $\Delta H_{TNT}=4.68\,$MJ/kg |
| Explosion Energy | $E = m_{TNT} \cdot \Delta H_{TNT}$ | kJ or MJ |
| Scaled Distance | $\bar{r}=\dfrac{r}{m_{TNT}^{1/3}}$ | m / kg$^{\tfrac{1}{3}}$ |
| Side-on Overpressure (TNO) | $\Delta P = f(\bar{r})$ — chart-based | kPa |
| Kst (Dust Explosion) | $K_{st}=\left(\dfrac{dP}{dt}\right)_{max}\cdot \sqrt{P_{max}}$ | bar·m/s |
| St Class | St 1 (Kst < 200), St 2 (200–300), St 3 (> 300) | — | KTU standard |

### 2.2.3 Toxicity & Exposure Models

| Parameter | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|
| TLV-TWA | Time-weighted average over 8 hrs | ppm or mg/m³ |
| TLV-STEL | 15-min average, max 4 exposures/day | ppm |
| IDLH | 30-min escape threshold | ppm |
| Probit Equation (general) | $Y = a + b\,\ln(C^{n}\cdot t)$ | dimensionless |
| Probit → % Fatality | $P = 50 \left[1 + \text{erf}\left(\dfrac{Y-5}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right]$ | % |

**Selected Probit Constants (CCPS):**
| Chemical | a | b | n |
|---|---|---|---|
| Chlorine (Cl₂) | −17.1 | 1.69 | 2.0 |
| Ammonia (NH₃) | −35.9 | 2.10 | 2.0 |
| Hydrogen Sulfide (H₂S) | −31.4 | 2.04 | 1.9 |
| Sulfur Dioxide (SO₂) | −19.2 | 1.69 | 2.0 |
| Phosgene (COCl₂) | −19.3 | 3.79 | 1.0 |

### 2.2.4 Thermal Radiation (Pool/Jet Fires)

| Formula | Expression | Use |
|---|---|---|
| Inverse-Square Law | $q''=\dfrac{Q}{4\pi r^{2}}$ for point source | Conservative estimate |
| Point Source Model | $q''(r)=q''(r_{0})\left(\dfrac{r_{0}}{r}\right)^{2}$ | $q''$ in kW/m² |
| Solid Flame Model | $q''(r)=F\cdot \tau\cdot \Delta H_{c}\cdot m''$ | $F$ = view factor |
| Heat Flux Damage | 1.2 kW/m² (skin pain), 6.3 kW/m² (ignition of wood) | — |

### 2.2.5 Electrical Hazard Constants

| Parameter | Value (KTU 2024 standard) |
|---|---|
| Human body resistance (wet skin) | ≈ 1000 Ω |
| Human body resistance (dry skin) | ≈ 100,000 Ω |
| Let-go threshold (60 Hz AC) | ≈ 10–20 mA |
| Ventricular fibrillation threshold | ≈ 100 mA (1 sec) |
| Safe voltage (DC, dry) | < 50 V |
| Safe voltage (AC, wet) | < 25 V |

---

## 2.3 Engineering Utility — Why This Matters

The hazard classification forms the **input layer** of every modern process safety methodology:

1. **HAZOP (Hazard and Operability Study)** uses deviations for every hazard class.
2. **LOPA (Layer of Protection Analysis)** requires frequency data per hazard class.
3. **Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA)** uses probit models and TNT equivalents.
4. **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)** determination per **IEC 61511** depends on hazard severity.
5. **Emergency Planning & Community Right-to-Know** (EPCRA / OSHA PSM) require hazard categorization.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — TNT Equivalent Mass of a Vapor Cloud Explosion

### Problem Statement
**A vapor cloud of 5,000 kg of propane escapes and ignites. Calculate the TNT equivalent mass and the energy released.**

**Given:**
- Mass of propane released: $m_{fuel}=5{,}000$ kg
- Heat of combustion of propane: $\Delta H_{c, fuel}=46{,}000$ kJ/kg
- Heat of explosion of TNT: $\Delta H_{TNT}=4{,}680$ kJ/kg

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Write the governing energy-equivalence formula.

$$
m_{TNT}=\frac{m_{fuel}\cdot \Delta H_{c, fuel}}{\Delta H_{TNT}}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute numerical values into the formula.

$$
m_{TNT}=\frac{5{,}000\,\text{kg}\times 46{,}000\,\text{kJ/kg}}{4{,}680\,\text{kJ/kg}}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the numerator (energy released by propane combustion).

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{fuel}&=m_{fuel}\cdot \Delta H_{c, fuel}\\
&=5{,}000\,\text{kg}\times 46{,}000\,\text{kJ/kg}\\
&=2.30\times 10^{8}\,\text{kJ}\\
&=230{,}000\,\text{MJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the TNT equivalent mass.

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{TNT}&=\frac{2.30\times 10^{8}\,\text{kJ}}{4{,}680\,\text{kJ/kg}}\\
&=49{,}145.3\,\text{kg}\\
&\approx 49.15\,\text{metric tons of TNT}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Compute the total explosion energy in MJ (verification).

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{TNT}&=m_{TNT}\cdot \Delta H_{TNT}\\
&=49{,}145.3\,\text{kg}\times 4.68\,\text{MJ/kg}\\
&\approx 230{,}000\,\text{MJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** The propane vapor cloud is equivalent to **≈ 49.15 tonnes of TNT** releasing **≈ 230,000 MJ** of explosion energy — comparable to small tactical weapon yield.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Probit Analysis for Toxic Gas Release (Chlorine)

### Problem Statement
**A chlorine leak produces a sustained ground-level concentration of 200 ppm for 25 minutes. Estimate the percentage fatality using the probit equation.**

**Given (CCPS, Chlorine):** $a=-17.1,\; b=1.69,\; n=2.0$

**Concentration:** $C=200$ ppm

**Exposure time:** $t=25$ min

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Write the generalized probit equation.

$$
Y=a+b\,\ln(C^{n}\cdot t)
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the constants for chlorine.

$$
Y=-17.1+1.69\,\ln(C^{2}\cdot t)
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the term inside the logarithm.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C^{2}\cdot t&=(200)^{2}\times 25\\
&=40{,}000\times 25\\
&=1{,}000{,}000\,\text{ppm}^{2}\cdot \text{min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Take the natural logarithm of the term.

$$
\ln(1{,}000{,}000)=\ln(10^{6})=6.0\times\ln(10)\approx 6.0\times 2.3026=13.8155
$$

**Step 5:** Substitute into the probit equation.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y&=-17.1+1.69\times 13.8155\\
&=-17.1+23.348\\
&=6.248
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6:** Convert probit value *Y* into percent fatality *P* using the standard normal transformation.

$$
P=50\left[1+\text{erf}\left(\frac{Y-5}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right]
$$

**Step 7:** Evaluate the argument of the error function.

$$
\frac{Y-5}{\sqrt{2}}=\frac{6.248-5}{1.4142}=\frac{1.248}{1.4142}=0.8825
$$

**Step 8:** Read the standard normal table / compute the error function value.

$$
\text{erf}(0.8825)\approx 0.7867
$$

**Step 9:** Compute the fatality percentage.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P&=50\,[1+0.7867]\\
&=50\times 1.7867\\
&=89.33\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** Approximately **89.3 % fatality** in the exposed population — this classifies the scenario as **Catastrophic (Level 5)** per the KTU 2024 consequence matrix.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Thermal Radiation via Inverse-Square Law

### Problem Statement
**A pool fire produces 100 kW/m² at a reference distance of 10 m from the flame center. Calculate the thermal radiation received at 50 m, 100 m, and 250 m. Identify the damage threshold distances.**

**Given:** $q''(r_0)=100$ kW/m² at $r_0=10$ m.

**Damage thresholds (industry standard):**
- **1.2 kW/m²** → Skin pain in 30 s
- **4.0 kW/m²** → First-degree burns
- **6.3 kW/m²** → Wood ignites within seconds
- **12.5 kW/m²** → Steel structural failure

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Write the inverse-square law for a point source.

$$
q''(r)=q''(r_0)\left(\frac{r_0}{r}\right)^{2}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute radiation at $r=50$ m.

$$
\begin{aligned}
q''(50)&=100\left(\frac{10}{50}\right)^{2}\\
&=100\times (0.2)^{2}\\
&=100\times 0.04\\
&=4.0\,\text{kW/m}^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute radiation at $r=100$ m.

$$
\begin{aligned}
q''(100)&=100\left(\frac{10}{100}\right)^{2}\\
&=100\times 0.01\\
&=1.0\,\text{kW/m}^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute radiation at $r=250$ m.

$$
\begin{aligned}
q''(250)&=100\left(\frac{10}{250}\right)^{2}\\
&=100\times (0.04)^{2}\\
&=100\times 0.0016\\
&=0.16\,\text{kW/m}^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Determine damage threshold distances by inversion.

$$
r_{threshold}=r_0\cdot\sqrt{\frac{q''(r_0)}{q''_{threshold}}}
$$

**Step 6:** For wood ignition (6.3 kW/m²):

$$
r_{wood}=10\cdot\sqrt{\frac{100}{6.3}}=10\times 3.984=39.84\,\text{m}
$$

**Step 7:** For skin pain (1.2 kW/m²):

$$
r_{skin}=10\cdot\sqrt{\frac{100}{1.2}}=10\times 9.129=91.29\,\text{m}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result Summary Table:**
>
> | Distance (m) | q'' (kW/m²) | Effect |
> |---|---|---|
> | 10 | 100 | Source (flame surface) |
> | 50 | 4.0 | First-degree burns |
> | 100 | 1.0 | Below pain threshold |
> | 250 | 0.16 | Safe (unaffected) |

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Limiting Oxygen Concentration (LOC) Calculation

### Problem Statement
**Methane has LFL = 5.0% v/v in air. The stoichiometric combustion reaction is:** $\text{CH}_4 + 2\,\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$. Calculate the Limiting Oxygen Concentration (LOC) for methane.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Write the LOC formula.

$$
LOC=LFL\times \frac{\text{Mole fraction O}_2 \text{ at stoichiometric}}{\text{Total moles of mixture}}
$$

**Step 2:** At LFL, stoichiometric O₂ to fuel ratio for methane = 2:1.

**Step 3:** At LFL = 5%, fuel = 5 moles, O₂ required = $5\times 2 = 10$ moles.

**Step 4:** Total mixture moles = 100 (basis). Remaining = N₂ + excess O₂.

**Step 5:** Normal air composition = 21% O₂, 79% N₂.

**Step 6:** O₂ in air = 21% of (100−5) = 21% × 95 = 19.95 moles.

**Step 7:** For inerting, LOC corresponds to O₂ when N₂ replaces O₂:

$$
LOC=LFL\times\frac{\text{O}_2\text{ stoichiometric}}{\text{Fuel}+\text{O}_2}
$$

**Step 8:** At stoichiometric condition for 5 moles fuel:

$$
LOC=5\times\frac{10}{5+10}=5\times 0.6667=3.33\,\%
$$

**Step 9:** Apply the standard safety factor (actual LOC is empirical).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** $LOC\approx 12\%$ (empirical safety value), meaning methane cannot burn if oxygen is reduced below 12% by volume — basis of **nitrogen blanketing** in storage tanks.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Hazard Frequency vs Consequence

| Hazard Class | Typical Frequency | Typical Consequence | Risk Priority |
|---|---|---|---|
| Toxic Release (large) | Low (10⁻⁴/yr) | Very High (mass fatality) | **Critical** |
| Vapour Cloud Explosion | Low (10⁻⁵/yr) | Very High (catastrophic) | **Critical** |
| Fire (pool/jet) | Medium (10⁻³/yr) | High | **High** |
| Mechanical Failure (rupture) | Medium (10⁻³/yr) | High | **High** |
| Electric Shock (worker) | Medium (10⁻²/yr) | Medium | **Medium** |
| Static Discharge (small) | High (10⁻¹/yr) | Low–Medium | **Medium** |
| Slip/Trip/Fall | Very High (10⁰/yr) | Low | **Low–Medium** |
| Environmental Release (chronic) | High | Long-term (chronic) | **Medium–High** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hazard Classification Tree (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    HA[HAZARD IN PROCESS INDUSTRY] --> HB[CHEMICAL HAZARDS]
    HA --> HC[PHYSICAL HAZARDS]
    HA --> HD[ELECTRICAL HAZARDS]
    HA --> HE[MECHANICAL HAZARDS]
    HA --> HF[ENVIRONMENTAL HAZARDS]
    HA --> HG[FIRE AND EXPLOSION HAZARDS]

    HB --> HB1[Toxic Substances]
    HB --> HB2[Corrosive Materials]
    HB --> HB3[Reactive and Unstable]
    HB --> HB4[Carcinogens and CMR]
    HB --> HB5[Asphyxiants]

    HC --> HC1[Temperature Extremes]
    HC --> HC2[Pressure Anomalies]
    HC --> HC3[Noise and Vibration]
    HC --> HC4[Radiation Ionizing Non]
    HC --> HC5[Ergonomic Strain]

    HD --> HD1[Electric Shock]
    HD --> HD2[Arc Flash and Arc Blast]
    HD --> HD3[Static Electricity]
    HD --> HD4[Lightning and Stray]

    HE --> HE1[Rotating Equipment]
    HE --> HE2[Moving Parts and Pinch]
    HE --> HE3[Pressurized System Failure]
    HE --> HE4[Lifting and Falling]
    HE --> HE5[Confined Space Entry]

    HF --> HF1[Air Pollution]
    HF --> HF2[Water Pollution]
    HF --> HF3[Soil Contamination]
    HF --> HF4[Noise Pollution]
    HF --> HF5[Climate and Natural]
```

## 4.2 Fire Triangle and Explosion Pentagon

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FT1[FUEL] --- FT2[OXIDIZER]
    FT2 --- FT3[IGNITION SOURCE]
    FT3 --- FT1
    FT1 -.-> FTC[COMBUSTION FIRE]
    FT2 -.-> FTC
    FT3 -.-> FTC

    EP1[FUEL] --- EP2[OXIDIZER]
    EP2 --- EP3[IGNITION SOURCE]
    EP3 --- EP4[CONFINEMENT]
    EP4 --- EP5[MIXING]
    EP5 --- EP1
    EP1 -.-> EPC[EXPLOSION]
    EP2 -.-> EPC
    EP3 -.-> EPC
    EP4 -.-> EPC
    EP5 -.-> EPC
```

## 4.3 Bow-Tie Diagram — Hazard Path to Consequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    THR[Threats] --> TOP[Hazardous Event]
    TOP --> CONS[Consequences]
    THR --- THR1[Equipment Failure]
    THR --- THR2[Human Error]
    THR --- THR3[External Event]

    TOP --- TOP1[Prevention Controls]
    CONS --- CONS1[Mitigation Controls]

    THR1 --> TOP
    THR2 --> TOP
    THR3 --> TOP

    TOP --> CONS

    CONS --> CONS2[Fire]
    CONS --> CONS3[Explosion]
    CONS --> CONS4[Toxic Release]
    CONS --> CONS5[Environmental Damage]

    TOP1 --- PC1[SIS Safety Instrumented System]
    TOP1 --- PC2[Procedural Controls]
    TOP1 --- PC3[Design Integrity]

    CONS1 --- MC1[Relief Valves]
    CONS1 --- MC2[Dike and Bund]
    CONS1 --- MC3[Emergency Shutdown]
```

## 4.4 NFPA Hazard Diamond Reference

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    NFPA[NFPA 704 HAZARD DIAMOND] --> NF1[Blue Left - Health]
    NFPA --> NF2[Red Top - Flammability]
    NFPA --> NF3[Yellow Right - Reactivity]
    NFPA --> NF4[White Bottom - Special Hazard]

    NF1 --> NF1A[0 No Hazard]
    NF1 --> NF1B[1 Slight]
    NF1 --> NF1C[2 Moderate]
    NF1 --> NF1D[3 Serious]
    NF1 --> NF1E[4 Fatal]

    NF2 --> NF2A[0 Will Not Burn]
    NF2 --> NF2B[1 FP above 93 C]
    NF2 --> NF2C[2 FP 38 to 93 C]
    NF2 --> NF2D[3 FP below 38 C]
    NF2 --> NF2E[4 FP below 23 C]

    NF3 --> NF3A[0 Stable]
    NF3 --> NF3B[1 Mild Reactive]
    NF3 --> NF3C[2 Moderate Reactive]
    NF3 --> NF3D[3 Severe Reactive]
    NF3 --> NF3E[4 Explosive]

    NF4 --> NF4A[OX Oxidizer]
    NF4 --> NF4B[W Water Reactive]
    NF4 --> NF4C[COR Corrosive]
    NF4 --> NF4D[Radioactive]
```

## 4.5 Toxicological Dose-Response Curve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C0[Dose Zero] --> C1[Threshold NOAEL]
    C1 --> C2[LOAEL First Effects]
    C2 --> C3[TLV Threshold Limit]
    C3 --> C4[PEL Permissible]
    C4 --> C5[STEL Short Term]
    C5 --> C6[IDLH]
    C6 --> C7[LC50 50 Percent Fatal]
    C7 --> C8[LC100 100 Percent Fatal]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Differentiate between **Hazard** and **Risk** in a process plant. Give one example for each in a petrochemical refinery.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Hazard | Risk |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Intrinsic property with potential to cause harm | Probability × Consequence of harm |
| Nature | Qualitative (Yes/No) | Quantitative (numeric) |
| Control | By inherent design | By barriers, mitigation, controls |
| Example | Toxicity of chlorine gas | Leak of 1-ton chlorine in populated area |

**Example (Refinery):**
- *Hazard:* Presence of **H₂S** in the hydrodesulfurization unit.
- *Risk:* Probability (e.g., 10⁻⁴/year) × Consequence (fatality) = unacceptable → requires mitigation.

**(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Define **LC₅₀** and **IDLH**. Why are these metrics critical in process plant emergency planning?

**Model Answer:**

- **LC₅₀ (Lethal Concentration 50):** The concentration of a substance in air (in ppm or mg/m³) that is expected to **kill 50% of the test population** within a specified exposure time (usually 4 hours). It is a measure of **acute toxicity**.
- **IDLH (Immediately Dangerous to Life or Health):** The maximum airborne concentration to which a worker **can be exposed for 30 minutes** without experiencing irreversible health effects or escape impairment.

**Criticality in Emergency Planning:**
1. Defines **escape zone radius** for toxic releases (IDLH contour).
2. Determines **PPE Level A/B/C** selection for emergency responders.
3. Used in **Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA)** for consequence modeling.
4. Drives **occupancy criteria** in plant layout (occupied buildings outside IDLH contour).

**(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]** *(Module 2 — Internal Choice Set 1)*

**(a)** Classify the **different hazards encountered in process industries**. Discuss in detail the **chemical hazards** with examples of five highly hazardous chemicals commonly encountered in a fertilizer plant. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A **pool fire** in an LPG storage facility produces a heat flux of **80 kW/m² at 15 m**. Determine the safe distance for **(i) skin pain (1.2 kW/m²)**, **(ii) wood ignition (6.3 kW/m²)**, and **(iii) structural damage (12.5 kW/m²)**. State the inverse-square law and its limitations. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Hazard Classification & Chemical Hazards

**Step 1:** State the formal classification (KTU syllabus-aligned).

Hazards in process industries are classified into **five major categories** plus a cross-cutting fire/explosion category:

1. **Chemical Hazards** — Toxicity, corrosivity, reactivity, carcinogenicity
2. **Physical Hazards** — Fire, explosion, pressure, temperature, radiation, noise
3. **Electrical Hazards** — Shock, arc flash, static, lightning, stray currents
4. **Mechanical Hazards** — Rotating equipment, moving parts, lifting, confined space
5. **Environmental Hazards** — Air, water, soil pollution, thermal, noise, climate

**Step 2:** Detail chemical hazards sub-classes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sub-classes of Chemical Hazards:**
>
> | Sub-class | Mechanism | Acute/Chronic |
> |---|---|---|
> | Toxic | Cellular damage, enzyme inhibition | Both |
> | Corrosive | pH-mediated tissue destruction | Acute |
> | Reactive | Uncontrolled exothermic reaction | Acute |
> | Carcinogenic (CMR) | DNA mutation | Chronic |
> | Asphyxiant | O₂ displacement | Acute |
> | Sensitizer | Immune response | Chronic |

**Step 3:** Five highly hazardous chemicals in a **fertilizer plant**:

| Chemical | Primary Hazard | Secondary Hazard | Storage |
|---|---|---|---|
| **NH₃ (Ammonia)** | Toxic (IDLH = 300 ppm) | Corrosive (alkaline) | Pressurized liquid |
| **HNO₃ (Nitric Acid)** | Oxidizer, Corrosive | Fire accelerant | Concentrated acid |
| **Cl₂ (Chlorine)** | Toxic (IDLH = 10 ppm) | Corrosive, reactive | Liquefied gas |
| **H₂SO₄ (Sulfuric Acid)** | Corrosive (dehydrating) | Exothermic with water | Concentrated acid |
| **Urea (molten)** | Thermal (160 °C) | NH₃ release on decomposition | Hot liquid |

**Step 4:** Conclusion.

Effective chemical hazard management requires **MSDS/SDS review**, **engineering controls (ventilation, containment)**, **PPE**, and **emergency response planning** with **IDLH-based evacuation**.

**(7 marks complete)**

---

#### Part (b) — Pool Fire Heat Flux Safe Distance

**Step 1:** State the governing law.

For a point source of thermal radiation, the **inverse-square law** applies:

$$
q''(r)=q''(r_0)\left(\frac{r_0}{r}\right)^{2}
$$

**Limitations of the inverse-square law:**
1. Assumes **point source** (valid only when $r \geq 3 \times$ flame diameter).
2. **No atmospheric absorption** (neglects humidity, CO₂, H₂O vapor attenuation).
3. **No view-factor correction** for non-spherical geometry.
4. **No wind effects** on flame tilt or plume rise.

**Step 2:** Invert the law to compute safe distance.

$$
r_{threshold}=r_0\cdot\sqrt{\frac{q''(r_0)}{q''_{threshold}}}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute values: $r_0=15$ m, $q''(r_0)=80$ kW/m².

**(i) Skin pain threshold (1.2 kW/m²):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_{1.2}&=15\times\sqrt{\frac{80}{1.2}}\\
&=15\times\sqrt{66.67}\\
&=15\times 8.165\\
&=122.47\,\text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Wood ignition threshold (6.3 kW/m²):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_{6.3}&=15\times\sqrt{\frac{80}{6.3}}\\
&=15\times\sqrt{12.70}\\
&=15\times 3.564\\
&=53.46\,\text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Structural damage threshold (12.5 kW/m²):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_{12.5}&=15\times\sqrt{\frac{80}{12.5}}\\
&=15\times\sqrt{6.40}\\
&=15\times 2.530\\
&=37.95\,\text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Tabulate the safe distances.

| Effect | Heat Flux (kW/m²) | Safe Distance (m) |
|---|---|---|
| Skin pain (30 s) | 1.2 | 122.5 |
| Wood ignition | 6.3 | 53.5 |
| Structural damage | 12.5 | 38.0 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Recommendation:** Critical equipment and occupied buildings must be located **outside the 12.5 kW/m² radius** (~ 38 m). Open spaces and access roads permitted within the 1.2 kW/m² contour if personnel wear thermal PPE.

**(7 marks complete)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *(Module 2 — Internal Choice Set 2)*

**(a)** Discuss the **electrical and mechanical hazards** in a process plant with at least **four examples each**. Explain the phenomenon of **arc flash** and its danger zone calculation as per NFPA 70E. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A tank containing **1000 m³ of gasoline** catches fire (pool fire). The heat of combustion is **43 MJ/kg**, and the burning rate is **0.05 kg/m²·s**. If **30% of the heat** is radiated, determine the heat radiated per second from a 100 m² pan area. Use the **solid flame model** with view factor $F = 0.4$. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Electrical & Mechanical Hazards

**Step 1:** Electrical hazards (4 examples).

| # | Hazard | Mechanism | Consequence |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Electric Shock | Current through body (>100 mA fatal) | Cardiac fibrillation, death |
| 2 | Arc Flash | Short-circuit through air (35,000 °F) | Severe burns, blindness |
| 3 | Static Electricity | Charge buildup on tanks/pipes | Ignition of flammable vapors |
| 4 | Lightning | Direct strike on tanks/pipes | Fire, structural damage |

**Step 2:** Mechanical hazards (4 examples).

| # | Hazard | Mechanism | Consequence |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Rotating Shafts | Entanglement at pumps/compressors | Limb amputation |
| 2 | Pressurized Vessels | Stored strain energy release | Vessel rupture, shrapnel |
| 3 | Lifting Operations | Crane failure, dropped loads | Crush injuries, fatalities |
| 4 | Confined Space | O₂-deficient, toxic atmosphere | Asphyxiation, death |

**Step 3:** Arc flash detailed explanation.

An **arc flash** occurs when an electrical fault creates a low-impedance path through ionized air. The **arc blast** produces:
- Temperatures up to **35,000 °F** (≈ 4× sun's surface temperature)
- Pressure wave up to **100 kPa** (can throw a worker across a room)
- Copper vaporizes → expands **67,000×** in volume
- Sound level ≈ **140 dB** (jet-engine equivalent)

**Step 4:** NFPA 70E Arc Flash Boundary.

The **arc flash boundary** (AFB) is the distance at which a worker receives **1.2 cal/cm²** (onset of 2nd-degree burn).

**Empirical formula (IEEE 1584 simplified):**

$$
AFB=\sqrt{\frac{kVA_{bolted}\cdot t}{1.2\cdot 4.184}}
$$

Where $kVA_{bolted}$ = available short-circuit power, $t$ = clearing time in seconds.

**(7 marks complete)**

---

#### Part (b) — Pool Fire Heat Release Rate (Solid Flame Model)

**Step 1:** Identify the given data.

- Burning rate: $\dot{m}''=0.05$ kg/m²·s
- Pan area: $A=100$ m²
- Heat of combustion: $\Delta H_c=43{,}000$ kJ/kg
- Radiative fraction: $\chi_R=0.30$
- View factor: $F=0.4$

**Step 2:** Calculate the total heat release rate (HRR).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_{total}&=\dot{m}''\cdot A\cdot \Delta H_c\\
&=0.05\times 100\times 43{,}000\\
&=215{,}000\,\text{kW}\\
&=215\,\text{MW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Calculate the radiative heat release rate.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_{rad}&=\chi_R\cdot \dot{Q}_{total}\\
&=0.30\times 215{,}000\\
&=64{,}500\,\text{kW}\\
&=64.5\,\text{MW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Apply the solid flame model — incident heat flux at receiver.

$$
q''(r)=F\cdot \chi_R\cdot \dot{m}''\cdot \Delta H_c
$$

Wait — the proper solid flame model is:

$$
q''(r)=\dfrac{F\cdot \chi_R\cdot \dot{Q}_{total}}{4\pi r^{2}}
$$

**Step 5:** Compute the total radiated power (incident at receiver distance):

For a given distance $r$, the incident radiation per unit area of receiver:

$$
q''(r)=\dfrac{F\cdot \dot{Q}_{rad}}{4\pi r^{2}}
$$

**Step 6:** Compute for $r=50$ m (typical safe distance):

$$
\begin{aligned}
q''(50)&=\frac{0.4\times 64{,}500}{4\pi \times 50^{2}}\\
&=\frac{25{,}800}{31{,}415.93}\\
&=0.821\,\text{kW/m}^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7:** Safe distance calculation for $q''=6.3$ kW/m² (wood ignition):

$$
r_{safe}=50\times\sqrt{\frac{0.821}{6.3}}=50\times 0.361=18.05\,\text{m}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer Summary:**
>
> | Parameter | Value |
> |---|---|
> | Total HRR ($\dot{Q}_{total}$) | 215 MW |
> | Radiative HRR ($\dot{Q}_{rad}$) | 64.5 MW |
> | Incident flux at 50 m | 0.82 kW/m² |
> | Safe distance (wood ignition) | ≈ 18 m |

**(7 marks complete)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (KTU Examiner's Perspective):**
>
> 1. **Confusing Hazard with Risk** (–1 to –2 marks): Students often use these interchangeably. **Hazard = source of harm**; **Risk = probability × consequence**.
> 2. **Missing Units in Numerical Answers** (–0.5 to –1 mark): Always write **ppm, mg/m³, kW/m², % v/v** etc. A correct number without units is *partially* credited.
> 3. **Forgetting Safety Margin in Safe Distance Calculations** (–1 mark): The inverse-square law gives the *theoretical* value; engineering practice requires a **1.5×–2× safety factor** for layout design.
> 4. **Ignoring the Fire Triangle / Explosion Pentagon** (–2 marks): Every fire/explosion question MUST mention the **fire triangle** (fuel + oxidizer + ignition) or the **explosion pentagon** (adds mixing + confinement).
> 5. **Wrong Probit Constants** (–2 marks): Each chemical has *specific* probit coefficients (a, b, n). Using ammonia's constants for chlorine leads to gross errors.
> 6. **NFPA Diamond Memory Error** (–1 mark): Color coding is **Blue = Health (left), Red = Fire (top), Yellow = Reactivity (right), White = Special (bottom)**. Reversing colors costs a mark.
> 7. **Static Electricity → Ignition Source** (–1 mark): Often missed that **static discharge IS an ignition source** for flammable vapors during tanker loading.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Hazards in Process Industry**
>
> **A. Definition & Scope**
> - Hazard = intrinsic property with potential to cause harm
> - Risk = probability × consequence
> - Classification: Chemical, Physical, Electrical, Mechanical, Environmental (+ Fire/Explosion)
>
> **B. Chemical Hazards**
> - TLV-TWA, TLV-STEL, TLV-C, IDLH, LC₅₀, LD₅₀
> - Sub-classes: Toxic, Corrosive, Reactive, CMR, Asphyxiant, Sensitizer
> - Examples: H₂S, Cl₂, NH₃, Benzene, HF, HNO₃
>
> **C. Physical Hazards**
> - Fire Triangle: Fuel + Oxidizer + Ignition
> - Explosion Pentagon: + Mixing + Confinement
> - Flash Point, Fire Point (FP + 1–5 °C), AIT
> - LEL, UEL, LOC, MIE
> - Thermal radiation: Inverse-square law $q'' \propto 1/r^{2}$
>
> **D. Electrical Hazards**
> - Shock (>100 mA fatal at 50 Hz AC)
> - Arc Flash (35,000 °F), Arc Blast (100 kPa)
> - Static electricity = ignition source for flammable vapors
> - Lightning, Stray currents, Ground faults
>
> **E. Mechanical Hazards**
> - Rotating equipment, Moving parts, Pressurized systems
> - Lifting, Confined space, Falling objects
> - Stored strain energy in pressurized vessels
>
> **F. Environmental Hazards**
> - Air pollution (SO₂, NOₓ, VOCs, PM₂.₅, CO₂)
> - Water pollution (oils, phenols, heavy metals)
> - Soil contamination, Thermal pollution, Noise pollution
> - Natural disasters (external hazards)
>
> **G. Key Formulas to Memorize**
> - $m_{TNT}=\dfrac{m_{fuel}\cdot \Delta H_c}{\Delta H_{TNT}}$
> - $Y = a + b\,\ln(C^{n}\cdot t)$
> - $q''(r)=q''(r_0)\left(\dfrac{r_0}{r}\right)^{2}$
> - Le Chatelier: $LFL_{mix}=\dfrac{100}{\sum y_i / LFL_i}$
> - $K_{st}=\left(\dfrac{dP}{dt}\right)_{max}\cdot \sqrt{P_{max}}$
>
> **H. Standard Reference Documents**
> - NFPA 704 (Hazard Diamond)
> - GHS (Globally Harmonized System)
> - OSHA 29 CFR 1910 (PSM)
> - IEC 61511 (Functional Safety)
> - CCPS Guidelines (Consequence Analysis)
> - API 521 / API 520 (Pressure Relief)
>
> **I. Mnemonic for Hazard Classes — "C-P-E-M-E-F"**
> **C**hemical — **P**hysical — **E**lectrical — **M**echanical — **E**nvironmental — **F**ire/Explosion
>
> **J. NFPA Diamond Color Order (Clockwise from Top)**
> Red (Fire) → Yellow (Reactivity) → Blue (Health) → White (Special)
>
> **K. Typical Exam Topics (Highest Frequency in KTU)**
> 1. TNT equivalent calculations
> 2. Probit analysis
> 3. Thermal radiation / Safe distance
> 4. Hazard classification with examples
> 5. LFL/UEL/Le Chatelier for mixtures
> 6. NFPA Diamond interpretation
> 7. IDLH and emergency planning
> 8. Arc flash and electrical safety

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hazardous Area Classification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT723 - PROCESS SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Hazardous Area Classification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned)

**Hazardous Area Classification (HAC)** is the systematic methodology of dividing a plant, facility, or installation into discrete three-dimensional zones based on the **frequency of occurrence** and **duration of persistence** of a flammable or explosive atmosphere. This classification dictates the type of electrical and mechanical equipment that may be safely installed in each region, ensuring that ignition-capable apparatus does not coincide with a combustible atmosphere under normal or fault operating conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot:** Hazardous Area Classification is the *engineering discipline* of evaluating a process plant environment against the **Fire Triangle** (Fuel + Oxidizer + Ignition Source) to assign zones where electrical apparatus must meet stringent **explosion-proof (Ex)** design requirements.

The classification is governed internationally by **IEC 60079-10-1** (gas/vapor) and **IEC 60079-10-2** (combustible dusts). In India, the **Oil Industry Safety Directorate (OISD) Standard 144** and **IS 5572** provide parallel guidelines, while the **National Electrical Code (NEC) / NFPA 70** governs the United States.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The House on the Riverbank"

Imagine a house built beside a river that floods unpredictably. The architect logically divides the property into three regions:
- **Region A (Living Room):** Always underwater — only submarine equipment can be placed here.
- **Region B (Kitchen):** Flooded only during heavy rain (abnormal conditions) — waterproof but not submarine equipment needed.
- **Region C (Attic):** Almost never floods; standard household electrical items are safe.

Hazardous Area Classification does the *exact same thing* for a chemical plant. Instead of water, the "flood" is a **flammable vapor cloud**, and instead of furniture, we choose between **flameproof, increased-safety, or ordinary electrical fittings**. The "flood prediction model" is determined by the **grade of release** (continuous, primary, secondary).

### 1.3 Core Variables and Constants

The following standardized constants govern zone boundaries:

| Symbol / Term | Standard Value | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| $LFL$ (Lower Flammable Limit) | Material-specific (e.g., propane = **2.1 % v/v**) | Minimum vapor concentration for flammability |
| $UFL$ (Upper Flammable Limit) | Material-specific (e.g., propane = **9.5 % v/v**) | Maximum vapor concentration for flammability |
| $MESG$ | Equipment group metric (mm) | Maximum Experimental Safe Gap |
| $MIC$ | Equipment group metric (ratio) | Minimum Ignition Current |
| $T_{amb}$ | Typically **−20 °C to +60 °C** | Ambient design range |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why HAC Matters:** Approximately **38 %** of major chemical plant fires globally originate from incorrect electrical equipment selection in unclassified or misclassified zones. HAC is therefore the **first line of defense** in the inherently safer design philosophy.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three-Step (STEP) Classification Logic

The international standard **IEC 60079-10-1** prescribes a rigorous three-step logical procedure for classifying any hazardous area:

1. **STEP 1 — Identify the Hazardous Material.** Determine the substance(s) handled, stored, or processed. Catalog its $LFL$, $UFL$, vapor density relative to air ($d_v$), flash point ($T_f$), auto-ignition temperature ($AIT$), and gas group (IIA, IIB, or IIC).

2. **STEP 2 — Identify the Source of Release.** A *source of release* is any point where the flammable substance can be released into the atmosphere (flanged joints, pump seals, sample points, breather valves, relief valve outlets). Each source must be characterized by its **grade**.

3. **STEP 3 — Determine the Extent of the Hazardous Zone.** Apply the zone designation rules based on the grade of release, the ventilation availability, and the release rate.

### 2.2 Grades of Release (The Core 'Why')

The **grade of release** is a qualitative measure of the *likelihood* that a flammable atmosphere will be present at a release point.

| Grade | Likelihood | Duration per Year | Process Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Continuous** | Always present during normal operation | $> 1000$ hours | Open surface of a fixed-roof storage tank containing a volatile flammable liquid above its flash point |
| **Primary** | Likely during normal operation | $10 - 1000$ hours | Flanged pipe joints, pump/compressor seals, sample points, filling connections |
| **Secondary** | Unlikely during normal operation | $< 10$ hours | Valve stem packings, sight glasses, threaded pipe joints, relief valve outlets discharging to atmosphere |

### 2.3 Zone Classification Logic — Gases & Vapors

The zone designation is a **direct mapping** from grade of release (with ventilation considered):

| Grade of Release | Zone (Hazardous) | Zone (Non-Hazardous, if ventilation sufficient) |
|---|---|---|
| Continuous | **Zone 0** | Non-hazardous only if ventilation is **high-grade** *and* monitored |
| Primary | **Zone 1** | Non-hazardous if ventilation is **high-grade** |
| Secondary | **Zone 2** | Non-hazardous if ventilation is **medium or high-grade** |

### 2.4 Zone Classification for Combustible Dusts

| Zone | Dust Definition | Engineering Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Zone 20** | Combustible dust cloud **continuously present** | Inside dust collectors, hoppers, silos during normal operation |
| **Zone 21** | Dust cloud **likely during normal operation** | Areas in the immediate vicinity of dust-handling equipment, bagging stations |
| **Zone 22** | Dust cloud **unlikely / short duration** | Areas where dust layers may be present and occasionally become airborne (e.g., through disturbance) |

### 2.5 Equipment Protection Levels (EPL) and Temperature Classes

Modern IEC standards unify equipment certification through the **EPL** system, replacing older regional "Ex d" / "Class I Div 1" terminology for global harmonization.

| EPL | Zone of Use | Acceptable Zone(s) | Equivalent IEC Marking |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Ga** | Zone 0 | 0, 1, 2 | Ex ia |
| **Gb** | Zone 1 | 1, 2 | Ex d, Ex e, Ex p, Ex ib |
| **Gc** | Zone 2 | 2 | Ex nA, Ex nR, Ex nC |
| **Da** | Zone 20 | 20, 21, 22 | Ex tD iaD |
| **Db** | Zone 21 | 21, 22 | Ex tD |
| **Dc** | Zone 22 | 22 | Ex tD |

**Temperature Classes** limit the maximum surface temperature of equipment so it remains below the **Auto-Ignition Temperature (AIT)** of the surrounding gas:

| Temperature Class | Max Surface Temperature (°C) | Typical Gas | AIT (°C) |
|---|---|---|---|
| T1 | 450 | Methane | 537 |
| T2 | 300 | Hydrogen sulfide | 260 |
| T3 | 200 | Heptane | 215 |
| T4 | 135 | Diethyl ether | 160 |
| T5 | 100 | Carbon disulfide | 90 |
| T6 | 85 | (Reserved for very low-AIT materials) | < 85 |

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are exam-critical for Module 3 numericals. Memorize the **release rate equation** and the **ventilation dilution formula** — these appear frequently in Part B questions.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{release} &= \frac{\pi \cdot d^{2}}{4} \cdot v \cdot \rho_{vapor} \quad &\text{(Mass release rate from a leak)} \\
X_{zone} &= K \cdot \left( \frac{Q_{release}}{LFL \cdot V_{vent}} \right)^{n} \quad &\text{(Hazardous extent estimator)} \\
C_{air}(x) &= \frac{Q_{release}}{2 \cdot \pi \cdot \sigma_y \cdot \sigma_z \cdot u} \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{x^{2}}{2\sigma_x^{2}} - \frac{H^{2}}{2\sigma_z^{2}}\right) \quad &\text{(Gaussian dispersion)} \\
V_{req} &= \frac{Q_{release}}{LFL \cdot \rho_{air}} \quad &\text{(Minimum ventilation air to dilute below LFL)} \\
\Delta T_{surface} &\le AIT - T_{ambient} \quad &\text{(Temperature class constraint)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $d$ = leak orifice diameter (m)
- $v$ = release velocity (m/s)
- $\rho_{vapor}$ = vapor density (kg/m³)
- $V_{vent}$ = ventilation flow rate (m³/s)
- $u$ = wind speed (m/s)
- $K, n$ = empirical constants (typically $K = 1.5$, $n = 0.5$ for heavy gas dispersion)

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

HAC underpins:
- **Oil & Gas platforms** (offshore drilling rigs require Zone 1 / Zone 2 boundaries around wellheads and pig receivers).
- **Petroleum refineries** (FCC units, hydrocracker compressors).
- **Pharmaceutical & solvent plants** (where VOC emissions exceed $LFL$).
- **Paint spray booths** (Zone 1 within 1 m radius of spray nozzles).
- **Hydrogen refueling stations** (Hydrogen is IIC group — highest ignition sensitivity).

A correct HAC drawing is also a **legal compliance document** — most process safety regulations (PSM, OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119, Indian Factories Act Section 41A) require it to be maintained and reviewed every 5 years.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations and Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Extent of Hazardous Zone Around a Grade 1 (Primary) Release

**Problem Statement:** A flange on a propane pipeline (4-inch nominal, schedule 40) leaks at a mass release rate of $Q_{release} = 0.05$ kg/s into an open-air process area. Propane properties: $LFL = 2.1 \% v/v$, $\rho_{vapor} = 1.8$ kg/m³ at 25 °C. Determine the **radial extent of Zone 1** and the **Zone 2 boundary**, assuming wind speed $u = 2$ m/s and ventilation is *medium-grade*.

**Step 1 — Convert LFL to mass concentration.**
The Lower Flammable Limit in mass per unit volume is calculated by multiplying the volume fraction by vapor density:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{LFL, mass} &= LFL_{vol} \times \rho_{vapor} \\
&= 0.021 \times 1.8 \\
&= 0.0378 \; \text{kg/m}^{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the simplified hazardous-extent correlation (IEC empirical).**

For a primary grade release with medium-grade ventilation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_{zone1} &= K \cdot \left( \frac{Q_{release}}{C_{LFL, mass} \cdot u} \right)^{n} \\
&= 1.5 \cdot \left( \frac{0.05}{0.0378 \times 2} \right)^{0.5} \\
&= 1.5 \cdot \left( \frac{0.05}{0.0756} \right)^{0.5} \\
&= 1.5 \cdot (0.6614)^{0.5} \\
&= 1.5 \times 0.8133 \\
&= 1.22 \; \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Zone 1 extends approximately **1.22 m radially** from the flange. This is the immediate hazardous zone — only flameproof (Ex d) or intrinsically safe (Ex ib) equipment may be installed here.

**Step 3 — Determine Zone 2 boundary.**
By IEC convention, the Zone 2 boundary is approximately **3 times** the Zone 1 extent when ventilation is medium-grade:

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_{zone2} &= 3 \times X_{zone1} \\
&= 3 \times 1.22 \\
&= 3.66 \; \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Zone 2 (occasional hazard) extends out to **3.66 m**. Equipment here must be Ex nA (non-sparking) or equivalent (EPL = Gc).

**Step 4 — Verification using Gaussian dispersion model (alternative).**

For a ground-level release, the centerline concentration at downwind distance $x$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C(x, 0, 0) &= \frac{Q_{release}}{\pi \cdot \sigma_y \cdot \sigma_z \cdot u}
\end{aligned}
$$

For Pasquill stability class D (neutral), at $x = 3.66$ m, dispersion coefficients are $\sigma_y \approx 0.6$ m, $\sigma_z \approx 0.5$ m. Plugging in:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C(3.66) &= \frac{0.05}{\pi \times 0.6 \times 0.5 \times 2} \\
&= \frac{0.05}{1.885} \\
&= 0.0265 \; \text{kg/m}^{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

Compare to $C_{LFL,mass} = 0.0378$ kg/m³. Since $0.0265 < 0.0378$, the **3.66 m boundary is consistent** with Zone 2.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Selecting Equipment by Zone

| Zone | Typical Process Location | Permissible Equipment Type | IEC Marking | EPL |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Zone 0** | Inside a gasoline storage tank vent space | Intrinsically safe | Ex ia IIC T4 | Ga |
| **Zone 1** | Within 1 m of a propane pump seal | Flameproof / Increased safety | Ex d IIB T3 | Gb |
| **Zone 2** | Around a chemical warehouse (general area) | Non-sparking | Ex nA IIA T4 | Gc |
| **Zone 20** | Inside a flour mill dust hopper | Dust-ignition-proof by enclosure | Ex tD iaD IIIC T125 °C | Da |
| **Zone 21** | Adjacent to grain elevator discharge | Dust-ignition-proof | Ex tD ibD IIIC T125 °C | Db |
| **Zone 22** | Around bagging line of cement plant | Dust-protected | Ex tD IIIC T125 °C | Dc |

### 3.3 Worked Example: Ventilation Dilution Requirement

A process vessel vents 0.02 kg/s of acetone vapor. Acetone $LFL = 2.6 \% v/v$, $\rho_{vapor} = 2.4$ kg/m³.

Compute the minimum fresh-air ventilation rate to maintain the room concentration **below 25 %** of $LFL$ (safety margin):

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{target} &= 0.25 \times LFL_{mass} \\
&= 0.25 \times (0.026 \times 2.4) \\
&= 0.25 \times 0.0624 \\
&= 0.0156 \; \text{kg/m}^{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

Applying the steady-state ventilation equation $V_{req} = Q_{release} / C_{target}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{req} &= \frac{0.02}{0.0156} \\
&= 1.282 \; \text{m}^{3}\text{/s} \\
&= 4615 \; \text{m}^{3}\text{/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** The plant's HVAC must deliver at least **4615 m³/h** of fresh air, or the room must be reclassified as hazardous.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture Flow — HAC Decision Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["STEP 1: IDENTIFY HAZARDOUS MATERIALS"] --> nodeB["Catalog LFL, UFL, AIT, Gas Group"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["STEP 2: IDENTIFY SOURCES OF RELEASE"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Is release Continuous Primary or Secondary?"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["STEP 3: EVALUATE VENTILATION GRADE"]
    nodeE --> nodeF{"VENTILATION QUALITY?"}
    nodeF -->|HIGH| nodeG["ZONE MAY BE DOWNGRADED"]
    nodeF -->|MEDIUM| nodeH["ZONE RETAINS STANDARD EXTENT"]
    nodeF -->|LOW| nodeI["ZONE EXTENT EXPANDS"]
    nodeG --> nodeJ["ASSIGN EPL: Ga, Gb, Gc, Da, Db, Dc"]
    nodeH --> nodeJ
    nodeI --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeK["SELECT TEMPERATURE CLASS T1 TO T6"]
    nodeK --> nodeL["DOCUMENT HAC DRAWING AND VALVE"]
    L --> nodeM["PERIODIC REVIEW EVERY 5 YEARS"]
```

### 4.2 Zone Geometry Schematic (3D Representation via Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PROC["PROCESS EQUIPMENT"]
        tank["FLAMMABLE LIQUID TANK ABOVE FLASH POINT"]
    end

    subgraph Z0["ZONE 0: INSIDE TANK VAPOR SPACE - EPL Ga REQUIRED"]
        z0a["INTRINSICALLY SAFE INSTRUMENTS ONLY"]
    end

    subgraph Z1["ZONE 1: 0 TO 1.5 M RADIUS AROUND FLANGES PUMPS - EPL Gb REQUIRED"]
        z1a["FLAMEPROOF Ex d MOTORS AND JUNCTION BOXES"]
    end

    subgraph Z2["ZONE 2: 1.5 M TO 3.5 M RADIUS - EPL Gc REQUIRED"]
        z2a["NON SPARKING Ex nA LIGHTING AND CABLES"]
    end

    subgraph NH["NON HAZARDOUS AREA: BEYOND 3.5 M - STANDARD EQUIPMENT"]
        nha["CONTROL ROOM GENERAL PURPOSE PANELS"]
    end

    PROC --> Z0
    Z0 --> Z1
    Z1 --> Z2
    Z2 --> NH
```

### 4.3 Source-of-Release Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R0["RELEASE POINT IDENTIFIED"] --> R1{"DURATION OF FLAMMABLE CLOUD PRESENT?"}
    R1 -->|>1000 HOURS PER YEAR| R2["GRADE CONTINUOUS LEADS TO ZONE 0 OR 20"]
    R1 -->|10 TO 1000 HOURS| R3["GRADE PRIMARY LEADS TO ZONE 1 OR 21"]
    R1 -->|<10 HOURS| R4["GRADE SECONDARY LEADS TO ZONE 2 OR 22"]
    R2 --> R5["APPLY HIGH INTEGRITY PROTECTION: Ex ia EPL Ga"]
    R3 --> R6["APPLY FLAMEPROOF OR INCREASED SAFETY: Ex d or Ex e EPL Gb"]
    R4 --> R7["APPLY NON SPARKING OR DUST PROTECTED: Ex nA or Ex tD EPL Gc Dc"]
```

### 4.4 Cross-Standard Comparison Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["IEC 60079 10 1 INTERNATIONAL"] --> B["ZONE 0 1 2 FOR GAS"]
    C["NEC NFPA 70 USA"] --> D["DIVISION 1 AND 2 FOR GAS"]
    E["IS 5572 INDIA"] --> F["ZONE 0 1 2 EQUIVALENT TO IEC"]
    B --> G["COMMON DESIGN INTENT"]
    D --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H["SELECT EPL AND TEMPERATURE CLASS"]
```
