<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**

**Q1. Define Hazardous Area Classification. State the three zones used for gas/vapor atmospheres as per IEC 60079-10-1.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Hazardous Area Classification is the systematic division of a plant into zones based on the **frequency and duration of occurrence of a flammable atmosphere**, used to select appropriate explosion-protected electrical equipment.

The three zones for gases/vapors (IEC 60079-10-1) are:

- **Zone 0:** A place in which an explosive gas-air mixture is **continuously present** or present for long periods (e.g., inside a gasoline storage tank).
- **Zone 1:** A place in which an explosive gas-air mixture is **likely to occur in normal operation** (e.g., immediately around pump seals, flanges).
- **Zone 2:** A place in which an explosive gas-air mixture is **not likely to occur in normal operation**, and if it does, persists only for a short time (e.g., general process area around solvent storage).

*(Mark allocation: Definition 1 m + Zone 0/1/2 explanation 2 m)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**Q2. Differentiate between Grade of Release "Continuous" and "Primary" with one process example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Criterion | Continuous | Primary |
|---|---|---|
| **Likelihood** | Present during normal operation **continuously or for long periods** | Likely to occur **periodically** during normal operation |
| **Duration / Year** | $> 1000$ hours | $10 - 1000$ hours |
| **Process Example** | Vapor space of a fixed-roof tank containing a flammable liquid above its flash point | Pump seal, flange, or sample point on a hydrocarbon pipeline |

*(Mark allocation: 1 m for definition + 1 m for duration + 1 m for example)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks, Module 3 Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question, Module 3 Internal Choice]**

### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**(a)** Describe in detail the **STEP methodology** for hazardous area classification as per IEC 60079-10-1. Illustrate how the grade of release and ventilation grade together determine the final zone designation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A relief valve on a butane storage vessel discharges to atmosphere. The butane release rate is **0.08 kg/s**. Butane properties: $LFL = 1.8 \% v/v$, vapor density $\rho_v = 2.5$ kg/m³. Wind speed $u = 1.5$ m/s, ventilation is medium-grade. Compute the **Zone 1** and **Zone 2** boundaries. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### Part (a) — STEP Methodology (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Identify the hazardous material.** Determine physical and flammability properties: $LFL$, $UFL$, flash point, auto-ignition temperature, gas group (IIA / IIB / IIC), and vapor density relative to air. This governs the **equipment group** and **temperature class** selection later.

**Step 2 — Identify the sources of release.** Walk down the P&ID and physically identify every potential leak point — flanges, valves, pumps, sample connections, breather valves, relief valves, sight glasses. Assign each a **grade**:
- Continuous: long-duration releases → Zone 0 / 20
- Primary: periodic releases → Zone 1 / 21
- Secondary: rare releases → Zone 2 / 22

**Step 3 — Determine zone extent.** For each source, estimate the **release rate** $Q$ and the **ventilation flow rate** $V$. Apply the empirical correlation $X_{zone} = K \cdot (Q/(LFL \cdot V))^{n}$ to estimate the radial extent.

**Ventilation × Release Grade Matrix:**

| Grade | High Ventilation | Medium Ventilation | Low Ventilation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Continuous | Non-hazardous (with monitoring) | Zone 0 | Zone 0 (extended) |
| Primary | Non-hazardous | Zone 1 | Zone 1 (extended) |
| Secondary | Non-hazardous | Zone 2 | Zone 1 |

The output is an **HAC drawing** showing Zone 0, Zone 1, Zone 2 boundaries in 3D plan and elevation views.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks)

**Given:** $Q_{release} = 0.08$ kg/s, $LFL_{vol} = 1.8 \% = 0.018$, $\rho_v = 2.5$ kg/m³, $u = 1.5$ m/s, $K = 1.5$, $n = 0.5$.

**Step 1: Convert LFL to mass concentration.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{LFL, mass} &= LFL_{vol} \times \rho_{vapor} \\
&= 0.018 \times 2.5 \\
&= 0.045 \; \text{kg/m}^{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Zone 1 extent.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_{zone1} &= K \cdot \left( \frac{Q_{release}}{C_{LFL, mass} \cdot u} \right)^{n} \\
&= 1.5 \cdot \left( \frac{0.08}{0.045 \times 1.5} \right)^{0.5} \\
&= 1.5 \cdot \left( \frac{0.08}{0.0675} \right)^{0.5} \\
&= 1.5 \cdot (1.1852)^{0.5} \\
&= 1.5 \times 1.0887 \\
&= 1.633 \; \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute Zone 2 extent (Zone 2 ≈ 3 × Zone 1 for medium ventilation).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_{zone2} &= 3 \times X_{zone1} \\
&= 3 \times 1.633 \\
&= 4.90 \; \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:**
- Zone 1 boundary: **≈ 1.63 m** from relief valve discharge
- Zone 2 boundary: **≈ 4.90 m** from relief valve discharge

**Engineering recommendation:** Install Ex d (flameproof, EPL Gb) equipment within 1.63 m; Ex nA (EPL Gc) equipment between 1.63 m and 4.90 m; standard equipment beyond 4.90 m.

*(Mark allocation: 1 m for LFL conversion, 3 m for Zone 1 calculation, 1 m for Zone 2, 2 m for engineering interpretation)*

---

### **Question B — Alternative 14-Mark Choice**

**(a)** Explain the **Equipment Protection Level (EPL)** system introduced by IEC 60079. Draw a comparison table of EPLs Ga, Gb, Gc, Da, Db, Dc with their zone applicability and one example each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pump handling toluene ($LFL = 1.2 \% v/v$, $\rho_v = 3.2$ kg/m³, $AIT = 480 °C$) leaks at a primary-grade rate of **0.03 kg/s**. Determine the required **temperature class** of electrical equipment installed within the Zone 1 boundary, and compute the minimum **ventilation flow rate** to dilute the cloud to 25 % of $LFL$. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### Part (a) — EPL System (7 marks)

The **Equipment Protection Level (EPL)** is a modern IEC framework that explicitly identifies the level of protection offered by equipment against becoming an ignition source. There are three EPLs for **gases** (Ga, Gb, Gc) and three for **dusts** (Da, Db, Dc).

| EPL | Zone(s) Allowed | Equipment Type | Process Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Ga** | Zone 0, 1, 2 | Intrinsically safe (Ex ia) — *very high* protection | Vapor-space instrumentation inside a hydrocarbon storage tank |
| **Gb** | Zone 1, 2 | Flameproof (Ex d), Increased safety (Ex e), Pressurized (Ex p) — *high* protection | Motor terminal box of a hydrocarbon pump |
| **Gc** | Zone 2 only | Non-sparking (Ex nA), Restricted breathing (Ex nR) — *enhanced* protection | Lighting fixture in a refinery process area |
| **Da** | Zone 20, 21, 22 | Dust-ignition-proof by enclosure (Ex tD iaD) | Sensor inside a flour silo |
| **Db** | Zone 21, 22 | Dust-ignition-proof (Ex tD ibD) | Junction box near a grain conveyor |
| **Dc** | Zone 22 only | Dust-protected (Ex tD) | Cable gland on a cement bagging line |

*(Mark allocation: 2 m for explanation of EPL concept, 4 m for full table, 1 m for example relevance)*

---

#### Part (b) — Temperature Class & Ventilation (7 marks)

**Step 1: Determine temperature class from AIT.**
For toluene, $AIT = 480 °C$. The temperature class must have a surface temperature limit **below** the AIT with at least a 5 °C safety margin.

$$
T_{max, surface} \le AIT - 5 = 480 - 5 = 475 \; °C
$$

Checking temperature classes:
- T1 → 450 °C (insufficient, 475 > 450)
- T2 → 300 °C (insufficient)

**Therefore, equipment in Zone 1 must be rated at least T1** with a maximum surface temperature of 450 °C, AND verified that the actual surface temperature under the worst-case fault is below 475 °C. In practice, **T3 (200 °C)** or **T4 (135 °C)** would be selected to provide a generous margin.

**Step 2: Compute minimum ventilation flow rate to dilute to 25 % of $LFL$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{target} &= 0.25 \times LFL_{mass} \\
&= 0.25 \times (0.012 \times 3.2) \\
&= 0.25 \times 0.0384 \\
&= 0.0096 \; \text{kg/m}^{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{req} &= \frac{Q_{release}}{C_{target}} \\
&= \frac{0.03}{0.0096} \\
&= 3.125 \; \text{m}^{3}\text{/s} \\
&= 11250 \; \text{m}^{3}\text{/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:**
- Equipment must be **T1 rated or lower** (practically T3 recommended).
- Minimum ventilation = **3.125 m³/s (11250 m³/h)** to keep toluene concentration below 25 % of LFL.

*(Mark allocation: 3 m for temperature class logic, 2 m for ventilation mass conversion, 2 m for ventilation flow result)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - Students frequently **mix up Zone 1 vs Zone 2** boundaries. Zone 2 is *not* the same as Zone 1 — it is a transition zone where the hazard is *occasional* and equipment of a *lower protection level* is acceptable.
> - **Temperature class direction confusion:** Always check that the **equipment surface temperature ≤ (AIT − safety margin)**. A higher *number* T-class means a *lower* surface temperature limit.
> - In Part B numericals, **forget to convert $LFL$ from volume % to mass/m³** using vapor density. This single step accounts for **2 marks** lost in most answer scripts.
> - Do **not** confuse *continuous* release with *primary* release. Open tank surface = continuous; flange = primary.
> - The relationship **Zone 2 ≈ 3 × Zone 1** holds *only* for medium-grade ventilation. With poor ventilation, this multiplier can rise to 5×–10×.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU Module 3 Revision Points — Read before every exam:**

- **HAC** = division of plant into zones based on *frequency* and *duration* of flammable atmosphere presence.
- **Three elements of the Fire Triangle** must coexist: Fuel + Oxidizer (air) + Ignition Source. HAC removes the ignition-source coincidence.
- **Three grades of release:** Continuous ($>$1000 h/yr), Primary (10–1000 h/yr), Secondary ($<$10 h/yr).
- **Three gas zones:** Zone 0 (continuous), Zone 1 (primary), Zone 2 (secondary).
- **Three dust zones:** Zone 20, Zone 21, Zone 22 — analogous to Zone 0/1/2.
- **EPL system:** Ga → Zone 0, Gb → Zone 1, Gc → Zone 2; Da → Zone 20, Db → Zone 21, Dc → Zone 22.
- **Temperature classes:** T1 (450 °C) down to T6 (85 °C). Equipment surface T must stay **below AIT**.
- **Gas groups by ignition sensitivity:** IIA (least) → IIB → IIC (most sensitive, e.g., hydrogen, acetylene).
- **Ventilation grade × release grade** jointly determines final zone — *high* ventilation can downgrade a zone.
- **Release rate formula:** $Q_{release} = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot v \cdot \rho_v$.
- **Zone extent correlation:** $X_{zone} = K \cdot (Q/(C_{LFL} \cdot V))^{n}$ — used in numericals.
- **Ventilation dilution:** $V_{req} = Q_{release} / C_{target}$, where $C_{target}$ is typically 25 % of $LFL_{mass}$.
- **Standards triad:** IEC 60079-10-1 (international), NEC/NFPA 70 (US Class/Div), IS 5572 + OISD-144 (India).
- **Review cycle:** HAC drawings must be revised **every 5 years** or after any major process change.
- **Zoning logic shortcut:** Continuous → 0, Primary → 1, Secondary → 2 (no ventilation correction needed for standard cases).
- **Conversion trap to remember:** $LFL_{mass} = LFL_{vol} \times \rho_{vapor}$ — applied in every numerical.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Safety in Transportation of Hazardous Chemicals by Road - HAZCHEM Code
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT723 - PROCESS SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Safety in Transportation of Hazardous Chemicals by Road - HAZCHEM Code

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HAZCHEM Code** (Hazardous Chemical Code) is an internationally recognized **emergency action code system** used by first responders (firefighters, spill responders, and emergency services) to identify the correct course of action during incidents involving **hazardous chemicals in transportation**, particularly by road, rail, and marine modes.

The HAZCHEM code is a **combination of numeric and alphabetic characters** printed on orange-colored placards attached to transport vehicles, tankers, and storage containers. It was originally developed by the **National Chemical Emergency Centre (NCEC)** in the **United Kingdom** and is now widely adopted in **Australia, New Zealand, and several Asian countries** including India (in modified form under the **Central Motor Vehicles Rules – CMVR**, 1989).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Under the KTU 2024 OECHT723 syllabus (Module 3 – Safety in Transportation), HAZCHEM codes are studied as a standardized **emergency response decision-making tool** that links the **UN Hazard Classification** of a chemical to its **on-scene mitigation tactics**.

The HAZCHEM system has three fundamental components:

1. **A single numeral (1, 2, 3, or 4)** → indicates the *recommended fire-fighting medium*.
2. **One or two capital letters (P, R, S, T, W, X, Y, or Z)** → indicates the *level of personal protection* and the *spill containment/dispersal action*.
3. **An optional letter "E"** (suffix) → indicates that **public evacuation** of the surrounding area must be considered.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine you are a **traffic police officer** who suddenly encounters a tanker truck that has toppled on a highway and is leaking an unknown chemical. You do not know what is inside, you do not have time to read a 200-page safety data sheet, and a crowd is gathering.

In that moment, the **HAZCHEM code acts like a "universal emergency remote control."** Just as a single colored button on a TV remote tells you exactly what to do (red = stop, green = go), the HAZCHEM code tells the trained responder *instantly*:
- **What to fight a fire with** (water spray, foam, or dry powder).
- **What protective suit to wear** (basic kit or full chemical suit with breathing apparatus).
- **Whether to contain the spill or dilute it.**
- **Whether to evacuate nearby civilians.**

> [!TIP]
> Think of HAZCHEM as the **"barcode of emergency response"** — a few characters that translate a complex chemical profile into a clear, life-saving action plan.

---

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics and Authorities

| Parameter | Standard / Authority |
|---|---|
| **Placard Background Color** | **Orange (Pantone 151)** |
| **Code Color** | **Black** |
| **Placard Size (Road)** | **300 mm × 300 mm** minimum |
| **Developing Body** | **NCEC (UK), ADF (Australia)** |
| **Indian Equivalent** | **CMVR 1989, Rule 137** & **UN Recommendations on the Transport of Dangerous Goods** |
| **Compatible System** | **Kemler Code (UN)**, **TREM Card**, **ERG (USA)** |

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a HAZCHEM Code

A complete HAZCHEM code is read from **left to right** as:

$$
\text{HAZCHEM Code} = \underbrace{N}_{\text{Fire Medium}} \; + \; \underbrace{L_1 L_2}_{\text{Action Code}} \; + \; \underbrace{[E]}_{\text{Evacuation Flag}}
$$

Where:
- $N \in \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$
- $L_1 L_2 \in \{P, R, S, T, W, X, Y, Z\}$ (one or two letters)
- $E$ is optional.

---

### 2.2 Decoding the Numeric Component (Fire-Fighting Medium)

| Code Digit | Fire-Fighting Medium | Best Used For |
|---|---|---|
| **1** | **Coarse Water Spray (Jet)** | Bulk liquid fires, cooling of tanks |
| **2** | **Fine Water Spray (Fog/Mist)** | Vapor suppression, cooling, prevents water damage to cargo |
| **3** | **Alcohol-Resistant Foam (AFFF-AR or AFFF-ATC)** | Hydrocarbon & polar solvent pool fires |
| **4** | **Dry Chemical Powder (DCP – Monoammonium Phosphate)** | Gas leaks, electrical fires, reactive chemical fires |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "3" is special:** Water alone is unsuitable for many flammable liquids (e.g., gasoline, alcohol) because the fuel floats on water and continues to burn. **Foam** forms a blanket that smothers the fire by excluding oxygen. Hence, $N=3$ is the **most common code** for petroleum products.

---

### 2.3 Decoding the Letter Component (Personal Protection & Spill Action)

The letter(s) define the **tactical action** — *dilute, contain, or evacuate* — and the **PPE level**.

| Letter | PPE Requirement | Tactical Action | Typical Chemical Class |
|---|---|---|---|
| **P** | Full Chemical Protective Clothing (CPC) with Breathing Apparatus (BA) | **Dilute spill with water; vapor cloud may form** | Water-miscible, low-toxicity liquids (e.g., ethanol) |
| **R** | Full CPC + BA | **Dilute spill; prevent entry to drains/waterways** | Water-soluble toxic liquids |
| **S** | Full CPC + BA | **Contain spill; do not dilute; prevent fire-water run-off** | Water-reactive chemicals |
| **T** | Full CPC + BA + **Liquid-tight chemical suit** | **Contain; high toxicity; use dry agents first** | Highly toxic / carcinogenic substances |
| **W** | Full CPC + BA | **Dilute or contain — responder's judgement** | Multi-hazard cargoes |
| **X** | **BA only** (no CPC); operate from safe distance | **Contain; large spill only; foam blanket** | Low-toxicity large-volume liquids |
| **Y** | BA only | **Contain; BA essential due to vapor toxicity** | Toxic vapors, e.g., chlorine |
| **Z** | Full CPC + BA | **Refer to manufacturer / specialist advice** | Unclassified or highly reactive chemicals |

---

### 2.4 The "E" Suffix — Evacuation Decision

If the code ends with **"E"** (e.g., **2WE**, **3YE**), it indicates that:

> Public evacuation of the **immediate downwind area** must be considered as a precautionary measure. This is typically assigned to chemicals that are:
> - **Highly toxic** (LC₅₀ < 500 ppm·V),
> - **Volatile and flammable** (Flash point < 0 °C),
> - Or **explosive** (UN Class 1).

**Quantitative Rule of Thumb for Evacuation Distance (D)**:

$$
D \approx k \cdot \sqrt{M \cdot V_p}
$$

Where:
- $D$ = Initial evacuation distance (m)
- $M$ = Mass of chemical released (kg)
- $V_p$ = Vapor pressure (kPa) at ambient temperature
- $k$ = Empirical constant ≈ **50** for non-reactive liquids

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU exam purposes, the qualitative use of the "E" flag is sufficient; the formula above is included for **analytical reasoning questions only**.

---

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet (Quick Recall Table)

| Element | Symbol | Engineering Decision |
|---|---|---|
| Fire-fighting medium | $N$ | Select agent from $\{1,2,3,4\}$ |
| PPE Class | $L_1$ | Choose suit + BA configuration |
| Spill Action | $L_2$ | Dilute / Contain / Disperse |
| Evacuation | $E$ | Yes / No based on toxicity-volatility index |
| Compatibility with UN | Yes | UN Class ↔ HAZCHEM Letter mapping is **not 1:1**; HAZCHEM is **action-based** |
| Compatibility with TREM | Yes | TREM = Transport Emergency Card; HAZCHEM = placard code |

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Road Transport** – Mandatory in India on tankers under **CMVR Rule 137 (1989)** and the **Manufacture, Storage and Import of Hazardous Chemical Rules, 1989 (MSIHC)**.
2. **Rail Transport** – Adopted in container-freight wagons of Indian Railways.
3. **Marine (IMDG)** – HAZCHEM supplements the **International Maritime Dangerous Goods (IMDG) Code** blue-brick labels.
4. **Plant Layout & On-Site Emergency** – Static storage tanks in process plants are also placarded with HAZCHEM.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Trick:** If asked *"What is the difference between HAZCHEM and the UN Number placard?"* — The answer is: **UN Number** = *what* the substance is (identification); **HAZCHEM** = *what to do* about it (action).

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analysis & Decision Matrix

### 3.1 Logical Sequence for Responder Action

When a HAZCHEM code is encountered, the trained responder follows this **standard operating procedure (SOP)**:

1. **Step 1 — Read the UN Number** to identify the chemical.
2. **Step 2 — Identify the HAZCHEM Code** on the orange placard.
3. **Step 3 — Decode the numeral ($N$)** to choose the correct fire-fighting medium.
4. **Step 4 — Decode the letter(s)** to choose PPE and tactical action.
5. **Step 5 — Check for "E" suffix** → initiate evacuation protocol if present.
6. **Step 6 — Cross-check with TREM card** for chemical-specific first aid and spill procedures.
7. **Step 7 — Communicate** with the **Emergency Response Centre (ERC)** using the decoded information.

---

### 3.2 Worked Analytical Example (Detailed Walkthrough)

**Scenario:**
> A road tanker carrying **Anhydrous Ammonia (UN 1005)** has overturned on a national highway. The orange placard displays the HAZCHEM code: **2XE**.

**Required:** Decode the code and outline the responder's SOP.

#### Solution:

**Step 1 — Identify the chemical:**
- UN 1005 = Anhydrous Ammonia (NH₃, liquefied gas under pressure).

**Step 2 — Decode the numeral $N = 2$:**
- $N = 2$ ⇒ **Fine water spray (fog)** must be used. 
- **Reasoning:** Water jets would mechanically disperse the toxic vapor cloud; fine spray absorbs the gas by partial dissolution and cools the tank.

**Step 3 — Decode the letter $L_1 = X$:**
- $L_1 = X$ ⇒ **Breathing Apparatus (BA) is mandatory**; responder operates from a **safe upwind distance**; no full chemical suit required (low skin toxicity of NH₃ in vapor form).
- **Tactical action:** **Contain** the liquid spill using water spray to absorb ammonia into a controlled lagoon; **prevent entry into water bodies** (ammonia is highly toxic to aquatic life).

**Step 4 — Decode the evacuation flag $E$:**
- $E$ is **PRESENT** ⇒ Public evacuation of the **downwind zone within a 1 km radius** is mandatory.

**Step 5 — Cross-check with TREM Card for UN 1005:**
- First aid: Move victim to fresh air, administer oxygen, eyes wash with water for 15 min.
- Spill: Large water spray curtain; neutralize with dilute acid only under expert supervision.

**Step 6 — Communication:**
- Contact: **Local Fire Control Room + National Emergency Response Centre (Bhopal ERC: 1077 in some states)**.
- Quote: *"UN 1005, HAZCHEM 2XE, large leak, downwind evacuation in progress."*

---

### 3.3 Worked Example – Code Assignment (Reverse Logic)

**Question:** A shipment of **Gasoline (UN 1203, Petrol)** is to be transported by road. Assign the appropriate HAZCHEM code.

**Step-by-step derivation:**

1. **Fire medium:** Gasoline is a **flammable hydrocarbon** (Flash point ≈ –40 °C). The best extinguishing medium is **Alcohol-Resistant Foam**. Therefore:

$$
N = 3
$$

2. **Tactical action:** Gasoline floats on water and is moderately toxic. The best action is to **contain** using foam, not to dilute. Therefore:

$$
L_1 = S \quad \text{(Full CPC + BA; CONTAIN)}
$$

3. **Evacuation:** Gasoline vapors are flammable; downwind vapor cloud can flash back. Therefore, **"E"** is assigned.

**Final HAZCHEM Code for Gasoline:**

$$
\boxed{3SE}
$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix (KTU-Style Summary)

| UN Class | Example Chemical | Typical HAZCHEM | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 (Explosives) | TNT, dynamite | **1WE / 1ZE** | Use water spray; expert only; evacuate |
| 2.1 (Flammable gas) | LPG, propane | **2WE** | Fine water spray; full CPC; evacuate |
| 2.3 (Toxic gas) | Chlorine, ammonia | **2XE** | Fog for absorption; BA; evacuate |
| 3 (Flammable liquid) | Petrol, ethanol | **3YE / 3SE** | Foam; BA; contain or evacuate |
| 5.1 (Oxidizer) | Hydrogen peroxide | **2PE** | Dilute; CPC; evacuate |
| 6.1 (Toxic) | Pesticides, cyanides | **3TE** | Foam; full liquid-tight suit; evacuate |
| 8 (Corrosive) | Sulfuric acid, NaOH | **2RE** | Dilute carefully; CPC; evacuate |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of HAZCHEM Code Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Incident Detected: Tanker Leak] --> B[Identify UN Number on Placard]
    B --> C[Read HAZCHEM Code on Orange Board]
    C --> D{Numeric Digit N}
    D -->|N=1| D1[Coarse Water Spray]
    D -->|N=2| D2[Fine Water Fog]
    D -->|N=3| D3[Alcohol-Resistant Foam]
    D -->|N=4| D4[Dry Chemical Powder]
    C --> E{Letter Code L}
    E -->|P or R| E1[Dilute Spill with Water]
    E -->|S or T| E2[Contain Spill - No Dilution]
    E -->|W| E3[Responder Judgement]
    E -->|X or Y| E4[BA Only - Vapor Hazard]
    E -->|Z| E5[Consult Manufacturer]
    C --> F{Is E Suffix Present?}
    F -->|Yes| F1[Initiate Downwind Evacuation]
    F -->|No| F2[In-Place Sheltering Allowed]
    D1 --> G[Cross-Verify with TREM Card]
    D2 --> G
    D3 --> G
    D4 --> G
    E1 --> G
    E2 --> G
    E3 --> G
    E4 --> G
    E5 --> G
    F1 --> G
    F2 --> G
    G --> H[Contact Emergency Response Centre]
    H --> I[Execute Containment + Medical Aid]
```

### 4.2 Functional Flow – Code Reading Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Read1 [Step A: Read the UN Number]
        U1[Locate the Blue Placard]
        U2[Note 4-digit UN ID]
    end
    subgraph Read2 [Step B: Read HAZCHEM Code]
        H1[Locate Orange Placard]
        H2[Parse Number]
        H3[Parse Letter]
        H4[Check E Suffix]
    end
    subgraph Decide [Step C: Decide Action]
        X1[Choose Extinguishing Medium]
        X2[Choose PPE Level]
        X3[Choose Spill Strategy]
        X4[Evacuate or Shelter]
    end
    U1 --> U2 --> H1 --> H2 --> H3 --> H4
    H2 --> X1
    H3 --> X2
    H3 --> X3
    H4 --> X4
    X1 --> Z[Execute Tactical Response]
    X2 --> Z
    X3 --> Z
    X4 --> Z
```

### 4.3 Schematic of Placard Placement on a Road Tanker

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph FrontTanker [Road Tanker — Side View]
        A1[Cylindrical Tank] --> A2[UN Number Placard - Blue Background]
        A2 --> A3[HAZCHEM Code Placard - Orange Background]
        A3 --> A4[Hazard Class Diamond - Red Yellow etc]
        A4 --> A5[Consignment Details and TREM Pocket]
    end
    A2 --> B1[Bottom of Tank - 1.5 m from ground]
    A3 --> B2[Adjacent to UN Placard - 300 mm square]
    A4 --> B3[Front and Rear of Vehicle - 250 mm square]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU:** The HAZCHEM placard is always placed **adjacent to (never on top of)** the UN Number placard, so that both are visible simultaneously during an emergency.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Q2(b)]**
> **Define HAZCHEM code. Mention any two essential elements of its placard system.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
HAZCHEM (Hazardous Chemical) Code is a standardized emergency action code used by first responders to identify the correct fire-fighting medium, level of personal protection, and spill control action to be taken during a chemical transport incident. *(2 marks)*
Two essential elements of its placard system are:
1. The **numeric digit** indicating the fire-fighting medium (water spray, foam, or dry powder). *(0.5 mark)*
2. The **alphabetic letter(s)** indicating the PPE level and tactical action (dilute, contain, or contain-with-BA). *(0.5 mark)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 3]**
> **What is the significance of the "E" suffix in a HAZCHEM code? Illustrate with one example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The "E" suffix indicates that **public evacuation of the downwind area** must be considered as a precautionary measure due to high toxicity, flammability, or explosive risk of the chemical. *(2 marks)*
**Example:** The HAZCHEM code **2XE** for Anhydrous Ammonia (UN 1005) instructs responders to use fine water fog for absorption, wear breathing apparatus, and **evacuate civilians in the downwind zone within ~1 km radius**. *(1 mark)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified Past Paper]*

> **(a)** Explain in detail the **four possible numeric digits** used in a HAZCHEM code. Mention the chemical classes for which each is most appropriate. *(7 marks)*

> **(b)** A road tanker containing **Acetone (UN 1090, Class 3)** has met with an accident. The HAZCHEM code on the placard is **3YE**.
> **(i)** Decode the code.
> **(ii)** Outline the step-by-step emergency response procedure. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Numeric Digits in HAZCHEM Code (7 marks):**

1. **Code 1 — Coarse Water Spray (Water Jet):** Used for bulk liquid fires where the chemical does **not** react violently with water. Best for **Class 3 flammable liquids** of low water-miscibility in unconfined spills. *(2 marks)*

2. **Code 2 — Fine Water Spray (Fog):** Used for **vapor suppression, cooling of pressure vessels**, and to absorb toxic gases (e.g., NH₃, Cl₂). Fog droplets maximize surface area for gas absorption. *(2 marks)*

3. **Code 3 — Alcohol-Resistant Foam (AFFF-AR):** Used for **hydrocarbon and polar solvent fires** (petrol, ethanol, methanol). Foam forms a blanket that excludes oxygen and prevents vapor release. *(2 marks)*

4. **Code 4 — Dry Chemical Powder (DCP):** Used for **gas leaks, electrical fires, and water-reactive chemicals** (e.g., sodium, magnesium). DCP interrupts the chemical chain reaction of combustion. *(1 mark)*

**(b) Decoding and Response for Acetone, HAZCHEM 3YE (7 marks):**

**(i) Decoding:** *(3 marks)*
- **N = 3** → Use **alcohol-resistant foam** (acetone is a polar solvent; water alone would be ineffective).
- **L = Y** → Wear **breathing apparatus only**; **contain** the spill; do not use water jet as it spreads the flammable liquid.
- **E = Yes** → **Public evacuation** of downwind area is required due to acetone's high vapor pressure (low flash point of –20 °C).

**(ii) Step-by-step response:** *(4 marks)*
1. **Approach only from upwind direction** with full BA.
2. **Apply foam blanket** at low pressure from a safe distance to suppress vapor and extinguish any fire.
3. **Contain the spill** using bunding, sand barriers, or absorbent pads — **do NOT wash into drains** (acetone is a water pollutant).
4. **Evacuate civilians** within 200–500 m downwind radius; cordon off ignition sources (no smoking, switch off vehicle engines).
5. **Communicate** with the **state Emergency Response Centre (ERC)** and quote UN 1090, HAZCHEM 3YE, quantity released, and weather conditions.
6. **Decontamination:** Wash responders' suits with detergent; medical check for inhalation exposure.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified Past Paper]*

> **(a)** Differentiate between the **UN Number placard** and the **HAZCHEM code placard** in terms of purpose, color, and information conveyed. *(7 marks)*

> **(b)** During the transport of **Methyl Ethyl Ketone (MEK, UN 1193)**, the responsible officer must assign the correct HAZCHEM code. **Derive** the correct HAZCHEM code step by step using the chemical's physical properties:
> - UN Class: 3 (Flammable Liquid)
> - Boiling Point: 79.6 °C
> - Flash Point: –9 °C
> - Water Solubility: Moderate
> - Toxicity: Mild (oral LD₅₀ = 2,730 mg/kg)
> *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) UN Placard vs. HAZCHEM Placard (7 marks):**

| Feature | UN Number Placard | HAZCHEM Code Placard |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | **Identification** — "What is the chemical?" | **Action** — "What should the responder do?" |
| **Background Color** | **Blue** (or orange for emergency use in some countries) | **Orange (Pantone 151)** |
| **Text Color** | White | Black |
| **Information Conveyed** | 4-digit UN Number + Hazard Class Diamond | Numeric fire medium + Letter PPE/action + optional E |
| **Governing Body** | UN Committee of Experts on Transport of Dangerous Goods | NCEC (UK) / National Fire Service |
| **Required At** | All four sides of vehicle | All four sides of vehicle |
| **Example** | UN 1090 (Acetone) | 3YE |

*(6 marks for table; 1 mark for concluding statement that the two systems are **complementary, not alternatives**.)*

**(b) Derivation of HAZCHEM Code for MEK (7 marks):**

1. **Fire medium analysis:** MEK is a **polar flammable liquid (Class 3)** with moderate water solubility. Water alone would spread the fire. Therefore, **alcohol-resistant foam** is most suitable. *(2 marks)*

$$
N = 3
$$

2. **PPE level:** MEK has **mild toxicity** but its low flash point (–9 °C) creates an explosive vapor-air mixture. Responders must use **breathing apparatus** to prevent inhalation. Skin contact requires basic chemical suit. *(2 marks)*

$$
L_1 = Y \quad \text{(BA essential, CONTAIN)}
$$

3. **Evacuation decision:** With a flash point below 0 °C and high volatility (BP 79.6 °C), the **vapor cloud ignition risk is high**. Public evacuation is justified. *(2 marks)*

$$
E = \text{Yes}
$$

**Derived HAZCHEM Code for MEK:**

$$
\boxed{3YE}
$$

*(1 mark for the final boxed answer.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes – Marks Lost:**
> 1. **Confusing UN Number with HAZCHEM code** — UN tells you the *identity*; HAZCHEM tells you the *action*. Examiners will deduct 1–2 marks if you interchange these.
> 2. **Writing "3" for water-miscible chemicals** — Water is ineffective on polar solvent fires like ethanol or MEK. You must specify **alcohol-resistant foam (AR-AFFF)**, not just "foam."
> 3. **Forgetting the "E" flag** — Many students ignore evacuation logic. Always justify "E" with a property like *low flash point, high vapor pressure, or LC₅₀ < threshold*.
> 4. **In PPE coding, missing "BA"** — Code "X" and "Y" specifically require **breathing apparatus (BA) only**, not full chemical suit. Examiners test this distinction.
> 5. **Not stating placard color** — The orange background of HAZCHEM is a frequently-asked 1-mark point. Memorize **Pantone 151 / orange**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📌 **Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- **HAZCHEM** = **Hazardous Chemical Emergency Action Code** — read left to right: *Number → Letter(s) → Optional E*.
- **Orange placard (300 mm × 300 mm)** with **black text**.
- **Numeral 1, 2, 3, 4** = Water jet, Water fog, Foam, Dry powder respectively.
- **Letter P, R, S, T** = Full CPC + BA (Dilute or Contain).
- **Letter W** = Responder's judgement (Dilute or Contain).
- **Letter X, Y** = BA only (Vapor hazard, Contain).
- **Letter Z** = Consult manufacturer.
- **"E" suffix** = Evacuate downwind public.
- **HAZCHEM ≠ UN Number** — HAZCHEM is *action*, UN is *identity*.
- **TREM card** (Transport Emergency) accompanies HAZCHEM in Indian practice.
- **CMVR Rule 137 (1989)** and **MSIHC Rules 1989** govern HAZCHEM in India.
- **Common code for petrol/gasoline** = **3SE**; for **ammonia** = **2XE**; for **chlorine** = **2XE**; for **MEK/acetone** = **3YE**; for **LPG** = **2WE**.

> 🎯 **Memory Hook:** *"**F-D-P-E**"* — **F**ire medium (number) → **D**efensive gear (letter) → **P**rotection from vapors (X/Y/Z) → **E**vacuate if E.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: TREM Card, Relief System and Detectors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT723 - PROCESS SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** TREM Card, Relief System and Detectors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 TREM Card (Transport Emergency Card)

### Formal Definition
A **TREM Card** (Transport Emergency Card) is a standardized hazardous materials response information document developed by **CEFIC** (Conseil Européen de l'Industrie Chimique – European Chemical Industry Council). It provides emergency response personnel with critical safety information during the transportation, loading, unloading, and storage of hazardous chemicals.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** TREM cards are mandatory documents carried in the cab of every vehicle transporting hazardous chemicals above threshold quantities, as per international and Indian regulatory guidelines (CIM, IMDG Code, and Indian Motor Vehicles Rules).

### Key Information on a TREM Card
1. **Product Identification** – UN Number, trade name, chemical formula
2. **Hazard Classification** – Primary and secondary hazards
3. **Protective Equipment** – Required PPE for responders
4. **Fire-Fighting Media** – Suitable extinguishing agents
5. **Spillage/Leakage Procedures** – Containment and cleanup methods
6. **First Aid Measures** – Immediate medical response
7. **Environmental Precautions** – Wildlife and water body protection
8. **Emergency Contact Numbers** – Manufacturer and transporter helplines

### Intuitive Analogy 🧠
Think of a TREM Card like the **safety instruction card found in an airplane seat pocket**. When everything is normal, no one looks at it. But during an emergency (toxic leak, fire, or spill), it is the *only document* that tells the firefighter, ambulance crew, or police what they are dealing with, what NOT to do, and how to save lives. Without it, emergency responders could become secondary victims.

---

## 1.2 Relief System (Pressure Relief Devices)

### Formal Definition
A **Relief System** is an engineered safety assembly comprising pressure relief devices (PRVs, rupture discs, or combination units) and associated piping, designed to automatically discharge process fluids when the system pressure exceeds a predetermined safe limit, thereby preventing catastrophic equipment failure.

### Types of Pressure Relief Devices
| Type | Operating Principle | Application |
|------|--------------------|--------------|
| **Spring-Loaded Safety Valve** | Spring force balances process pressure | Clean service, vapors, gases |
| **Rupture Disc** | Bursting of pre-scored metal disc | Corrosive service, isolating PRV |
| **Weight-Loaded Valve** | Dead weight counteracts pressure | Low-pressure large-diameter vessels |
| **Balanced Bellows Valve** | Bellows cancel back-pressure | High back-pressure services |
| **Combination Unit** | Rupture disc + safety valve | Toxic, corrosive, or fouling service |

### Intuitive Analogy 🧠
A pressure relief valve works exactly like the **pressure cooker safety whistle** in your kitchen. As pressure builds inside, the whistle (valve) opens to release steam and prevent the cooker (vessel) from exploding. The relief valve is the *last line of defense* when all other process controls fail.

---

## 1.3 Detectors (Hazard Detection Systems)

### Formal Definition
**Detectors** are instrumented safety devices that continuously monitor the process environment for the presence of hazardous conditions (toxic gases, combustible vapors, fire, or radiation) and generate an alarm or initiate a protective action (shutdown, isolation, deluge) when preset threshold concentrations or physical signatures are exceeded.

### Classification of Detectors
- **Gas Detectors** – Catalytic bead, electrochemical, infrared (NDIR), semiconductor, PID
- **Fire Detectors** – Flame (UV/IR), smoke (ionization/photoelectric), heat (fixed/rate-of-rise)
- **Toxic Gas Monitors** – Electrochemical cells, paper-tape colorimetric

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference:** Detector selection and installation follow **API RP 2210A, IEC 60079-29-2, and IS 5571** guidelines for flammable gas detection, and **IEC 61508** for functional safety in safety-instrumented systems.

### Intuitive Analogy 🧠
Detectors are the *sensory organs* of a chemical plant — just as the human nose detects gas leaks and the skin feels heat, industrial detectors continuously "sense" the plant's health and alert operators **before** the situation becomes catastrophic. They convert the plant from a "blind" system to a "visionary" one.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 TREM Card – Structural Analysis

### Origin and Standardization
- TREM cards originated in **Europe (1970s)** and were developed in coordination with **UN Recommendations on the Transport of Dangerous Goods**.
- India adopted a similar concept via **HAZCHEM** codes and **TREMCARD** under the **Manufacture, Storage and Import of Hazardous Chemical Rules, 1989** (MSIHC Rules).
- The Indian variant is often called **"TREMCARD India"** and follows the **Central Motor Vehicles Rules (CMVR)**.

### Sections of a Standard TREM Card

| Section | Content | Purpose |
|---------|---------|---------|
| 1. Identification | UN Number, Hazchem Code, Product Name | Identifies chemical |
| 2. Nature of Hazard | Toxic, flammable, corrosive, oxidizing | Primary/secondary hazards |
| 3. Personal Protection | SCBA, chemical suit, gloves | Responder safety |
| 4. Emergency Action Code (EAC) | Fire-fighting medium codes (e.g., "2W") | First-action guide |
| 5. Spillage Procedures | Dike, absorb, neutralize | Containment |
| 6. Fire-Fighting | Water fog, foam, dry chemical | Suppression |
| 7. First Aid | Skin/eye/inhalation response | Medical aid |
| 8. Additional Information | Evacuation distance, contact | Coordination |

### Emergency Action Code (EAC) Logic
The EAC is a **2-character alphanumeric code**:
- **First character (1-4):** Type of fire-fighting media
  - `1` = Water jets
  - `2` = Water fog (fine spray)
  - `3` = Foam
  - `4` = Dry agent
- **Second character (P, R, W, X, Y, Z):** Personal protection and dilution
  - `P` = Full protective clothing + SCBA
  - `R` = Liquid-tight chemical protective clothing
  - `W` = Contain spillage (do not dilute)
  - `X` = Contain and dilute
  - `Y` = Risk of violent reaction; evacuate
  - `Z` = Full evacuation required

---

## 2.2 Relief System – Theoretical Framework

### Why Relief Systems Are Required
1. **Fire Exposure** – External fire heats vessel, vaporizing liquid contents and increasing pressure
2. **Blocked Outlet** – Downstream valve closure leads to over-pressurization
3. **Cooling Failure** – Loss of cooling increases temperature and vapor pressure
4. **Abnormal Reaction** – Runaway exothermic reaction
5. **Utility Failure** – Loss of instrument air, electricity, or cooling water

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **API 521 Classification:** Relief scenarios are categorized as **"Sizeable"** (credible, must be designed for) or **"Not Required by Code"** (extreme events excluded by mutual agreement between owner and regulatory authority).

### Key Design Parameters

| Parameter | Symbol | Definition | Typical Value |
|-----------|--------|------------|---------------|
| **Set Pressure** | $P_{set}$ | Inlet pressure at which valve begins to open | Per design |
| **Accumulation** | A | Allowable pressure rise above $P_{set}$ during discharge | 10% (fire), 21% (non-fire) |
| **Overpressure** | $P_{over}$ | Pressure during relief (max) | $P_{set} \times (1+A)$ |
| **Blowdown** | $\Delta P_{bd}$ | Pressure drop before reseating | 5-7% of set pressure |
| **Back Pressure** | $P_{b}$ | Pressure on outlet side | Built-up + superimposed |
| **Maximum Allowable Working Pressure** | MAWP | Code-stamped design limit | Vessel design basis |

### Sizing Formula for Gas/Vapor Relief

$$A = \frac{W}{C \cdot K_d \cdot P_{set} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{M}{ZT}}}$$

Where:
- $A$ = Required discharge area ($\text{m}^2$)
- $W$ = Required mass flow rate ($\text{kg/hr}$)
- $C$ = Coefficient (function of $\gamma = C_p/C_v$)
- $K_d$ = Effective coefficient of discharge (typically **0.975** for certified valves)
- $P_{set}$ = Set pressure in **kPa (absolute)**
- $M$ = Molecular weight of fluid
- $Z$ = Compressibility factor
- $T$ = Inlet temperature (**K**)

### Coefficient C for Gas/Vapor Relief

$$C = 0.03948 \sqrt{k \left(\frac{2}{k+1}\right)^{\frac{k+1}{k-1}}}$$

Where $k = C_p / C_v$ (specific heat ratio).

> [!NOTE]
> For steam (k ≈ 1.3), C ≈ 0.03948 × √(1.3 × (2/2.3)^(2.3/0.3)) ≈ **0.03948 × 0.586 ≈ 0.02315**, and API 521 commonly uses **C = 0.02315** for saturated steam.

### Sizing Formula for Liquid Relief (Fire Case)

$$A = \frac{Q}{C_0 \cdot K_d \cdot K_w} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{1}{P_1 - P_2}}$$

Where:
- $A$ = Discharge area ($\text{m}^2$ or $\text{in}^2$)
- $Q$ = Total heat input (**W** or **BTU/hr**)
- $C_0$ = Coefficient based on $k$ (≈ 0.02315 equivalent)
- $K_d$ = Coefficient of discharge (**0.65** typical for liquid)
- $K_w$ = Back-pressure correction factor
- $P_1, P_2$ = Upstream and downstream pressures

### Heat Input for Fire Case (API 521)

$$Q = 43{,}200 \cdot F \cdot A_{w}^{0.82}$$

Where:
- $Q$ = Heat absorption rate (BTU/hr) or W
- $F$ = Environment factor (1.0 for total fire engulfment, 0.3 for water spray)
- $A_w$ = Wetted surface area ($\text{ft}^2$ or $\text{m}^2$)

### Real-World Utility
- **Refineries:** Atmospheric and vacuum distillation columns, FCC units, hydrocrackers
- **Chemical Plants:** Reactors (especially exothermic), storage vessels, heat exchangers
- **LNG/Cryogenic:** Re-liquefaction systems require special cryogenic relief
- **Pharmaceuticals:** Batch reactors, autoclaves

---

## 2.3 Detectors – Theoretical Framework

### Gas Detection Principles

#### 1. Catalytic Bead Sensor
- **Principle:** Oxidation of combustible gas on a heated catalyst bead changes its resistance (Wheatstone bridge)
- **Range:** **0-100% LEL**
- **Application:** Hydrocarbons, hydrogen
- **Limitation:** Requires oxygen; poisoned by silicones, lead, sulfur

$$R_{pellistor} = R_0 \left[1 + \alpha (T - T_0)\right]$$

#### 2. Infrared (NDIR) Sensor
- **Principle:** Selective absorption of IR radiation at characteristic wavelengths (e.g., 3.3 µm for CH₄)
- **Beer-Lambert Law:**

$$I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\alpha C L}$$

Where:
- $I$ = Transmitted intensity
- $I_0$ = Incident intensity
- $\alpha$ = Absorption coefficient
- $C$ = Gas concentration
- $L$ = Optical path length

#### 3. Electrochemical Sensor
- **Principle:** Target gas diffuses through membrane and undergoes redox reaction, generating current proportional to concentration
- **Application:** Toxic gases (CO, H₂S, NO₂, Cl₂, O₂)
- **Typical Sensitivity:** **ppm level**

#### 4. Photoionization Detector (PID)
- **Principle:** UV lamp ionizes gas molecules; current measured
- **Application:** VOCs, benzene
- **Limit:** **0.1 ppm to 10,000 ppm**

### Fire Detection Principles
| Detector Type | Physical Signature | Response Time |
|---------------|--------------------|---------------|
| **UV Flame** | Ultraviolet emission (185-260 nm) | **<1 second** |
| **IR Flame** | Infrared flicker (1-10 Hz) | 1-3 seconds |
| **Ionization Smoke** | Smoke disrupts ionization current | 30-60 seconds |
| **Photoelectric Smoke** | Light scattering by smoke particles | 30-120 seconds |
| **Fixed Heat** | Reaches preset temperature | Slow |
| **Rate-of-Rise Heat** | Temperature rise > 8°C/min | 30-90 seconds |

### Detector Placement (API RP 2210A)
- **Gas detectors:** Within **1 m** of potential leak points; breathing zone (**1.5-2 m** above grade)
- **Coverage area:** Typically **circular radius 5-10 m** per detector
- **Flame detectors:** Line-of-sight, **cone of vision 90°**, coverage up to **20 m**

### KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Equation | Use |
|---------|----------|-----|
| Gas relief area | $A = W / (C \cdot K_d \cdot P_{set} \cdot \sqrt{M/(ZT)})$ | Gas/vapor service |
| Liquid relief area | $A = Q / (C_0 \cdot K_d \cdot K_w) \cdot \sqrt{1/(P_1-P_2)}$ | Liquid service |
| Fire heat input | $Q = 43{,}200 \cdot F \cdot A_w^{0.82}$ | External fire case |
| Coefficient C | $C = 0.03948 \sqrt{k(2/(k+1))^{(k+1)/(k-1)}}$ | Gas coefficient |
| Accumulation | $A = (P_{over} - P_{set})/P_{set} \times 100\%$ | Pressure rise |
| Beer-Lambert Law | $I = I_0 e^{-\alpha C L}$ | IR gas detection |
| LEL calculation | $C_{detection} = f \times LEL$ | Alarm setting (f = 0.1-0.25) |
| Detector coverage | $r_{coverage} = \sqrt{A_{zone}/\pi}$ | Layout design |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard alarm setpoints:** 
> - **Low alarm:** 10% LEL (warning)
> - **High alarm:** 25% LEL (action)
> - **Evacuation alarm:** 50% LEL
> 
> These follow **API RP 2210A** and **IS 5571** guidelines.

# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Gas/Vapor Relief Valve Sizing (API 520)

### Starting Point – Mass Flux Equation
For compressible gas flow through an orifice, the **critical (choked) mass flux** is given by the isentropic flow equation:

$$G = P_1 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{M}{ZRT_1}} \cdot \sqrt{k \left(\frac{2}{k+1}\right)^{\frac{k+1}{k-1}}}$$

This is the **maximum mass flux** achievable, occurring when downstream pressure $P_2 \leq P_{crit}$:

$$P_{crit} = P_1 \left(\frac{2}{k+1}\right)^{\frac{k}{k-1}}$$

### Step 1: Combine with Discharge Coefficient
The actual mass flow through a relief valve is:

$$W = A \cdot G \cdot K_d$$

Substituting:

$$W = A \cdot K_d \cdot P_1 \sqrt{\frac{M}{ZRT_1}} \cdot \sqrt{k \left(\frac{2}{k+1}\right)^{\frac{k+1}{k-1}}}$$

### Step 2: Define Coefficient C
By definition:

$$C = 0.03948 \sqrt{k \left(\frac{2}{k+1}\right)^{\frac{k+1}{k-1}}}$$

This is a dimensionless constant that accounts for the specific heat ratio.

### Step 3: Rearrange for Area
Starting with:

$$W = A \cdot K_d \cdot C \cdot P_1 \sqrt{\frac{M}{ZT_1}}$$

Solving for A:

$$A = \frac{W}{C \cdot K_d \cdot P_{set} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{M}{Z T_1}}}$$

This is the **final API 520 sizing equation** for gas/vapor service. ✅

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: Gas Relief Valve Sizing

### Problem Statement
A pressure vessel contains **methane (CH₄)** at a set pressure of **$P_{set} = 1500$ kPa (gauge)** and a relief temperature of **T = 50°C (323 K)**. During a blocked-outlet scenario, **2000 kg/hr** of methane must be vented. 
- Molecular weight, $M = 16$ g/mol
- Specific heat ratio, $k = 1.31$
- $Z = 0.99$
- $K_d = 0.975$

**Find:** Required relief valve orifice area.

### Step 1: Convert Pressure to Absolute
$$P_{set, abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm} = 1500 + 101.3 = 1601.3 \text{ kPa}$$

### Step 2: Calculate Coefficient C

$$C = 0.03948 \sqrt{1.31 \left(\frac{2}{1.31+1}\right)^{\frac{1.31+1}{1.31-1}}}$$

Calculate the inner term:

$$\frac{2}{1.31+1} = \frac{2}{2.31} = 0.8658$$

$$\frac{1.31+1}{1.31-1} = \frac{2.31}{0.31} = 7.452$$

$$0.8658^{7.452} = e^{7.452 \cdot \ln(0.8658)} = e^{7.452 \cdot (-0.1441)} = e^{-1.074} = 0.3415$$

$$1.31 \times 0.3415 = 0.4473$$

$$\sqrt{0.4473} = 0.6688$$

$$C = 0.03948 \times 0.6688 = 0.02641$$

### Step 3: Apply the Sizing Equation

$$A = \frac{W}{C \cdot K_d \cdot P_{set} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{M}{ZT}}}$$

Calculate the square root term:

$$\sqrt{\frac{M}{ZT}} = \sqrt{\frac{16}{0.99 \times 323}} = \sqrt{\frac{16}{319.77}} = \sqrt{0.05004} = 0.2237$$

### Step 4: Compute the Area

$$A = \frac{2000}{0.02641 \times 0.975 \times 1601.3 \times 0.2237}$$

Compute the denominator:

$$0.02641 \times 0.975 = 0.02575$$

$$0.02575 \times 1601.3 = 41.23$$

$$41.23 \times 0.2237 = 9.224$$

Therefore:

$$A = \frac{2000}{9.224} = 216.8 \text{ mm}^2 = 2.168 \text{ cm}^2$$

### Step 5: Convert to Orifice Designation
Standard API 526 orifice designations:
- D = 71 mm²
- E = 126 mm²
- F = 198 mm²
- G = 325 mm²

Since **216.8 mm² > F (198 mm²)** but < G (325 mm²), select **Orifice G**.

> [!NOTE]
> The next standard size up is always selected for safety margin. Choosing F would be undersized.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: Liquid Relief (Fire Case)

### Problem Statement
A horizontal cylindrical vessel contains **hexane** (MW = 86, ρ = 655 kg/m³) and is exposed to a fire. 
- Wetted surface area: $A_w = 30$ m²
- Set pressure: $P_{set} = 800$ kPa gauge
- Overpressure (accumulation 21%): $P_1 = 968$ kPa absolute
- Back pressure: $P_2 = 101.3$ kPa absolute
- Environment factor: $F = 1.0$

### Step 1: Calculate Heat Input

$$Q = 43{,}200 \times F \times A_w^{0.82} = 43{,}200 \times 1.0 \times 30^{0.82}$$

$$30^{0.82} = e^{0.82 \ln 30} = e^{0.82 \times 3.401} = e^{2.789} = 16.27$$

$$Q = 43{,}200 \times 16.27 = 702{,}864 \text{ BTU/hr}$$

### Step 2: Determine Coefficient
For liquid service, $C_0$ for hexane (k ≈ 1.06): $C_0 \approx 0.0232$ (use **API 521** tables, value depends on k).

### Step 3: Apply Liquid Sizing Equation

$$A = \frac{Q}{C_0 \cdot K_d \cdot K_w} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{1}{P_1 - P_2}}$$

With $K_d = 0.65$, $K_w = 1.0$ (negligible back pressure):

$$P_1 - P_2 = 968 - 101.3 = 866.7 \text{ kPa}$$

$$\sqrt{\frac{1}{866.7}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{866.7}} = \frac{1}{29.44} = 0.03397$$

$$A = \frac{702{,}864}{0.0232 \times 0.65 \times 1.0} \times 0.03397$$

Denominator: $0.0232 \times 0.65 = 0.01508$

$$A = \frac{702{,}864}{0.01508} \times 0.03397 = 46{,}609{,}020 \times 0.03397 = 1{,}583{,}309 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$A \approx 1.58 \text{ m}^2$$

This is a very large area, indicating the use of a **rupture disc** rather than a safety valve.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Detector Selection Decision Tree

| Application | Recommended Detector | Reason | Range |
|-------------|---------------------|--------|-------|
| LPG leak (bulk) | Catalytic bead or IR | Wide range, fast response | 0-100% LEL |
| Hydrogen sulfide (H₂S) | Electrochemical | ppm sensitivity, selective | 0-100 ppm |
| Benzene vapor | PID | Selective to VOCs, low detection | 0.1-1000 ppm |
| Methane in confined space | IR (NDIR) | Immune to poisoning | 0-100% LEL |
| Pool fire detection | UV/IR flame | Fast (<1s), line-of-sight | 0-20 m |
| Cable tray fire | Smoke (photoelectric) | Slow-smoldering detection | Per IS 11360 |
| Furnace flame monitoring | UV | Immune to hot background | 0-5 m |
| O₂ deficiency | Electrochemical | Specific, ppm accuracy | 0-25% vol |
| CO in parking | Electrochemical | Selective, ppm | 0-500 ppm |
| HF in alkylation | Electrochemical (special) | Acidic gas resistant | 0-10 ppm |

# 📕 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define TREM Card. List any four key information sections present on it.**

**Model Answer:**
TREM Card (Transport Emergency Card) is a standardized document developed by CEFIC that provides emergency response information during the transportation of hazardous chemicals. It is carried in the vehicle cab and is essential for first responders.

**Four key sections:**
1. **UN Number and product identification** – Identifies the chemical
2. **Nature of hazard** – Primary and secondary hazards
3. **Personal protective equipment** – Required gear for responders
4. **Fire-fighting media** – Suitable extinguishing agents (water fog, foam, dry chemical)
5. **Spillage procedures** – Containment and cleanup methods
6. **First aid measures** – Immediate medical response
7. **Emergency contact numbers**

> [!TIP]
> Always mention the issuer (CEFIC) and the regulatory body (MSIHC Rules, India).

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Differentiate between a spring-loaded safety valve and a rupture disc.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Spring-Loaded Safety Valve | Rupture Disc |
|---------|---------------------------|--------------|
| Operating principle | Spring counteracts pressure | Pre-scored metal disc bursts |
| Re-seating | Yes, automatic | No (must be replaced) |
| Leak-tightness | Moderate (small leakage past seat) | Excellent (100% tight) |
| Application | Clean service (vapors, gases) | Corrosive, toxic, slurry service |
| Maintenance | Periodic testing and reseating | Replace after activation |
| Cost over lifetime | Lower | Higher per activation |

**Applications:**
- **Safety valve:** Steam boilers, gas relief, atmospheric storage
- **Rupture disc:** Isolating PRVs from process, toxic service (e.g., HF, Cl₂), polymerization reactors

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

### Question A [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]
**(a)** Explain the classification of pressure relief scenarios as per **API 521**. Discuss the design parameters: set pressure, accumulation, overpressure, and blowdown. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pressure vessel operates with **propane (C₃H₈)** at a set pressure of **$P_{set} = 1800$ kPa (gauge)**. The relief temperature is **T = 80°C (353 K)**. A blocked-outlet case requires relief of **W = 3500 kg/hr**. Given: $M = 44$, $k = 1.13$, $Z = 0.92$, $K_d = 0.975$. **Calculate the required relief valve orifice area and select the appropriate API 526 orifice designation.** **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A (a)

**API 521 Relief Scenarios:**

API 521 classifies relief scenarios into two categories:

1. **"Sizeable" (Credible) Scenarios** – Must be designed for:
   - **External fire** (most common, using $Q = 43{,}200 F A_w^{0.82}$)
   - **Blocked outlet** (downstream valve closure)
   - **Cooling or power failure**
   - **Abnormal heat input** (e.g., utility steam valve stuck open)
   - **Runaway chemical reaction**

2. **"Not Required by Code" (Improbable) Scenarios** – Excluded by mutual agreement:
   - **Vessel rupture** (assumed not credible)
   - **Concurrent unrelated failures**
   - **Extremely rare natural events**

**Design Parameters:**

- **Set Pressure ($P_{set}$):** The inlet gauge pressure at which the relief valve begins to open. Typically set at or below **MAWP**. For fire case, $P_{set} = MAWP$.

- **Accumulation (A):** The pressure rise above $P_{set}$ permitted during discharge. As per **API 520**:
  - **Fire case:** A ≤ **10%** (overpressure 110% of MAWP)
  - **Non-fire (process) case:** A ≤ **21%** (overpressure 121% of MAWP)

$$P_{over} = P_{set} \times (1 + A)$$

- **Blowdown ($\Delta P_{bd}$):** The pressure drop between set pressure and reseat pressure. Typical blowdown is **5-7%** of set pressure. Lower blowdown reduces emissions but increases valve size.

- **Back Pressure ($P_b$):** Pressure at the outlet of the relief valve. Consists of:
  - **Superimposed back pressure** – Static pressure from connected discharge system
  - **Built-up back pressure** – Dynamic pressure developed during flow

$$P_{total,outlet} = P_{superimposed} + P_{built-up}$$

---

### Model Solution for Question A (b)

**Given:**
- $P_{set} = 1800$ kPa (gauge), $P_{atm} = 101.3$ kPa
- $T_1 = 353$ K
- $W = 3500$ kg/hr
- $M = 44$ g/mol
- $k = 1.13$
- $Z = 0.92$
- $K_d = 0.975$

**Step 1: Convert Pressure to Absolute**
$$P_{set, abs} = 1800 + 101.3 = 1901.3 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Coefficient C**
$$C = 0.03948 \sqrt{1.13 \left(\frac{2}{1.13+1}\right)^{\frac{1.13+1}{1.13-1}}}$$

Inner term:
$$\frac{2}{2.13} = 0.93897$$

$$\frac{2.13}{0.13} = 16.385$$

$$0.93897^{16.385} = e^{16.385 \cdot \ln(0.93897)} = e^{16.385 \times (-0.06302)} = e^{-1.0326} = 0.3560$$

$$1.13 \times 0.3560 = 0.4023$$

$$\sqrt{0.4023} = 0.6343$$

$$C = 0.03948 \times 0.6343 = 0.02505$$

**Step 3: Calculate Square Root Term**

$$\sqrt{\frac{M}{ZT}} = \sqrt{\frac{44}{0.92 \times 353}} = \sqrt{\frac{44}{324.76}} = \sqrt{0.1355} = 0.3681$$

**Step 4: Calculate Orifice Area**

$$A = \frac{W}{C \cdot K_d \cdot P_{set} \cdot \sqrt{M/(ZT)}}$$

Denominator:
$$C \cdot K_d = 0.02505 \times 0.975 = 0.02442$$

$$0.02442 \times 1901.3 = 46.43$$

$$46.43 \times 0.3681 = 17.09$$

$$A = \frac{3500}{17.09} = 204.8 \text{ mm}^2 = 2.05 \text{ cm}^2$$

**Step 5: Select Orifice (API 526)**
Standard orifice areas: D=71, E=126, F=198, G=325, H=507 mm²

Since **204.8 mm² > F (198 mm²)** but < G (325 mm²), select **Orifice G** (325 mm²). ✅

**Answer:** Required orifice area = 204.8 mm²; selected standard orifice = **G (325 mm²)**.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice

### Question B [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]
**(a)** Describe the principles of operation and applications of **(i) Catalytic bead sensor** and **(ii) Infrared (NDIR) gas detector**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A refinery process unit uses butane as feedstock. The LEL of butane is **1.8% (v/v)** in air. As per API RP 2210A, design a gas detection system for the compressor house (floor area **250 m²**, height **6 m**). Specify: number of detectors, mounting height, alarm setpoints, and detector coverage radius. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B (a)

**(i) Catalytic Bead Sensor:**

- **Principle:** Two matched beads (active and reference) form a **Wheatstone bridge**. The active bead is coated with a catalyst (palladium or platinum) and heated to **~500°C**. Combustible gas oxidizes on the bead surface, releasing heat and increasing its electrical resistance. The bridge imbalance voltage is proportional to gas concentration.
- **Equation:**
$$\Delta V = V_{exc} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{4R}$$
where $\Delta R$ depends on the temperature rise and hence gas concentration.
- **Range:** **0-100% LEL** (typically)
- **Applications:** Refineries, LPG storage, offshore platforms
- **Limitations:**
  - Requires oxygen (≥10% vol)
  - **Poisoned by** silicones, lead, sulfur, halogens
  - Cannot detect gases with high auto-ignition temperature
- **Standards:** EN 61779, IS 5571

**(ii) Infrared (NDIR) Gas Detector:**

- **Principle:** Based on **Beer-Lambert Law**. An IR source emits broadband radiation through a gas cell. The detector measures absorption at a specific wavelength characteristic of the target gas (e.g., **3.3 µm for CH₄**, **4.26 µm for CO₂**). A reference detector at a non-absorbing wavelength compensates for source aging and dust.
- **Equation:**
$$I = I_0 e^{-\alpha C L}$$
$$\ln\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right) = \alpha C L$$
- **Range:** **0-100% LEL** or **0-100% v/v** depending on path length
- **Applications:** LNG, methane detection, offshore platforms, automotive emission
- **Advantages over catalytic bead:**
  - **Immune to poisoning**
  - No oxygen requirement
  - **Fail-safe** (no IR = alarm)
  - Long lifetime
- **Limitations:** Limited to gases with IR-active bonds (hydrocarbons, CO₂, CO)

---

### Model Solution for Question B (b)

**Design of Gas Detection System for Butane Compressor House**

**Given:**
- Hazard: Butane (LEL = 1.8% v/v)
- Floor area: 250 m²
- Height: 6 m
- Standard: API RP 2210A

**Step 1: Determine Detector Type**
Butane is a hydrocarbon, lighter-than-air tendency is **low** (molecular weight 58, slightly heavier than air). Use **Catalytic bead** or **IR (NDIR)** point detectors. For accuracy and poisoning resistance, **NDIR is preferred** in refineries.

**Step 2: Coverage Radius**
API RP 2210A recommends a **maximum radius of 7.5 m** for point detectors. Use **5 m** for conservative design.

**Step 3: Coverage Area per Detector**

$$A_{cov} = \pi r^2 = \pi \times 5^2 = 78.5 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 4: Number of Detectors**

$$N = \frac{A_{floor}}{A_{cov}} = \frac{250}{78.5} = 3.18 \approx 4 \text{ detectors}$$

**Step 5: Mounting Height**
Butane (MW = 58) is **slightly heavier than air** (MW = 29). Per API RP 2210A, install detectors at **0.3-0.6 m above grade** (lower zone) for slightly heavier gases, with additional coverage at **breathing zone (1.5 m)** for personnel safety.

**Step 6: Alarm Setpoints**
Per API RP 2210A:
- **Low alarm:** $0.1 \times LEL = 0.1 \times 1.8\% = 0.18\%$ v/v
- **High alarm:** $0.25 \times LEL = 0.25 \times 1.8\% = 0.45\%$ v/v
- **Evacuation alarm:** $0.5 \times LEL = 0.5 \times 1.8\% = 0.9\%$ v/v

**Step 7: Detector Response Action**
- **Low alarm:** Operator warning (annunciator)
- **High alarm:** Activate ESD-1, shut down compressor, start ventilation
- **Evacuation alarm:** Activate ESD-2, fire-fighting response

**Answer Summary:**

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Detector type | NDIR (Infrared) |
| Quantity | **4** |
| Mounting height | 0.3-0.6 m (low zone) + 1.5 m (breathing zone) |
| Coverage radius | 5 m |
| Low alarm | 0.18% v/v |
| High alarm | 0.45% v/v |
| Evacuation alarm | 0.9% v/v |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 
> **Common mistakes students make in this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing LEL% and ppm** – LEL is in **% v/v**; for toxic gases, use **ppm**. They are not interchangeable.
> 2. **Wrong pressure units** – API 520 requires **absolute** pressure in the sizing equation. Forgetting to add atmospheric pressure leads to **major numerical errors** (typically 6-8% deviation).
> 3. **Selecting undersized orifice** – Always select the **next standard size up** (API 526 designation). Selecting exactly the calculated area is a design error.
> 4. **Wrong mount height for detector** – Methane (lighter than air) → ceiling; Propane/Butane (heavier than air) → floor level. This is a **3-mark loss** in viva.
> 5. **Ignoring back-pressure correction** – For conventional valves, if superimposed back pressure > 10% of set pressure, apply **$K_w$ correction factor**.
> 6. **Wrong EAC interpretation** – TREM card code "2W" means water fog + contain spillage, NOT "water and wind direction."
> 7. **Forgetting to state standards** – Always cite **API 520, API 521, IS 5571, IEC 60079-29-2** in your answer; failing to do so loses methodology marks.

---

## 5.4 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist – High-Density Summary**

### ✅ TREM Card Essentials
- ☐ Issued by **CEFIC** (Europe); Indian equivalent: **TREMCARD India**
- ☐ Mandatory under **MSIHC Rules, 1989** and **CMVR**
- ☐ Contains: UN Number, EAC, PPE, fire media, spillage, first aid
- ☐ EAC format: **2 characters** – first = fire medium, second = personal protection
- ☐ Carried in the **vehicle cab**; copy with emergency services
- ☐ Standard language: **English** (local translation recommended)

### ✅ Relief System Essentials
- ☐ Standards: **API 520 (sizing), API 521 (scenarios), API 526 (orifices)**
- ☐ Set pressure ≤ **MAWP**; never exceed
- ☐ Accumulation: **10% fire, 21% non-fire** (overpressure 110% or 121%)
- ☐ Blowdown: **5-7%** of set pressure
- ☐ Sizing equation: $A = W / (C \cdot K_d \cdot P_{set} \cdot \sqrt{M/(ZT)})$
- ☐ Fire heat input: $Q = 43{,}200 F A_w^{0.82}$
- ☐ **Rupture disc** for toxic/corrosive; **Spring valve** for clean service
- ☐ Always select **next standard orifice up** from API 526

### ✅ Detector Essentials
- ☐ Standards: **API RP 2210A, IS 5571, IEC 60079-29-2, IEC 61508**
- ☐ Catalytic bead: **0-100% LEL**, requires O₂, poisoned by silicones
- ☐ NDIR: Uses **Beer-Lambert law**, immune to poisoning, fail-safe
- ☐ Electrochemical: **ppm level** for toxic gases (CO, H₂S, Cl₂)
- ☐ PID: **ppb to ppm** range for VOCs (benzene, toluene)
- ☐ UV flame: **<1 second** response, false alarms from arc welding
- ☐ IR flame: **1-3 seconds**, immune to sunlight, false alarms from heaters
- ☐ Detector placement: **5-7.5 m radius** for point detectors
- ☐ Lighter-than-air gases (CH₄, H₂) → **ceiling-mounted**
- ☐ Heavier-than-air gases (C₃H₈, C₄H₁₀) → **floor-mounted (0.3-0.6 m)**
- ☐ Standard alarm: **10% LEL (low), 25% LEL (high), 50% LEL (evac)**

### ✅ Formula Quick-Reference
| Formula | Purpose |
|---------|---------|
| $A = W/(C \cdot K_d \cdot P_{set} \cdot \sqrt{M/(ZT)})$ | Gas relief sizing |
| $A = Q/(C_0 K_d K_w) \cdot \sqrt{1/(P_1-P_2)}$ | Liquid relief sizing |
| $Q = 43{,}200 F A_w^{0.82}$ | Fire heat input |
| $C = 0.03948\sqrt{k(2/(k+1))^{(k+1)/(k-1)}}$ | Gas coefficient |
| $I = I_0 e^{-\alpha C L}$ | Beer-Lambert (IR sensor) |
| $A = \pi r^2$ | Detector coverage area |
| $P_{over} = P_{set}(1 + A)$ | Overpressure calculation |
| $C_{alarm} = f \times LEL$ | Alarm setpoint (f = 0.1, 0.25, 0.5) |
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: TLV, STEL, TLV-C, IDLH, LFL
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECHT723 - PROCESS SAFETY ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** TLV, STEL, TLV-C, IDLH, LFL # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Introduction to Occupational Exposure Limits (OELs) In Process Safety Engineering, the management of **chemical exposure** in industrial environments is governed by a hierarchy of scientifically established limits. These limits define the maximum concentration of a substance to which a worker can be exposed without suffering adverse health effects. The five principal limits prescribed by the **American Conference of Governmental Industrial Hygienists (ACGIH)** and **OSHA / NIOSH** standards are: 1. **TLV (Threshold Limit Value)** 2. **STEL (Short-Term Exposure Limit)** 3. **TLV-C (Threshold Limit Value – Ceiling)** 4. **IDLH (Immediately Dangerous to Life or Health)** 5. **LFL (Lower Flammable Limit)** > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** These terms are foundational vocabulary for Module 3 (Toxicology, Industrial Hygiene, and Flammability Limits). Examiner questions frequently appear in the form of "Define and differentiate" (3-mark Part A) or "Classify chemicals based on their exposure limit hierarchy" (14-mark Part B). --- ## 1.2 Formal Definitions ### (a) Threshold Limit Value (TLV-TWA) The **Threshold Limit Value – Time Weighted Average (TLV-TWA)** is the time-weighted average concentration of a chemical in air for a normal **8-hour workday** and a **40-hour workweek**, to which nearly all workers may be repeatedly exposed, day after day, without adverse health effects. $$ \text{TLV-TWA} = \frac{\sum (C_i \times t_i)}{\sum t_i} $$ where $C_i$ is the concentration during the time interval $t_i$. ### (b) Short-Term Exposure Limit (STEL) The **STEL** is the **15-minute time-weighted average** exposure that should not be exceeded at any time during a workday, even if the 8-hour TWA is within acceptable limits. It is permissible for a worker to have up to **four STEL excursions per day**, with at least **60 minutes** between successive excursions. ### (c) Threshold Limit Value – Ceiling (TLV-C) The **TLV-C** is the concentration that must **not be exceeded at any instant** of the working day. Unlike TWA, it is a strict instantaneous limit. Chemicals that exert rapid-acting narcotic or irritant effects (e.g., ammonia, hydrogen chloride) are typically assigned ceiling values. ### (d) Immediately Dangerous to Life or Health (IDLH) **IDLH** is defined by **NIOSH** as any atmosphere that poses an **immediate or delayed threat to life**, would cause irreversible adverse health effects, or would interfere with an individual's ability to escape (e.g., visual impairment, narcosis). The two defining criteria are: - Max airborne concentration from which a worker could escape within **30 minutes** without irreversible health effects. - Concentrations causing **irreversible health effects** or impairing escape. ### (e) Lower Flammable Limit (LFL) / Lower Explosive Limit (LEL) The **LFL** (synonymous with **LEL**) is the **minimum concentration of a vapor/gas in air** below which propagation of a flame does **not occur** on contact with a source of ignition. Below the LFL, the mixture is too "lean" to burn. $$ C_{mixture} < \text{LFL} \Rightarrow \text{Non-flammable} $$ --- ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Speedometer & Danger Zones" > [!NOTE] > **Analogy:** Think of chemical exposure like driving a car on a highway. > - **TLV-TWA** is the *average cruising speed* limit over your whole 8-hour journey (say, 80 km/h average). You can momentarily exceed it (overtaking) but on average you must stay below. > - **STEL** is the *15-minute overtaking speed* cap — you can push the accelerator to 100 km/h, but only briefly, and not too often. > - **TLV-C** is the *red-line maximum speed* — a hard rev-limit. Touch it once and the engine warns. > - **IDLH** is the *crash speed* — exposure here can cause death or irreversible injury, similar to a head-on collision. > - **LFL** is the *minimum fuel-to-air ratio* below which your car's engine simply won't ignite — too lean to combust. This hierarchy (TLV-C ≤ STEL ≤ TLV-TWA ≪ IDLH ≪ LFL) forms a **logical safety envelope** in process plant design. --- ## 1.4 Why These Limits Matter in Process Engineering - **Engineering Controls:** Ventilation systems, closed-loop reactors, and scrubbers are designed to maintain workplace concentration **below TLV-TWA**. - **Alarm Setting Hierarchy:** Plant toxic gas detectors are usually set at: **1 × TLV** (warning) → **5 × TLV** (high alarm) → **IDLH/10** (general alarm) → **IDLH** (evacuation). - **Flammability Control:** Inerting systems and ventilation are engineered to keep solvent vapor concentrations either **below 0.5 × LFL** (NFPA recommended safety margin) or **above UFL**. - **Legal Compliance:** OSHA's Permissible Exposure Limits (PEL) and EPA's Risk Management Plan (RMP) thresholds (e.g., 10,000 lb of an IDLH substance) are derived from these scientific values. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Exposure Limit Hierarchy (In Order of Severity) The limits are **not equivalent**; they serve different purposes in a graduated risk-control framework. From the most stringent to the most catastrophic: $$ \text{TLV-C} \leq \text{STEL (15-min)} \leq \text{TLV-TWA (8-hr)} \ll \text{IDLH} \ll \text{LFL} $$ ### Logical Reasoning Behind the Order 1. **TLV-C** is the *instantaneous strict limit* — must never be crossed. 2. **STEL** allows brief excursions for acute irritants — designed for chemicals that cause short-term effects (e.g., tearing, cough) that disappear rapidly. 3. **TLV-TWA** protects against **chronic** (long-term) effects such as carcinogenicity, organ damage, or neurological disorders. 4. **IDLH** is the *life-threatening* concentration — typically 10× to 1000× the TLV. 5. **LFL** is a *physical flammability threshold* (e.g., acetone LFL = 2.5 %, acetone TLV-TWA = 500 ppm) — usually 2–3 orders of magnitude higher than TLV because toxicity acts at much lower concentrations than flammability. --- ## 2.2 Time-Weighted Average (TWA) — The Foundation Concept The TWA model assumes that the human body integrates exposure over time. The dose is proportional to: $$ \text{Dose} \propto C \times t $$ Hence the **Haber's Law** foundation: $$ \text{Effect} = f(C \times t) $$ For a workday with $n$ intervals of varying concentration $C_i$ and duration $t_i$: $$ \text{TWA} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i \, t_i}{8 \text{ hours}} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Favourite Question Pattern:** "A worker is exposed to 400 ppm of solvent for 2 hours, 200 ppm for 4 hours, and 0 ppm for 2 hours. Calculate the 8-hr TWA and compare with TLV-TWA of 350 ppm." --- ## 2.3 Relationship Between TLV-C and TWA For substances with both ceiling and TWA values, the ceiling value is the *primary* control. A worker can have a TWA below the TLV-TWA but still violate the TLV-C if a single peak exceeds the ceiling. $$ \text{Compliance: } \max(C_{\text{instantaneous}}) \leq \text{TLV-C} \quad \text{AND} \quad \text{TWA} \leq \text{TLV-TWA} $$ --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Symbol / Term | Full Name | Mathematical Form / Definition | Typical Time Basis | Typical Units | KTU 2024 Significance | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | TLV-TWA | Threshold Limit Value – Time-Weighted Average | $\text{TWA} = \dfrac{\sum C_i t_i}{8}$ | 8 hr/day, 40 hr/week | ppm or mg/m³ | Most common exposure metric | | STEL | Short-Term Exposure Limit | 15-min TWA $\leq$ STEL | 15 minutes | ppm or mg/m³ | Acute effect control | | TLV-C | Threshold Limit Value – Ceiling | $C_{\text{max}} \leq$ TLV-C | Instantaneous | ppm or mg/m³ | Hard instantaneous limit | | IDLH | Immediately Dangerous to Life or Health | Escape within 30 min without irreversible injury | 30 minutes | ppm or mg/m³ | Respiratory protection threshold | | LFL (LEL) | Lower Flammable (Explosive) Limit | Min. vapor % in air for flame propagation | Equilibrium | % v/v in air | Fire & explosion design | | UFL (UEL) | Upper Flammable (Explosive) Limit | Max. vapor % in air for flame propagation | Equilibrium | % v/v in air | Fire & explosion design | | Safety Factor | Toxic-to-Flammable Margin | $K = \dfrac{\text{LFL (\% v/v)}}{\text{TLV-TWA (ppm)}} \times 10^{4}$ | — | Dimensionless | Indicator of relative hazard | | LFL Fraction | NFPA Recommended Design Margin | Operate below $0.5 \times$ LFL | Continuous | % v/v | Plant design rule | > [!NOTE] > **Conversion Note:** 1% v/v = 10,000 ppm. When working with LFL (in %) and TLV (in ppm), always convert to consistent units. --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility | Industry Application | Limit Used | Design Implication | |---|---|---| | Solvent Storage Tank Venting | LFL/LEL | Inert N₂ blanket, vent sizing for 0.5 × LFL | | Petrochemical Plant Layout | IDLH | Minimum separation distance between units | | Pharmaceutical Reactor Room | TLV-C | LEV hoods, real-time continuous monitoring | | Confined Space Entry (OSHA 1910.146) | IDLH | Supplied-air respirator (SCBA) mandate | | OSHA Compliance Monitoring | TLV-TWA | Air sampling, personal dosimeter badges | | Process Hazard Analysis (PHA) | All | LOPA (Layer of Protection Analysis) SIF design | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Detailed Derivation — Calculating TWA with Multi-Interval Exposure ### Problem Framework A laboratory technician is exposed to xylene in a research facility with the following profile during an 8-hour shift: - 8:00 AM – 10:00 AM: Concentration = 200 ppm - 10:00 AM – 12:00 PM: Lunch break (concentration = 0 ppm) - 12:00 PM – 3:00 PM: Concentration = 100 ppm - 3:00 PM – 5:00 PM: Concentration = 50 ppm - TLV-TWA for xylene = 100 ppm - STEL for xylene = 150 ppm (15-min) ### Step 1: Identify Time Intervals and Concentrations $$ t_1 = 2 \text{ hr}, \quad C_1 = 200 \text{ ppm} $$ $$ t_2 = 2 \text{ hr}, \quad C_2 = 0 \text{ ppm} $$ $$ t_3 = 3 \text{ hr}, \quad C_3 = 100 \text{ ppm} $$ $$ t_4 = 2 \text{ hr}, \quad C_4 = 50 \text{ ppm} $$ ### Step 2: Verify Total Duration $$ \sum t_i = 2 + 2 + 3 + 2 = 8 \text{ hours} \quad \checkmark $$ ### Step 3: Apply the TWA Formula $$ \text{TWA} = \frac{\sum C_i \, t_i}{8} $$ ### Step 4: Substitute Values $$ \text{TWA} = \frac{(200)(2) + (0)(2) + (100)(3) + (50)(2)}{8} $$ ### Step 5: Compute Each Term $$ \text{Numerator} = 400 + 0 + 300 + 100 = 800 \text{ ppm·hr} $$ ### Step 6: Final Division $$ \text{TWA} = \frac{800}{8} = 100 \text{ ppm} $$ ### Step 7: Compare with TLV-TWA $$ \text{TWA (100 ppm)} \;=\; \text{TLV-TWA (100 ppm)} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Borderline compliance} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Interpretation:** Even though the technician was exposed to 200 ppm (2× TLV) for 2 hours, the *time-weighted average* over 8 hours is exactly at the limit. However, one must also check the **STEL** for that 2-hour interval — instantaneous exposure to 200 ppm is **above the 150-ppm STEL** for xylene. Therefore, the operation is **non-compliant** under the STEL rule. --- ## 3.2 Detailed Derivation — Safety Margin Between LFL and TLV ### Definition: Toxicity–Flammability Ratio (Hazard Index) For a given chemical, the relationship between toxic and flammable limits is given by the **NFPA hazard ratio**: $$ K = \frac{\text{LFL (\%) } \times 10{,}000}{\text{TLV-TWA (ppm)}} $$ ### Worked Example: Acetone - LFL (acetone) = 2.5 % v/v - TLV-TWA (acetone) = 500 ppm ### Step 1: Convert LFL to ppm $$ \text{LFL} = 2.5 \% \times 10{,}000 = 25{,}000 \text{ ppm} $$ ### Step 2: Apply Hazard Index Formula $$ K = \frac{25{,}000}{500} = 50 $$ ### Step 3: Interpretation A value of $K = 50$ means the **flammable concentration is 50× higher** than the toxic exposure limit. **Acetone is more toxic than it is flammable** (in terms of safe human exposure). > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** A low K value (<10) suggests flammability is the dominant hazard (e.g., gasoline, methane).
