A high K value (>100) suggests toxicity is the dominant hazard (e.g., phosgene, HCN). --- ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Quick Reference) | Property | TLV-TWA | STEL | TLV-C | IDLH | LFL | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Purpose** | Chronic protection | Acute protection (15-min) | Instantaneous cap | Life-saving escape | Ignition threshold | | **Time Basis** | 8 hr | 15 min | Instantaneous | 30 min escape | Equilibrium | | **Type of Hazard** | Toxicity | Acute toxicity | Acute toxicity / irritancy | Acute systemic | Fire / explosion | | **Measurement** | Air sampling badge | Continuous monitor | Continuous monitor | Continuous monitor | Combustible gas detector | | **Typical Unit** | ppm or mg/m³ | ppm or mg/m³ | ppm or mg/m³ | ppm or mg/m³ | % v/v | | **OSHA 1910 Use** | PEL-TWA | PEL-STEL | PEL-Ceiling | Hazard communication | Hazardous location classification | | **Source Authority** | ACGIH | ACGIH | ACGIH | NIOSH | NFPA / ASTM E681 | | **Magnitude for Acetone (example)** | 500 ppm | 750 ppm | — | 2,500 ppm (10% LEL) | 25,000 ppm (2.5%) | | **Cross-Exceedance Rule** | 4 STELs/day | 4× per day, ≥60 min apart | Never exceed | 30 min escape | 0.5 × LFL design margin | --- ## 3.4 Derivation: LFL Mixture Calculation (Le Chatelier's Rule) For a multi-component vapor mixture, the **LFL of the mixture** is calculated using Le Chatelier's rule: $$ \text{LFL}_{\text{mixture}} = \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{y_i}{\text{LFL}_i}} $$ where $y_i$ is the mole fraction of component $i$ in the vapor phase (on a non-flammable basis), and $\text{LFL}_i$ is the LFL of pure component $i$. ### Example: A vapor stream contains 70 mol% n-hexane and 30 mol% toluene. - LFL(hexane) = 1.1 % - LFL(toluene) = 1.2 % ### Step 1: Apply Le Chatelier's Formula $$ \text{LFL}_{\text{mixture}} = \frac{1}{\dfrac{0.70}{1.1} + \dfrac{0.30}{1.2}} $$ ### Step 2: Compute Denominators $$ \frac{0.70}{1.1} = 0.6364 $$ $$ \frac{0.30}{1.2} = 0.2500 $$ ### Step 3: Sum and Take Reciprocal $$ \text{LFL}_{\text{mixture}} = \frac{1}{0.6364 + 0.2500} = \frac{1}{0.8864} = 1.128 \, \% $$ ### Step 4: Conclusion The mixture LFL is approximately **1.13 % v/v**, slightly higher than either pure component, but still in the highly flammable range. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Exposure Limit Severity Pyramid The relationship among the five limits is most clearly visualized as a **concentration pyramid** — narrow at the top (safe operating range) and widening as harm escalates to catastrophic events (explosion, death). ```mermaid flowchart TB A["SAFE OPERATING ZONE
Concentration less than TLV-TWA
No observable health effects"] B["ACUTE EFFECT ZONE
TLV-TWA to STEL
Short-term irritation, reversible"] C["CEILING VIOLATION ZONE
STEL to TLV-C exceeded
Sharp reversible symptoms"] D["CHRONIC TOXICITY ZONE
Repeated exposures
Organ damage, cancer risk"] E["LIFE THREAT ZONE
TLV-TWA to IDLH
Escape impairment onset"] F["IDLH CONCENTRATION
30 min escape limit
Irreversible injury or death"] G["FLAMMABLE ENVELOPE
LFL to UFL
Fire and explosion risk"] H["LEL DESIGN MARGIN
Operate below 0.5 LFL
NFPA Recommended"] A --> B B --> C C --> D D --> E E --> F F --> G H -.-> G ``` --- ## 4.2 Decision Flowchart — Workplace Exposure Compliance A logic block commonly used in process plant Industrial Hygiene monitoring to determine compliance with exposure limits: ```mermaid flowchart TD Start(["Air Sample Data
Continuous Monitor or Badge"]) --> Q1{Ceiling Limit
Assigned?} Q1 -- Yes --> Check1["Compare max instantaneous
concentration with TLV-C"] Check1 --> Result1{Concentration
less or equal TLV-C?} Result1 -- No --> Action1["CEILING VIOLATION
Immediate evacuation"] Result1 -- Yes --> CalcTWA["Compute 8-hr TWA"] Q1 -- No --> CalcTWA CalcTWA --> Q2{TWA less or equal
TLV-TWA?} Q2 -- No --> Action2["TWA EXCEEDANCE
Engineering controls required
Respiratory protection"] Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{STEL
Assigned?} Q3 -- No --> Compliance["COMPLIANT
Maintain controls"] Q3 -- Yes --> CheckSTEL["Evaluate 15-min rolling
average peaks"] CheckSTEL --> Q4{Any 15-min avg
exceeds STEL?} Q4 -- Yes --> Action3["STEL VIOLATION
Reduce task duration or
increase ventilation"] Q4 -- No --> Compliance Action1 --> Stop(["STOP OPERATION"]) Action2 --> Stop Action3 --> Stop Compliance --> Continue(["CONTINUE WITH MONITORING"]) ``` --- ## 4.3 Process Plant Detector Alarm Hierarchy This block diagram shows the typical alarm setpoints in a process control system (DCS / SIS) for a toxic gas detector: ```mermaid flowchart LR LFL["Actual Vapor
Concentration"] --> Sensor["Toxic Gas Detector
Continuous Sensor"] Sensor --> Alarm1["Alarm 1: WARNING
Setpoint = 1 x TLV-TWA
Operator attention"] Sensor --> Alarm2["Alarm 2: HIGH
Setpoint = 5 x TLV-TWA
Ventilation ON"] Sensor --> Alarm3["Alarm 3: GENERAL
Setpoint = IDLH divide 10
Initiate evacuation protocol"] Sensor --> Alarm4["Alarm 4: CRITICAL
Setpoint = IDLH
SCBA mandatory, rescue"] Sensor --> Alarm5["Alarm 5: FIRE
Setpoint = 0.5 x LFL
Inerting system activates"] Alarm1 --> OpAction1["Operator investigates"] Alarm2 --> OpAction2["Process shutdown sequence"] Alarm3 --> OpAction3["Site-wide evacuation"] Alarm4 --> OpAction4["Hazmat team response"] Alarm5 --> OpAction5["Fire suppression and
nitrogen purge"] ``` --- ## 4.4 Comparison Block Diagram: TLV vs LFL Hazard Domains A two-domain diagram showing the typical concentration ranges for the same chemical — illustrating the orders-of-magnitude gap between toxic and flammable limits. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Toxic["TOXICITY DOMAIN (ppm)"] T1["TLV-TWA
0 to 1000 ppm"] --> T2["STEL
1000 to 5000 ppm"] --> T3["IDLH
5000 to 50000 ppm"] end subgraph Flamm["FLAMMABILITY DOMAIN (% v/v)"] F1["0.5 x LFL
Design Margin"] --> F2["LFL
Lower Flammable"] --> F3["UFL
Upper Flammable"] end Toxic --- Note["Gap of 2 to 3 orders
of magnitude for
most hydrocarbons"] Flamm --- Note ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### **Q1. Define Threshold Limit Value (TLV). Distinguish between TLV-TWA and TLV-C.** [KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model] **Model Answer:** **TLV (Threshold Limit Value):** A guideline value published by the American Conference of Governmental Industrial Hygienists (ACGIH) representing the airborne concentration of a chemical to which a worker can be exposed daily without adverse health effects. | Feature | TLV-TWA | TLV-C | |---|---|---| | **Full Form** | Time-Weighted Average | Ceiling | | **Time Basis** | 8-hour workday average | Instantaneous | | **Basis** | Chronic / long-term effects | Acute / rapid-acting effects | | **Example** | Benzene: 0.5 ppm TWA | Hydrogen chloride: 2 ppm C | **Difference:** TLV-TWA is averaged over 8 hours, allowing brief excursions; TLV-C is an absolute instantaneous limit that must never be exceeded. --- ### **Q2. What is IDLH? How is it different from TLV-TWA?** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model] **Model Answer:** **IDLH (Immediately Dangerous to Life or Health)** is the airborne concentration of a substance that poses an immediate or delayed threat to life, causes irreversible health effects, or interferes with escape. It is defined by NIOSH based on the ability to escape within 30 minutes. | Feature | IDLH | TLV-TWA | |---|---|---| | **Time basis** | 30-min escape criterion | 8-hr day average | | **Purpose** | Life safety / respirator selection | Routine daily exposure | | **Magnitude** | 10× to 1000× higher than TLV | Lowest sustained-effect level | | **Action** | Requires SCBA / supplied air | Engineering controls adequate | --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice --- ### **Question A: 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam – Model 2024] #### **(a) Define and explain the following with suitable examples: (i) TLV-TWA, (ii) STEL, (iii) TLV-C. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(i) TLV-TWA (Threshold Limit Value – Time-Weighted Average):** The time-weighted average concentration for a normal 8-hour workday and 40-hour workweek to which nearly all workers may be repeatedly exposed without adverse effects. - *Example:* Toluene TLV-TWA = 20 ppm; n-Hexane TLV-TWA = 50 ppm. - *Application:* Used to assess chronic exposure — calculated from personal dosimeter data. **(ii) STEL (Short-Term Exposure Limit):** The 15-minute time-weighted average exposure that should not be exceeded at any point during the workday. - *Example:* Toluene STEL = 150 ppm (15-min); Acetone STEL = 750 ppm. - *Application:* Protects against acute effects such as narcosis or respiratory irritation. - *Rules:* Maximum 4 STEL excursions per day; minimum 60 minutes between excursions. **(iii) TLV-C (Threshold Limit Value – Ceiling):** The concentration that must not be exceeded at any instant. - *Example:* Ammonia TLV-C = 35 ppm; HCl TLV-C = 2 ppm. - *Application:* Used for fast-acting irritants and chemicals with very low safe instantaneous concentrations. --- #### **(b) A worker is exposed to 300 ppm of a solvent for 1 hour, 150 ppm for 3 hours, and 0 ppm for the remaining 4 hours of an 8-hour shift. The TLV-TWA is 200 ppm and the STEL is 250 ppm. Determine compliance. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Time Intervals:** $$ t_1 = 1 \text{ hr},\ C_1 = 300 \text{ ppm} $$ $$ t_2 = 3 \text{ hr},\ C_2 = 150 \text{ ppm} $$ $$ t_3 = 4 \text{ hr},\ C_3 = 0 \text{ ppm} $$ **Step 2 — TWA Calculation:** $$ \text{TWA} = \frac{(300)(1) + (150)(3) + (0)(4)}{8} = \frac{300 + 450 + 0}{8} = \frac{750}{8} $$ $$ \text{TWA} = 93.75 \text{ ppm} $$ **Step 3 — Compare with TLV-TWA:** $$ 93.75 \text{ ppm} \;<\; 200 \text{ ppm (TLV-TWA)} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{TWA compliant} \;\;\checkmark $$ **Step 4 — Check STEL:** The 1-hour exposure to 300 ppm contains a 15-minute average of 300 ppm, which is: $$ 300 \text{ ppm} \;>\; 250 \text{ ppm (STEL)} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{STEL VIOLATION} \;\;\times $$ **Step 5 — Conclusion:** The 8-hr TWA is compliant, but the worker has exceeded the STEL. **The exposure is non-compliant.** Process ventilation must be increased or the task duration reduced. --- ### **Question B: 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam – Model 2024] #### **(a) Define IDLH and LFL. Explain the relationship between LFL and TLV for a given chemical. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **IDLH Definition:** Concentration at which a worker can be exposed for up to 30 minutes without irreversible health effects or escape impairment. IDLH is used to select appropriate respiratory protection equipment. **LFL Definition:** The minimum concentration of a vapor in air below which the mixture is too lean to propagate a flame on ignition. LFL is determined experimentally (closed-cup apparatus per ASTM E681). **Relationship Between LFL and TLV:** The toxic limit (TLV) is typically **2 to 3 orders of magnitude lower** than the flammable limit (LFL). For example: - **Acetone:** TLV = 500 ppm, LFL = 25,000 ppm (factor 50) - **Benzene:** TLV = 0.5 ppm, LFL = 12,000 ppm (factor 24,000) - **Hydrogen Cyanide:** TLV = 4.7 ppm, LFL = 56,000 ppm (factor 11,915) **Hazard Ratio K:** $$ K = \frac{\text{LFL (ppm)}}{\text{TLV-TWA (ppm)}} $$ A small K (< 10) suggests flammability is the dominant hazard (e.g., gasoline K = 8). A large K (> 1000) suggests toxicity dominates (e.g., benzene K = 24,000). The NFPA hazard diamond 0–4 ratings are derived from this relationship. --- #### **(b) A storage vessel in a process plant contains a vapor mixture with the following composition (mol %): n-Pentane 40%, n-Hexane 35%, n-Heptane 25%. The LFL values are: n-Pentane 1.5%, n-Hexane 1.1%, n-Heptane 1.05%. Calculate the LFL of the mixture using Le Chatelier's rule. If the plant safety policy requires operation below 50% of the LFL, what is the maximum allowable vapor concentration? (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Apply Le Chatelier's Rule:** $$ \text{LFL}_{\text{mixture}} = \frac{1}{\dfrac{y_1}{\text{LFL}_1} + \dfrac{y_2}{\text{LFL}_2} + \dfrac{y_3}{\text{LFL}_3}} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute Values:** $$ \text{LFL}_{\text{mixture}} = \frac{1}{\dfrac{0.40}{1.5} + \dfrac{0.35}{1.1} + \dfrac{0.25}{1.05}} $$ **Step 3 — Compute Each Term:** $$ \frac{0.40}{1.5} = 0.2667 $$ $$ \frac{0.35}{1.1} = 0.3182 $$ $$ \frac{0.25}{1.05} = 0.2381 $$ **Step 4 — Sum the Denominators:** $$ \Sigma = 0.2667 + 0.3182 + 0.2381 = 0.8230 $$ **Step 5 — Reciprocal:** $$ \text{LFL}_{\text{mixture}} = \frac{1}{0.8230} = 1.215 \,\% \text{ v/v} $$ **Step 6 — Apply 50% Safety Margin:** $$ C_{\text{allowable}} = 0.5 \times 1.215 = 0.6075 \,\% \text{ v/v} $$ **Step 7 — Convert to ppm:** $$ C_{\text{allowable}} = 0.6075 \times 10{,}000 = 6{,}075 \text{ ppm} $$ **Step 8 — Conclusion:** The maximum allowable vapor concentration is **0.61 % v/v (6075 ppm)** to comply with the 50% LFL safety margin policy. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Most students lose marks by mixing **% v/v and ppm** in LFL/TLV problems. Always remember: **1% v/v = 10,000 ppm**. > 2. **TWA vs STEL Confusion:** Do not declare compliance based on TWA alone. Many chemicals are **TWA-compliant but STEL-violating** (as seen in Q3b above). Always check both. > 3. **TLV-C Overlooked:** If a chemical has a TLV-C, the **instantaneous** reading is the governing criterion — not the time average. > 4. **Le Chatelier Mole Fraction Basis:** In mixture LFL calculations, the mole fractions $y_i$ must be on a **combustible-only basis**. Exclude inert species (N₂, CO₂) from the denominator. > 5. **Forgetting 0.5 × LFL Rule:** NFPA recommends operation below **50% of LFL**, not at LFL. Direct quote: "Operating at LFL is the boundary, not the safe zone." --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [x] **TLV-TWA = 8-hr average** (chronic protection). - [x] **STEL = 15-min average** (acute protection; max 4 excursions/day, ≥60 min apart). - [x] **TLV-C = instantaneous ceiling** (must never be exceeded). - [x] **IDLH = 30-min escape limit** (used for respirator selection — SCBA required). - [x] **LFL = lower flammable limit** (in % v/v; design margin is **0.5 × LFL** per NFPA). - [x] Severity order: **TLV-C ≤ STEL ≤ TLV-TWA ≪ IDLH ≪ LFL**. - [x] **LFL and LEL are synonyms** — both denote the lower flammable limit. - [x] **Le Chatelier's rule** applies for multi-component LFL calculation. - [x] **Toxic limits (ppm) are 10× to 100,000× smaller than flammability limits (% v/v).** - [x] **Hazard ratio K = LFL(ppm) / TLV(ppm)** indicates whether flammability or toxicity dominates. - [x] Always check **both TWA and STEL** for compliance — TWA alone is insufficient. - [x] ACGIH publishes TLVs; NIOSH publishes IDLH values; NFPA publishes flammability limits. - [x] TWA formula: $\text{TWA} = \dfrac{\sum C_i t_i}{8}$ - [x] Mixture LFL formula: $\text{LFL}_{\text{mixture}} = \dfrac{1}{\sum y_i / \text{LFL}_i}$ - [x] **Conversion:** 1% v/v = **10,000 ppm** (always keep units consistent).

topics.title: Hazard Rating of Chemical Plants: Dow Index and Toxicity Index
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT723 - PROCESS SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Hazard Rating of Chemical Plants: Dow Index and Toxicity Index

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Dow Fire and Explosion Index (F&EI)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> The **Dow Fire and Explosion Index (F&EI)** is a quantitative hazard rating methodology developed by the Dow Chemical Company (first published in 1964, with subsequent editions through 1994) that objectively ranks the relative fire and explosion hazard potential of a process unit. It transforms complex loss-of-containment scenarios into a single numerical score derived from the **Material Factor** and two categories of process penalties.

The Dow F&EI is universally recognized as the *first* and most widely applied *quantitative* hazard index in the chemical process industry (CPI). It provides a **preliminary hazard evaluation** to:
- Identify the highest-risk process units in a plant
- Quantify the expected radius of exposure to fire/explosion damage
- Calculate the **Maximum Probable Property Damage (MPPD)** and **Business Interruption (BI)**
- Prioritize resources for detailed QRA (Quantitative Risk Assessment)

### 1.2 Dow Chemical Exposure Index (CEI) / Toxicity Index

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> The **Dow Chemical Exposure Index (CEI)**, often referred to as the **Toxicity Index (T&EI — Toxicity Exposure Index)**, was introduced by Dow in 1986 and revised in 1993. It provides a relative ranking of the acute toxic hazard from a release of a defined chemical substance, extending the philosophy of the F&EI to the airborne toxic exposure domain.

It answers the question: *"If a defined quantity of toxic chemical is released, what is the radius within which the airborne concentration could cause serious irreversible health effects or death?"*

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy (House Insurance Risk Scoring):**
> Imagine you call an insurance company to insure your house against fire. The insurer does not just look at *one* factor (e.g., whether you have a fireplace). Instead, they assign a **base score** based on the *materials* used in construction (wood vs. concrete — analogous to the **Material Factor**), and then add **multipliers** for:
> - General factors (the local fire-department response time, neighbourhood hydrant access — analogous to **General Process Hazards**)
> - Special factors (presence of a gas line, kerosene storage, old wiring — analogous to **Special Process Hazards**)
>
> The final score tells them how risky your house is. The Dow Index does the **exact same thing** for a chemical reactor: it starts with the inherent hazard of the chemical, then "loads" the score with operational and process-specific risk multipliers to give a single actionable number.

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Reference Metrics (Bolded for Quick Reference)

| Constant / Metric | Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Material Factor (MF)** | 1 to 40 | Inherent chemical hazard potential (NFPA-based) |
| **GPH Factor (F₂)** | ≥ 1.00 | General Process Hazard factor (minimum = 1.00) |
| **SPH Factor (F₃)** | ≥ 1.00 | Special Process Hazard factor (minimum = 1.00) |
| **F&EI Range (Light)** | 1 – 60 | "Light" hazard classification |
| **F&EI Range (Moderate)** | 61 – 96 | "Moderate" hazard classification |
| **F&EI Range (Heavy)** | 97 – 127 | "Heavy" hazard classification |
| **F&EI Range (Extreme)** | 128 – 158 | "Extreme" hazard classification |
| **F&EI Range (Very Extreme)** | > 158 | "Very Extreme / Severe" hazard classification |
| **ERPG-2 / TLV-TWA** | Reference toxicology limits | Used to define the "danger zone" radius |
| **Radius of Exposure (R)** | metres / feet | Distance within which ERPG-2 may be exceeded |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 Scheme examiner expects students to know the **exact F&EI classification bands** and be able to perform a complete step-by-step calculation. The 5-tier severity classification (Light → Very Extreme) is a *high-weightage* item in ESE.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why an "Index"?

The motivation for the Dow F&EI arose from the recognition that chemical plant risk is **multiplicative**, not additive. A small inventory of a benign chemical in a process unit may be far safer than a small inventory of a highly reactive substance, even if both are exposed to the *same* operational hazards. The Dow Index captures this through its **multiplicative structure** (F&EI = MF × GPH × SPH), ensuring that a low MF cannot be "rescued" by a low SPH and vice versa.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of F&EI Calculation

The calculation proceeds in **four logical stages**:

**Stage 1 — Identification of the Process Unit**
- Select the *smallest* unit (reactor, distillation column, storage tank, etc.) that represents a meaningful process unit. Selection is **not arbitrary**: a unit is usually chosen such that an inventory break (e.g., a shutoff valve) can isolate it.

**Stage 2 — Determination of Material Factor (MF)**
- The MF is the **only term in the F&EI** that is purely a chemical property. It is derived from the **NFPA 704 hazard ranking** for flammability (N_F) and reactivity (N_R) as:
  $$\text{MF} = \frac{N_F \times N_R}{3} \quad \text{(rounded, with adjustment)}$$
- For hydrocarbons, MF is typically 16 (e.g., acetone = 16, gasoline = 16, propylene = 21). For hydrogen, MF = 24. For inert materials, MF = 1.
- **Special cases** (oxidizers, water-reactives, etc.) are assigned MFs that override the NFPA-based calculation.

**Stage 3 — Calculation of General Process Hazards (GPH)**
- GPH captures the **generic, unit-independent** risk contributors. There are **6 categories**:
  1. Exothermic chemical reactions
  2. Endothermic processes
  3. Material handling and transfer
  4. Enclosed or indoor process units
  5. Access
  6. Drainage and spill control
- Each category carries a *penalty value* (e.g., 0 to 1.50). The GPH Factor is:
  $$F_2 = 1.0 + \sum_{i=1}^{6} \text{GPH}_i \quad \text{with the constraint} \quad F_2 \geq 1.0$$

**Stage 4 — Calculation of Special Process Hazards (SPH)**
- SPH captures the **unit-specific, operational** risk contributors. There are **12 categories**:
  1. Toxic materials
  2. Sub-atmospheric pressure
  3. Operation in or near flammable range
  4. Dust explosion
  5. Relief pressure
  6. Low temperature
  7. Quantity of flammable / unstable material
  8. Corrosion and erosion
  9. Leakage — joints and packing
  10. Use of fired heaters
  11. Hot oil heat exchange system
  12. Rotating equipment
- The SPH Factor is:
  $$F_3 = 1.0 + \sum_{i=1}^{12} \text{SPH}_i \quad \text{with the constraint} \quad F_3 \geq 1.0$$

**Stage 5 — Final F&EI Calculation**
- Combine multiplicatively to obtain the index:
  $$\text{F\&EI} = F_1 \times F_2 \times F_3 \quad \text{where} \quad F_1 = \text{MF}$$

### 2.3 Logic of the Toxicity Index (CEI / T&EI)

The Toxicity Index extends the F&EI philosophy to **acute airborne toxic release**. Key differences:

- The MF is **replaced or supplemented by a Toxicity Penalty (T_P)** based on ERPG-2 / TLV-TWA values.
- A **Release Scenario** (e.g., 10-minute release of total inventory, or catastrophic rupture) must be defined.
- The output is the **Radius of Exposure (R)** in metres, beyond which airborne concentration falls below the toxicological threshold.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The radius R is used in *land-use planning*, *plant layout* (separation distances), *emergency response planning*, and *toxic gas dispersion modelling*. The Toxicity Index is the precursor to modern ALOHA / PHAST software dispersion calculations.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula / Definition | Typical Range | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Material Factor | $F_1 = \text{MF}$ | NFPA-derived, $N_F \times N_R / 3$ | 1 – 40 | dimensionless |
| 2 | GPH Factor | $F_2$ | $F_2 = 1.0 + \sum \text{GPH}_i$, $F_2 \geq 1.0$ | 1.00 – 8.00 | dimensionless |
| 3 | SPH Factor | $F_3$ | $F_3 = 1.0 + \sum \text{SPH}_i$, $F_3 \geq 1.0$ | 1.00 – 8.00 | dimensionless |
| 4 | F&EI | F&EI | $\text{F\&EI} = F_1 \times F_2 \times F_3$ | 1 – >400 | dimensionless |
| 5 | Toxicity Penalty | $T_P$ | Based on ERPG-2 / TLV-TWA | 0 – 1.50 | dimensionless |
| 6 | Toxicity Index (T&EI) | T&EI | $\text{T\&EI} = F_1 \times F_2 \times (F_3 + T_P)$ | 1 – >500 | dimensionless |
| 7 | Radius of Exposure | $R$ | $R = \sqrt{(V / C_T) \cdot K}$ (simplified) | 10 – 10,000 | m or ft |
| 8 | Damage Factor | DF | Empirically fitted to F&EI | 0 – 1.00 | dimensionless |
| 9 | MPPD | $\text{MPPD}$ | $\text{MPPD} = \text{Replacement Cost} \times \text{DF}$ | currency | ₹ or \$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** Students frequently compute the F&EI by *adding* MF + GPH + SPH. This is **wrong** — the structure is **multiplicative**, not additive. The F&EI scales *non-linearly* with operational hazards, which is precisely the point of the index.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example — F&EI Calculation for an Acetone Storage Tank

**Problem Statement (KTU-Style):**
> A process unit consists of an atmospheric storage tank containing **50,000 kg of acetone** at ambient temperature. The tank is outdoors, in a *diked* area, with a *fixed water spray* deluge system and *foam* pourers. The unit is accessed via a single road. Acetone is a *highly flammable* liquid with NFPA ratings $N_F = 3$, $N_R = 0$, and is mildly toxic by inhalation. Calculate the Dow F&EI and classify the hazard. (Use the standard penalty table given below.)

**Step 1 — Material Factor (MF) for Acetone**

Acetone is a flammable liquid with $N_F = 3$ and $N_R = 0$. The standard lookup assigns **MF = 16** for acetone.

$$F_1 = \text{MF} = 16$$

**Step 2 — General Process Hazards (GPH)**

We apply the 6 GPH categories with the following penalty values:

| # | GPH Category | Penalty Selected | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Exothermic chemical reactions | 0.00 | Storage only; no reaction |
| 2 | Endothermic processes | 0.00 | Not applicable |
| 3 | Material handling and transfer | 0.50 | Loading/unloading via pump |
| 4 | Enclosed or indoor process units | 0.00 | Tank is *outdoor* |
| 5 | Access | 0.20 | Single road only |
| 6 | Drainage and spill control | 0.50 | Diked bund present |
| | **Sum of GPH penalties** | **1.20** | |

Apply the GPH factor formula:

$$F_2 = 1.0 + \sum \text{GPH}_i = 1.0 + 1.20 = 2.20$$

Since $F_2 = 2.20 \geq 1.0$, the lower bound is satisfied.

**Step 3 — Special Process Hazards (SPH)**

We apply the 12 SPH categories with the following penalty values:

| # | SPH Category | Penalty Selected | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Toxic materials | 0.20 | Mildly toxic, but stored at low T |
| 2 | Sub-atmospheric pressure | 0.00 | Atmospheric storage |
| 3 | Operation in or near flammable range | 0.50 | Acetone is *above* flash point in summer |
| 4 | Dust explosion | 0.00 | Liquid, not dust |
| 5 | Relief pressure | 0.20 | Standard conservation vent |
| 6 | Low temperature | 0.00 | Ambient |
| 7 | Quantity of flammable material | 0.60 | 50,000 kg of flammable liquid |
| 8 | Corrosion and erosion | 0.10 | Mild (mild steel tank) |
| 9 | Leakage — joints and packing | 0.10 | Flanged joints typical |
| 10 | Use of fired heaters | 0.00 | None |
| 11 | Hot oil heat exchange system | 0.00 | Not used |
| 12 | Rotating equipment | 0.30 | Transfer pump present |
| | **Sum of SPH penalties** | **2.00** | |

Apply the SPH factor formula:

$$F_3 = 1.0 + \sum \text{SPH}_i = 1.0 + 2.00 = 3.00$$

Since $F_3 = 3.00 \geq 1.0$, the lower bound is satisfied.

**Step 4 — Final F&EI Calculation**

$$\text{F\&EI} = F_1 \times F_2 \times F_3 = 16 \times 2.20 \times 3.00$$

$$\text{F\&EI} = 16 \times 6.60 = 105.6$$

**Step 5 — Hazard Classification**

Since F&EI = 105.6 lies in the band **97 – 127**, the unit is classified as **"HEAVY"** hazard.

**Step 6 — Radius of Exposure (for MPPD) — Standard Dow Look-up**

For F&EI ≈ 105, the **radius of exposure** is approximately **21.6 m (70 ft)** from the unit's centroid.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Quick-Reference F&EI Bands

| F&EI Band | Severity | Industry Action Required |
|---|---|---|
| 1 – 60 | **Light** | Routine safety; standard SOPs sufficient |
| 61 – 96 | **Moderate** | Active engineering controls; HAZOP recommended |
| 97 – 127 | **Heavy** | Formal QRA, LOPA, and dedicated safety systems required |
| 128 – 158 | **Extreme** | Redundant safety systems; quantitative risk management |
| > 158 | **Very Extreme** | Plant redesign or relocation strongly considered |

### 3.3 Worked Example — Toxicity Index (CEI) for a Chlorine Vessel

**Problem Statement:**
> A 1,000 kg chlorine storage vessel ruptures catastrophically. Chlorine has NFPA toxicity $N_H = 3$. The relevant ERPG-2 threshold is **3 ppm**. The release forms a denser-than-air cloud. Estimate the **Radius of Exposure**.

**Step 1 — Material Factor for Chlorine**

Chlorine is highly reactive and toxic. Standard MF = 24 (compressed, toxic oxidizer).

$$F_1 = 24$$

**Step 2 — Toxicity Penalty (T_P)**

Based on ERPG-2 = 3 ppm, the lookup assigns $T_P = 1.50$ (highest toxic penalty).

$$T_P = 1.50$$

**Step 3 — GPH and SPH (assume same as acetone case for illustration)**

$$F_2 = 2.20, \quad F_3 = 3.00$$

**Step 4 — Toxicity Index (T&EI)**

$$\text{T\&EI} = F_1 \times F_2 \times (F_3 + T_P)$$

$$\text{T\&EI} = 24 \times 2.20 \times (3.00 + 1.50)$$

$$\text{T\&EI} = 24 \times 2.20 \times 4.50$$

$$\text{T\&EI} = 237.6$$

**Step 5 — Radius of Exposure**

For T&EI > 200, the radius of exposure is **in the kilometre range** (1 – 5 km) depending on release conditions, justifying *off-site* emergency planning.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** A T&EI of 237.6 indicates a **catastrophic off-site consequence** potential. This single number justifies evacuation planning, scrubber installation, and double-walled containment — i.e., the index *drives engineering decisions* directly.

### 3.4 Conversion Logic Summary (For Examiner Convenience)

| From | To | Conversion Logic |
|---|---|---|
| F&EI | Damage Factor | Empirical monotonic curve (Dow's tabulated DF table) |
| F&EI | Radius of Exposure | Empirical relation $R \approx f(\text{F\&EI})$ |
| Radius of Exposure | MPPD | $\text{MPPD} = \text{Plant Replacement Cost} \times \text{DF} \times \text{Damage Factor Curve}$ |
| Inventory (kg) | Mass Penalty | $P_{\text{mass}} = \log_{10}(\text{mass in lb}) - 1$ (typical) |
| ERPG-2 | Toxicity Penalty | Lookup table; smaller ERPG-2 → larger $T_P$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Dow F&EI Calculation Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Select Process Unit"] --> B["Step 1: Identify Inventory and Chemicals"]
    B --> C["Step 2: Determine Material Factor MF"]
    C --> D["Step 3: Evaluate 6 General Process Hazards"]
    D --> E["Compute GPH Factor F2 = 1.0 + sum GPH penalties"]
    E --> F["Step 4: Evaluate 12 Special Process Hazards"]
    F --> G["Compute SPH Factor F3 = 1.0 + sum SPH penalties"]
    G --> H["Step 5: Calculate F and EI = MF x F2 x F3"]
    H --> I["Step 6: Classify Hazard from F and EI Band"]
    I --> J["Step 7: Determine Radius of Exposure"]
    J --> K["Step 8: Calculate Damage Factor and MPPD"]
    K --> L["Step 9: Recommend Risk Mitigation Measures"]
    L --> M["End: Documented F and EI Score"]
    
    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style H fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style L fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Toxicity Index (CEI) Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["Process Unit Selection"] --> B1["Inventory and Release Scenario Definition"]
    B1 --> C1["Material Factor MF Lookup"]
    C1 --> D1["ERPG-2 or TLV-TWA Toxicity Reference"]
    D1 --> E1["Toxicity Penalty TP Determination"]
    E1 --> F1["GPH and SPH Penalty Evaluation"]
    F1 --> G1["Compute T and EI = MF x F2 x F3 + TP"]
    G1 --> H1["Compute Radius of Exposure R"]
    H1 --> I1["Emergency Planning Zone EPZ Delineation"]
    I1 --> J1["Off-Site Consequence Assessment"]
    
    style A1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D1 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style G1 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style I1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Comparison of F&EI and T&EI Inputs

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUTS["Common Inputs"]
        I1["Material Factor MF"]
        I2["General Process Hazards GPH"]
        I3["Special Process Hazards SPH"]
    end
    
    subgraph F_EI["Dow F and EI Branch"]
        F1["Fire Explosion Hazard Factor"]
        F2["Radius of Exposure fire"]
        F3["MPPD Calculation"]
    end
    
    subgraph T_EI["Toxicity Index Branch"]
        T1["Toxicity Penalty TP"]
        T2["ERPG-2 Lookup"]
        T3["Radius of Exposure toxic"]
        T4["EPZ Delineation"]
    end
    
    I1 --> F1
    I2 --> F1
    I3 --> F1
    F1 --> F2 --> F3
    
    I1 --> T1
    T2 --> T1
    I2 --> T1
    I3 --> T1
    T1 --> T3 --> T4
    
    style F1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style T1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Hazard Decision Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Computed F and EI Value"] --> B{"F and EI Range?"}
    B -->|1 to 60| C["LIGHT - Routine SOPs"]
    B -->|61 to 96| D["MODERATE - HAZOP Recommended"]
    B -->|97 to 127| E["HEAVY - QRA and LOPA Required"]
    B -->|128 to 158| F["EXTREME - Redundant Safety Systems"]
    B -->|greater than 158| G["VERY EXTREME - Plant Redesign"]
    
    style C fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#8B0000,color:#fff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*What is the Dow Fire and Explosion Index (F&EI) and how is the Material Factor (MF) determined?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The Dow Fire and Explosion Index (F&EI) is a quantitative hazard rating method developed by the Dow Chemical Company to rank the relative fire and explosion hazard potential of a process unit in a chemical plant. The Material Factor (MF) is a measure of the inherent energy release potential of a chemical and is determined from the NFPA 704 ratings for **flammability ($N_F$)** and **reactivity ($N_R$)**. The MF is typically computed as $MF = (N_F \times N_R)/3$ with appropriate rounding and special adjustments for oxidizers, water-reactives, and pyrophorics. It is the *only* purely chemical-property term in the F&EI and forms the base score upon which process-specific penalties are applied multiplicatively. (3 marks)

#### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*List the six categories of General Process Hazards (GPH) used in the Dow F&EI calculation.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The six categories of General Process Hazards are:
> 1. Exothermic chemical reactions
> 2. Endothermic processes
> 3. Material handling and transfer
> 4. Enclosed or indoor process units
> 5. Access (number and adequacy of access roads)
> 6. Drainage and spill control
>
> These categories capture the *generic* risk contributors that are not unique to a particular unit's design and are summed additively to compute the GPH Factor $F_2 = 1.0 + \sum \text{GPH}_i$, subject to $F_2 \geq 1.0$. (3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU ESE Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. The student attempts **one** of the two alternatives provided. Both alternatives below are independent, full questions.

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Set B]**
*An atmospheric storage tank in a petrochemical plant contains 80,000 kg of n-hexane at ambient conditions. The tank is enclosed in a diked bund, located *indoors* in a partially open shed, accessible via two roads, and equipped with transfer pumps. The NFPA ratings for n-hexane are $N_F = 3$ and $N_R = 0$. Use the penalty values given below.*

*Required penalties:*
- GPH penalties: Material handling = 0.50, Enclosed unit = 0.30, Access (2 roads) = 0.20, Drainage = 0.50, all others = 0.00
- SPH penalties: Toxicity = 0.20, Near flammable range = 0.50, Quantity of material = 0.85, Corrosion = 0.10, Joint leakage = 0.10, Rotating equipment = 0.30, Relief = 0.20, all others = 0.00

**(a)** Calculate the Material Factor, GPH Factor, SPH Factor, and the Dow F&EI. (7 marks)

**(b)** Classify the hazard severity, determine the approximate radius of exposure, and recommend **two engineering controls** to reduce the F&EI. (7 marks)

**Complete Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**(a) Calculation of F&EI (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Material Factor (MF) for n-hexane:**
For n-hexane with $N_F = 3$ and $N_R = 0$:
$$F_1 = \text{MF} = 16 \quad \text{(standard value for Class IB flammable liquid)}$$

**Step 2 — GPH Factor $F_2$:**
$$\sum \text{GPH}_i = 0.50 + 0.30 + 0.20 + 0.50 = 1.50$$
$$F_2 = 1.0 + 1.50 = 2.50$$

**Step 3 — SPH Factor $F_3$:**
$$\sum \text{SPH}_i = 0.20 + 0.50 + 0.85 + 0.10 + 0.10 + 0.30 + 0.20 = 2.25$$
$$F_3 = 1.0 + 2.25 = 3.25$$

**Step 4 — F&EI Calculation:**
$$\text{F\&EI} = F_1 \times F_2 \times F_3 = 16 \times 2.50 \times 3.25$$
$$\text{F\&EI} = 16 \times 8.125 = 130.0$$

**(b) Classification, Radius, and Controls (7 marks):**

**Step 5 — Hazard Classification:**
Since F&EI = 130.0 lies in the band **128 – 158**, the unit is classified as **"EXTREME"** hazard. (2 marks)

**Step 6 — Radius of Exposure (Dow lookup):**
For F&EI ≈ 130, the radius of exposure is approximately **26.4 m (≈ 87 ft)** from the unit centroid. (2 marks)

**Step 7 — Engineering Control Recommendations (2 of the following for 3 marks):**
1. **Convert the indoor storage to outdoor storage** to eliminate the "Enclosed unit" penalty (0.30), thereby reducing $F_2$ from 2.50 to 2.20, dropping the F&EI by approximately 12%.
2. **Install a fixed foam pourer and water deluge system** combined with improved **drainage** to mitigate the fire damage potential.
3. **Substitute n-hexane with a less flammable solvent** (where feasible) to reduce the Material Factor.
4. **Install vapor recovery and inert gas blanketing** on the tank to reduce the "Operation in or near flammable range" penalty.
5. **Use welded piping instead of flanged joints** to reduce the "Joint leakage" penalty to near zero.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Set A]**
*(a) Discuss the limitations of the Dow Fire and Explosion Index. How does the Dow Toxicity Index (Chemical Exposure Index) overcome these limitations in the context of chemical plant safety?* (7 marks)

*(b) A 1,500 kg storage vessel of **anhydrous ammonia** ruptures catastrophically. The relevant toxicological limit is **ERPG-2 = 150 ppm**. Ammonia has NFPA ratings $N_F = 1$, $N_R = 0$, and a Material Factor of 9. The process has GPH = 1.80 and SPH = 2.20. Calculate the **Dow Toxicity Index (T&EI)** and determine the **severity band** using an assumed Toxicity Penalty of $T_P = 1.25$.* (7 marks)

**Complete Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**(a) Limitations of Dow F&EI and how Toxicity Index overcomes them (7 marks):**

**Limitations of Dow F&EI (4 marks):**
1. **Fire/Explosion bias**: The F&EI was originally conceived for *fire* and *explosion* hazards and does not directly address the airborne *toxic* exposure problem. A unit with low MF but high toxicity (e.g., phosgene) may have a moderate F&EI yet pose an extreme off-site risk.
2. **Inventory Insensitivity**: The MF is a *property* of the chemical, not its *quantity*. Inventory penalties (in SPH) are logarithmic, and a small change in inventory may not significantly alter the F&EI.
3. **Static Snapshot**: The F&EI is a *point-in-time* evaluation; it does not explicitly model dynamic transient conditions (e.g., runaway reactions).
4. **Subjectivity in Penalties**: Although the penalty values are tabulated, their assignment involves engineering judgement, leading to variability between evaluators.
5. **No Frequency/Probability**: The F&EI estimates *consequence severity* but does not incorporate *release frequency* — i.e., it is purely a *severity* index, not a *risk* index.
6. **Outdated Empirical Curves**: The damage factor and radius-of-exposure curves were developed using 1960s–1980s incident data and may not be fully representative of modern process units.

**How the Toxicity Index Overcomes These (3 marks):**
- The **CEI (Chemical Exposure Index)** specifically addresses **acute toxic exposure**, providing a **radius of exposure** based on defined release scenarios and toxicological limits (ERPG-2). This directly informs **off-site consequence assessment** and **Emergency Planning Zone (EPZ)** delineation.
- The CEI uses a **Toxicity Penalty** (T_P) that integrates the inherent chemical toxicity (via NFPA Health rating and ERPG values) directly into the index, ensuring that highly toxic chemicals with low flammability (which F&EI would underweight) are still flagged as high risk.
- The CEI supports **land-use planning** and **regulatory compliance** (e.g., EPA RMP, OSHA PSM) by translating the index into actionable separation distances.

**(b) Toxicity Index Calculation (7 marks):**

**Given Data:**
- $F_1 = \text{MF} = 9$
- $F_2 = 1.80$
- $F_3 = 2.20$
- $T_P = 1.25$

**Formula:**
$$\text{T\&EI} = F_1 \times F_2 \times (F_3 + T_P)$$

**Step-by-Step Substitution:**
$$\text{T\&EI} = 9 \times 1.80 \times (2.20 + 1.25)$$

$$\text{T\&EI} = 9 \times 1.80 \times 3.45$$

**Step 1 — First multiplication:**
$$9 \times 1.80 = 16.20$$

**Step 2 — Second multiplication:**
$$16.20 \times 3.45 = 55.89$$

**Final Result:**
$$\text{T\&EI} = 55.89$$

**Severity Band Interpretation:**
A T&EI of 55.89 places the ammonia storage unit in the **"Moderate"** toxicity hazard band (typically 30 – 100), requiring:
- **On-site** toxic gas detection and alarms
- **Self-contained breathing apparatus (SCBA)** for emergency responders
- **Off-site** community notification under EPA RMP
- **Mitigation**: Install water scrubber, secondary containment, and pressure relief interlocks.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Formula Confusion**: Students often confuse $\text{F\&EI} = F_1 \times F_2 \times F_3$ with $\text{T\&EI} = F_1 \times F_2 \times (F_3 + T_P)$. The Toxicity Penalty is **added inside** the SPH bracket, **not** multiplied separately. **(Loses 1–2 marks)**
> 2. **Lower-Bound Constraint**: Forgetting to enforce $F_2 \geq 1.0$ and $F_3 \geq 1.0$ can lead to *underestimation* of the index. **(Loses 1 mark)**
> 3. **Severity Band Memorization**: Students frequently mis-remember the F&EI band boundaries. The correct bands are 1–60 (Light), 61–96 (Moderate), 97–127 (Heavy), 128–158 (Extreme), >158 (Very Extreme). **(Loses 1 mark)**
> 4. **Units of Radius**: Forgetting to mention the *unit* of radius (m or ft) and the *empirical* nature of the radius correlation. **(Loses 0.5–1 mark)**
> 5. **Confusing F&EI with T&EI**: Examiners explicitly distinguish the two; a single-number answer without the right index label is penalized. **(Loses 1 mark)**

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> **Important Things to Remember (Last-Minute KTU Revision):**

- **Dow F&EI** = Material Factor × GPH Factor × SPH Factor (all multiplicative, never additive).
- **Material Factor (MF)** is derived from **NFPA 704** flammability and reactivity ratings; it is the *only* chemical property term.
- **GPH** has **6** categories; **SPH** has **12** categories; both have minimum value **1.0**.
- **F&EI Severity Bands (5 tiers)**: 1–60 Light | 61–96 Moderate | 97–127 Heavy | 128–158 Extreme | >158 Very Extreme.
- **Toxicity Index (T&EI)** uses the same structure but **adds a Toxicity Penalty $T_P$** inside the SPH bracket: $\text{T\&EI} = F_1 \times F_2 \times (F_3 + T_P)$.
- **Toxicity Penalty** is based on **ERPG-2** or **TLV-TWA** values; smaller toxicological limits → larger $T_P$.
- **Radius of Exposure** is an *empirical* output of the F&EI/T&EI used for *plant layout* and *emergency planning*.
- **MPPD (Maximum Probable Property Damage)** uses the **Damage Factor (DF)** from the empirical F&EI-vs-DF curve.
- **Limitations of Dow F&EI**: fire/explosion bias, no probability, subjectivity in penalties, static snapshot.
- **Use of CEI/T&EI**: off-site consequence assessment, land-use planning, EPA RMP compliance, toxic gas dispersion planning.
- The Dow F&EI is the **precursor** to modern QRA, LOPA, and HAZOP methodologies and remains the *first-pass* index for any chemical process plant.
- For a 14-mark KTU question, expect a **2-page solution** with **explicit numerical substitution** and **clear interpretation** of the final F&EI value.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Emergency Planning: Onsite and Offsite
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT723 - PROCESS SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Emergency Planning: Onsite and Offsite

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Emergency Planning (EP)** is a systematic, documented, and proactive framework of pre-arranged actions, resources, and command structures designed to mitigate the consequences of process upsets, accidental releases of hazardous materials, fires, explosions, and natural disasters in chemical process industries. As per **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.38**, **EPA Risk Management Program (40 CFR Part 68)**, and **NDMA Guidelines (India, 2007)**, emergency planning is legally mandated and divided into two distinct domains:
>
> 1. **Onsite Emergency Plan (OEP)** — The internal, plant-level plan covering actions within the boundary fence (fence-to-fence), governed primarily by factory/site management.
> 2. **Offsite Emergency Plan (OEP-External)** — The external, community-level plan covering actions beyond the plant boundary, coordinated by district/local civil authorities.

The boundary between these two plans is typically the **plant perimeter wall / battery limit**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE] > **Think of Emergency Planning like the safety system of a commercial aircraft.**
>
> - The **Onsite Plan** is what happens *inside the cockpit and cabin* during turbulence — the crew takes immediate, hands-on control using cockpit instruments and cabin crew protocols.
> - The **Offsite Plan** is the **Air Traffic Control (ATC)** and **nearby airport emergency response teams** that activate if the aircraft declares an emergency — the plane lands at the nearest airport, fire trucks line the runway, hospitals are alerted.
>
> Both layers must work in harmony. A perfect cockpit response is useless if the destination airport isn't informed, and vice versa. In a refinery or chemical plant, the **Process Operator (Onsite)** and the **District Disaster Management Authority (Offsite)** play exactly these roles.

## 1.3 Physical Constants, Standards & Regulatory Metrics

| Standard | Authority | Scope |
|---|---|---|
| **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.38** | USA - OSHA | Emergency Action Plans |
| **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.39** | USA - OSHA | Fire Prevention Plans |
| **EPA 40 CFR Part 68** | USA - EPA | RMP - Worst Case & Alternative Case Scenarios |
| **OHSA / OHSAS 18001** | International | Emergency Preparedness & Response |
| **ISO 45001 (Cl. 8.2)** | International | Emergency Preparedness |
| **MFR (Manufacture, Storage & Import of Hazardous Chemical Rules), 1989 (India)** | Govt. of India | Onsite & Offsite Emergency Plans |
| **NDMA Guidelines (2007)** | National Disaster Management Authority, India | Chemical Disasters |
| **OISD-STD-130** | Oil Industry Safety Directorate | Cross-country pipelines and refineries |
| **NFPA 600 / NFPA 1561** | National Fire Protection Association | Industrial fire brigades |

## 1.4 Why This Topic is Exam-Critical

> [!IMPORTANT] > Emergency Planning is a **direct, high-weight, 14-mark favorite** in KTU ESE question papers for Process Safety Engineering. It is frequently paired with **HAZOP, QRA, and consequence analysis** because emergency plans are derived *from* the outcomes of QRA studies. Students commonly lose marks by:
> - Confusing Onsite vs. Offsite responsibilities
> - Forgetting to list the **three tiers** of emergency
> - Skipping the role of **Mutual Aid** networks
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Onsite Emergency Planning — Detailed Architecture

The **Onsite Emergency Plan (also called the Site Emergency Plan, SEP)** is prepared by the **plant occupier/management** and submitted to the **Chief Inspector of Factories** (under India's Factories Act, 1948) and the **District Authority**. Its sole objective is to **limit damage to life, property, and environment within the plant boundary**.

### 2.1.1 Hierarchical Command Structure

The Onsite plan operates under the **Incident Command System (ICS)** principles:

- **Site Main Controller (SMC)** — Senior-most person on-site (typically Plant Manager). Declares emergency and "All Clear."
- **Incident Controller (IC)** — Operations Head. Directs tactical actions at the incident scene.
- **Emergency Control Centre (ECC)** — The nerve center; typically located in a hardened, blast-resistant building away from process areas.
- **Functional Teams (Fire, Medical, Security, Communication, Evacuation)**.

### 2.1.2 Key Elements of the Onsite Emergency Plan

A KTU-compliant onsite plan must contain the following structural blocks:

1. **Hazard Identification & Risk Assessment Summary** (input from HAZOP, QRA).
2. **Emergency Organization & Chain of Command** (org chart with names, alternates, 24×7 contact numbers).
3. **Emergency Classification (Tier System)** — see Section 2.3.
4. **Communication Protocol** — Public Address (PA), Siren codes, Hotline phone, Mobile, Radio (VHF/UHF).
5. **Emergency Resources Inventory** — Fire trucks, foam tenders, ambulances, SCBA sets, spill kits.
6. **Evacuation Plan & Assembly Points** — Wind-direction dependent muster zones.
7. **Mutual Aid Agreements** — Neighbouring industries.
8. **Emergency Shutdown (ESD) Procedures** — Levels 1, 2, 3 shutdown logic.
9. **Medical & First Aid Plan** — Onsite occupational health centre, tie-up hospitals.
10. **Training & Mock Drill Schedule** — Frequency: minimum twice per year (per MFR 1989).
11. **Plan Updating & Review Cycle** — Annual or post-incident.

## 2.2 Offsite Emergency Planning — Detailed Architecture

The **Offsite Emergency Plan** is the responsibility of the **District Magistrate / District Disaster Management Authority (DDMA)** under the **Disaster Management Act, 2005 (India)** and **MFR 1989 Rule 14**.

### 2.2.1 Triggering Conditions

Offsite plan activates when:
- Release crosses the plant boundary fence.
- Toxic gas concentration at fence-line exceeds **AEGL-2 / IDLH** levels.
- Fire/explosion cannot be contained within plant despite mutual aid.
- Population evacuation is required.

### 2.2.2 Stakeholders in Offsite Plan

| Stakeholder | Role |
|---|---|
| **District Magistrate / DDMA** | Overall command |
| **District Emergency Operation Centre (DEOC)** | Coordination hub |
| **Fire & Emergency Services** | Off-site firefighting |
| **Police** | Crowd control, traffic, cordoning |
| **Health Department / Hospitals** | Mass casualty management |
| **CPCB / SPCB** | Environmental monitoring |
| **Indian Red Cross / NGOs** | Relief, shelter |
| **Armed Forces / NDRF** | Catastrophic-level response (Tier 3) |
| **Media Cell** | Public communication |

### 2.2.3 Key Contents of Offsite Plan

1. **Demographic profile** of the affected zone (population, schools, hospitals).
2. **Vulnerability mapping** using ALOHA / PHAST dispersion contours.
3. **Shelter-in-place vs. Evacuation decision matrix.**
4. **Mass notification system** (SMS, sirens, radio, TV).
5. **Decontamination corridors and assembly areas.**
6. **Transport plan for casualties** to empanelled hospitals.
7. **Recovery and rehabilitation strategy.**

## 2.3 The Three-Tier Emergency Classification (CRITICAL FOR KTU)

This is the most frequently tested framework. Every chemical process facility classifies emergencies into **three tiers** based on severity and escalation need:

| Tier | Name | Description | Authority to Declare | Offsite Activation? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Tier 1** | **Operational / Minor Emergency** | Confined to unit, manageable with onsite resources, no offsite impact. | Shift In-charge | No |
| **Tier 2** | **Site / Major Emergency** | Affects multiple units / significant property damage / minor offsite impact possible. | Plant Manager / Site Main Controller | Mutual Aid activated; District Authority on standby |
| **Tier 3** | **Community / Disaster Emergency** | Major offsite consequences; evacuation needed; resources overwhelmed. | District Magistrate / State Disaster Authority | YES — Full Offsite Plan + NDRF / Armed Forces |

## 2.4 Emergency Shutdown (ESD) Hierarchy

In a process plant, shutdown is graded to avoid unnecessary production loss:

- **ESD-0 (Local Shutdown)** — Operator pushes local push-button at the unit.
- **ESD-1 (Unit Shutdown)** — Isolates a single process unit (e.g., one reactor train).
- **ESD-2 (Plant Shutdown)** — Entire plant shutdown except utilities.
- **ESD-3 (Total Site Shutdown)** — Including utilities; "Dead Man Switch" — everything off, including steam, power, instrument air.

> [!NOTE] > The **Dead Man Switch / ESD-3** is a fail-safe device that automatically initiates shutdown if the operator becomes incapacitated — found on offshore platforms and high-hazard plants.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT] > The following equations are the standard mathematical models used during **Emergency Planning** for consequence assessment and zone demarcation.

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C = \dfrac{Q}{u \cdot H^2}$ | Pasquill-Gifford dispersion for continuous release — ground-level concentration (Gaussian) | $\text{mg/m}^3$ |
| 2 | $ERPG_{AEGL}$ | Emergency Response Planning Guidelines / Acute Exposure Guideline Levels used for offsite zone setting | ppm |
| 3 | $t_{evac} = \dfrac{D}{v_{crowd}}$ | Minimum evacuation time = distance / walking speed of crowd (typical $v_{crowd} = 1.2 \text{ m/s}$) | seconds |
| 4 | $A_{zone} = \pi R_{toxic}^2$ | Area of toxic impact zone for shelter-in-place planning | $\text{m}^2$ |
| 5 | $\dot{m}_{release} = C_d A \sqrt{2 \rho \Delta P}$ | Mass release rate through a hole (used to size Tier escalation) | kg/s |
| 6 | $t_{90} = \dfrac{V}{Q_{vent}}$ | Time for concentration to reach 90 % of final value in a confined space | s |
| 7 | $IDLH$ | Immediately Dangerous to Life or Health concentration (NIOSH) | ppm |
| 8 | $R = k \cdot \dot{m}^{0.4}$ | TNT-equivalent blast radius scaling | m |

> **KTU Pitfall Avoidance:** When the question asks for **offsite zone planning**, never quote ERPG values without specifying the time-window (e.g., **ERPG-3 = 60-min ceiling concentration**). Examiners deduct marks if you omit the duration.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

In real engineering practice, Emergency Planning is the **bridge between Safety Report and Real-World Action**. A perfectly executed HAZOP and QRA means nothing if the findings are not translated into actionable emergency procedures, trained personnel, and tested drills. Companies like **Reliance Industries (Jamnagar)**, **ONGC**, and **Indian Oil** maintain dedicated **Onsite Emergency Operation Centers (OEOC)** running 24×7 with real-time CCTV, gas detectors, and weather stations feeding live dispersion models.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Sizing the Toxic Emergency Planning Zone (Gaussian Plume)

For a **continuous ground-level release** of a toxic gas (e.g., chlorine, ammonia) from a process unit, the offsite evacuation zone must be drawn based on the **IDLH** or **AEGL-2** concentration contour. The standard **Pasquill-Gifford Gaussian Plume** model is used.

$$C(x, y, 0) = \dfrac{Q}{2\pi \cdot u \cdot \sigma_y \cdot \sigma_z} \cdot \exp\left(-\dfrac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2}\right) \cdot \exp\left(-\dfrac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$$

Where:
- $C(x,y,0)$ — ground-level concentration at point $(x, y)$ $\left[\text{mg/m}^3\right]$
- $Q$ — emission rate $\left[\text{mg/s}\right]$
- $u$ — wind speed at release height $\left[\text{m/s}\right]$
- $\sigma_y, \sigma_z$ — horizontal and vertical dispersion coefficients (functions of downwind distance $x$ and stability class)
- $H$ — effective release height $\left[\text{m}\right]$

**Simplification for worst-case ground-level release ($H = 0$, centerline $y = 0$):**

$$C(x, 0, 0) = \dfrac{Q}{\pi \cdot u \cdot \sigma_y \cdot \sigma_z}$$

**Step-by-step derivation to find downwind distance $x$ for IDLH threshold:**

**Step 1:** Set the desired ground concentration equal to the IDLH (or AEGL-2) threshold, $C_{threshold}$.

$$C_{threshold} = \dfrac{Q}{\pi \cdot u \cdot \sigma_y(x) \cdot \sigma_z(x)}$$

**Step 2:** Express dispersion coefficients using the Pasquill power-law correlations:

$$\sigma_y = a \cdot x^{b}, \quad \sigma_z = c \cdot x^{d}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute and solve for $x$:

$$\pi \cdot u \cdot C_{threshold} \cdot a \cdot c \cdot x^{b+d} = Q$$

$$x^{b+d} = \dfrac{Q}{\pi \cdot u \cdot C_{threshold} \cdot a \cdot c}$$

**Step 4:** Final closed-form equation for emergency planning distance:

$$x = \left(\dfrac{Q}{\pi \cdot u \cdot C_{threshold} \cdot a \cdot c}\right)^{\frac{1}{b+d}}$$

This **$x$** is the **downwind distance** used by the **Site Main Controller** to decide whether to shelter-in-place or evacuate downwind communities. It is also the radius used to define the **Initial Isolation Zone** and **Protective Action Zone (PAZ)** under the **Emergency Response Guidebook (ERG)** of the US Department of Transportation.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Evacuation Time Estimation (Macroscopic Model)

For KTU problems on population evacuation modeling:

$$t_{evac} = t_{detect} + t_{alarm} + t_{decision} + t_{mobilize} + \dfrac{L_{road}}{v_{vehicle}}$$

Where:
- $t_{detect}$ — time between release and detection (sensor response time) [s]
- $t_{alarm}$ — time to disseminate public alarm [s]
- $t_{decision}$ — time for DDMA to issue evacuation order [s]
- $t_{mobilize}$ — time for population to begin movement [s]
- $L_{road}$ — total road network length to be traversed [m]
- $v_{vehicle}$ — average vehicle speed under evacuation (typically $30\text{ km/h}$)

**Numerical Example (for KTU clarity):**

A chlorine release occurs at 02:00. Compute evacuation time for a village $5 \text{ km}$ downwind.

$$t_{detect} = 60 \text{ s (sensor lag)}, \quad t_{alarm} = 120 \text{ s (PA system)}$$

$$t_{decision} = 300 \text{ s (DDMA approval)}, \quad t_{mobilize} = 600 \text{ s (nighttime delay)}$$

$$L_{road} = 5000 \text{ m}, \quad v_{vehicle} = 30 \text{ km/h} = 8.33 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 1:** Compute driving time:

$$\dfrac{L_{road}}{v_{vehicle}} = \dfrac{5000}{8.33} = 600.24 \text{ s}$$

**Step 2:** Sum all components:

$$t_{evac} = 60 + 120 + 300 + 600 + 600.24 = 1680.24 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3:** Convert to minutes:

$$t_{evac} = \dfrac{1680.24}{60} \approx 28.0 \text{ min}$$

> [!IMPORTANT] > If **toxic travel time** (time taken by cloud to reach the village) is **less than 28 minutes**, the offsite plan is **failsafe**. If it is more, the village should be on **shelter-in-place** with sealed rooms and HVAC shut down.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Categorization of Hazardous Inventory for Tier Classification

Many KTU problems ask: *"Given an inventory, classify the emergency tier."* Use:

$$TNT_{eq} = \dfrac{m_{explosive} \cdot \Delta H_{comb}}{E_{TNT}}$$

Where:
- $TNT_{eq}$ — mass of TNT equivalent [kg]
- $m_{explosive}$ — mass of released flammable material [kg]
- $\Delta H_{comb}$ — heat of combustion [kJ/kg]
- $E_{TNT}$ — heat of explosion of TNT = **4680 kJ/kg**

**Threshold criteria (industry standard):**
- $TNT_{eq} < 1000 \text{ kg}$ → **Tier 1**
- $1000 \leq TNT_{eq} < 10000 \text{ kg}$ → **Tier 2**
- $TNT_{eq} \geq 10000 \text{ kg}$ → **Tier 3**

**Numerical Example:**

Compute the TNT equivalent for **5000 kg propane release**:

$$TNT_{eq} = \dfrac{5000 \times 46,000}{4680} = \dfrac{2.3 \times 10^{8}}{4680}$$

$$TNT_{eq} = 49,145.30 \text{ kg} \approx 49.15 \text{ tonnes of TNT}$$

Since $TNT_{eq} \gg 10000 \text{ kg}$, this is unambiguously a **Tier 3 (Community Emergency)**.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix: Onsite vs. Offsite Responsibilities

| Function | Onsite Plan | Offsite Plan |
|---|---|---|
| **Prepared by** | Plant Occupier / Management | District Magistrate / DDMA |
| **Legal basis** | Factories Act 1948, MFR 1989 Rule 13 | DM Act 2005, MFR 1989 Rule 14 |
| **Geographic scope** | Inside battery limit | Outside battery limit |
| **Primary objective** | Limit damage to personnel, equipment, environment within fence | Protect public, environment, infrastructure beyond fence |
| **Authority hierarchy** | SMC → IC → Functional Teams | District Magistrate → DEOC → Multi-agency task force |
| **Communication tool** | PA System, Sirens, Mobile, Radio | Mass SMS, Loudspeakers, TV/Radio broadcast |
| **Resource pool** | Plant fire service, Occupational Health Centre, SCBA | Fire stations, Civil Hospitals, NDRF, Armed Forces |
| **Drill frequency** | Min. twice/year (MFR 1989) | Annually (Table-Top + Mock) |
| **Documentation** | Emergency Action Plan (EAP) | District Offsite Emergency Plan (DOEP) |

## 3.5 Step-by-Step Logic for Emergency Drill Design (for theory questions)

1. **Define Drill Objectives** — Test communication, response time, evacuation efficiency.
2. **Select Scenario** — Worst-case from QRA (e.g., toxic gas release in night shift).
3. **Notify Without Pre-warning** — Ensures realistic response.
4. **Activate ECC** — Time-stamp from alarm to ECC manning.
5. **Deploy Teams** — Fire, Medical, Evacuation — record mobilization time.
6. **Simulate Casualties** — Use moulage (mock injuries).
7. **Conduct Headcount** — Compare with muster zone register.
8. **Hot Debrief** — Within 24 hours.
9. **Cold Review & CAPA** — Corrective and Preventive Actions, file in **Document of Recognized Practices (DORP)**.
10. **Update Plan** — Incorporate lessons learned.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]`

**Q: Differentiate between Onsite and Offsite Emergency Plans.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Onsite Emergency Plan | Offsite Emergency Plan |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Within plant boundary | Beyond plant boundary |
| **Prepared by** | Plant management | District Collector / DDMA |
| **Trigger** | Internal incident | Release crosses fence-line |
| **Resources** | Plant's internal teams | State / national agencies |
| **Legal basis** | Factories Act, MFR Rule 13 | DM Act 2005, MFR Rule 14 |

*(Full marks require: 2 marks for the table + 1 mark for quoting the legal basis.)*

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: List the three tiers of emergency classification with one example scenario for each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **Tier 1 — Operational Minor Emergency** — Example: Small flange leak within a unit, contained by operators with spill kit.
2. **Tier 2 — Site Major Emergency** — Example: Pump seal failure leading to a localized pool fire affecting adjacent units; Mutual Aid activated.
3. **Tier 3 — Community Disaster Emergency** — Example: Major ammonia cloud release crossing the fence-line, requiring village evacuation; NDRF deployed.

*(1 mark per tier, partial marks for examples.)*

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Many students write only the *definition* of Onsite/Offsite without explaining the **command hierarchy** (Deducts 3 marks).
> - Listing the tiers without giving the **decision authority** for declaration loses 2 marks.
> - Failing to quote **Indian regulatory framework (MFR 1989 / DM Act 2005)** loses 2 marks.
> - In numerical problems, forgetting to **convert units (km/h to m/s)** results in -1 mark.

---

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]` — Internal Choice Option A

#### **Q: (a) Explain in detail the components of an Onsite Emergency Plan. (7 marks)**
#### **Q: (b) Describe the structure of an Offsite Emergency Plan as per MFR 1989 Rule 14. (7 marks)**

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — Internal Choice Option B

#### **Q: (a) With a neat flowchart, explain the three-tier emergency classification system. Discuss the decision authority at each tier. (7 marks)**
#### **Q: (b) A 5000 kg propane cylinder catastrophically ruptures. Compute the TNT equivalent and classify the emergency tier. Assume $\Delta H_{comb} = 46,000$ kJ/kg and $E_{TNT} = 4680$ kJ/kg. (7 marks)**

---

### SOLUTION FOR QUESTION A

#### (a) Components of an Onsite Emergency Plan (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

An Onsite Emergency Plan is a comprehensive document that ensures rapid, organized response to incidents within the plant battery limit. Its key components are:

1. **Hazard Inventory and Risk Assessment Summary (1 mark)** — A consolidated summary of HAZOP, QRA, and past incident findings that identifies credible scenarios.

2. **Emergency Organization Chart (1 mark)** — Defines the Site Main Controller (SMC), Incident Controller (IC), and functional team leaders (Fire, Medical, Communication, Security, Evacuation) with deputies for 24×7 coverage.

3. **Emergency Classification Protocol (1 mark)** — Specifies the three-tier system (Tier 1 / 2 / 3), the trigger conditions, and the authority to declare each.

4. **Communication Plan (1 mark)** — Siren codes (e.g., one long blast for fire, two short for toxic), PA system, hotlines, mobile, and radio fallback in case primary systems fail.

5. **Emergency Shutdown (ESD) Procedures (1 mark)** — Tiered shutdown logic from ESD-1 to ESD-3 (Dead Man Switch), with isolation of hydrocarbon inventory.

6. **Resource Inventory (1 mark)** — Foam stocks, SCBA sets, ambulances, fire tenders, spill kits, decontamination showers.

7. **Training, Drills and Plan Review (1 mark)** — Twice-yearly mock drills, induction training for all employees, contractor training, and annual plan revision.

#### (b) Offsite Emergency Plan as per MFR 1989 Rule 14 (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**MFR 1989 Rule 14 mandates** that the occupier of an industrial activity, in consultation with the District Emergency Authority, prepare an Offsite Emergency Plan for any activity handling hazardous chemicals above threshold quantities.

The Offsite Plan must contain the following:

1. **Identification of Major Hazard Installations (MHI)** (1 mark) — A list of nearby factories handling hazardous chemicals.

2. **Population Data within Vulnerability Zones** (1 mark) — Census details, schools, hospitals, vulnerable groups (elderly, disabled) within the dispersion contours of the worst-case scenarios.

3. **Communication and Warning Systems** (1 mark) — Mass notification through sirens, SMS gateways, FM radio broadcasts, and digital display boards.

4. **Emergency Response Coordination Structure** (1 mark) — District Collector leads; assisted by DEOC; integrates Police, Fire, Health, SPCB, Army/NDRF.

5. **Evacuation Plan and Shelter-In-Place Decision Matrix** (1 mark) — Specifies when to evacuate vs. shelter based on toxic travel time.

6. **Medical and Casualty Management** (1 mark) — Empanelled hospitals, mass casualty triage protocols, ambulance staging areas.

7. **Training, Mock Drills and Plan Audit** (1 mark) — Annual table-top and field exercises; review by State Crisis Group.

---

### SOLUTION FOR QUESTION B

#### (a) Three-Tier Emergency Classification Flowchart (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

The three-tier emergency classification is a structured escalation model used universally in process industries.

**Flowchart (3 marks):**

```
       [Incident Detected at Unit]
                  |
                  v
        [First-Line Response by Operator]
                  |
        +---------+----------+
        |                    |
   [Contained]          [Not Contained]
        |                    |
        v                    v
    [Tier 1]        [Shift In-charge Triggers]
    [Site In-Charge     Tier 2 - SMC declares]
    Standdown]               |
                             v
                  [Mutual Aid Activated]
                             |
                  [Offsite Impact Detected?]
                  /                       \
               [No]                      [Yes]
                |                          |
                v                          v
         [Stand Down]            [District Magistrate Declares]
                                       Tier 3
                                          |
                                          v
                              [NDRF, Armed Forces, Hospitals Activated]
```

**Decision Authority (4 marks):**

| Tier | Authority |
|---|---|
| Tier 1 | Shift In-charge / Unit Engineer |
| Tier 2 | Site Main Controller (Plant Manager) |
| Tier 3 | District Magistrate / State Crisis Group |

#### (b) Numerical Solution — TNT Equivalent and Tier Classification (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Identify given values.
$$m_{propane} = 5000 \text{ kg}, \quad \Delta H_{comb} = 46{,}000 \text{ kJ/kg}, \quad E_{TNT} = 4680 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Compute total combustion energy released.
$$E_{total} = m \cdot \Delta H_{comb} = 5000 \times 46{,}000 = 2.3 \times 10^{8} \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute TNT equivalent.
$$TNT_{eq} = \dfrac{E_{total}}{E_{TNT}} = \dfrac{2.3 \times 10^{8}}{4680} = 49{,}145.30 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Convert to tonnes for clarity.
$$TNT_{eq} \approx 49.15 \text{ tonnes of TNT}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Apply threshold criterion.
Since $TNT_{eq} \gg 10{,}000 \text{ kg}$, this exceeds the Tier 3 threshold by a factor of nearly 5.

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Conclusion.
This is unambiguously a **Tier 3 — Community Disaster Emergency**. The District Magistrate must declare the emergency, activate the Offsite Plan, deploy NDRF, and initiate evacuation of all downwind villages.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Emergency Planning: Onsite and Offsite**
>
> - **Onsite Plan = Within battery limit; Prepared by plant; Legal basis: Factories Act + MFR Rule 13.**
> - **Offsite Plan = Beyond battery limit; Prepared by District Authority; Legal basis: DM Act 2005 + MFR Rule 14.**
> - **Three Tiers: Tier 1 (Operational) → Tier 2 (Site) → Tier 3 (Community).**
> - **Tier Authority: Shift In-charge → SMC → District Magistrate.**
> - **ESD Hierarchy: ESD-0 (local) → ESD-1 (unit) → ESD-2 (plant) → ESD-3 (Dead Man Switch).**
> - **Drill Frequency: Minimum twice per year (MFR 1989).**
> - **Evacuation Zone Radius is set using AEGL-2 / IDLH contours from Gaussian Plume.**
> - **Mutual Aid = Pre-arranged agreement with neighbouring industries to share resources during Tier 2.**
> - **NDRF = National Disaster Response Force — central government body for Tier 3 catastrophic response.**
> - **TNT equivalent formula = $TNT_{eq} = \dfrac{m \cdot \Delta H_{comb}}{E_{TNT}}$ where $E_{TNT} = 4680$ kJ/kg.**
> - **Plan must be a living document — updated annually or after any incident.**
> - **Always quote Indian statutes (Factories Act 1948, MFR 1989, DM Act 2005, NDMA Guidelines 2007) in answers for full KTU marks.**

> **End of Module 3 Topic Notes — Emergency Planning: Onsite and Offsite**
