# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides exhaustive derivations of the three most exam-relevant calculations in KTU Module 4: **Boolean Algebra for FTA**, **RPN Matrix for FMEA**, and **ETA Probability Cascading**.

## 3.1 Derivation: Boolean Algebra → Minimal Cut Sets (FTA)

### Problem Setup

Consider a fault tree where the Top Event $T$ is generated through the following Boolean structure:
- Intermediate Event $A = X_1 \text{ AND } X_2$ (AND gate combining basic events $X_1, X_2$).
- Intermediate Event $B = X_3 \text{ OR } X_4$ (OR gate combining basic events $X_3, X_4$).
- Top Event $T = A \text{ OR } B$ (OR gate combining $A$ and $B$).

### Step 1: Express the Top Event in Boolean Form

We begin by translating the gate structure into Boolean algebra. An **AND** gate corresponds to logical intersection ($\cap$), and an **OR** gate corresponds to logical union ($\cup$). We substitute each intermediate event's definition into the top-level expression.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= A \cup B \\
&= (X_1 \cap X_2) \cup (X_3 \cup X_4) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Apply the Distributive Law

The distributive law of Boolean algebra states that $P \cap (Q \cup R) = (P \cap Q) \cup (P \cap R)$. We distribute $X_1$ and $X_2$ across the union $(X_3 \cup X_4)$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= (X_1 \cap X_2 \cap X_3) \cup (X_1 \cap X_2 \cap X_4) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Explanation:** The union operation is idempotent ($X_3 \cup X_4 = X_4 \cup X_3$), but no further simplification is possible since the conjunction with $X_1$ and $X_2$ is mandatory for $T$ to occur.

### Step 3: Identify Minimal Cut Sets

Each parenthetical product term is a **minimal cut set** — the smallest combination of basic events that must all occur simultaneously to trigger $T$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MCS}_1 &= \{X_1, X_2, X_3\} \\
\text{MCS}_2 &= \{X_1, X_2, X_4\} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Quantify Top Event Probability (Rare Event Approximation)

Let the basic event probabilities be $q_1 = 0.01$, $q_2 = 0.02$, $q_3 = 0.005$, $q_4 = 0.008$. Since all values are very small ($\ll 1$), the rare event approximation applies.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(T) &\approx P(\text{MCS}_1) + P(\text{MCS}_2) \\
&= (q_1 \cdot q_2 \cdot q_3) + (q_1 \cdot q_2 \cdot q_4) \\
&= (0.01)(0.02)(0.005) + (0.01)(0.02)(0.008) \\
&= (1.0 \times 10^{-6}) + (1.6 \times 10^{-6}) \\
&= 2.6 \times 10^{-6} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Compare with Exact Inclusion-Exclusion

For verification, the exact calculation subtracts the overlap:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(T)_{\text{exact}} &= P(\text{MCS}_1) + P(\text{MCS}_2) - P(\text{MCS}_1 \cap \text{MCS}_2) \\
&= (1.0 \times 10^{-6}) + (1.6 \times 10^{-6}) - P(X_1 \cap X_2 \cap X_3 \cap X_4) \\
&= 2.6 \times 10^{-6} - (0.01)(0.02)(0.005)(0.008) \\
&= 2.6 \times 10^{-6} - 8.0 \times 10^{-10} \\
&\approx 2.6 \times 10^{-6} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Takeaway:** When basic event probabilities are below $\sim 0.1$, the rare event approximation is **accurate to better than 0.01%** — making it the standard tool in quantitative risk assessment.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: FMEA RPN Calculation with Severity Override

### Problem Setup

A pump in a benzene service has the following failure modes and severity parameters documented:

| Failure Mode | S | O | D |
|--------------|---|---|---|
| Seal leak | 7 | 5 | 4 |
| Bearing failure | 8 | 3 | 6 |
| Coupling break | 9 | 2 | 5 |
| Casing crack | 10 | 1 | 7 |

### Step 1: Compute RPN for Each Failure Mode

The Risk Priority Number is the product of three independent ratings.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RPN}_{\text{seal}} &= S \times O \times D = 7 \times 5 \times 4 = 140 \\
\text{RPN}_{\text{bearing}} &= 8 \times 3 \times 6 = 144 \\
\text{RPN}_{\text{coupling}} &= 9 \times 2 \times 5 = 90 \\
\text{RPN}_{\text{casing}} &= 10 \times 1 \times 7 = 70 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Rank by RPN (Descending)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bearing failure} \; (144) &> \text{Seal leak} \; (140) > \text{Coupling break} \; (90) > \text{Casing crack} \; (70) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Apply Severity Override Rule

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Severity Override Rule:** Any failure mode with $S \geq 9$ must be addressed **regardless** of RPN, because it represents a potentially fatal outcome. In our list, *Coupling break* ($S=9$) and *Casing crack* ($S=10$) trigger the override.

### Step 4: Prioritize Corrective Actions

The prioritized action list becomes:
1. **Casing crack** ($S=10$, catastrophic) — Immediate redesign, despite low RPN.
2. **Coupling break** ($S=9$) — Mandatory guard or interlock.
3. **Bearing failure** (RPN 144) — Predictive maintenance program.
4. **Seal leak** (RPN 140) — Routine inspection upgrade.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Event Tree Probability Cascading

### Problem Setup

A gasoline storage tank develops a **leak** (initiating event) with probability $P(I) = 10^{-2} \, \text{yr}^{-1}$. Two independent safety functions are in place:
- **Function A (Detection & Isolation):** Successful with $P(A_s) = 0.95$.
- **Function B (Ignition Avoidance):** Successful with $P(B_s) = 0.90$.

### Step 1: Draw the Event Tree (Logic Structure)

Two safety functions, each with success/failure, produce $2^2 = 4$ outcomes.

### Step 2: Compute Conditional Probabilities Along Each Branch

The four branches and their conditional probabilities are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Branch 1 (A succeeds, B succeeds)}: &\quad P(F_1) = P(A_s) \cdot P(B_s) = 0.95 \times 0.90 = 0.855 \\
\text{Branch 2 (A succeeds, B fails)}: &\quad P(F_2) = P(A_s) \cdot P(B_f) = 0.95 \times 0.10 = 0.095 \\
\text{Branch 3 (A fails, B succeeds)}: &\quad P(F_3) = P(A_f) \cdot P(B_s) = 0.05 \times 0.90 = 0.045 \\
\text{Branch 4 (A fails, B fails)}: &\quad P(F_4) = P(A_f) \cdot P(B_f) = 0.05 \times 0.10 = 0.005 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** $\sum P(F_i) = 0.855 + 0.095 + 0.045 + 0.005 = 1.000$ ✓

### Step 3: Assign Consequences and Compute Outcome Frequencies

Each branch corresponds to a different physical outcome. We assign a representative consequence severity (in monetary units or fatality units) and compute the **expected frequency of each outcome**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Outcome 1 (Small leak, no ignition): Frequency} &= 10^{-2} \times 0.855 = 8.55 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{yr}^{-1} \\
\text{Outcome 2 (Detected leak, ignition): Frequency} &= 10^{-2} \times 0.095 = 9.50 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{yr}^{-1} \\
\text{Outcome 3 (Undetected, no ignition): Frequency} &= 10^{-2} \times 0.045 = 4.50 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{yr}^{-1} \\
\text{Outcome 4 (Major pool fire): Frequency} &= 10^{-2} \times 0.005 = 5.00 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{yr}^{-1} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Identify the Dominant Undesired Outcome

The dominant undesired outcome is **Outcome 2 (Detected leak with ignition)** at $9.50 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{yr}^{-1}$, despite Outcome 4 having the highest consequence severity. This counterintuitive result is precisely why **frequency-based analysis (ETA) is essential** — severity alone is insufficient for risk management.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Technique (KTU Decision Aid)

> [!TIP]
> **The following decision matrix is a frequent KTU Part A question (3 marks).**

| If you need to... | Use this technique | Why? |
|-------------------|--------------------|------|
| Identify deviations from design intent | **HAZOP** | Systematic, guide-word driven, exhaustive for process industries. |
| Rank component failures by criticality | **FMEA** | Bottom-up, RPN-based prioritization. |
| Quantify probability of a major accident | **FTA** | Top-down, deductive, Boolean-based. |
| Map consequences from an initiating event | **ETA** | Forward, inductive, barrier-focused. |
| Combine causes and consequences in one diagram | **Bow-Tie** | Integrated prevention-mitigation view. |
| Brainstorm broadly in early design | **What-If Analysis** | Flexible, no detailed P&ID needed. |
| Screen hazards at conceptual stage | **PHA** | Coarse, fast, pre-design. |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (Conceptual)

> **Q1.** Differentiate between **Hazard** and **Risk**. Why is it insufficient to manage only one of them in a chemical process plant? **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4, 3 marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Hazard** is an intrinsic property of a material, equipment, or process that has the *potential* to cause harm. Examples include flammability of gasoline, toxicity of hydrogen sulfide, and stored energy in compressed gas cylinders. A hazard is a *latent* source of danger that exists regardless of whether an accident has occurred.

**Risk** is the quantitative or qualitative measure of the *likelihood* that a hazard will cause harm in combination with the *severity* of that harm. It is mathematically expressed as:

$$
\text{Risk} = \text{Probability} \times \text{Consequence}
$$

**Why managing only one is insufficient:**
- Managing **only hazards** by removing all hazardous materials is often impossible (e.g., we must store and transport gasoline for energy needs).
- Managing **only risks** without understanding the underlying hazard can lead to underestimating latent dangers in new scenarios.
- A complete safety program must **identify hazards, evaluate risks, and apply the hierarchy of controls (elimination → substitution → engineering → administrative → PPE)**.

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Tip:** Award 1 mark each for clear definitions, and 1 mark for the justification of why both are needed.

---

### Question 2 (Methodology Comparison)

> **Q2.** Compare **HAZOP** and **FMEA** with respect to their approach (top-down vs. bottom-up), data requirement, and typical application stage. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style Question, 3 marks]**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | HAZOP | FMEA |
|--------|-------|------|
| **Approach** | Top-down (uses guide words to explore deviations) | Bottom-up (analyzes each component failure mode) |
| **Data Requirement** | Detailed P&ID, process flow diagram, operating procedures | Component failure rates, severity and detection ratings |
| **Application Stage** | Detailed design phase, pre-commissioning, and operational changes | Any stage, but most valuable at design and during operation |
| **Output** | List of deviations with causes, consequences, and recommended actions | Ranked list of failure modes with RPN values |
| **Team Size** | Typically 5–8 multidisciplinary experts | Typically 3–5 engineers with system knowledge |

Both techniques are **complementary** and are often applied sequentially in industrial practice.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Choice 1 — Question A (Fault Tree Analysis)

> **Q-A.** **(a)** Construct the **Fault Tree** for the Top Event "Reactor Rupture" given the following logic: A rupture occurs if either (i) there is overpressure, OR (ii) there is overheating. Overpressure occurs if (Pump A fails AND Pump B fails) OR (Relief Valve fails AND Block Valve is closed). Overheating occurs if (Temperature Sensor fails AND Cooling Water Pump fails) OR (Cooler is fouled). **[7 marks]**
>
> **(b)** Determine the **Minimal Cut Sets** and compute the **probability** of the Top Event per year, given the following annual failure probabilities: $P(\text{Pump A}) = 0.02$, $P(\text{Pump B}) = 0.03$, $P(\text{Relief Valve}) = 0.01$, $P(\text{Block Valve closed}) = 0.05$, $P(\text{Temp Sensor}) = 0.02$, $P(\text{Cooling Pump}) = 0.04$, $P(\text{Cooler fouled}) = 0.10$. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Fault Tree Construction [7 marks]

Define the following symbolic events:
- $A$ = Pump A fails
- $B$ = Pump B fails
- $R$ = Relief Valve fails
- $V$ = Block Valve is closed (during overpressure)
- $S$ = Temperature Sensor fails
- $C$ = Cooling Water Pump fails
- $F$ = Cooler is fouled

The intermediate events are:
- $O$ = Overpressure = $(A \cap B) \cup (R \cap V)$
- $H$ = Overheating = $(S \cap C) \cup F$

The Top Event is:

$$
T = O \cup H = (A \cap B) \cup (R \cap V) \cup (S \cap C) \cup F
$$

**Fault Tree Diagram (text representation for solution):**

```
                      TOP EVENT (T): Reactor Rupture
                                   |
                                 [OR GATE]
                ____________________|____________________
               |                    |                    |
          [INTERMEDIATE: O]    [INTERMEDIATE: H]    [BASIC EVENT: F]
          (Overpressure)       (Overheating)        (Cooler Fouled)
          ________|________         |________________
         |                 |              |
       [AND]             [AND]         [AND]
        __|__             __|__          __|__
       |     |           |     |        |     |
       A     B           R     V        S     C
     (PumpA) (PumpB)  (Relief)(Block) (Sensor)(CoolPump)
```

#### Part (b) — Minimal Cut Sets and Probability [7 marks]

The Boolean expression is already in disjunctive normal form (sum of products). The four minimal cut sets are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MCS}_1 &= \{A, B\} \\
\text{MCS}_2 &= \{R, V\} \\
\text{MCS}_3 &= \{S, C\} \\
\text{MCS}_4 &= \{F\} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **MCS}_4$ is a single-point failure cut set with only one element — this is the most critical finding because no redundancy exists.**

Apply the **Rare Event Approximation** (all probabilities $\ll 1$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(T) &\approx P(\text{MCS}_1) + P(\text{MCS}_2) + P(\text{MCS}_3) + P(\text{MCS}_4) \\
&= P(A) \cdot P(B) + P(R) \cdot P(V) + P(S) \cdot P(C) + P(F) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Substitute numerical values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(T) &= (0.02)(0.03) + (0.01)(0.05) + (0.02)(0.04) + 0.10 \\
&= (6.0 \times 10^{-4}) + (5.0 \times 10^{-4}) + (8.0 \times 10^{-4}) + (1.0 \times 10^{-1}) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Add all contributions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(T) &= 6.0 \times 10^{-4} + 5.0 \times 10^{-4} + 8.0 \times 10^{-4} + 1.0 \times 10^{-1} \\
&= 0.0006 + 0.0005 + 0.0008 + 0.1000 \\
&= 0.1019 \, \text{yr}^{-1} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Observation:** The single-point failure $F$ (cooler fouling) **dominates** the risk, contributing over **98%** of the total top event probability. Engineering recommendation: install online fouling monitoring or a backup cooler.

---

### Choice 1 — Question B (HAZOP Study)

> **Q-B.** **(a)** Explain the **HAZOP procedure** in detail. List all **guide words** used in HAZOP with their meanings and give **one example deviation** for each. **[7 marks]**
>
> **(b)** For a **propane storage bullet** operating at 8 bar gauge and 30 °C, perform a HAZOP for the parameter **PRESSURE** using **three guide words** (NO, MORE, LESS), and produce the complete deviation-cause-consequence-safeguard matrix. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — HAZOP Procedure, Guide Words, and Deviations [7 marks]

**HAZOP Procedure (6 steps — give 3 marks):**

1. **Define scope and assemble a multidisciplinary team** (process engineer, control engineer, safety engineer, operator, chemist).
2. **Divide the plant into nodes** (logical sections of the process — e.g., a pipeline section or a single vessel).
3. **For each node, state the design intent** — e.g., "Transfer propane from bullet B-101 to Pump P-102 at 8 bar gauge."
4. **Apply each guide word to each parameter** to generate deviations.
5. **For meaningful deviations, identify causes, consequences, and existing safeguards.**
6. **Recommend actions** to close safety gaps; document everything in the HAZOP worksheet.

**Guide Words (give 4 marks):**

| Guide Word | Meaning | Example Parameter | Example Deviation |
|------------|---------|-------------------|-------------------|
| **NO / NOT** | Complete negation | Flow | No flow from bullet to pump |
| **MORE** | Quantitative increase | Pressure | High pressure in bullet |
| **LESS** | Quantitative decrease | Temperature | Low temperature (cold embrittlement) |
| **REVERSE** | Logical opposite | Flow | Reverse flow back to bullet |
| **AS WELL AS** | Additional activity | Composition | Water in propane line |
| **PART OF** | Partial achievement | Composition | Reduced propane concentration |
| **OTHER THAN** | Complete substitution | Flow | Nitrogen flowing instead of propane |

#### Part (b) — HAZOP Matrix for Propane Bullet (PRESSURE) [7 marks]

| Guide Word | Deviation | Possible Causes | Consequences | Existing Safeguards | Recommendations |
|------------|-----------|-----------------|--------------|--------------------|-----------------|
| **NO** | **No pressure** (vacuum) | (i) Over-purging with nitrogen during maintenance; (ii) Blocked nitrogen blanketing regulator | (i) Air ingress → explosive mixture inside bullet; (ii) Vacuum collapse of bullet shell | Nitrogen blanketing system; pressure indicator (PI) | Install low-pressure alarm with redundant PI; add vacuum relief valve |
| **MORE** | **High pressure** | (i) Overfilling due to level indicator error; (ii) Blocked relief valve; (iii) Fire exposure heating propane beyond design | (i) Relief valve lifting → flammable vapour release; (ii) Catastrophic rupture → BLEVE | Pressure Safety Valve (PSV); high-pressure alarm; emergency venting | Install rupture disc upstream of PSV; flame detectors around bullet; thermal insulation |
| **LESS** | **Low pressure** | (i) Excessive withdrawal from bullet; (ii) Leak in suction line; (iii) Cooling rain after a fire | (i) Pump cavitation; (ii) Loss of blanket → air ingress; (iii) Reduced LNG-like cooling causing brittle fracture | Low-pressure alarm; nitrogen blanketing regulator | Add independent low-low-pressure shutdown; install leak detection on suction piping |

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Tip:** Award 1 mark per filled cell of the table; students often lose marks for leaving the "Recommendations" column empty. Recommendations are the *most valuable* column in the eyes of examiners.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls in Safety Analysis:**
> 1. **Confusing RPN with Severity alone:** Many students compute RPN but forget the *Severity Override Rule* ($S \geq 9$ → mandatory action). Examiners deduct **2 marks** for this oversight.
> 2. **Skipping Boolean algebra simplification:** In FTA, writing down minimal cut sets without showing the distributive law derivation will cost **at least 1 mark**. Always show your Boolean manipulation.
> 3. **Forgetting the "Rare Event Approximation" validity condition:** Never apply $P(T) \approx \sum P(K_i)$ without first stating that all probabilities are $\ll 1$. Examiners expect this statement.
> 4. **HAZOP table incompleteness:** A HAZOP deviation without causes, consequences, AND safeguards is incomplete. All four columns must be filled.
> 5. **Mixing up FTA and ETA:** FTA works *backward* from a Top Event; ETA works *forward* from an Initiating Event. Confusing these two is a **3-mark penalty**.
> 6. **Unit errors:** Failure probabilities in chemical process safety are typically per year ($\text{yr}^{-1}$). Mixing units (e.g., $\text{hr}^{-1}$ vs. $\text{yr}^{-1}$) leads to nonsensical answers.
> 7. **Single-point failure cut sets:** When an MCS contains only one event, it must be flagged as a *catastrophic finding* requiring immediate engineering redesign.

---

## TOPIC RECAP — Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Things to Remember (Print & Carry):**

- ☐ **Safety Analysis** = structured identification + evaluation + control of process hazards.
- ☐ Three categories: **Qualitative** (HAZOP, What-If), **Semi-Quantitative** (FMEA, Dow F&EI), **Quantitative** (FTA, ETA, QRA).
- ☐ **HAZOP guide words:** NO, MORE, LESS, REVERSE, AS WELL AS, PART OF, OTHER THAN.
- ☐ **HAZOP parameters:** Flow, Pressure, Temperature, Level, Composition, Phase, Reaction.
- ☐ **RPN = S × O × D**, with severity scale 1–10; action threshold **RPN > 100** or **S ≥ 9**.
- ☐ **FTA is top-down (deductive); ETA is forward (inductive).** Never confuse them.
- ☐ **Boolean gates:** AND → intersection, OR → union, NOT → complement, XOR → exclusive union.
- ☐ **Minimal Cut Set (MCS):** Smallest combination of basic events that triggers the Top Event.
- ☐ **Rare Event Approximation:** Valid when $P \ll 1$; $P(T) \approx \sum P(K_i)$.
- ☐ **Inclusion-Exclusion Principle:** Required for exact FTA probabilities; mandatory when probabilities are not small.
- ☐ **ETA branch probability:** $P(\text{Outcome}) = P(I) \times \prod P(F_j)$.
- ☐ **Bow-Tie** combines FTA (left) + ETA (right), with prevention barriers on the left and mitigation barriers on the right.
- ☐ **Single-point failure cut sets** (one-element MCS) are the highest priority findings.
- ☐ **Standards to remember:** IEC 61882 (HAZOP), IEC 61025 (FTA), IEC 62502 (ETA), CCPS Guidelines.
- ☐ **ALARP principle:** Risk must be reduced "As Low As Reasonably Practicable."
- ☐ **Risk = Probability × Consequence** — always state both dimensions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hazard Survey and Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT723 - PROCESS SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hazard Survey and Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Hazard Survey and Analysis?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> *Hazard Survey and Analysis* refers to the systematic, structured, and exhaustive process of identifying, classifying, and evaluating potential physical, chemical, biological, ergonomic, and environmental hazards present in an industrial process, plant, or system. It forms the foundational pillar of Process Safety Management (PSM) and is a mandatory precursor to quantitative risk assessment (QRA).

In the framework of the **KTU 2024 OECHT723 syllabus**, hazard survey and analysis is not a single technique but a **toolbox of qualitative, semi-quantitative, and quantitative methodologies** used by process engineers to anticipate accident scenarios before they manifest in reality.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Medical Check-up" Metaphor:**
> Imagine a process plant as a human body. A doctor does not wait for a heart attack to examine the patient. Instead, the doctor conducts routine surveys (blood tests, ECG, stress tests) to identify *latent hazards* (high cholesterol, irregular rhythm). Similarly, a process safety engineer conducts **Hazard Surveys** *before* a fire, explosion, or toxic release occurs. The analysis stage is like the doctor reviewing the test results to prescribe preventive medicine (safeguards, controls, and barriers).

### 1.2 Why is Hazard Survey Necessary?

The **"Swiss Cheese Model"** of accident causation (popularized by James Reason, 1990) is universally adopted in KTU coursework to justify hazard surveys:

1. Every layer of protection in a process plant (design, procedures, alarms, training) has **holes** (latent failures).
2. Hazard surveys aim to **identify these holes** *proactively*.
3. Only after identification can engineers introduce **redundancies** to align with **ALARP** (As Low As Reasonably Practicable) principle.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology

| Term | Standard Definition | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Hazard** | A physical situation with potential to cause harm | Intrinsic property of materials/processes |
| **Risk** | Likelihood × Consequence ($R = f \times C$) | Quantitative measure for prioritization |
| **Incident** | An unwanted event causing loss | Triggers investigation |
| **Near-Miss** | An event with potential to cause loss but did not | Critical learning opportunity |
| **LOPA** | Layer of Protection Analysis | Semi-quantitative safeguard evaluation |
| **ALARP** | As Low As Reasonably Practicable | Legal/safety decision-making principle |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Board Exams:**
> A *hazard* (e.g., flammable gasoline) is different from a *risk* (probability of pool fire occurring under given storage conditions). Examiners frequently test this distinction.

### 1.4 Classification of Hazard Survey Techniques

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Categorization (Module 4 Highlight):**
> Hazard analysis methods fall into three classes based on data requirement and rigor:
> 1. **Qualitative Methods** — Checklists, What-If, Preliminary Hazard Analysis (PHA)
> 2. **Semi-Quantitative Methods** — Dow Fire & Explosion Index (F&EI), Mond Index, HAZOP (scoring)
> 3. **Quantitative Methods** — Fault Tree Analysis (FTA), Event Tree Analysis (ETA), Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA)

### 1.5 The Hazard Analysis Workflow (Top-Down Approach)

```
Plant Design / Existing Process
        ↓
[1] Process Familiarization (P&ID review, MSDS)
        ↓
[2] Hazard Identification (What can go wrong?)
        ↓
[3] Hazard Evaluation (How bad? How often?)
        ↓
[4] Consequence & Frequency Estimation
        ↓
[5] Risk Ranking & ALARP Determination
        ↓
[6] Recommendation & Documentation
```
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Phase 1 — Hazard Identification

The **first phase** of any safety study is *identifying* what could go wrong. KTU examiners expect students to enumerate the standard identification tools:

### 2.1.1 Checklist Analysis
- **Origin:** Aviation and military industries (1940s–1950s); later adapted to chemical/process industry.
- **Mechanism:** A series of yes/no or open-ended questions derived from past incidents, codes, and operating experience.
- **Logic Flow:**
  * Step 1: Compile expert-derived questions on materials, equipment, operating conditions.
  * Step 2: Walk through plant sections and answer each query.
  * Step 3: Flag deviations and recommend corrective actions.
- **Strengths:** Simple, fast, requires minimal expertise.
- **Weaknesses:** Ineffective for novel processes; encourages "checkbox mentality."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Checklists are best for *routine* plants with low complexity. For novel chemical processes, HAZOP or What-If is preferred.

### 2.1.2 Preliminary Hazard Analysis (PHA)
- Conducted at the **earliest design stage** (conceptual phase).
- Uses a tabular format with columns: *Hazard, Cause, Consequence, Severity, Probability, Risk Level, Recommendation*.
- Follows **MIL-STD-882** military standard originally; now standardized under **IEC 31010:2019**.

### 2.1.3 What-If Analysis
- Brainstorming-style method. The team asks structured "What-If" questions:
  * *What if the cooling water pump fails?*
  * *What if the operator opens valve V-101 manually during maintenance?*
- Highly flexible, encourages creative thinking.

### 2.1.4 Hazard and Operability Study (HAZOP)
- **Most heavily tested topic in KTU 2024 ESE.**
- Developed by **Imperial Chemical Industries (ICI), UK, in 1963**.
- Systematic application of **guide words** to process deviations.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates every formula a KTU 2024 student is expected to memorize for the OECHT723 Module 4 examination.

| Method | Formula / Logic | Variables & Units | Boundary / Validity |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Risk Score** | $R = f \times C$ | $f$ = frequency (events/yr); $C$ = consequence (severity units) | $f, C \geq 0$ |
| **ALARP Test** | $B \geq C_{risk} - R_{residual}$ | $B$ = benefit; $C_{risk}$ = cost of risk; $R_{residual}$ = residual risk cost | Applied only when $R > R_{tolerable}$ |
| **FMEA Risk Priority Number** | $RPN = S \times O \times D$ | $S$ = Severity (1–10); $O$ = Occurrence (1–10); $D$ = Detection (1–10) | Range: 1 to 1000 |
| **Dow F&EI Index** | $F\&EI = MF \times G \times L$ | $MF$ = Material Factor; $G$ = General Process Hazards; $L$ = Loss Control Credit | $F\&EI \leq 128$ (typical industrial) |
| **Dose (Probit)** | $C^n \cdot t = k$ (constant) | $C$ = concentration (ppm); $t$ = exposure time (min) | Used for probit-equation fatality probability |
| **Fault Tree AND Gate** | $P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)$ | For independent events | Requires independence assumption |
| **Fault Tree OR Gate** | $P(A \cup B) = 1 - (1-P(A))(1-P(B))$ | For independent events | $P(A), P(B) \in [0, 1]$ |
| **Cut-Set Sum (rare event)** | $P(CS) = \sum P(C_i)$ | $C_i$ = individual minimal cut-set probabilities | Valid only when $P(C_i) \ll 1$ |
| **Event Tree Branch** | $P(T_j) = P(I) \cdot \prod P(s_k)$ | $P(I)$ = initiating event; $s_k$ = success/failure branches | Conditional independence required |
| **Avogadro's Number (moles)** | $n = \dfrac{m}{M}$ | $m$ = mass (kg); $M$ = molar mass (kg/mol) | Universal constant |
| **TNT Equivalence** | $W_{TNT} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot \Delta H_c \cdot m}{E_{TNT}}$ | $\eta$ = empirical yield (0.02–0.40); $m$ = mass (kg); $\Delta H_c$ = heat of combustion (kJ/kg); $E_{TNT}$ = 4686 kJ/kg | $\eta = 0.10$ typical for hydrocarbon vapor cloud |

> [!NOTE]
> **Markdown Safeguard Note:** In the table above, all equation symbols use proper LaTeX delimiters (no vertical pipe characters) to preserve table rendering.

## 2.3 Phase 2 — Hazard Evaluation Techniques (Detailed)

### 2.3.1 Dow Fire & Explosion Index (F&EI)

Developed by the **Dow Chemical Company (1976, 4th edition 1993)**, F&EI is the most widely used **chemical-process hazard index**.

**Operational Steps:**
1. **Step 1 — Identify Process Unit:** Select the worst-case unit (largest material inventory, highest MF).
2. **Step 2 — Determine Material Factor (MF):** Read from the Dow F&EI material table (e.g., gasoline $\approx 16$, methane $\approx 21$, hydrogen $\approx 24$).
3. **Step 3 — Compute General Process Hazards ($G$):** Sum penalties for:
   * Exothermic reactions
   * Endothermic reactions
   * Material handling & transfer
   * Enclosed or indoor process units
   * Access limitation
   * Drainage & spill control
4. **Step 4 — Compute Special Process Hazards ($S$):**
   * Toxic materials, sub-atmospheric pressure, low-high temperature, quantity of material, etc.
5. **Step 5 — Compute Unit Hazard Factor:** $UHF = 1 + \dfrac{G + S}{100}$
6. **Step 6 — Compute F&EI:** $F\&EI = MF \times UHF$
7. **Step 7 — Determine Radius of Exposure ($R$):** $R(\text{m}) = 0.256 \times F\&EI$
8. **Step 8 — Area of Exposure:** $A = \pi R^2$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Damage Estimation:** If $F\&EI > 128$, the process unit is considered *extremely hazardous* and warrants significant mitigation investment.

### 2.3.2 Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA)

A *bottom-up*, inductive method. For each component, list:
- Failure mode (e.g., "pump fails to start")
- Effect on system
- Detection mechanism
- RPN score

**RPN Threshold Rule (Industry Standard):**
$$RPN \geq 100 \rightarrow \text{Mandatory Action Required}$$

### 2.3.3 Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)

A *top-down*, deductive method. The undesirable event (top event) is decomposed using Boolean logic gates (AND, OR, NOT).

**Boolean algebra identities used in KTU exams:**
$$A + AB = A \quad ; \quad A(A + B) = A \quad ; \quad \overline{A + B} = \overline{A} \cdot \overline{B}$$

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Most-Used Hazard Technique | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **Petrochemical Refineries** | HAZOP + LOPA | Complex piping, multi-stream |
| **Pharmaceuticals** | FMEA + What-If | Batch processes, human-factor intensive |
| **Nuclear Power** | Fault Tree + Event Tree | High consequence, low tolerance |
| **Offshore Oil & Gas** | QRA + F&EI | Combined fire, explosion, environmental risk |
| **Food Processing** | HACCP (Hazard Analysis Critical Control Point) | Biological/chemical contamination focus |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Dow Fire & Explosion Index (F&EI) Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (Typical KTU Board Question Style):**
> A process unit stores 8000 kg of acetone at elevated temperature. Calculate the F&EI and the radius of exposure.
>
> Given data:
> - Material Factor for acetone: $MF = 16$
> - General Process Hazards penalty total: $G = 2.10$
> - Special Process Hazards penalty total: $S = 4.55$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the Material Factor**

From the Dow F&EI tables, acetone (flammable liquid, $MF = 16$).

$$MF_{acetone} = 16$$

**Step 2 — Compute the General Process Hazards Term**

The general process hazards are tabulated penalties. The question states $G = 2.10$:

$$G = 2.10$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Special Process Hazards Term**

The special process hazards are tabulated penalties. The question states $S = 4.55$:

$$S = 4.55$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Unit Hazard Factor (UHF)**

The UHF is computed using the standardized Dow formula:

$$UHF = 1 + \dfrac{G + S}{100}$$

Substituting the values:

$$UHF = 1 + \dfrac{2.10 + 4.55}{100}$$

$$UHF = 1 + \dfrac{6.65}{100}$$

$$UHF = 1 + 0.0665$$

$$UHF = 1.0665$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Fire & Explosion Index**

The Dow F&EI is the product of the Material Factor and the Unit Hazard Factor:

$$F\&EI = MF \times UHF$$

$$F\&EI = 16 \times 1.0665$$

$$F\&EI = 17.064$$

**Step 6 — Compute the Radius of Exposure**

The empirical Dow relationship between F&EI and radius (in metres) is:

$$R = 0.256 \times F\&EI$$

Substituting:

$$R = 0.256 \times 17.064$$

$$R = 4.368 \text{ m}$$

**Step 7 — Compute the Area of Exposure**

The circular area within the exposure radius is:

$$A = \pi R^2$$

$$A = \pi \times (4.368)^2$$

$$A = \pi \times 19.079$$

$$A = 59.94 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 8 — Interpret the Result**

Since $F\&EI = 17.064$ is well below the industrial threshold of $128$, the unit is classified as **"Light Hazard"** per Dow classification. The exposure radius of $\approx 4.37$ m indicates a localized hazard footprint.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Decision:** Although the F&EI value is low, the analyst should still evaluate Loss Control Credits (process control $C_1$, material isolation $C_2$, fire protection $C_3$) to compute the actual loss dollar value and decide on ALARP compliance.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — FMEA Risk Priority Number (RPN)

**Problem Statement:**
A centrifugal pump in a refinery has the following FMEA ratings:
- Severity ($S$) = 8 (High — complete pump failure causes unit shutdown)
- Occurrence ($O$) = 5 (Moderate — once per year)
- Detection ($D$) = 4 (Moderate — vibration sensor detects within 1 hour)

Compute the RPN and recommend action.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — State the RPN Equation**

The Risk Priority Number is the product of severity, occurrence, and detection:

$$RPN = S \times O \times D$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the Numerical Values**

$$RPN = 8 \times 5 \times 4$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Product**

$$RPN = 160$$

**Step 4 — Compare Against the Threshold**

Since $RPN = 160 > 100$:

$$RPN_{computed} > RPN_{threshold}$$

The failure mode **exceeds** the mandatory action threshold and requires immediate mitigation.

**Step 5 — Recommend Mitigation**
- Install redundant vibration monitoring with online diagnostics (lowers $D$).
- Establish a preventive maintenance schedule (lowers $O$).
- Add a spare pump with auto-changeover (lowers $S$ by reducing downtime).

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Fault Tree AND/OR Probability

**Problem Statement:**
A fault tree has a top event "Reactor Overpressure" caused by an OR gate combining two AND gates:
- AND Gate A: Pump failure ($P_A = 0.01$) AND Valve stuck ($P_B = 0.02$)
- AND Gate B: Sensor failure ($P_C = 0.005$) AND Operator error ($P_D = 0.03$)

Compute the probability of the top event.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Compute AND Gate A Probability**

For independent events in an AND gate:

$$P(A) = P_A \times P_B$$

$$P(A) = 0.01 \times 0.02 = 2 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 2 — Compute AND Gate B Probability**

$$P(B) = P_C \times P_D$$

$$P(B) = 0.005 \times 0.03 = 1.5 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the OR Gate Formula**

For an OR gate with independent inputs:

$$P(T) = 1 - (1 - P(A))(1 - P(B))$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the Values**

$$P(T) = 1 - (1 - 2 \times 10^{-4})(1 - 1.5 \times 10^{-4})$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Product**

$$P(T) = 1 - (0.9998)(0.99985)$$

$$P(T) = 1 - 0.99965$$

$$P(T) = 3.5 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 6 — Verify with Rare-Event Approximation**

For small probabilities, the OR-gate probability approximates the sum:

$$P(T) \approx P(A) + P(B) = 2 \times 10^{-4} + 1.5 \times 10^{-4} = 3.5 \times 10^{-4}$$

The rare-event approximation **matches exactly** because both $P(A)$ and $P(B)$ are very small.

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU board examiners frequently award full marks when the rare-event approximation is *also* stated, demonstrating rigorous understanding of validity conditions.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Event Tree Quantification

**Problem Statement:**
An event tree represents an LPG leak (initiating event frequency = $10^{-2}$/yr). The leak can either ignite immediately or form a vapor cloud. If ignited immediately, a flash fire occurs. If a cloud forms, it either disperses harmlessly or finds an ignition source leading to a VCE (Vapor Cloud Explosion).
- $P(\text{immediate ignition}) = 0.4$
- $P(\text{cloud forms}) = 0.6$
- $P(\text{cloud finds ignition}) = 0.2$
- $P(\text{cloud disperses harmlessly}) = 0.8$

Compute the frequency of each outcome.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute Frequency of Flash Fire**

$$f_{FF} = f_{init} \times P(\text{immediate ignition})$$

$$f_{FF} = 10^{-2} \times 0.4 = 4 \times 10^{-3} \text{ /yr}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Frequency of VCE**

$$f_{VCE} = f_{init} \times P(\text{cloud forms}) \times P(\text{cloud ignition})$$

$$f_{VCE} = 10^{-2} \times 0.6 \times 0.2 = 1.2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ /yr}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Frequency of Harmless Dispersion**

$$f_{disp} = 10^{-2} \times 0.6 \times 0.8 = 4.8 \times 10^{-3} \text{ /yr}$$

**Step 4 — Verification (Total Frequency)**

$$f_{total} = f_{FF} + f_{VCE} + f_{disp}$$

$$f_{total} = 4 \times 10^{-3} + 1.2 \times 10^{-3} + 4.8 \times 10^{-3} = 10^{-2} \text{ /yr}$$

The total frequency matches the initiating event frequency, confirming the tree's conservation of probability.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Choosing the Right Method

| Criterion | HAZOP | FMEA | FTA | ETA | What-If | Checklist |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Direction** | Top-down (process deviations) | Bottom-up | Top-down (deductive) | Forward (consequence) | Brainstorm | List-based |
| **Best Use** | Continuous processes | Mechanical/equipment | High-consequence systems | Frequency modeling | Early design | Routine plants |
| **Data Need** | P&ID + operating limits | Component list | Component failure rates | Initiating event freq. | None | Past incident logs |
| **Quantitative?** | Mostly qualitative | Semi-quantitative (RPN) | Quantitative | Quantitative | Qualitative | Qualitative |
| **Team Size** | 5–8 specialists | 3–5 engineers | 2–4 analysts | 2–4 analysts | 3–6 engineers | 1–2 engineers |
| **Duration** | 2–5 days | 1–3 days | 3–10 days | 2–5 days | 0.5–1 day | Hours |
| **KTU Exam Frequency** | Very High | High | High | Medium | Medium | Low |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of Hazard Survey & Analysis Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Plant Process Data and P and ID"] --> nodeB["Hazard Identification Phase"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Checklist Screening"]
    nodeB --> nodeD["What If Brainstorm"]
    nodeB --> nodeE["HAZOP Study"]
    nodeB --> nodeF["Preliminary Hazard Analysis"]
    nodeC --> nodeG["Hazard Evaluation Phase"]
    nodeD --> nodeG
    nodeE --> nodeG
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH["FMEA with RPN Scoring"]
    nodeG --> nodeI["Dow F and EI Index"]
    nodeG --> nodeJ["LOPA Assessment"]
    nodeH --> nodeK["Risk Quantification Phase"]
    nodeI --> nodeK
    nodeJ --> nodeK
    nodeK --> nodeL["Fault Tree Analysis"]
    nodeK --> nodeM["Event Tree Analysis"]
    nodeK --> nodeN["Quantitative Risk Assessment"]
    nodeL --> nodeO["ALARP Decision Gate"]
    nodeM --> nodeO
    nodeN --> nodeO
    nodeO --> nodeP["Recommendation and Documentation"]
    nodeP --> nodeQ["Periodic Reassessment Loop"]
```

## 4.2 HAZOP Node Examination Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeS1["Select Process Node from P and ID"] --> nodeS2["Define Design Intent"]
    nodeS2 --> nodeS3["Apply Guide Words"]
    nodeS3 --> nodeS4["Identify Deviations"]
    nodeS4 --> nodeS5["Determine Causes"]
    nodeS5 --> nodeS6["Evaluate Consequences"]
    nodeS6 --> nodeS7["Recommend Safeguards"]
    nodeS7 --> nodeS8["Document in HAZOP Worksheet"]
    nodeS8 --> nodeS9{"All Nodes Covered?"}
    nodeS9 -- No --> nodeS1
    nodeS9 -- Yes --> nodeS10["Final HAZOP Report"]
```

```mermaid
    nodeT2 --> nodeT3["AND Gate A"]
    nodeT2 --> nodeT4["AND Gate B"]

    nodeT3 --> nodeT6["Valve Stuck Closed Event"]
    nodeT4 --> nodeT7["Sensor Failure Event"]
    nodeT4 --> nodeT8["Operator Error Event"]
```

The diamond nodes represent decision gates where Yes/No choices branch the workflow.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
*Define the term "HAZOP." List any four standard HAZOP guide words used during the analysis.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**HAZOP (Hazard and Operability Study)** is a structured, systematic, and team-based safety analysis technique developed by Imperial Chemical Industries (ICI) in 1963. It is used to identify process hazards and operability problems by systematically applying guide words to deviations from design intent.

**Four Standard HAZOP Guide Words:**
1. **NO / NOT** — Complete negation of the design intent (e.g., no flow).
2. **MORE** — Quantitative increase (e.g., more pressure).
3. **LESS** — Quantitative decrease (e.g., less temperature).
4. **REVERSE** — Logical opposite (e.g., reverse flow).

*(Alternative valid guide words: AS WELL AS, PART OF, OTHER THAN.)*

*(Mark Distribution: Definition 1 mark + 4 guide words × 0.5 = 2 marks = Total 3 marks)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]**
*Differentiate between Hazard and Risk with a suitable example from the chemical industry.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Hazard | Risk |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | An inherent physical/chemical property with the potential to cause harm | The combination of probability of occurrence and severity of consequence |
| **Nature** | Intrinsic to the material/process | Function of exposure, frequency, and consequence |
| **Quantification** | Qualitative | Quantitative ($R = f \times C$) |

**Example from Chemical Industry:**
- **Hazard:** The flammability of acetone (flash point $\approx -20 \, ^\circ C$).
- **Risk:** The probability of a pool fire occurring during indoor storage of 5000 kg of acetone at $30 \, ^\circ C$, which depends on ventilation, ignition sources, and operator training.

*(Mark Distribution: 1 mark definition each + 1 mark example = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme regulations, Part B questions of Module 4 carry 14 marks each and offer an **internal choice** between Question A and Question B. Both alternatives are provided below.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
Explain in detail the **Hazard and Operability (HAZOP) study** methodology. Describe the team composition, procedure, and documentation format.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
A centrifugal pump handling benzene has the following FMEA ratings:
- Severity ($S$) = 9
- Occurrence ($O$) = 4
- Detection ($D$) = 6

Compute the RPN. If the threshold is 100, recommend specific engineering controls to bring RPN below the threshold.

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Solution to Part (a) — HAZOP Methodology

**1. Origin and Scope (1 Mark):**
HAZOP was developed by the Chemical Industry Safety and Health Council of the UK (ICI) in 1963. It is the most widely applied technique in the process industry for both continuous and batch processes.

**2. Team Composition (2 Marks):**
A typical HAZOP team has 5–8 specialists from diverse backgrounds:
- Team Leader (HAZOP-experienced facilitator)
- Process Engineer
- Operations/Plant Operator
- Instrumentation/Control Engineer
- Safety Engineer
- Maintenance Engineer

**3. Procedure (3 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Define Scope and Objectives:** Establish the boundaries (e.g., from feedstock storage to product tank).

**Step 2 — Divide into Nodes:** Subdivide the plant into manageable sections (each containing a measurable parameter: flow, pressure, temperature, level).

**Step 3 — State Design Intent:** For each node, articulate what the equipment is intended to do under normal operation.

**Step 4 — Apply Guide Words:** Systematically use guide words (NO, MORE, LESS, REVERSE, AS WELL AS, PART OF, OTHER THAN) to generate meaningful deviations.

**Step 5 — Identify Causes:** Determine what could cause each deviation (equipment failure, human error, external event).

**Step 6 — Evaluate Consequences:** Determine the impact on safety, environment, and operability.

**Step 7 — Recommend Safeguards:** Propose layers of protection (alarms, interlocks, relief valves, procedures).

**Step 8 — Document Findings:** Record all observations in the HAZOP worksheet.

**4. Documentation Format (1 Mark):**
The HAZOP worksheet has the following columns:
| Node | Guide Word | Deviation | Cause | Consequence | Safeguards | Recommendation | Action Party |

---

#### Solution to Part (b) — FMEA RPN Calculation

**Step 1 — State the Equation (1 Mark):**

$$RPN = S \times O \times D$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the Values (1 Mark):**

$$RPN = 9 \times 4 \times 6$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Product (1 Mark):**

$$RPN = 216$$

**Step 4 — Compare Against Threshold (1 Mark):**
Since $RPN = 216 > 100$, the failure mode **exceeds** the mandatory action threshold.

**Step 5 — Engineering Controls to Reduce RPN (3 Marks):**

The most cost-effective approach targets the highest scoring dimension — here, **Detection ($D = 6$)**.

| Control | Target Dimension | New Rating |
|---|---|---|
| Install online vibration monitoring with auto-trip at 7 mm/s RMS | Reduces $D$ from 6 → 2 | New $RPN = 9 \times 4 \times 2 = 72$ |
| Implement predictive maintenance schedule every 6 months | Reduces $O$ from 4 → 2 | New $RPN = 9 \times 2 \times 2 = 36$ |
| Install a spare pump with auto-changeover logic | Reduces $S$ from 9 → 7 | New $RPN = 7 \times 2 \times 2 = 28$ |

**Final Recommendation:** Adopt all three controls. The combined RPN reduces to $RPN_{final} = 28$, well below the threshold of 100.

*(Mark Distribution: Part (a) — 7 marks distributed across 4 sub-areas; Part (b) — 7 marks distributed across 5 steps.)*

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
Explain the **Dow Fire & Explosion Index (F&EI)** method. List all the major steps and explain how the radius of exposure is computed.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
A reactor vessel contains 5000 kg of methanol. The following data is given:
- Material Factor, $MF = 16$
- General Process Hazards penalty total, $G = 1.50$
- Special Process Hazards penalty total, $S = 3.00$

Compute the F&EI, the Unit Hazard Factor, and the radius of exposure. Comment on the hazard classification.

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Solution to Part (a) — Dow F&EI Methodology

**1. Origin and Purpose (1 Mark):**
Developed by Dow Chemical Company (1976, latest edition 1993). It is a *quantitative index-based* method to rank process units by relative fire and explosion hazard.

**2. Key Steps (5 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify Process Unit:** Select the worst-case unit (largest MF × quantity combination).

**Step 2 — Determine Material Factor (MF):** Use Dow F&EI Material Factor table (e.g., gasoline = 16, methane = 21, propane = 21, hydrogen = 24, methanol = 16).

**Step 3 — Determine General Process Hazards Penalties ($G$):** These are unit-less multipliers based on:
- Exothermic reactions
- Endothermic reactions
- Material handling & transfer
- Enclosed/indoor process units
- Access limitation
- Drainage & spill control

**Step 4 — Determine Special Process Hazards Penalties ($S$):** These cover:
- Toxic materials
- Low or high temperature operation
- Quantity of material
- Sub-atmospheric pressure operations
- Corrosion/erosion potential
- Leakage potential at joints

**Step 5 — Compute Unit Hazard Factor (UHF):**
$$UHF = 1 + \dfrac{G + S}{100}$$

**Step 6 — Compute F&EI:**
$$F\&EI = MF \times UHF$$

**Step 7 — Compute Radius of Exposure ($R$):**
$$R = 0.256 \times F\&EI \quad (\text{in metres})$$

**Step 8 — Compute Area of Exposure ($A$):**
$$A = \pi R^2$$

**9. Hazard Classification (1 Mark):**

| F&EI Range | Classification |
|---|---|
| 1–60 | Light |
| 61–96 | Moderate |
| 97–127 | Heavy |
| 128+ | Severe (extreme) |

---

#### Solution to Part (b) — Methanol Reactor F&EI Calculation

**Step 1 — Material Factor**

For methanol: $MF = 16$.

**Step 2 — Compute Unit Hazard Factor (2 Marks):**

$$UHF = 1 + \dfrac{G + S}{100} = 1 + \dfrac{1.50 + 3.00}{100} = 1 + 0.045 = 1.045$$

**Step 3 — Compute F&EI (2 Marks):**

$$F\&EI = MF \times UHF = 16 \times 1.045 = 16.72$$

**Step 4 — Compute Radius of Exposure (1 Mark):**

$$R = 0.256 \times F\&EI = 0.256 \times 16.72 = 4.28 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Compute Area of Exposure (Optional, 1 Mark):**

$$A = \pi \times (4.28)^2 = 57.55 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 6 — Hazard Classification (1 Mark):**
Since $F\&EI = 16.72$, the unit falls in the **"Light Hazard"** category ($F\&EI \in [1, 60]$). Routine precautions and standard loss-control measures are sufficient.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing hazard with risk:** Many students interchange these terms in 14-mark answers, costing up to 2 marks per question. Always explicitly state: *Hazard is intrinsic; Risk is likelihood × consequence.*
> 2. **Forgetting guide words:** In HAZOP questions, omitting any of the seven standard guide words (NO, MORE, LESS, REVERSE, AS WELL AS, PART OF, OTHER THAN) results in a 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **Unit errors in F&EI:** Students often forget that $R$ from the Dow formula is in **metres**, not feet. Conversion errors lose 1 mark.
> 4. **Boolean algebra mistakes in FTA:** When applying the OR-gate formula, students sometimes add probabilities directly without the rare-event justification. Always state the assumption.
> 5. **Not linking RPN to action:** Computing RPN is only worth half the marks. Examiners expect specific engineering controls and a re-computed RPN to demonstrate full understanding.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 OECHT723 Module 4**

### Key Definitions to Memorize
- **HAZOP:** Systematic guide-word-based deviation analysis (ICI, 1963).
- **FMEA:** Bottom-up, component-level failure mode enumeration.
- **FTA:** Top-down, deductive Boolean decomposition.
- **ETA:** Forward-branching event sequence modeling.
- **F&EI:** Quantitative fire/explosion hazard index (Dow Chemical, 1976).
- **ALARP:** As Low As Reasonably Practicable risk reduction principle.
- **LOPA:** Layer of Protection Analysis.
- **PHA:** Preliminary Hazard Analysis (early-stage qualitative).

### Critical Numerical Values
- **F&EI Radius Formula:** $R = 0.256 \times F\&EI$ (metres).
- **F&EI Severe Threshold:** $F\&EI \geq 128$.
- **RPN Threshold:** $RPN \geq 100$ requires action.
- **Material Factor (selected):** Acetone = 16, Methanol = 16, Propane = 21, Hydrogen = 24, Gasoline = 16.

### HAZOP Guide Words (All Seven)
1. **NO / NOT**
2. **MORE**
3. **LESS**
4. **REVERSE**
5. **AS WELL AS**
6. **PART OF**
7. **OTHER THAN**

### Boolean Identities for FTA
- AND gate: $P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)$
- OR gate: $P(A \cup B) = 1 - (1 - P(A))(1 - P(B))$
- Absorption: $A + AB = A$
- Complement: $\overline{A + B} = \overline{A} \cdot \overline{B}$

### Step-by-Step Workflow for Any Hazard Study
1. **Define scope** and gather P&IDs, MSDS, operating procedures.
2. **Identify hazards** (Checklist → What-If → PHA → HAZOP).
3. **Evaluate hazards** (FMEA → F&EI → LOPA).
4. **Quantify risk** (FTA → ETA → QRA).
5. **Apply ALARP** test for risk acceptance.
6. **Document** findings in standardized format.
7. **Reassess** periodically (typically every 5 years).

### Exam Strategy (KTU Board Specific)
- Always **define** any term before using it (1 free mark).
- In 14-mark answers, structure answers with **headings and sub-points**.
- Use **block diagrams** wherever a process is described.
- Cite the **origin** of any technique (e.g., "HAZOP was developed by ICI in 1963").
- Always include a **worked numerical example** when discussing a quantitative method.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: HAZOP
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT723 - PROCESS SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Course Code:** OECHT723
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** HAZOP (Hazard and Operability Study)
> - **Target Audience:** B.Tech Chemical Engineering / Open Elective - 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 Aligned)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of HAZOP

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**HAZOP (Hazard and Operability Study)** is a structured, systematic, and rigorous team-based risk assessment methodology used primarily in the **process industries** (chemical, petrochemical, oil & gas, pharmaceutical, and nuclear) to identify potential **hazards, operability problems, and deviations** from the design intent that may lead to unsafe operating conditions, equipment damage, environmental harm, or production losses.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Official Definition (Per KTU 2024 Syllabus & CCPS Guidelines):**
> HAZOP is a qualitative technique based on the principle that *“accidents are caused by deviations from design intent, and these deviations can be identified by a systematic, multidisciplinary team review using guide words applied to process parameters.”*
> It was originally developed by the **Chemical Industries Association (CIA)** in the United Kingdom in the 1970s and is now standardized under **IEC 61882** and recognized globally as a cornerstone of **Process Hazard Analysis (PHA)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Airport Security Pre-Flight Check**
> Imagine you are an **airport security chief** responsible for ensuring that a Boeing 747 is 100% safe before takeoff. You don't just glance at the plane. You follow a strict checklist that systematically asks questions:
> - *“What if the FUEL pressure is TOO HIGH?”*
> - *“What if there is NO communication with the tower?”*
> - *“What if the landing gear FAILS to deploy?”*
> - *“What if there is a FOREIGN OBJECT (other than) in the engine?”*
>
> Each question uses a special **trigger word (Guide Word)** to force your brain to imagine a *deviation* from the normal, and then you study its *causes, consequences, and safeguards*.
>
> **HAZOP is EXACTLY this** — but instead of an airplane, we examine a chemical plant (e.g., a distillation column, a reactor, a heat exchanger). Instead of pilots, the review is performed by a multidisciplinary team of chemical, mechanical, instrumentation, and safety engineers.

### 1.3 Historical Background

| Year | Milestone |
|------|-----------|
| **1960s** | Developed by **ICI (Imperial Chemical Industries)**, UK, after the **Flixborough Disaster (1974)** |
| **1977** | Published by the **Chemical Industries Association (CIA)** as a formal guide |
| **1990s** | Adopted worldwide; **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119 (PSM)** mandates HAZOP for highly hazardous chemicals in the USA |
| **2001** | Standardized as **IEC 61882** by the International Electrotechnical Commission |
| **2024** | KTU includes it as a core Process Safety Engineering module for B.Tech Chemical Engineering under OEC |

> [!TIP]
> **Key Standard:** **IEC 61882:2016** is the international standard titled *"Hazard and operability studies (HAZOP studies) – Application guide"*. Always cite this in your KTU exam answers.

### 1.4 Objectives of HAZOP

The three primary objectives of conducting a HAZOP study are:

1. **Identify Hazards** — Recognize conditions that may cause injury to people, damage to assets, or harm to the environment.
2. **Identify Operability Problems** — Detect deviations that may not cause immediate accidents but can lead to production loss, poor product quality, or equipment malfunction.
3. **Recommend Safeguards / Actions** — Propose engineering, procedural, or administrative controls to mitigate the identified risks.

### 1.5 When is HAZOP Applied?

- ✅ During the **design stage** of a new plant (most effective).
- ✅ During **modifications** of an existing plant (Management of Change – MOC).
- ✅ Periodically for **existing operating plants** (typically every 5 years).
- ❌ NOT applied during normal day-to-day operations.

> [!WARNING]
> HAZOP is a **brainstorming technique**, not a substitute for engineering calculations. It identifies *what can go wrong*, but a separate **Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA)** or **Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA)** is required to determine *how likely* and *how severe* the consequences are.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Pillars of HAZOP (Theoretical Foundation)

HAZOP is built on **four fundamental pillars** that must be present in every study. If any one is missing, the study is invalid.

#### Pillar 1: **Guide Words** (The "Triggers" of Imagination)
Guide words are simple, standardized words used to question the **design intent** and stimulate the team's imagination to think about deviations.

| Guide Word | Meaning | Engineering Interpretation | Example |
|------------|---------|---------------------------|---------|
| **NO / NOT** | Complete negation of the design intent | Flow stops, no reaction occurs, no signal | *No flow in the feed line* |
| **MORE** | Quantitative increase | Pressure, temperature, flow, level rises | *More pressure in the reactor* |
| **LESS** | Quantitative decrease | Pressure, temperature, flow, level drops | *Less temperature in the reboiler* |
| **REVERSE** | Logical opposite of intent | Flow in opposite direction, reverse reaction | *Reverse flow of product back to feed* |
| **AS WELL AS** | Qualitative increase / additional component present | Extra phase, extra component, contamination | *Water as well as hydrocarbon in the line* |
| **PART OF** | Qualitative decrease / less of what should be there | Lower concentration, missing component | *Lower concentration of catalyst* |
| **OTHER THAN** | Complete substitution / something entirely different | Wrong material, different state | *Steam other than cooling water* |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** In the exam, always pair a *Guide Word* with a *Process Parameter* to form a meaningful **Deviation**. Example: `MORE + PRESSURE = HIGH PRESSURE`. Just writing "MORE" alone carries **zero marks**.

#### Pillar 2: **Process Parameters** (The "Properties" of the System)
Process parameters are the measurable physical or chemical quantities that define the design intent of the system.

| Parameter | Description | Common Guide Word Application |
|-----------|-------------|------------------------------|
| **Flow** | Rate of movement of fluid | NO FLOW, REVERSE FLOW, LESS FLOW |
| **Pressure** | Force per unit area | HIGH PRESSURE, LOW PRESSURE, VACUUM |
| **Temperature** | Degree of hotness/coldness | HIGH TEMP, LOW TEMP |
| **Level** | Height of liquid in a vessel | HIGH LEVEL, LOW LEVEL, NO LEVEL |
| **Composition** | Chemical makeup | AS WELL AS (contaminant), PART OF (low concentration) |
| **Phase** | Physical state (solid, liquid, gas, vapor) | OTHER THAN phase (e.g., liquid becomes vapor) |
| **Reaction** | Chemical transformation | NO REACTION, REVERSE REACTION, MORE REACTION |
| **Time / Sequence** | Timing of operations | EARLIER, LATER, OUT OF SEQUENCE |
| **Start / Stop** | Equipment operation status | NO START, NO STOP |
| **Maintenance** | Servicing operations | INADEQUATE MAINTENANCE |

#### Pillar 3: **Nodes** (The "Examination Points")
A **Node** is a specific, well-defined location in the process where process parameters have a single design intent.

- It is usually a **pipeline section, vessel, or equipment** where the chemistry, flow, or operating conditions are uniform.
- Example: *“The pipe between Pump P-101 and Heat Exchanger E-102”* is one node.
- The HAZOP team goes **node by node**, applying each guide word to each parameter.

#### Pillar 4: **Team Composition** (The "Collective Intelligence")
HAZOP is fundamentally a **team effort**. A solo HAZOP has no credibility.

| Role | Responsibility | Typical Profile |
|------|---------------|-----------------|
| **Team Leader / Facilitator** | Guides the study, ensures discipline, summarizes deviations | Senior Process Safety Engineer with formal HAZOP training |
| **Scribe / Recorder** | Documents every discussion in the HAZOP worksheet | Junior engineer, dedicated note-taker |
| **Process Engineer** | Explains design intent, P&ID interpretation | Design / Plant process engineer |
| **Operations / Production Engineer** | Provides field reality, operating procedures | Plant shift manager / operator |
| **Mechanical / Equipment Engineer** | Discusses equipment integrity, relief devices | Static / Rotating equipment specialist |
| **Instrumentation / Control Engineer** | Addresses interlocks, alarms, control loops | DCS / PLC engineer |
| **Safety Engineer** | Identifies consequences, recommends safeguards | Process safety specialist |
| **Other Specialists** | As needed (corrosion, electrical, environmental) | Invited ad-hoc |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ideal Team Size:** **5 to 8 members** (per IEC 61882). Smaller teams miss hazards; larger teams become chaotic and slow.

### 2.2 The Standard HAZOP Worksheet Format

The HAZOP worksheet is the **legal documentation** of the study. KTU expects students to be able to draw and fill this table.

| Node | Guide Word | Parameter | Deviation | Causes | Consequences | Safeguards | Action Items |
|------|------------|-----------|-----------|--------|--------------|------------|--------------|
| 1 | NO | FLOW | No flow in feed line F-101 | Pump failure, manual valve closed, line blockage | Reactor R-101 overheats → explosion risk | Flow indicator (FI), Low flow alarm (FAL), Pump trip on low flow | Install Flow Switch (FSL) with interlock to shutdown feed |
| 1 | MORE | PRESSURE | High pressure in feed line | Blockage at downstream valve, thermal expansion | Pipe rupture, flange leak | Pressure Safety Valve (PSV), High Pressure Alarm (PAH) | Verify PSV set pressure matches design |
| 2 | LESS | TEMPERATURE | Low temperature at reactor inlet | Cooling water valve stuck open, heat exchanger fouling | Reaction rate drops → incomplete conversion → off-spec product | Temperature Indicator (TI), Low Temp Alarm (TAL) | Schedule cleaning of E-102 |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> Unlike core chemical engineering subjects, HAZOP is **qualitative**, so there are very few formulas. However, the following quantitative risk indices (often asked in KTU 14-mark questions) are mandatory:

| Symbol | Formula / Term | Description | Unit |
|--------|----------------|-------------|------|
| **SIL** | $\text{SIL} = f(\text{PFD})$ | Safety Integrity Level (1 to 4) | Dimensionless |
| **PFD** | $\text{PFD} = \frac{1}{\text{PFH} \times T_{proof}}$ | Probability of Failure on Demand (for SIF) | failures / demand |
| **PFH** | Average frequency of dangerous failure per hour | Probability of Failure per Hour | failures / hour |
| **T_proof** | $T_{proof} = \frac{T_{test}}{2} + \text{MRT}$ | Proof test interval (Mean Repair Time added) | hours |
| **Risk** | $\text{Risk} = \text{Frequency} \times \text{Consequence}$ | Fundamental risk equation | Dimensionless |
| **FAR** | $\text{FAR} = \frac{\text{No. of fatalities}}{\text{Total person-hours worked}} \times 10^8$ | Fatal Accident Rate | fatalities per 100 million hours |
| **Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)** | $\text{RRF} = \frac{1}{\text{PFD}}$ | Required reduction in risk for a SIF | Dimensionless |

> [!WARNING]
> **Absolute Value Rule:** Since the HAZOP worksheet is a markdown table, **never** use `|` (vertical bar) for "given that" or "such that" inside a table cell. Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead. (Example: `PFD \vert_{avg}`).

### 2.4 Phases of a HAZOP Study

| Phase | Activity | Output |
|-------|----------|--------|
| **Phase 1: Definition** | Define scope, objectives, boundaries, nodes | HAZOP scope document |
| **Phase 2: Information Gathering** | Collect P&IDs, PFDs, operating procedures, MSDS, equipment data | Design intent document |
| **Phase 3: Team Selection** | Appoint a multidisciplinary team | Team list |
| **Phase 4: Workshop Sessions** | Conduct node-by-node review using guide words | HAZOP worksheet (filled) |
| **Phase 5: Documentation** | Prepare HAZOP report with all deviations, causes, consequences, safeguards, and recommendations | HAZOP Report |
| **Phase 6: Follow-up** | Implement recommendations, close out action items | Action item tracker, close-out report |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step HAZOP Procedure & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Step-by-Step HAZOP Procedure

Below is the **complete, exhaustive, KTU 2024-aligned HAZOP procedure** that you must memorize for both 3-mark and 14-mark questions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every algebraic / logical step below is **explicitly written** — no shortcuts, no placeholders, no truncation.

---

#### **Step 1: Define the Scope and Objectives of the Study**

The first logical step is to **clearly define** what the HAZOP study is supposed to cover.

- The team leader (facilitator) meets with the project manager and defines:
  - **Physical boundaries** (e.g., from feed tank T-101 inlet flange to product storage T-201 outlet flange).
  - **Operational boundaries** (e.g., normal startup, normal operation, shutdown, emergency).
  - **Exclusions** (e.g., offsite pipelines, office buildings).
- The output is a **HAZOP Scope Document**, which is approved by the plant head and signed.

**Logical Reasoning:** Without a defined scope, the team may waste time on unrelated systems or miss critical areas.

---

#### **Step 2: Gather All Relevant Process Information**

The team must collect and review all design documents **before** the workshop begins.

- **Process Flow Diagrams (PFDs)** — to understand mass and energy balance.
- **Piping & Instrumentation Diagrams (P&IDs)** — to understand piping, valves, instruments, interlocks.
- **Material Safety Data Sheets (MSDS)** — to understand chemical hazards.
- **Equipment Data Sheets** — to know design pressures, temperatures, capacities.
- **Operating Procedures** — to understand how the plant is actually run (not just designed).
- **Cause & Effect Matrix** — to understand existing interlocks and shutdown logic.
- **Plot Plan and Layout** — to understand spatial hazards (e.g., proximity to ignition sources).

**Logical Reasoning:** The team cannot identify deviations if they do not first understand the *design intent*.

---

#### **Step 3: Divide the Plant into Logical Nodes**

A **node** is a small, manageable section of the process where the design intent of all parameters is well-defined.

- Example: For a distillation column system, nodes could be:
  - **Node 1:** Feed line from pump P-101 to preheater E-101.
  - **Node 2:** Preheater E-101 outlet to column C-101 inlet.
  - **Node 3:** Column C-101 reboiler circuit (reboiler E-102 + bottom pump P-102).
  - **Node 4:** Column C-101 condenser circuit (condenser E-103 + reflux drum D-101 + reflux pump P-103).
  - **Node 5:** Overhead vapor line from column to condenser.
  - **Node 6:** Bottoms product line to storage.

**Selection Rule:** The start and end of a node is usually a **major equipment** (pump, vessel, heat exchanger, control valve) where the design intent changes.

---

#### **Step 4: Select and Brief the Multidisciplinary Team**

A competent HAZOP team is selected, and a pre-meeting briefing is conducted.

- Team size: **5 to 8 members** (per IEC 61882).
- Members: Process, Mechanical, Instrumentation, Operations, Safety engineers + Scribe.
- Pre-briefing covers: scope, schedule, ground rules, confidentiality, and the HAZOP methodology.

**Logical Reasoning:** A study by a single engineer has cognitive bias. A team provides diverse perspectives and reduces blind spots.

---

#### **Step 5: For Each Node, State the Design Intent**

The team leader reads out the **Design Intent** for the node. The design intent is a clear, one-sentence statement of what the node is *supposed to do* under normal conditions.

- Example for Node 1: *"Transfer liquid hydrocarbon feed from storage tank T-101 to preheater E-101 at a flow rate of 50 m³/h and a pressure of 5 bar gauge."*

**Logical Reasoning:** The guide words are applied *relative to* the design intent. Without stating it first, "MORE" or "LESS" has no reference.

---

#### **Step 6: Apply Each Guide Word to Each Process Parameter**

For the current node, the team systematically goes through every combination of *Guide Word* and *Process Parameter* to generate **deviations**.

The standard deviation matrix is:

$$
\text{Deviation} = \text{Guide Word} + \text{Process Parameter}
$$

**Example deviations for Node 1:**
- `NO` + `FLOW` → **No flow** in feed line
- `MORE` + `PRESSURE` → **High pressure** in feed line
- `LESS` + `TEMPERATURE` → **Low temperature** at preheater inlet
- `REVERSE` + `FLOW` → **Reverse flow** from preheater back to tank
- `AS WELL AS` + `COMPOSITION` → **Contamination** (water in hydrocarbon)
- `OTHER THAN` + `PHASE` → **Vapor instead of liquid** in feed line
- `PART OF` + `COMPOSITION` → **Loss of light component** in feed

---

#### **Step 7: For Each Deviation, Identify Causes, Consequences, and Existing Safeguards**

This is the heart of the HAZOP study. For each deviation, the team asks three sequential questions:

$$
\text{(i) What could CAUSE this deviation?}
$$
$$
\text{(ii) What are the CONSEQUENCES if it occurs?}
$$
\text{(iii) What existing SAFEGUARDS protect against it?}

$$

**Worked Example for "No Flow in Feed Line" (Node 1):**

| Element | Identification |
|---------|----------------|
| **Cause 1** | Feed pump P-101 fails (mechanical seizure, power loss, coupling break) |
| **Cause 2** | Manual valve HV-101 mistakenly closed by operator |
| **Cause 3** | Line blockage due to debris, solidified product, or foreign object |
| **Cause 4** | Suction side vapor lock (NPSH problem) |
| **Consequence 1** | Loss of feed to downstream reactor → reactor temperature drops → catalyst deactivation, off-spec product |
| **Consequence 2** | Pump runs dead-headed against closed valve → pump overheating, mechanical seal failure → fire hazard |
| **Consequence 3** | Low flow trips upstream feed tank low-level interlock (LALL-101) → plant shutdown, production loss |
| **Existing Safeguard 1** | Flow transmitter (FT-101) with low-low flow alarm (FALL) |
| **Existing Safeguard 2** | Pump P-101 has a mechanical seal temperature monitor with high alarm |
| **Existing Safeguard 3** | Check valve CV-101 prevents reverse siphoning |

---

#### **Step 8: Recommend Additional Safeguards / Action Items**

If the team feels that the existing safeguards are **insufficient**, they recommend additional ones. The 4-tier hierarchy is:

$$
\text{Hierarchy of Controls: Eliminate} \rightarrow \text{Substitute} \rightarrow \text{Engineer} \rightarrow \text{Admin} \rightarrow \text{PPE}
$$

**Recommended Action Item for "No Flow" example:**
- Install a **Flow Switch (FSL-101)** with a hardwired interlock to trip pump P-101 on sustained low flow.
- Add a **Pump Dead-Head Pressure Relief Valve (PSV-101B)** to protect against overpressure during dead-heading.
- Update the **Standard Operating Procedure (SOP)** with a step to verify pump discharge pressure before opening the manual valve.

---

#### **Step 9: Document Everything in the HAZOP Worksheet**

The scribe records every discussion in the HAZOP worksheet (refer Section 2.2). This becomes a **legal document** under PSM regulations (OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119).

---

#### **Step 10: Prepare the HAZOP Report and Follow-Up**

A formal HAZOP report is prepared with:
- Executive summary
- Scope, objectives, team members
- Methodology used
- All HAZOP worksheets (signed by the team)
- List of action items with **risk ranking** (e.g., High / Medium / Low)
- Action item tracker
- Management close-out report

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: HAZOP vs Other PHA Techniques

A common KTU 14-mark question asks students to **compare HAZOP with other PHA techniques**. The following matrix is mandatory memorization.

| Feature | HAZOP | What-If | FMEA | Fault Tree | Checklists |
|---------|-------|---------|------|------------|------------|
| **Full Form** | Hazard and Operability | What-If Analysis | Failure Mode and Effects Analysis | Fault Tree Analysis | Checklist Analysis |
| **Approach** | Forward, deductive | Open-ended brainstorming | Bottom-up, inductive | Top-down, deductive | Pre-defined lists |
| **Tool** | Guide Words + Parameters | Free-form questions | Tabular format | Boolean logic gates | Question lists |
| **Best For** | Complex continuous processes (chemical plants) | Early design, novel processes | Mechanical/equipment failures | Quantifying system reliability | Repetitive, well-known systems |
| **Output** | List of deviations + actions | List of scenarios | RPN (Risk Priority Number) | Cut sets, top event probability | Compliance / gap list |
| **Team Size** | 5–8 | 3–5 | 3–5 | 2–4 specialists | 1–2 (can be solo) |
| **Quantification** | Qualitative | Qualitative | Semi-quantitative (RPN) | Quantitative | Qualitative |
| **KTU Module Coverage** | ✅ Module 4 (Primary) | ✅ Module 4 (Briefly) | ❌ Module 5 | ❌ Module 5 | ✅ Module 4 (Briefly) |

### 3.3 Advantages and Limitations of HAZOP

#### Advantages
- ✅ Highly systematic — leaves little room for oversight.
- ✅ Exhaustive — covers a very large number of potential deviations.
- ✅ Team-based — leverages multidisciplinary expertise.
- ✅ Generates a **written record** that is legally defensible.
- ✅ Excellent for **complex, continuous chemical processes**.

#### Limitations
- ❌ **Time-consuming and expensive** — can take weeks for a large plant.
- ❌ Requires a **highly experienced facilitator** — poor leadership = poor study.
- ❌ **Not suitable** for software, human factors, or ergonomic issues.
- ❌ Generates a **large number of action items** that may not all be addressed.
- ❌ Quality is **highly dependent on team competence** and P&ID accuracy.
- ❌ Does **not quantify risk** — must be supplemented by **LOPA / QRA**.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 HAZOP Procedure Flowchart

The following Mermaid flowchart illustrates the **end-to-end HAZOP procedure** that must be memorized for KTU exams.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Define HAZOP Scope and Objectives] --> B[Gather Process Information - P and IDs, PFDs, MSDS]
    B --> C[Select Multidisciplinary HAZOP Team - 5 to 8 members]
    C --> D[Divide Plant into Logical Nodes]
    D --> E[For Each Node, State Design Intent]
    E --> F[Apply Guide Words to Process Parameters]
    F --> G[Generate Deviation: Guide Word plus Parameter]
    G --> H{Is Deviation Meaningful?}
    H -- No --> F
    H -- Yes --> I[Identify Causes of Deviation]
    I --> J[Identify Consequences of Deviation]
    J --> K[Identify Existing Safeguards]
    K --> L{Are Safeguards Sufficient?}
    L -- No --> M[Recommend Additional Action Items]
    L -- Yes --> N[Record in HAZOP Worksheet]
    M --> N
    N --> O{All Nodes Covered?}
    O -- No --> E
    O -- Yes --> P[Prepare Final HAZOP Report]
    P --> Q[Implement Action Items and Close Out]
    Q --> R[END]
```

### 4.3 Guide Word vs Parameter Deviation Matrix (Reference Grid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph PARAMETERS[PROCESS PARAMETERS]
        P1[FLOW]
        P2[PRESSURE]
        P3[TEMPERATURE]
        P4[LEVEL]
        P5[COMPOSITION]
        P6[PHASE]
    end

    subgraph GUIDEWORDS[GUIDE WORDS]
        G1[NO or NOT]
        G2[MORE]
        G3[LESS]
        G4[REVERSE]
        G5[AS WELL AS]
        G6[PART OF]
        G7[OTHER THAN]
    end

    G1 --> D1[Deviation: NO FLOW, NO PRESSURE, etc]
    G2 --> D2[Deviation: HIGH FLOW, HIGH PRESSURE, etc]
    G3 --> D3[Deviation: LOW FLOW, LOW PRESSURE, etc]
    G4 --> D4[Deviation: REVERSE FLOW, REVERSE REACTION, etc]
    G5 --> D5[Deviation: CONTAMINATION, EXTRA PHASE, etc]
    G6 --> D6[Deviation: LOW CONCENTRATION, MISSING COMPONENT, etc]
    G7 --> D7[Deviation: WRONG MATERIAL, WRONG STATE, etc]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Reading Guide for Mermaid Diagrams:** Each rectangle is a *process step* (or data element), and each arrow shows the *direction of information flow*. The team layer feeds expertise into the core analysis layer, which in turn produces documented outputs.

### 4.4 Hierarchy of Controls (Safeguard Selection Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Deviation Identified] --> B{Eliminate the Hazard}
    B -- Possible --> C[Inherent Safe Design - Modify Process]
    B -- Not Possible --> D{Substitute with Less Hazardous Material}
    D -- Possible --> E[Substitution - Replace Chemical]
    D -- Not Possible --> F{Engineered Controls - Add SIF, PSV, Interlock}
    F -- Possible --> G[Add Safety Instrumented Function - SIL Rated]
    F -- Not Possible --> H{Administrative Controls - Procedures, Training}
    H -- Possible --> I[Add SOPs, Permits, Signage]
    H -- Not Possible --> J[PPE - Last Resort]
```
