## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 KTU Examiner's Note on Mark Distribution

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU 2024 Mark Pattern for Process Safety Engineering (OECHT723):**
> - **Part A:** 2 questions × 3 marks = **6 marks** (answer any 2 out of 3)
> - **Part B:** Module Internal Choice — 1 question × 14 marks = **14 marks** (must be answered)
> - **Total per module end assessment:** ~20 marks
> - **Cognitive Levels tested (per KTU Bloom's mapping):** *Remember* (L1), *Understand* (L2), *Apply* (L3), *Analyze* (L4)

---

### 5.2 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define HAZOP. List any four guide words used in a HAZOP study with their meanings.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
HAZOP (Hazard and Operability Study) is a structured, systematic, team-based risk assessment technique used in process industries to identify potential hazards and operability problems by applying guide words to process parameters at specific nodes, in order to find deviations from the design intent.

**Four Guide Words with Meanings (4 × 0.5 = 2 Marks):**

1. **NO / NOT** — Complete negation of the design intent. *Example:* No flow in the line.
2. **MORE** — Quantitative increase. *Example:* More pressure (i.e., high pressure).
3. **LESS** — Quantitative decrease. *Example:* Less temperature (i.e., low temperature).
4. **REVERSE** — Logical opposite of design intent. *Example:* Reverse flow in a feed line.

*(Note: If student writes any other four guide words like AS WELL AS, PART OF, OTHER THAN — full marks are awarded.)*

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: What is a "node" in a HAZOP study? Why is a multidisciplinary team essential for HAZOP?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Node (1.5 Marks):**
A *node* is a specific, well-defined location in the process (such as a pipeline section, a vessel, or a piece of equipment) where the design intent of all process parameters is uniform and clearly defined. The HAZOP team examines one node at a time, applying each guide word to each process parameter to generate deviations.

**Multidisciplinary Team (1.5 Marks):**
A multidisciplinary team is essential because:
- A single engineer has **cognitive bias** and may overlook hazards outside their domain.
- Process engineers understand the design intent, operations engineers know real field behavior, instrumentation engineers address control loops, mechanical engineers address equipment integrity, and safety engineers focus on consequences and safeguards.
- Together, they provide a **360° view** of the system, ensuring that no significant hazard is missed.
- The standard **IEC 61882** recommends a team of 5 to 8 members from different disciplines.

---

### 5.3 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 Scheme, every Part B question has an **OR option**. Below, **Question A** and **Question B** are two completely independent alternatives — a student answers ONE in full.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: With a neat flowchart, explain the step-by-step procedure for conducting a HAZOP study. Discuss the role of the team leader and the importance of guide words in this study. (14 Marks)**

**Sub-part (a): Flowchart and Step-by-Step Procedure (7 Marks)**

**Step 1: Define Scope (0.5 Mark)**
Clearly define the physical and operational boundaries, exclusions, and objectives of the study. The output is the HAZOP scope document.

**Step 2: Gather Information (0.5 Mark)**
Collect P&IDs, PFDs, MSDS, equipment data sheets, operating procedures, cause & effect matrix, and plot plans. Review the design intent.

**Step 3: Team Selection (0.5 Mark)**
Select a multidisciplinary team of 5 to 8 members including process, operations, mechanical, instrumentation, safety engineers, and a scribe.

**Step 4: Node Definition (0.5 Mark)**
Divide the plant into logical nodes — small, manageable sections where the design intent is uniform (e.g., feed line section, heat exchanger circuit, column overhead system).

**Step 5: State Design Intent for Each Node (0.5 Mark)**
For each node, the team leader reads out a one-sentence statement of what the node is *supposed to do* under normal conditions.

**Step 6: Apply Guide Words to Parameters (1.0 Mark)**
For each parameter (flow, pressure, temperature, level, composition, phase), apply each guide word (NO, MORE, LESS, REVERSE, AS WELL AS, PART OF, OTHER THAN) to generate deviations.

**Step 7: Identify Causes, Consequences, Safeguards (1.5 Marks)**
For every meaningful deviation, the team identifies:
- **Causes** (what could lead to the deviation)
- **Consequences** (what happens if the deviation occurs)
- **Existing safeguards** (what protects against it)

**Step 8: Recommend Action Items (1.0 Mark)**
If existing safeguards are insufficient, recommend additional controls following the hierarchy: Eliminate → Substitute → Engineered → Administrative → PPE.

**Step 9: Document in Worksheet (0.5 Mark)**
The scribe records every discussion in the HAZOP worksheet with columns: Node | Guide Word | Parameter | Deviation | Causes | Consequences | Safeguards | Actions.

**Step 10: Report and Follow-Up (0.5 Mark)**
Prepare the final HAZOP report, list action items with risk ranking, and track them to closure.

*(Flowchart to be drawn as shown in Section 4.1 — carries 1.0 Mark within the 7 marks.)*

**Sub-part (b): Role of Team Leader and Importance of Guide Words (7 Marks)**

**Role of Team Leader / Facilitator (3.5 Marks):**
1. **Methodology Expert** — Ensures the team follows the structured HAZOP methodology without deviation.
2. **Time Keeper** — Keeps the workshop on schedule; prevents the team from going into excessive detail on trivial issues.
3. **Discussion Moderator** — Encourages quiet members to speak, manages dominant members, resolves disagreements.
4. **Deviation Validator** — Decides which guide-word-parameter combinations are *meaningful* for the node (e.g., "REVERSE + TEMPERATURE" is meaningless).
5. **Quality Assurer** — Ensures the HAZOP worksheet is complete, accurate, and signed off.
6. **Final Reporter** — Presents the findings to plant management.

**Importance of Guide Words (3.5 Marks):**
1. **Cognitive Trigger** — Guide words force the team to systematically think of *deviations* they might otherwise overlook. They act as "mental shortcuts" to failure modes.
2. **Standardization** — They provide a *common language* that any trained HAZOP team worldwide can understand.
3. **Completeness** — The 7 standard guide words (NO, MORE, LESS, REVERSE, AS WELL AS, PART OF, OTHER THAN) ensure that almost all possible deviations are covered.
4. **Applicability to All Parameters** — A single guide word can be combined with any process parameter (flow, pressure, temperature, etc.) to generate a meaningful deviation.
5. **Efficiency** — They reduce the time required to brainstorm — the team does not have to invent questions; the questions are pre-defined.
6. **Documentation Clarity** — They enable a clean, uniform worksheet format that is auditable and legally defensible.
7. **Limitation Awareness** — Knowing the guide words also helps the team recognize *non-meaningful* combinations (e.g., REVERSE + LEVEL), which improves discussion quality.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: (a) Explain the four pillars of HAZOP: guide words, parameters, nodes, and team. (7 Marks)**
**(b) For a feed line carrying liquid hydrocarbon from a storage tank to a reactor, prepare a HAZOP worksheet for the deviation "No Flow" — listing at least 3 causes, 3 consequences, and 3 safeguards. (7 Marks)**

**Sub-part (a): Four Pillars of HAZOP (7 Marks)**

**Pillar 1: Guide Words (1.75 Marks)**
- Guide words are standardized trigger words used to deviate from the design intent.
- The 7 standard guide words are: **NO / NOT, MORE, LESS, REVERSE, AS WELL AS, PART OF, OTHER THAN**.
- They are combined with process parameters to form deviations.
- Example: MORE + PRESSURE = HIGH PRESSURE deviation.

**Pillar 2: Process Parameters (1.75 Marks)**
- Process parameters are the measurable quantities that define the design intent of a node.
- Common parameters: **Flow, Pressure, Temperature, Level, Composition, Phase, Reaction, Time, Start/Stop, Maintenance**.
- They represent the *what* of the design intent that the guide words deviate from.

**Pillar 3: Nodes (1.75 Marks)**
- A node is a specific, well-defined location in the process (pipe section, vessel, equipment) where the design intent of all parameters is uniform.
- The plant is divided into multiple nodes, and the HAZOP study proceeds **one node at a time**.
- Example: *“Pipe between Pump P-101 and Heat Exchanger E-101”* is one node.
- Each node has a clearly stated design intent written in a single sentence.

**Pillar 4: Multidisciplinary Team (1.75 Marks)**
- HAZOP is fundamentally a *team-based* exercise — it cannot be performed by a single individual.
- A standard team of 5 to 8 members includes: **Team Leader (Facilitator), Scribe (Recorder), Process Engineer, Operations Engineer, Mechanical Engineer, Instrumentation Engineer, Safety Engineer**.
- Each member brings a unique perspective, ensuring comprehensive hazard identification.
- The team leader is the most critical member — they drive the methodology, manage the discussion, and validate the deviations.

**Sub-part (b): HAZOP Worksheet for "No Flow" in Feed Line (7 Marks)**

**Node Description:** *Liquid hydrocarbon feed line from Storage Tank T-101 to Reactor R-101, transfer pump P-101 in between. Design intent: Transfer 50 m³/h of liquid hydrocarbon at 5 bar gauge and 30 °C.*

**HAZOP Worksheet:**

| Element | Identification |
|---------|----------------|
| **Node** | Feed line from T-101 outlet flange to R-101 inlet flange (including pump P-101) |
| **Guide Word** | NO |
| **Parameter** | FLOW |
| **Deviation** | **No Flow in the feed line** |
| **Cause 1** | Pump P-101 mechanical failure (impeller seizure, coupling break, motor burn-out) |
| **Cause 2** | Manual isolation valve HV-101 on suction side left closed by mistake after maintenance |
| **Cause 3** | Line blockage due to solidified wax, debris, or foreign object lodged in strainer |
| **Consequence 1** | Loss of feed to reactor R-101 → temperature rise in exothermic reaction → potential runaway reaction → explosion hazard |
| **Consequence 2** | Pump P-101 running dead-headed against closed downstream valve → pump overheating, mechanical seal failure → flammable hydrocarbon leak → fire hazard |
| **Consequence 3** | Loss of production → off-spec product, reactor catalyst deactivation, plant shutdown, revenue loss |
| **Existing Safeguard 1** | Flow Transmitter FT-101 with Low-Low Flow Alarm (FALL) at panel |
| **Existing Safeguard 2** | Pump motor ammeter with high current alarm (motor overload) |
| **Existing Safeguard 3** | Check valve CV-101 in discharge line prevents reverse siphoning back to tank |
| **Recommended Action 1** | Install Flow Switch FSL-101 with hardwired interlock to trip pump P-101 on sustained low flow |
| **Recommended Action 2** | Install Pressure Safety Valve PSV-101B on pump discharge to protect against dead-head overpressure |
| **Recommended Action 3** | Update Standard Operating Procedure to verify pump discharge pressure before opening manual valve HV-101 |

**Mark Distribution for Sub-part (b):**
- Node, Guide Word, Parameter, Deviation: **1 Mark**
- 3 Causes: **1.5 Marks (0.5 each)**
- 3 Consequences: **1.5 Marks (0.5 each)**
- 3 Existing Safeguards: **1.5 Marks (0.5 each)**
- 3 Recommended Actions: **1.5 Marks (0.5 each)**

---

### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in HAZOP Questions:**
>
> 1. **Writing guide words without parameters** — "MORE" alone carries no marks; "MORE + PRESSURE = HIGH PRESSURE" carries full marks. *(−1 to −2 marks lost)*
>
> 2. **Confusing "Cause" with "Consequence"** — Students often swap these. *Cause* = what makes the deviation happen. *Consequence* = what happens after. Mixing them up = −1.5 marks lost.
>
> 3. **Writing too many guide words** — Many students list 8 to 10 guide words. Only the **7 standard ones** (NO, MORE, LESS, REVERSE, AS WELL AS, PART OF, OTHER THAN) are accepted. Inventing new ones = no credit.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the multidisciplinary team** — A common 14-mark question asks for the "team composition." Students often write only "engineers and safety officer" — too vague. Always specify roles: **Team Leader, Scribe, Process, Operations, Mechanical, Instrumentation, Safety**.
>
> 5. **Confusing HAZOP with FMEA or Fault Tree** — These are different techniques. HAZOP is *qualitative, guide-word-based, and forward-thinking*. Mixing methodologies in a comparison question = −2 to −3 marks.
>
> 6. **Not stating the design intent before deviations** — In a worksheet, the design intent must be written *first*, before the deviations. Skipping this = −1 mark.
>
> 7. **Ignoring the hierarchy of controls** — When recommending safeguards, follow: *Eliminate → Substitute → Engineered → Administrative → PPE*. Jumping straight to "PPE" = −1 mark.

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — HAZOP in 60 Seconds**

- ✅ **Full Form:** Hazard and Operability Study — a *qualitative, team-based* PHA technique.
- ✅ **Standard:** **IEC 61882** (international HAZOP standard).
- ✅ **Origin:** Developed by **ICI (UK)** in the 1970s after the **Flixborough disaster**.
- ✅ **7 Guide Words:** **NO, MORE, LESS, REVERSE, AS WELL AS, PART OF, OTHER THAN** — *memorize verbatim*.
- ✅ **Key Parameters:** **Flow, Pressure, Temperature, Level, Composition, Phase, Reaction, Time**.
- ✅ **Four Pillars:** **Guide Words + Parameters + Nodes + Team** — must all be present.
- ✅ **Team Size:** **5 to 8 members**, multidisciplinary, with a dedicated **Team Leader + Scribe**.
- ✅ **Procedure:** Define scope → Gather info → Select team → Define nodes → State design intent → Apply guide words → Identify causes/consequences/safeguards → Recommend actions → Document → Report.
- ✅ **HAZOP Worksheet Columns:** *Node | Guide Word | Parameter | Deviation | Causes | Consequences | Safeguards | Action Items*.
- ✅ **Hierarchy of Controls:** **Eliminate → Substitute → Engineer → Admin → PPE**.
- ✅ **Advantages:** Systematic, exhaustive, legally defensible, team-based.
- ✅ **Limitations:** Time-consuming, requires expert facilitator, qualitative only, dependent on P&ID accuracy.
- ✅ **Difference from FMEA:** HAZOP is for *process* deviations; FMEA is for *equipment* failure modes.
- ✅ **Difference from Fault Tree:** HAZOP is *forward, deductive, qualitative*; FTA is *top-down, Boolean, quantitative*.
- ✅ **Always state the design intent first** — never apply guide words without it.
- ✅ **Cause ≠ Consequence** — common trap; causes come *before*, consequences *after* the deviation.
- ✅ **Always cite IEC 61882** in theory questions — it shows KTU 2024 standard awareness.

---

> **END OF NOTES — MODULE 4: HAZOP**
> *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme | OECHT723 - Process Safety Engineering*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bow Tie Diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT723 - PROCESS SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Bow Tie Diagram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Bow Tie Diagram (BTD)** is a graphical risk evaluation and management tool used in process safety engineering that integrates two classical safety analysis methodologies — **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)** on the left-hand side (causation pathway) and **Event Tree Analysis (ETA)** on the right-hand side (consequence pathway) — joined at a central pivotal event known as the **Top Event (TE)**. The visual shape resembles a bow tie (or hourglass), hence the nomenclature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *A Bow Tie diagram is a risk management diagram used to describe and analyze the pathways of a risk from causes (threats) to consequences, displaying the preventive and mitigative barriers in place to control the hazard.*

**Standard KTU Terminology Table:**

| Term | KTU Standard Notation |
|------|---------------------|
| Top Event | TE |
| Preventive Barrier | PB |
| Mitigative Barrier | MB |
| Loss of Primary Containment | LOPC |
| Safety Critical Element | SCE |
| Risk Based Decision | RBD |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The "Dam Analogy":**
> Imagine a large water reservoir (the **hazard source**) held back by a dam. On one side of the dam (left side) are potential **causes** that could crack the dam: an earthquake, a flood, structural fatigue. On the other side (right side) is the **village downstream** that would be flooded if the dam fails. The **bow tie** essentially shows: threats on the left → dam breach (top event) → flooding consequences on the right. The **barriers** are the monitoring systems, reinforcement walls, and emergency evacuation plans placed both upstream and downstream of the dam. If multiple barriers fail, disaster occurs. This is exactly how a chemical plant's Bow Tie works!

### 1.3 Origin and Industry Standard

The Bow Tie methodology was developed by **Ian C. G. (1968)** and later formalized under standards such as:
- **ISO 31000** — Risk Management
- **IEC 31010** — Risk Assessment Techniques
- **OGP (International Association of Oil & Gas Producers) Report 544** — Bow Tie standards
- **CCPS (Center for Chemical Process Safety)** Guidelines

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Metric in Bow Tie Analysis:**
> The fundamental measure of barrier effectiveness is the **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD)**, denoted symbolically as **PFD**. Industry-acceptable PFD values typically range from **10⁻¹ to 10⁻⁶**, depending on Safety Integrity Level (SIL).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Components of a Bow Tie Diagram

The Bow Tie consists of **five principal structural elements**:

1. **Hazard (H)** — The source of potential harm (e.g., hydrocarbon inventory under pressure).
2. **Top Event (TE)** — The moment of control loss (e.g., loss of containment).
3. **Threats (T₁, T₂, ... Tₙ)** — Pathway events leading FROM the hazard TO the top event (causes).
4. **Consequences (C₁, C₂, ... Cₙ)** — Outcome pathways FROM the top event TO harm realization.
5. **Barriers** — Two types:
   - **Preventive Barriers (PB)** — Located on the left side; prevent threats from reaching the top event.
   - **Mitigative Barriers (MB)** — Located on the right side; reduce severity/impact of consequences.

### 2.2 Logical Construction — Step-by-Step Reasoning

**Step 1: Identify the Hazard**
- Determine the energy source or hazardous material inventory capable of causing harm.
- Example: 10,000 kg of propane stored at 8 bar(g).

**Step 2: Define the Top Event**
- The specific undesired event representing loss of control.
- Example: "Catastrophic rupture of propane storage vessel."

**Step 3: Enumerate Threats (Causes)**
- List credible initiating events from HAZOP, FMEA, or historical incident databases.
- Examples: corrosion, overpressure, external impact, procedural error.

**Step 4: Identify Preventive Barriers**
- For each threat pathway, list controls that PREVENT threat realization.
- Examples: pressure relief valve, corrosion monitoring, procedural SOP.

**Step 5: Enumerate Consequences**
- Map all potential outcomes if the top event occurs (fire, explosion, toxic release).

**Step 6: Identify Mitigative Barriers**
- For each consequence pathway, list controls that LIMIT impact.
- Examples: firewater deluge, gas detection, evacuation plan, bund wall.

### 2.3 Relationship to Other Safety Methodologies

| Methodology | Side of Bow Tie | Function |
|-------------|----------------|----------|
| Fault Tree Analysis (FTA) | LEFT side | Boolean logic decomposition of threats |
| Event Tree Analysis (ETA) | RIGHT side | Sequential branching of consequences |
| Swiss Cheese Model (Reason, 1990) | BOTH sides | Conceptual basis for layered barriers |
| HAZOP Study | LEFT side | Source of threat identification |
| LOPA | CENTER | Quantifies barrier effectiveness |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMULA — Barrier Failure Probability:**

The probability of the Top Event occurring when all preventive barriers fail is calculated using the multiplicative law of independent events:

$$
P_{TE} = P_{H} \times \prod_{i=1}^{n} PFD_{PB,i}
$$

Where:
- $P_{TE}$ = Probability of Top Event realization
- $P_{H}$ = Probability of hazard exposure (typically 1.0 for continuously present hazards)
- $PFD_{PB,i}$ = Probability of Failure on Demand of the $i$-th Preventive Barrier
- $n$ = Number of preventive barriers on a given threat pathway

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMULA — Consequence Risk Frequency:**

$$
f_{C,j} = P_{TE} \times \prod_{k=1}^{m} PFD_{MB,k}
$$

Where:
- $f_{C,j}$ = Frequency of the $j$-th consequence occurring (events/year)
- $m$ = Number of mitigative barriers on that consequence pathway
- $PFD_{MB,k}$ = Probability of Failure on Demand of the $k$-th Mitigative Barrier

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMULA — Individual Risk:**

$$
IR = \sum_{j=1}^{J} f_{C,j} \times P_{d,j}
$$

Where:
- $IR$ = Individual Risk (per year)
- $P_{d,j}$ = Probability of fatality given consequence $j$ occurs
- $J$ = Total number of consequence pathways

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMULA — Societal Risk (F-N Curve Data):**

$$
F(N) = \sum_{j=1}^{J} f_{C,j} \quad \text{for all consequences with fatalities} \geq N
$$

### 2.5 Engineering Utility

Bow Tie diagrams are used industrially for:
- **ALARP demonstration** under COMAH (UK) and SEVESO III (EU) directives.
- **Safety Case** preparation for offshore platforms (UK HSE).
- **Operational discipline** in refinery and petrochemical plants.
- **Audit documentation** for ISO 45001 and OSHA 1910 compliance.
- **Emergency Response Planning (ERP)** training.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivative Derivation 1: Combined Pathway Risk Frequency

Consider a Bow Tie pathway with hazard H, single threat T₁, single Top Event TE, and two consequences C₁ and C₂. The pathway contains:
- Preventive Barriers: PB-A (PFD = 0.1), PB-B (PFD = 0.01)
- Mitigative Barriers: MB-1 (PFD = 0.05) on C₁; MB-2 (PFD = 0.1) on C₂

**Step 1 — Compute Top Event probability:**

$$
P_{TE} = P_{H} \times PFD_{PB,A} \times PFD_{PB,B}
$$

Substituting $P_{H} = 1.0$:

$$
P_{TE} = 1.0 \times 0.1 \times 0.01
$$

$$
P_{TE} = 0.001 \quad \text{(or } 1 \times 10^{-3} \text{ per year, assuming annual demand rate of 1)}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute consequence frequencies:**

For C₁:

$$
f_{C,1} = P_{TE} \times PFD_{MB,1}
$$

$$
f_{C,1} = 0.001 \times 0.05 = 5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ events/year}
$$

For C₂:

$$
f_{C,2} = P_{TE} \times PFD_{MB,2}
$$

$$
f_{C,2} = 0.001 \times 0.1 = 1 \times 10^{-4} \text{ events/year}
$$

**Step 3 — Combined Pathway Risk:**

$$
f_{Total} = f_{C,1} + f_{C,2}
$$

$$
f_{Total} = 5 \times 10^{-5} + 1 \times 10^{-4} = 1.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ events/year}
$$

This translates to approximately **1 event every 6,667 years** for this pathway.

### 3.2 Derivative Derivation 2: Barrier Risk Reduction Factor (BRRF)

The effectiveness of a Bow Tie barrier system is quantified through the **Barrier Risk Reduction Factor (BRRF)**:

**Step 1 — Define unprotected pathway risk:**

$$
R_{unprotected} = P_{H} \times P_{TE \mid \text{no barriers}} \times P_{d}
$$

**Step 2 — Define protected pathway risk:**

$$
R_{protected} = P_{H} \times P_{TE} \times \prod PFD_{MB} \times P_{d}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute BRRF:**

$$
BRRF = \frac{R_{unprotected}}{R_{protected}}
$$

$$
BRRF = \frac{P_{TE \mid \text{no barriers}}}{P_{TE} \times \prod PFD_{MB}}
$$

**Step 4 — Express in logarithmic form (dB scale):**

$$
BRRF_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(BRRF)
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Industry typically requires BRRF ≥ 1000 (30 dB) for high-consequence process hazards.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Barrier Hierarchy Types

| Barrier Class | Type | PFD Range | Example |
|--------------|------|-----------|---------|
| Passive Hardware | Preventive | $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-6}$ | Rupture disc, bund wall |
| Active Hardware (Instrumented) | Preventive | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-4}$ | SIS with SIL 1/2/3 |
| Active Hardware (Mechanical) | Preventive | $10^{-1}$ to $10^{-3}$ | Block valve, isolation |
| Procedural | Preventive | $10^{-1}$ to $10^{-2}$ | SOP, permit-to-work |
| Human Action | Mitigative | $10^{-1}$ to $10^{-2}$ | Emergency shutdown by operator |
| Detection + Response | Mitigative | $10^{-1}$ to $10^{-3}$ | Gas detection + evacuation |

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem:** A naphtha storage tank has the following Bow Tie pathway:
> - Hazard: 50,000 kg naphtha @ atmospheric pressure
> - Threat: External forklift impact (frequency = 0.1/year)
> - Preventive Barrier: Concrete crash barrier (PFD = 0.05)
> - Top Event: Tank shell puncture
> - Mitigative Barrier 1: Foam pourer system (PFD = 0.1)
> - Mitigative Barrier 2: Emergency response team (PFD = 0.3)
> - Consequence: Pool fire with P(d) = 0.4 fatality probability in exposure zone

**Solution — Step by Step:**

Step 1: Compute TE probability.

$$
P_{TE} = 0.1 \times 0.05 = 0.005 \text{ /year}
$$

Step 2: Compute combined mitigative failure probability.

$$
\prod PFD_{MB} = 0.1 \times 0.3 = 0.03
$$

Step 3: Compute consequence frequency.

$$
f_{C} = 0.005 \times 0.03 = 1.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ events/year}
$$

Step 4: Compute individual risk.

$$
IR = 1.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.4 = 6 \times 10^{-5} \text{ /year}
$$

Step 5: Compare with acceptable IR.

> Industry tolerability: $IR \leq 10^{-6}$ /year (ALARP region). Here $6 \times 10^{-5}$ > $10^{-6}$, so additional risk reduction is mandatory.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Schematic — Generic Bow Tie Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H[hazard] --> T1[threat 1]
    H --> T2[threat 2]
    H --> T3[threat 3]
    T1 --> PB1[preventive barrier 1]
    PB1 --> PB2[preventive barrier 2]
    PB2 --> TE[top event]
    T2 --> PB3[preventive barrier 3]
    PB3 --> TE
    T3 --> PB4[preventive barrier 4]
    PB4 --> TE
    TE --> MB1[mitigative barrier 1]
    TE --> MB2[mitigative barrier 2]
    MB1 --> C1[consequence 1 fire]
    MB2 --> C2[consequence 2 explosion]
    MB1 --> MB3[mitigative barrier 3]
    MB3 --> C3[consequence 3 toxic release]
    style H fill:#ffcc99,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style TE fill:#ff6666,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px
    style PB1 fill:#99ccff
    style PB2 fill:#99ccff
    style PB3 fill:#99ccff
    style PB4 fill:#99ccff
    style MB1 fill:#99ff99
    style MB2 fill:#99ff99
    style MB3 fill:#99ff99
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Schematic — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[input: process data] --> B[business context]
    B --> C[hazard identification]
    C --> D[top event definition]
    D --> E[threat enumeration]
    D --> F[consequence mapping]
    E --> G[preventive barrier assessment]
    F --> H[mitigative barrier assessment]
    G --> I[PFD calculation]
    H --> I
    I --> J[risk matrix evaluation]
    J --> K{ALARP demonstration}
    K -->|yes| L[bow tie approved]
    K -->|no| M[additional controls]
    M --> G
    M --> H
    style D fill:#ff6666
    style K fill:#ffff66
```

### 4.3 Symbolic Representation Matrix

| Position in Bow Tie | Symbol | Color Code | Functional Role |
|---------------------|--------|-----------|-----------------|
| Far left | H | Orange | Hazard source |
| Left of TE | PB₁, PB₂ | Blue | Preventive controls |
| Center | TE | Red | Loss event |
| Right of TE | MB₁, MB₂ | Green | Mitigative controls |
| Far right | C₁, C₂ | Yellow | Outcomes |

> **Visual Mnemonic:** Orange (energy source) → Blue (defense) → Red (failure) → Green (recovery) → Yellow (impact).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define the Bow Tie diagram. List its essential components.*

**Model Answer:**

The Bow Tie diagram is a risk assessment tool that graphically displays the relationship between causes and consequences of a hazardous event, with preventive and mitigative barriers shown along the pathways.

**Essential components:**
1. **Hazard** — Source of potential harm
2. **Top Event** — Central loss-of-control event
3. **Threats** — Causes leading to the top event
4. **Consequences** — Outcomes following the top event
5. **Preventive Barriers** — Left side controls
6. **Mitigative Barriers** — Right side controls

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*Differentiate between preventive barriers and mitigative barriers in a Bow Tie diagram.*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Preventive Barrier | Mitigative Barrier |
|-----------|-------------------|-------------------|
| Location | Left side of Bow Tie | Right side of Bow Tie |
| Function | Prevents threat → top event | Reduces severity post top event |
| Timing | Acts BEFORE the event | Acts AFTER the event |
| Examples | Relief valve, SOP, training | Firewater system, evacuation, medical aid |
| Objective | Prevent occurrence | Limit consequence |
| PFD Meaning | Failure allows TE to occur | Failure worsens consequence |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive Bow Tie Construction

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] *Construct the Bow Tie diagram for an atmospheric LPG storage bullet (capacity = 500 m³, pressure = 7 bar(g)). Identify the hazard, top event, at least 4 threats, and the corresponding preventive barriers.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Define the Hazard**
Hazard: 500 m³ of pressurized LPG (highly flammable hydrocarbon) at 7 bar(g).

**Step 2: Define the Top Event**
Top Event: "Loss of primary containment of LPG resulting in a major vapour cloud release."

**Step 3: Enumerate Threats & Preventive Barriers:**

| Threat | Preventive Barrier(s) |
|--------|----------------------|
| T₁: External impact (vehicle) | Concrete perimeter wall, vehicle impact barrier |
| T₂: Overpressure | Pressure Safety Valve (PSV), rupture disc |
| T₃: Corrosion | Cathodic protection, periodic UT thickness gauging |
| T₄: Procedural error during filling | Permit-to-work system, SOP, level transmitter interlock |
| T₅: Foundation failure | Geotechnical survey, pile-supported foundation |

**Diagram Construction (text-form):**
```
Hazard (H: LPG inventory) → T₁/T₂/T₃/T₄/T₅ → PB₁/PB₂/PB₃ → [TOP EVENT: LOPC] → MB₁/MB₂ → C₁/C₂
```

**(b)** [7 Marks] *For the above Bow Tie, evaluate the frequency of a pool fire consequence assuming P(TE) = 1×10⁻³/year and a single mitigative barrier with PFD = 0.05. State the ALARP criterion.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify Given Data:**
- $P_{TE} = 1 \times 10^{-3}$ per year
- Single mitigative barrier with $PFD_{MB} = 0.05$
- Consequence of interest: Pool Fire

**Step 2 — Apply Consequence Frequency Formula:**

$$
f_{C, poolfire} = P_{TE} \times PFD_{MB}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Values:**

$$
f_{C, poolfire} = (1 \times 10^{-3}) \times (0.05)
$$

$$
f_{C, poolfire} = 5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ events/year}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Return Period:**

$$
\text{Return Period} = \frac{1}{5 \times 10^{-5}} = 20{,}000 \text{ years}
$$

**Step 5 — ALARP Criterion Statement:**
As per UK HSE, an individual risk of $< 10^{-6}$/year is broadly acceptable. A pool fire frequency of $5 \times 10^{-5}$/year lies in the **TOLERABLE IF ALARP** region, requiring demonstration that further risk reduction is impracticable or grossly disproportionate.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Bow Tie Application

**[KTU University Exam — June 2023]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] *With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction of a Bow Tie diagram. Discuss its relationship with Fault Tree and Event Tree analyses.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Construction Procedure:**
- Step 1: Identify hazard (left-most node)
- Step 2: Place Top Event at center
- Step 3: Draw threat paths from hazard to TE (LEFT side)
- Step 4: Insert preventive barriers on each threat path
- Step 5: Draw consequence paths from TE (RIGHT side)
- Step 6: Insert mitigative barriers on each consequence path

**Step 2: Relationship Analysis:**

| Side | Methodology | Logic Used | Output |
|------|------------|-----------|--------|
| Left | Fault Tree Analysis | AND/OR Boolean gates | Identifies minimal cut sets (threats) |
| Right | Event Tree Analysis | Sequential branching | Identifies outcome pathways (consequences) |
| Center | Top Event | Common junction | Loss-of-control event |

**Step 3: Integration Logic:**
- FTA is used to systematically derive the **threats** and **preventive barrier failure combinations** (cut sets).
- ETA is used to systematically derive the **consequences** and the role of **mitigative barriers**.
- The Bow Tie serves as the **visual synthesis** of both methodologies.

**(b)** [7 Marks] *A reactor contains toluene at 200°C. Threat T₁ (cooling water failure) has PFD = 0.1, and Threat T₂ (overcharging) has PFD = 0.05. The reactor rupture top event leads to a vapour cloud explosion with P(d) = 0.6. Compute the Individual Risk assuming single combined preventive barrier (PB-1 with PFD = 0.01) and single mitigative barrier (MB-1 with PFD = 0.02).*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify Data:**
- $T_1$ PFD = 0.1 (frequency contribution)
- $T_2$ PFD = 0.05 (frequency contribution)
- $PFD_{PB,1}$ = 0.01
- $PFD_{MB,1}$ = 0.02
- $P_d$ = 0.6

**Step 2 — Compute Combined Threat Frequency:**

$$
f_{combined} = 0.1 + 0.05 = 0.15 \text{ /year (assuming OR gate)}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute TE Probability:**

$$
P_{TE} = 0.15 \times 0.01 = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ /year}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Consequence Frequency:**

$$
f_{C} = P_{TE} \times PFD_{MB,1} = (1.5 \times 10^{-3}) \times 0.02
$$

$$
f_{C} = 3 \times 10^{-5} \text{ events/year}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Individual Risk:**

$$
IR = f_{C} \times P_d = (3 \times 10^{-5}) \times 0.6
$$

$$
IR = 1.8 \times 10^{-5} \text{ /year}
$$

**Step 6 — Comparison with ALARP:**
Since $IR = 1.8 \times 10^{-5}$/year $> 10^{-6}$/year, the system lies in the **TOLERABLE IF ALARP** region, requiring additional control measures.

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Hazard with Top Event** — The hazard is the *source* (e.g., LPG inventory); the top event is the *loss event* (e.g., tank rupture). Many students lose **2 marks** here.
> 2. **Placing barriers on the wrong side** — Preventive barriers go on the LEFT; mitigative barriers on the RIGHT. Swapping them costs **1–2 marks**.
> 3. **Omitting barrier PFD values** — KTU examiners explicitly award marks for quantified barrier effectiveness. A qualitative answer without PFD values loses up to **3 marks**.
> 4. **Forgetting to multiply PFDs in series** — Students often add PFDs instead of multiplying them. For independent barriers in series, **MULTIPLY, do NOT add**.
> 5. **Not mentioning ALARP** — Every consequence-frequency calculation MUST end with an ALARP verdict statement for full credit.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Bow Tie = FTA (left) + ETA (right)** joined by a **Top Event**.
- ✅ **Hazard ≠ Top Event** — Hazard is the energy/material; Top Event is the loss moment.
- ✅ Two barrier classes: **Preventive** (left, prevent) and **Mitigative** (right, limit).
- ✅ Independent barrier failure uses the **multiplicative** rule: $P_{TE} = \prod PFD$.
- ✅ Consequence frequency: $f_C = P_{TE} \times \prod PFD_{MB}$.
- ✅ Individual Risk: $IR = f_C \times P_d$ must satisfy **ALARP** under HSE tolerability.
- ✅ BRRF in **dB scale** = $10 \log_{10}(R_{unprotected}/R_{protected})$.
- ✅ Standards: **ISO 31000, IEC 31010, OGP 544, CCPS guidelines** govern Bow Tie use.
- ✅ Bow Tie is a **communication tool** for ALARP demonstration and audit compliance.
- ✅ Always state **ALARP verdict** at the end of any risk quantification answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fault Tree Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT723 - PROCESS SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fault Tree Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Syllabus Definition**
> **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)** is a deductive (top-down), graphical failure analysis technique used in process safety engineering to determine the combinations of equipment failures, human errors, and external events that can lead to an undesired **Top Event** (typically a major accident or hazardous incident). It employs **Boolean logic gates** (AND, OR, etc.) to combine lower-level events into a structured tree that culminates in the undesired top event.

FTA was originally developed in **1961** by **H. A. Watson** of Bell Telephone Laboratories for the U.S. Air Force **Minuteman missile system**, and later adopted by the nuclear industry (NRC) and process industries.

### 1.2 Key Terminology

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Top Event** | The undesired event being analyzed (e.g., reactor runaway, vessel rupture) |
| **Intermediate Event** | A logical consequence of lower events, occurring below the top event |
| **Basic Event** | A fundamental failure requiring no further development (component failure, human error) |
| **Undeveloped Event** | A basic event with insufficient data or not further analyzed |
| **External Event** | An event normally expected to occur (e.g., earthquake, power supply) |
| **Conditioning Event** | A condition that must exist for a gate to produce its output |
| **AND Gate** | Output occurs only if **ALL** input events occur |
| **OR Gate** | Output occurs if **ANY ONE OR MORE** input events occur |
| **Cut Set** | A set of basic events whose simultaneous occurrence causes the top event |
| **Minimal Cut Set (MCS)** | A cut set that cannot be reduced without losing its failure capability |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Family Tree of Failures"

> [!NOTE] **Intuitive Analogy**
> Imagine you want to find out **why a factory caught fire (Top Event)**. You trace backwards: was it due to **flammable leak OR electrical short**? If it was electrical short, you ask: was it due to **faulty wiring AND power surge**? Each "why" forms a branch, and the events combine through logic gates.

Think of FTA like a **reverse domino effect**: instead of watching dominoes fall, you work backward from the catastrophic event to identify the **smallest combinations of root causes** that could trigger it.

### 1.4 Why FTA is Used in Process Safety

- Identifies **single-point failures** and **common-cause failures**
- Quantifies **probability** of the top event
- Provides a **visual roadmap** for risk assessment
- Forms the basis of **Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA)**
- Recommended by **IEC 61025** (International Standard for Fault Tree Analysis)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

### 2.1 FTA Methodology — Step-by-Step Logic

The construction of a fault tree follows a structured deductive workflow:

1. **Define the Top Event**: Specify precisely the undesired outcome (e.g., "Rupture of Reactor R-101").
2. **Identify Immediate Causes**: List direct causes (e.g., overpressure, corrosion, external impact).
3. **Apply Logic Gates**: Connect each cause using AND/OR gates to build higher-level logic.
4. **Resolve Basic Events**: Continue until only basic events remain (component failures, human errors).
5. **Determine Cut Sets**: Find all combinations causing the top event.
6. **Reduce to Minimal Cut Sets**: Apply Boolean algebra to eliminate redundancy.
7. **Quantitative Evaluation**: Compute top event probability using failure data.

### 2.2 Standard FTA Symbols (Per IEC 61025)

| Symbol | Name | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Rectangle | Event | Represents an intermediate or top event |
| Circle | Basic Event | A fundamental failure requiring no further development |
| Diamond | Undeveloped Event | Not analyzed in detail |
| Oval | Conditioning Event | A specific condition enabling an event |
| House-shaped | External/Switch Event | Event normally expected to occur |
| Flat D-shape (AND) | AND Gate | Output occurs if **ALL** inputs occur |
| Crescent (OR) | OR Gate | Output occurs if **ANY** input occurs |
| Triangle | Transfer Symbol | Indicates continuation of tree elsewhere |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | AND Gate Probability | $P(AND) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P_i$ | Top event fails only if all $n$ inputs fail simultaneously |
| 2 | OR Gate Probability | $P(OR) = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - P_i)$ | Top event fails if any one input fails |
| 3 | Rare-Event Approximation (AND) | $P(AND) \approx \prod_{i=1}^{n} P_i$ | Valid when all $P_i \ll 1$ |
| 4 | Rare-Event Approximation (OR) | $P(OR) \approx \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i$ | Valid when all $P_i \ll 1$ (Upper Bound) |
| 5 | Upper Bound (Rare Event) | $P_{TOP} \leq \sum_{j=1}^{N} P(C_j)$ | Sum of all minimal cut set probabilities |
| 6 | Exact Probability (MCS) | $P_{TOP} = 1 - \prod_{j=1}^{N}(1 - P(C_j))$ | Accounts for cut set overlap |
| 7 | Minimal Cut Set Order | Order = number of basic events in the set | 1st order = single point failure |
| 8 | Minimal Cut Set Logic | $T = C_1 + C_2 + C_3 + \cdots$ | Top event = union (OR) of all MCS |
| 9 | Boolean Representation (AND) | $T = A \cdot B$ | AND = Boolean multiplication |
| 10 | Boolean Representation (OR) | $T = A + B$ | OR = Boolean addition |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Nuclear Industry**: Reactor Safety Studies (Reactor Safety Study, WASH-1400)
- **Offshore Oil & Gas**: Platform fire/explosion assessment (API RP 581)
- **Chemical Plants**: Quantified Risk Assessment (QRA) per **CCPS guidelines**
- **Aviation & Aerospace**: System safety analysis (SAE ARP 4761)
- **Pharmaceutical & Semiconductor**: Hazard identification (HAZOP integration)

> [!TIP] **Engineering Insight**
> In KTU examinations, the OR gate with rare-event approximation $P = \sum P_i$ is the **most frequently tested formula**. Always state the rare-event assumption before applying.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example — Boolean Algebra Reduction

**Problem:** A reactor overpressure top event **T** is governed by the structure shown below:
- An OR gate (G1) receives two inputs: a basic event **A** and an AND gate (G2)
- G2 is an AND gate with inputs **B** and **C**

#### Step 1: Write the Boolean Expression for Gate G2

$$G2 = B \cdot C$$

#### Step 2: Write the Boolean Expression for Top Event T

Since T is the output of OR gate G1 with inputs A and G2:

$$T = A + G2$$

#### Step 3: Substitute G2

$$T = A + (B \cdot C)$$

#### Step 4: Identify Minimal Cut Sets

By applying the **Distributive Law** $A + (B \cdot C) = (A + B) \cdot (A + C)$:

The minimal cut sets are: **{A}** and **{B, C}**.

- Cut set {A} — Single point failure (order 1)
- Cut set {B, C} — Requires both B and C to occur (order 2)

### 3.2 Worked Example — Probability Calculation

**Given:**
- $P(A) = 10^{-2}$ (pump failure probability)
- $P(B) = 10^{-3}$ (valve fails closed)
- $P(C) = 10^{-3}$ (interlock failure)

Since all $P_i \ll 1$ (rare-event regime), we use the **upper bound approximation**:

#### Step 1: Compute Probability of Cut Set 1 (Single Event A)

$$P(C_1) = P(A) = 1 \times 10^{-2}$$

#### Step 2: Compute Probability of Cut Set 2 (B AND C)

$$P(C_2) = P(B) \cdot P(C) = 10^{-3} \times 10^{-3} = 10^{-6}$$

#### Step 3: Compute Top Event Probability (Upper Bound)

$$P_{TOP} \leq P(C_1) + P(C_2) = 10^{-2} + 10^{-6} \approx 1.01 \times 10^{-2}$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Cut Set Importance

| Minimal Cut Set | Order | Probability | Dominance Ratio | Engineering Priority |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| {A} | 1 | $10^{-2}$ | 99.99% | **Highest — Fix immediately** |
| {B, C} | 2 | $10^{-6}$ | 0.01% | Lower — redundant safety layer |

> [!NOTE] **Observation**
> The single-point failure {A} dominates the top event probability by a factor of **10,000**. This is typical: lower-order cut sets (1st, 2nd order) always dominate higher-order ones in industrial systems.

### 3.4 Derivation — Exact vs Upper Bound

**Exact Probability:**

$$P_{TOP} = P(C_1) + P(C_2) - P(C_1 \cap C_2)$$

Substituting:

$$P_{TOP} = 10^{-2} + 10^{-6} - (10^{-2} \cdot 10^{-6})$$

$$P_{TOP} = 1.01 \times 10^{-2} - 10^{-8}$$

$$P_{TOP} \approx 1.01 \times 10^{-2}$$

Since the overlap term $10^{-8}$ is negligible, the upper bound is essentially exact in this rare-event scenario.

### 3.5 Boolean Laws Reference for KTU

| Law | Boolean Form |
|---|---|
| Commutative | $A + B = B + A$; $A \cdot B = B \cdot A$ |
| Associative | $(A + B) + C = A + (B + C)$ |
| Distributive | $A + (B \cdot C) = (A + B)(A + C)$ |
| Absorption | $A + (A \cdot B) = A$ |
| Idempotent | $A + A = A$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

                ┌──────────────┴──────────────┐
                │                             │
        ┌───────┴────────┐         ┌─────────┴──────────┐

        │ Pump Failure   │         │                    │
        └────────────────┘         └─────────┬──────────┘
                                            │
                                  ┌─────────┴──────────┐

                          ┌───────┴────────┐ ┌────────┴────────┐
