<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define Fault Tree Analysis. List any **four symbols** used in fault tree construction.

**Model Answer:**
> **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)** is a deductive, top-down failure analysis technique that uses Boolean logic to identify combinations of basic events leading to an undesired top event.
>
> Four symbols used:
> 1. **Rectangle** — Event (intermediate/top)
> 2. **Circle** — Basic Event
> 3. **AND gate** — All inputs must occur
> 4. **OR gate** — Any input occurring produces output

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
Differentiate between **AND gate** and **OR gate** in fault tree analysis. Write their probability expressions.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | AND Gate | OR Gate |
|---|---|---|
| Logic | Output occurs only when ALL inputs occur | Output occurs when ANY input occurs |
| Boolean | $T = A \cdot B$ | $T = A + B$ |
| Probability | $P = P_A \cdot P_B$ | $P = 1 - (1-P_A)(1-P_B)$ |
| Example | Two relief valves must both fail | Either pump OR backup can overpressure |

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *Fault Tree Construction + Probability*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** 

*(a)* [7 Marks] Construct a fault tree for the **Top Event: "Spillage of Flammable Material from Tank T-101"** based on the following causes:
- Spillage occurs if either the tank ruptures **OR** the level sensor fails AND the overflow alarm fails.
- Tank rupture occurs if overpressure occurs AND the relief valve fails to open.
- Overpressure occurs if inlet pump fails AND the pressure sensor fails.

*(b)* [7 Marks] Given failure probabilities:
- $P(\text{pump fail}) = 10^{-3}$
- $P(\text{pressure sensor fail}) = 10^{-4}$
- $P(\text{relief valve fail}) = 10^{-2}$
- $P(\text{level sensor fail}) = 10^{-2}$
- $P(\text{alarm fail}) = 10^{-2}$

Calculate the probability of the top event using the **rare-event approximation**.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a): Fault Tree Construction**

```
TOP: Spillage of Flammable Material
│
├── OR Gate
│   ├── Tank Rupture (AND Gate)
│   │    ├── Overpressure (AND Gate)
│   │    │    ├── Pump Fails
│   │    │    └── Pressure Sensor Fails
│   │    └── Relief Valve Fails
│   │
│   └── Spillage via Overflow (AND Gate)
│        ├── Level Sensor Fails
│        └── Alarm Fails
```

**Part (b): Probability Calculation**

**Step 1: Minimal Cut Sets**
- MCS1: {Pump fail, Pressure sensor fail, Relief valve fail} — Order 3
- MCS2: {Level sensor fail, Alarm fail} — Order 2

**Step 2: Compute P(MCS1) — All three must fail**

$$P(MCS_1) = 10^{-3} \cdot 10^{-4} \cdot 10^{-2} = 10^{-9}$$

**Step 3: Compute P(MCS2) — Both must fail**

$$P(MCS_2) = 10^{-2} \cdot 10^{-2} = 10^{-4}$$

**Step 4: Apply Rare-Event Upper Bound**

$$P_{TOP} \leq P(MCS_1) + P(MCS_2) = 10^{-9} + 10^{-4} \approx 10^{-4}$$

**Conclusion:** The dominant cut set is the **overflow path (MCS2)**. Engineering priority: improve level sensor reliability.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *Minimal Cut Sets + Boolean Reduction*

**[KTU University Exam – May 2022]**

*(a)* [7 Marks] Explain the procedure to determine **Minimal Cut Sets** from a fault tree. Apply the procedure to the Boolean expression:
$$T = A + A \cdot B + C \cdot D$$

*(b)* [7 Marks] With given failure probabilities $P(A) = 0.01$, $P(B) = 0.005$, $P(C) = 0.02$, $P(D) = 0.02$, compute the top event probability. Identify which cut set **dominates**.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a): Procedure + Boolean Reduction**

**Steps to determine Minimal Cut Sets:**
1. Identify all basic events at the lowest level.
2. Trace upwards through the gates.
3. Substitute gate logic into Boolean expression.
4. Apply Boolean algebra (Idempotent, Absorption, Distributive laws).

**Applying to $T = A + A \cdot B + C \cdot D$:**

**Step 1:** Apply **Absorption Law** $A + A \cdot B = A$:

$$T = A + C \cdot D$$

**Step 2:** Identify Minimal Cut Sets:
- **MCS1:** {A} — Order 1
- **MCS2:** {C, D} — Order 2

**Part (b): Probability + Dominance Analysis**

**Step 1: Compute P(MCS1)**

$$P(MCS_1) = P(A) = 0.01$$

**Step 2: Compute P(MCS2)**

$$P(MCS_2) = P(C) \cdot P(D) = 0.02 \cdot 0.02 = 4 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 3: Apply Upper Bound**

$$P_{TOP} \leq 0.01 + 4 \times 10^{-4} = 0.0104$$

**Step 4: Dominance**

$$\text{Dominance of MCS1} = \frac{0.01}{0.0104} \approx 96.15\%$$

**Conclusion:** MCS1 {A} **dominates**. Address A first.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING] **Common Mark Deductions in KTU Valuation**
> 1. **Forgetting the rare-event assumption** — Always state $P_i \ll 1$ before applying the upper-bound formula.
> 2. **Confusing AND/OR gate logic** — AND requires *all* events; OR requires *any*.
> 3. **Skipping Boolean reduction steps** — Show each application of absorption/distributive laws.
> 4. **Not identifying MCS order** — Lower-order (1st, 2nd) cut sets are always dominant; mention this.
> 5. **Ignoring units** — Probability is **dimensionless** between 0 and 1; do not write units.
> 6. **Not drawing the fault tree** — In Part (a) of 7-mark questions, the **fault tree diagram is mandatory**; expect 2-3 marks reserved for it.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid Revision Checklist**
> - FTA is a **deductive (top-down)** analysis — opposite of FMEA which is **inductive (bottom-up)**.
> - Standard symbols: **Rectangle = Event**, **Circle = Basic Event**, **AND/OR gates** are the most critical.
> - Probability of AND gate: $P = \prod P_i$ (multiply).
> - Probability of OR gate: $P = 1 - \prod(1 - P_i)$ (rare-event: $P = \sum P_i$).
> - **Minimal Cut Set (MCS)** is the smallest combination of basic events causing the top event.
> - **Lower-order MCS dominate** the top event probability (rule of thumb: order 1 > order 2 > order 3).
> - **Absorption law** $A + AB = A$ is the most-used Boolean simplification in KTU exams.
> - FTA provides an **upper bound** on probability (overestimates due to ignoring cut-set overlap).
> - Applications: **Nuclear, Chemical, Oil & Gas, Aerospace** — recommended by **IEC 61025**.
> - FTA is **quantitative** — it computes a numerical probability, unlike HAZOP which is purely qualitative.
> - Always **state assumptions**: rare-event, independence of basic events, mutually exclusive cut sets (for upper bound).
> - **Top event must be precisely defined** before tree construction — vague top events lead to invalid analysis.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Failure Mode and Effect Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT723 - PROCESS SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Failure Mode and Effect Analysis**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA)** is a systematic, **bottom-up**, **inductive** (forward-logic) qualitative reliability analysis technique used to identify, evaluate, and prioritize potential failure modes of a system, subsystem, component, or process, along with their effects on system performance, operational safety, and mission success.

In the KTU 2024 *Process Safety Engineering* framework, FMEA is positioned as the **frontline hazard identification methodology** of the **Hazard Identification and Risk Assessment (HIRA)** cluster. It systematically answers three core questions:

1. **How** can a component fail? (Failure Mode)
2. **What** happens if it fails? (Effect on system, environment, personnel)
3. **What** is the *priority* of fixing it? (Quantified via RPN)

> [!NOTE]
> The 2024 Scheme emphasizes **Design FMEA (DFMEA)**, **Process FMEA (PFMEA)**, and **Hazard and Operability Study (HAZOP)** as the three cornerstones of Module 4 risk identification. FMEA is mandatory under **IEC 60812** and **SAE J1739** standards, and is a recognized subset of **AIChE/CCPS** process safety protocols.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — The "Pre-Monsoon Car Service":**
> Imagine you are driving an old car into a Kerala monsoon. Before the journey, a mechanic mentally walks through every part: *brake pads, wipers, battery, tires, headlights.* For each part, the mechanic asks: *How can this fail? What happens if it does? How likely? Can I detect it in time?* That is exactly what FMEA does to a chemical reactor, a distillation column, or a heat exchanger — only much more rigorously, with scoring tables and a Risk Priority Number (RPN) that decides *which* failure deserves a fix **first**.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in FMEA

The following standardized scoring scales (per **SAE J1739** and **AIChE Guidelines**) are used:

- **Severity (S)** — magnitude of the consequence (scale 1–10)
- **Occurrence (O)** — likelihood of the failure happening (scale 1–10)
- **Detection (D)** — ability of controls to detect the failure **before** it reaches the customer/operator (scale 1–10)
- **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** — composite priority index (range 1–1000)

> [!CAUTION]
> The Detection score **D** is intentionally **inverse**: a *high* D value means *poor* detection capability (a *bad* thing). A *low* D value means *excellent* detection controls are in place.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why and How" — FMEA Logic Decomposition

FMEA does not emerge from a single equation; it emerges from a **structured logical flow**. Below is the step-by-step intellectual framework demanded by the KTU board.

### Step 1 — System Decomposition (Block Definition)
- Break the process plant (e.g., a reactor loop) into its **smallest functional elements**: sensors, control valves, pumps, pipes, instruments.
- **Why?** You cannot analyze a failure you have not yet defined. Decomposition forces granularity.

### Step 2 — Function Definition
- For every component, state its **intended function** in measurable terms.
- Example: *The pressure relief valve shall open at 12 barg and discharge at 25 barg.*

### Step 3 — Failure Mode Identification
- Identify **all plausible ways** a component can fail to meet its function.
- Use mnemonic guides: **FIVE** — *Fails to open, Fails closed, Leaks, Ruptures, Spurious operation, Degraded performance*.

### Step 4 — Effect Analysis (Local → System → End Effect)
- Trace the failure's consequence up the chain: **Component → Subsystem → System → End user/environment**.
- End effects categorize into **Safety, Environmental, Operational, Financial**.

### Step 5 — Cause Identification
- Determine the *root mechanism* generating the failure mode.
- Use **5-Why analysis** or **Fishbone (Ishikawa)** diagrams.

### Step 6 — Scoring (S, O, D) and **RPN Calculation**
- Score using the standard 1–10 tables. Compute RPN.

### Step 7 — Risk Prioritization and Action Recommendation
- **Threshold rule (KTU convention):** RPN > 100, or Severity ≥ 9, mandates immediate corrective action.

### Step 8 — Re-Assessment (Closure Loop)
- After corrective actions, **re-score** and confirm RPN reduction. This makes FMEA a *living* document.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Concept | Symbolic Form | Typical Range / Unit | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Risk Priority Number** | $RPN = S \times O \times D$ | $1 \le RPN \le 1000$ | Rank-order failure modes for action |
| 2 | Severity Ranking | $S \in \{1, 2, \ldots, 10\}$ | dimensionless, ordinal | Magnitude of consequence |
| 3 | Occurrence Ranking | $O \in \{1, 2, \ldots, 10\}$ | dimensionless, ordinal | Failure frequency proxy |
| 4 | Detection Ranking | $D \in \{1, 2, \ldots, 10\}$ | dimensionless, ordinal (inverse) | Detectability before event |
| 5 | Logarithmic Approximation of Failure Rate | $\lambda \approx 10^{-(O-1)/k}$ | failures / hr | Maps O-rating to rate (for reference) |
| 6 | Action Threshold (Classical) | $RPN_{crit} \ge 100$ | — | Mandatory corrective action trigger |
| 7 | Severity Override Rule | $S \ge 9 \Rightarrow$ Stop | — | Bypasses RPN; force action |
| 8 | Revised RPN (post-mitigation) | $RPN_{new} = S_{new} \cdot O_{new} \cdot D_{new}$ | dimensionless | Verify mitigation effectiveness |
| 9 | Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) | $MTBF = 1 / \lambda$ | hours | Used to back-calc O-rating |
| 10 | Action Priority (AP, AIAG-VDA 2019) | AP $\in \{$Low, Medium, High $\}$ | categorical | Modern replacement for raw RPN |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the **AIAG-VDA FMEA Handbook (2019)**, the classical RPN was officially supplemented by the **Action Priority (AP)** approach. KTU may pose questions contrasting both — know both.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

FMEA is the **engineer's safety passport** in:

- **Pharmaceutical industry** — PFMEA on tablet-coating lines.
- **Oil & Gas** — Safety Integrity Level (SIL) determination for **Safety Instrumented Functions (SIF)** per **IEC 61511**.
- **Aero-engine design** — DFMEA on turbine blade assemblies.
- **Automotive** — Required for ASIL classification under **ISO 26262**.
- **Food processing** — Cross-contamination mode mapping.

In each case, FMEA is the **documented evidence** an auditor requests to prove that *every reasonable failure was considered*.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Examples

## 3.1 Derivation: From Occurrence to RPN

The Occurrence rating $O$ is not arbitrary — it is anchored to **failure rate data**. Most textbooks (e.g., the classic *McDermott et al., 2009*, and the *SAE J1739* table) map $O$ to failure rate $\lambda$ using the following exponential approximation:

$$
\lambda \approx A \cdot 10^{-(O-1)/k}
$$

where $A$ is a scale constant (typically 1 failure per $10^6$ hours at $O=1$) and $k$ is the log-decay constant (typically 1).

For a standard scale where $O = 1$ corresponds to extremely rare and $O = 10$ corresponds to very high failure probability:

$$
\lambda \approx 10^{-(O-1)}
$$

Therefore, the **MTBF** is:

$$
MTBF = \frac{1}{\lambda} = 10^{(O-1)}
$$

This allows the auditor to verify whether the $O$ score claimed by the FMEA team is consistent with operational data.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Refrigerated Liquefied Gas Storage Tank

> A KTU board favourite: a **cryogenic ammonia storage tank** with the following protection chain:
> - Pressure Transmitter (PT-101)
> - Pressure Safety Valve (PSV-201)
> - Emergency Shutdown Valve (ESD-301)
>
> You are the process safety officer tasked with performing a **PFMEA**.

### Sub-problem 1 — Calculate the RPN of the PSV-201 failing to open on demand.

**Given (from the team scoring):**
- Severity $S = 9$ (catastrophic: overpressure rupture)
- Occurrence $O = 3$ (rare: tested annually)
- Detection $D = 4$ (moderate — failure detected only at proof-test)

**Solution:**

$$
RPN_{PSV} = S \times O \times D
$$

Substitute:

$$
RPN_{PSV} = 9 \times 3 \times 4
$$

Compute stepwise:

$$
RPN_{PSV} = 9 \times (3 \times 4)
$$

$$
RPN_{PSV} = 9 \times 12
$$

$$
\boxed{RPN_{PSV} = 108}
$$

**Interpretation:** $RPN > 100$ and $S = 9$. The **Severity Override Rule** fires — *mandatory* corrective action is required **regardless** of RPN.

### Sub-problem 2 — Re-evaluate RPN after installing a redundant Pressure Safety Valve (PSV-202 in parallel).

**Revised scoring (post-mitigation):**
- Severity $S_{new} = 9$ (consequence unchanged; only likelihood reduced)
- Occurrence $O_{new} = 1$ (very rare — two independent PSVs)
- Detection $D_{new} = 2$ (continuous online leak monitoring installed)

**Solution:**

$$
RPN_{new} = S_{new} \times O_{new} \times D_{new}
$$

$$
RPN_{new} = 9 \times 1 \times 2
$$

$$
\boxed{RPN_{new} = 18}
$$

**Resulting RPN reduction ratio:**

$$
\eta_{RPN} = \frac{RPN_{old} - RPN_{new}}{RPN_{old}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\eta_{RPN} = \frac{108 - 18}{108} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\eta_{RPN} = \frac{90}{108} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{RPN} \approx 83.33\%}
$$

> [!TIP]
> This **83.33% reduction** is a board-style deliverable: it quantifies, in audit language, the *efficacy* of the safety recommendation.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of FMEA Variants

| Variant | Acronym | Application | Reference Standard | Failsafe Focus |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Design FMEA | **DFMEA** | Product / equipment design | SAE J1739, AIAG-VDA | Hardware functions |
| Process FMEA | **PFMEA** | Manufacturing / chemical process | AIAG-VDA 2019, IEC 60812 | Process parameters |
| System FMEA | **SFMEA** | Whole-system interactions | MIL-STD-1629 | Interface failures |
| Software FMEA | **SwFMEA** | Embedded control software | IEC 60812 Annex | Code-driven faults |
| Concept FMEA | **CFMEA** | Early design stage | NASA NPR 8705.5C | Mission-level risks |

## 3.4 Mathematical Equivalence Check — Why S = 1 Cannot Offset D = 10

A common student error: assigning $S = 1$ to a "minor cosmetic" failure and assuming the RPN becomes negligible even if $D = 10$. The board specifically tests this:

For $S = 1$, $O = 1$, $D = 10$:

$$
RPN = 1 \times 1 \times 10 = 10
$$

For $S = 10$, $O = 1$, $D = 1$:

$$
RPN = 10 \times 1 \times 1 = 10
$$

**Conclusion:** Equal RPN does *not* imply equal priority. The **Severity Override Rule** ($S \ge 9$) ensures that catastrophic-but-rare failures are still addressed.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 FMEA Process Flow (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define System Boundary] --> B[Build Process Block Diagram]
    B --> C[List Components and Functions]
    C --> D[Identify Failure Modes per Component]
    D --> E[Determine Effects Local to System]
    E --> F[Identify Root Causes via 5-Why Ishikawa]
    F --> G[Assign Severity S 1 to 10]
    G --> H[Assign Occurrence O 1 to 10]
    H --> I[Assign Detection D 1 to 10]
    I --> J["Compute RPN equal to S times O times D"]
    J --> K{RPN greater than 100 OR S greater than equal 9}
    K -- Yes --> L[Trigger Corrective Action CA]
    L --> M[Re-score and verify RPN reduction]
    M --> N[Update FMEA Sheet and Close Loop]
    K -- No --> O[Document and Monitor]
    O --> N
    N --> P[End: Living FMEA Document]
```

## 4.2 FMEA Cross-Functional Team Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FAM[FMEA Facilitator Chair] --> T1[Process Engineer]
    FAM --> T2[Design Engineer]
    FAM --> T3[Quality and Reliability Lead]
    FAM --> T4[Operations and Maintenance Rep]
    FAM --> T5[Safety Health Environment SME]
    FAM --> T6[Customer Voice Representative]
    T1 --> WS[FMEA Working Sheet PFMEA]
    T2 --> WS
    T3 --> WS
    T4 --> WS
    T5 --> WS
    T6 --> WS
    WS --> OUT1[Risk Priority Number Matrix]
    WS --> OUT2[Action Priority AP Table]
    WS --> OUT3[Corrective Action Log]
    WS --> OUT4[Re-validation Post-mitigation]
```

## 4.3 Plant-Level Application Block Diagram (Cryogenic Ammonia Storage Example)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN[Process Inlet NH3 Vapor] --> CR[Cryogenic Storage Tank]
    CR --> PT[Pressure Transmitter PT-101]
    CR --> PSV1[Pressure Safety Valve PSV-201]
    CR --> PSV2[Pressure Safety Valve PSV-202 Redundant]
    PT --> SIS[Safety Instrumented System PLC]
    SIS --> ESDV[Emergency Shutdown Valve ESD-301]
    ESDV --> OUT[Isolated Process Outlet]
    PSV1 --> VENT[Atmospheric Vent Scrubber]
    PSV2 --> VENT
    CR --> DRAIN[Leak Detection Sensor LDS-401]
    DRAIN --> DGS[Distributed Control System Alarm]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **block diagram** maps the FMEA study scope. Each block becomes a **row** in the FMEA worksheet. Every flow arrow represents a potential **failure propagation pathway** that the team must score.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**

**Q1. Define Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA). List the three numerical indices used in its scoring, and write the formula relating them.**

**Model Answer:**

> **FMEA** is a **systematic, bottom-up, inductive reliability analysis method** that identifies potential failure modes of a system component, evaluates their consequences on the overall process, and prioritizes them for corrective action using a **Risk Priority Number (RPN)**.
>
> The three numerical indices are:
> 1. **Severity (S)** — magnitude of the consequence (1–10)
> 2. **Occurrence (O)** — likelihood of the failure (1–10)
> 3. **Detection (D)** — probability of detecting the failure before impact (1–10)
>
> The governing equation is:
>
> $$
> RPN = S \times O \times D
> $$
>
> The classical action threshold is $RPN \ge 100$ or $S \ge 9$ (Severity Override).

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for indices, 1 for formula)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

**Q2. Differentiate between DFMEA and PFMEA. When is each applied?**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | **DFMEA** (Design FMEA) | **PFMEA** (Process FMEA) |
|---|---|---|
| **Focus** | Product / equipment design | Manufacturing / chemical process |
| **Subject** | Component failure modes | Process step variations |
| **Trigger** | New product introduction | New process / line change |
| **Standard** | SAE J1739 | AIAG-VDA, IEC 60812 |
| **Used in** | Mechanical/electrical design | Plant / batch operations |
| **Severity source** | Customer/regulator | Operator/environment/quality |

*(3 marks: 1 for DFMEA, 1 for PFMEA, 1 for distinguishing application)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **Instructions per KTU 2024 Scheme:** *Answer any **one** full question from the Module 4 choice. Each sub-part carries 7 marks.*

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Internal Choice]**

> **A.** *(a)* Explain the step-by-step methodology of conducting a **Process FMEA (PFMEA)** for a chemical reactor system. Mention the role of the **cross-functional team** and the **RPN action threshold**. *(7 marks)*
>
> **A.** *(b)* A centrifugal pump in a hydrocarbon transfer line has the following FMEA scoring: $S = 8$, $O = 6$, $D = 5$. Calculate the RPN. After installing a **vibration monitoring sensor** the new $D = 2$. Compute the **revised RPN** and the **percentage reduction**. Decide whether further action is required. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part A(a)

1. **Define scope and system boundary** — identify which part of the reactor train (feed, jacket, agitator, instrumentation) is being analyzed.
2. **Assemble cross-functional team** — facilitator, process engineer, design engineer, maintenance, **SHE** (Safety, Health, Environment) expert, and quality lead.
3. **Construct P&ID / block diagram** — pictorially capture interfaces.
4. **List all components and their intended functions** — e.g., *jacket cooling coil maintains T < 80 °C*.
5. **Identify failure modes** — e.g., jacket cooling coil fails, leaks, plugs, corrodes.
6. **Determine effects** — local, system, end (overheating → runaway reaction).
7. **Identify causes** — scaling, fouling, sensor failure.
8. **Score S, O, D** using standard 1–10 tables (SAE J1739 / AIAG-VDA).
9. **Compute RPN = S × O × D**.
10. **Decision rule:** $RPN \ge 100$ or $S \ge 9$ triggers corrective action.
11. **Recommend actions** — redesign, add controls, increase proof-tests.
12. **Re-score** and close the loop; document in the FMEA sheet.

*(7 marks — breakup: 4 marks methodology, 2 marks role of team, 1 mark RPN threshold rule)*

#### Model Solution — Part A(b)

**Step 1 — Initial RPN:**

$$
RPN_{initial} = S \times O \times D = 8 \times 6 \times 5
$$

$$
RPN_{initial} = 48 \times 5 = 240
$$

**Step 2 — Revised RPN** (vibration monitoring lowers D):

$$
RPN_{revised} = S \times O \times D_{new} = 8 \times 6 \times 2
$$

$$
RPN_{revised} = 48 \times 2 = 96
$$

**Step 3 — Percentage Reduction:**

$$
\Delta RPN\,\% = \frac{240 - 96}{240} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\Delta RPN\,\% = \frac{144}{240} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\Delta RPN\,\% = 0.60 \times 100\% = 60\%
$$

**Step 4 — Decision:**

Initial RPN = **240** (> 100) → *action required*.
Revised RPN = **96** (< 100), and $S = 8$ (< 9) → *Severity Override not triggered*.

**Conclusion:** The mitigation is **acceptable**; the RPN dropped by 60% and is now below the 100 threshold. **Document the action and close the FMEA item.**

*(7 marks — breakup: 1 for initial RPN, 1 for revised RPN, 2 for percentage reduction, 3 for decision logic)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **Alternative Choice**

> **B.** *(a)* Describe the **Action Priority (AP) approach** introduced in the **AIAG-VDA FMEA Handbook (2019)**. Compare it with the classical **RPN** method, and justify why AP is considered more meaningful for **process safety** decisions. *(7 marks)*
>
> **B.** *(b)* Two failure modes in a distillation column reflux system were scored as follows:
>
> - Mode-1: $S = 9$, $O = 4$, $D = 5$
> - Mode-2: $S = 5$, $O = 8$, $D = 3$
>
> Compute both RPNs. Apply the **Severity Override Rule** and **explain which failure mode must be addressed first**, irrespective of RPN comparison. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part B(a)

- The **Action Priority (AP)** method classifies each failure mode into **High, Medium, or Low** action level, derived from a **lookup matrix** of S, O, and D values rather than a single composite number.
- It eliminates the **false equivalence** of equal RPNs arising from different combinations of S, O, D.
- AP emphasizes **Severity first**, which is the dimension most relevant to **process safety** (because life, environment, and asset loss dominate).
- It also **reduces subjectivity** in the D score (which is the most debated factor).

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Classical RPN | **Action Priority (AP)** |
|---|---|---|
| Output | Numeric 1–1000 | Categorical: High/Medium/Low |
| Aggregation | Multiplicative | Tabular look-up |
| Severity weight | Implicit (multiplicative) | **Explicit (dominant)** |
| Detection emphasis | Strong (multiplicative) | Moderate |
| Risk of ranking error | Higher (compensating scores) | Lower (matrix-based) |
| Process safety suitability | Limited | **High** |

*(7 marks — breakup: 2 for AP definition, 3 for comparison, 2 for process-safety justification)*

#### Model Solution — Part B(b)

**Mode-1:**

$$
RPN_1 = 9 \times 4 \times 5 = 36 \times 5 = 180
$$

**Mode-2:**

$$
RPN_2 = 5 \times 8 \times 3 = 40 \times 3 = 120
$$

**Comparison:** $RPN_1 = 180 > RPN_2 = 120$.

**Severity Override Application:** Mode-1 has $S = 9$, which **triggers the override rule**.

**Conclusion:** Although both RPNs are high, **Mode-1 must be addressed first** because $S = 9$ represents a **catastrophic** consequence. The Severity Override **bypasses** the RPN comparison and forces immediate action on Mode-1. The classical RPN ranking happens to align here, but the *principle* of override is what a board examiner wants to see articulated.

*(7 marks — breakup: 2 for RPN calculations, 2 for RPN comparison, 3 for severity override reasoning)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Do not** add S, O, D — the formula is **multiplicative**, not additive. Wrong formula = full 7-mark loss.
> 2. **Detection D is inverse**: a *low* D score is *good* (easy to detect). Many students invert this in narrative answers.
> 3. **Severity Override ($S \ge 9$)** must be stated as a *separate* rule, not folded into RPN comparison. Examiners allocate 1–2 marks explicitly for this.
> 4. **Write the unit "dimensionless"** when asked; some students write $RPN = 720$ *failure-units*, which is incorrect.
> 5. Always **state the threshold** ($RPN \ge 100$) before concluding *action required / not required*.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **FMEA = Bottom-up, inductive** hazard identification. It is *not* HAZOP (which is a *team-based, deviation-driven* study using guide words).
- **RPN = S × O × D** ranges from **1 to 1000**.
- **S, O, D** are scored on a **1–10 ordinal scale** as per **SAE J1739** / **AIAG-VDA**.
- **Detection (D) is inverse**: high D = poor detection.
- **Action threshold** — RPN > 100 OR $S \ge 9$ (Severity Override).
- **FMEA variants** — DFMEA, PFMEA, SFMEA, SwFMEA, CFMEA — know at least the first three.
- **RPN reduction percentage** $\eta_{RPN} = \dfrac{RPN_{old} - RPN_{new}}{RPN_{old}} \times 100\%$ is the **audit-grade metric** of mitigation efficacy.
- **AIAG-VDA 2019** introduced the **Action Priority (AP)** approach as a supplement to the classical RPN — be ready to discuss both.
- **FMEA is a living document** — must be re-scored after every corrective action.
- The **cross-functional team** is mandatory; FMEA performed by a single engineer is invalid per IEC 60812.
- Always **re-score after mitigation** and document the **percentage RPN reduction** to demonstrate safety improvement quantitatively.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Event Tree Analysis with Examples
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECHT723 - PROCESS SAFETY ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Event Tree Analysis with Examples # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Event Tree Analysis (ETA)** is an *inductive*, *forward-looking* (bottom-up) probabilistic risk assessment technique used in process safety engineering to model the chronological sequence of outcomes that may follow a specified **Initiating Event (IE)**, as it propagates through a series of **Safety Functions (SFs)** or **Barriers** placed in the plant. Mathematically, ETA maps a single initiating event into **2ⁿ mutually exclusive outcome paths**, where `n` is the number of safety functions considered, each path being assigned a frequency (or probability) equal to the product of the branch probabilities along that path. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key KTU Distinction:** ETA is the **inductive (forward)** counterpart of **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)**, which is **deductive (backward)**. ETA is initiated by an event and looks forward; FTA starts from an undesired top event and looks backward through AND/OR logic gates. | Aspect | ETA (Inductive) | FTA (Deductive) | |---|---|---| | Direction | Forward (cause → consequence) | Backward (consequence → cause) | | Starting Point | Initiating Event | Top Event (Undesired) | | Logic Type | Binary branch points | AND / OR gates | | Outcome | Frequency of each scenario | Probability of Top Event | | Application | Consequence pathways, barrier analysis | Root-cause identification | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Domino of Decisions" Imagine a railway track switch at every junction. Once a train (the **initiating event**, say a pump failure) starts moving, it encounters one switch after another (each switch is a **safety function** such as alarm, relief valve, sprinkler). At each switch, the train is routed either to the **"Up"** (safety function **S**ucceeds) or **"Down"** (safety function **F**ails) line. After passing all the switches, the train finally arrives at a terminal platform — which represents the **final outcome** (safe shutdown, minor release, major fire, catastrophic explosion). > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Intuition:** ETA does not ask *"why did the disaster happen?"* — it asks *"given that a pump has failed, what are ALL the things that can happen next, and how likely is each?"* This makes ETA the most powerful tool for evaluating the effectiveness of independent protection layers (IPLs) in a LOPA framework. ## 1.3 Standard Nomenclature (Must Memorize for KTU) - **Initiating Event (IE):** The first event in the sequence (e.g., pipe rupture, pump trip, loss of cooling). Frequency: `f_IE` (events/year). - **Safety Function / Barrier:** Any system (active or passive) that responds to mitigate the IE (e.g., relief valve, ESD, dike, foam system). - **Branch Point / Top Event of Subtree:** A node where the sequence bifurcates into Success (S) or Failure (F). - **Outcome Path / Consequence Sequence:** A unique end-to-end route through the tree, described by a **Boolean Product Term**. - **Frequency of Outcome `i`:** `f_i = f_IE × Π (P_branch)` along that path. - **Standard Metric:** Outcome frequencies expressed in **events/year** (yr⁻¹). Risks compared against tolerability bands (e.g., **1×10⁻⁶/yr** for individual fatality risk). # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Step-by-Step Construction of an Event Tree 1. **Identify the Initiating Event (IE).** Choose a single, well-defined IE (e.g., *loss of cooling water to a reactor*). Document its base frequency `f_IE`. 2. **Identify Safety Functions (SFs).** List every protection layer that could respond — alarms, interlocks, relief devices, containment, emergency response. Order them **chronologically** (earliest response first). 3. **Assign Branch Probabilities.** For each SF, determine the **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD)** — i.e., the probability the barrier *fails* when called upon. The success probability is `1 − PFD`. 4. **Construct the Tree.** Draw binary branches at each SF. Continue until the sequence reaches a stable end state (a defined consequence). 5. **Identify Outcome Paths.** Each terminal branch is one outcome, labeled with a consequence (e.g., Safe Shutdown, Minor Leak, Major Fire, VCE). 6. **Apply the Product Rule.** The frequency of each outcome = `f_IE` × (product of branch probabilities along that path). 7. **Sum Frequencies for Equivalent Consequences.** If multiple paths lead to the same consequence category, sum their frequencies. 8. **Document Assumptions.** Independence of safety functions, single IE assumption, common-cause failure exclusions. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Independence Assumption (Common Exam Trap):** ETA strictly assumes that the success/failure of each safety function is **statistically independent** of the others. If two barriers share a common sensor, common-mode failure must be modeled explicitly or risk is **underestimated**. ## 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Product Rule Each branch point is a Bernoulli trial. The probability of *simultaneously* traversing a specific sequence of S/F outcomes equals the product of individual probabilities, because we treat the safety functions as independent. The frequency of the IE is then multiplied by this conditional probability to get the absolute frequency of the outcome. ## 2.3 Boolean Algebra Representation of Outcomes Each outcome in an event tree is uniquely described by a **conjunctive Boolean term** in which each safety function appears once, either in its true (success) or complemented (failure) form. The complete set of 2ⁿ terms is **mutually exclusive and collectively exhaustive (MECE)** — guaranteeing that the sum of all outcome frequencies equals the original IE frequency. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Symbol / Term | Formula / Definition | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | Number of outcomes | `N = 2ⁿ` | dimensionless | `n` = number of safety functions | | Outcome frequency | `f_i = f_IE × Π P_branch` | **events·yr⁻¹** | Product of branch probs along path `i` | | PFD (Probability of Failure on Demand) | `PFD = 1 − P_S` | dimensionless | Sourced from LOPA / IEC 61511 tables | | Success probability | `P_S = 1 − PFD` | dimensionless | Complement of PFD | | Frequency conservation check | `Σ f_i (all paths) = f_IE` | events·yr⁻¹ | Validates tree construction | | Individual Risk (IR) | `IR = f_outcome × P_fatality` | yr⁻¹ | P_fatality = conditional fatality prob | | Societal Risk (F–N curve) | Plot of `F` (frequency) vs `N` (number of fatalities) | — | Cumulative exceedance curve | | Common-cause factor (β) | `PFD_total = PFD_i + β·PFD_j` | dimensionless | IEC 61511 simplification | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** ETA is the backbone of **Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA)**, **Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA)** for onshore/offshore facilities, and **emergency-response planning** under the **COMAH / Seveso-III** regulatory frameworks. It is also used to demonstrate **ALARP** (As Low As Reasonably Practicable) compliance to statutory authorities. ## 2.5 Minimal Cut-Set Thinking in ETA Although ETA does not directly yield minimal cut sets (that is FTA territory), the *failure branch* of each safety function is a **minimal cut set in disguise** — the combination of SF failures along a path constitutes the *smallest combination of failures* leading to that outcome. Therefore, the most safety-critical outcomes in an ETA are those whose path traverses the **most "F" branches** (i.e., the *worst-case* sequences). # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Canonical Worked Example — LPG Storage Sphere Fire Scenario > [!NOTE] > **Problem Statement (Standard KTU Bench Problem):** > An LPG storage sphere develops a **major leak of 50 mm equivalent diameter** at the bottom flange. The base frequency of such a leak is `f_IE = 1 × 10⁻² /yr`. > Four independent safety functions respond in this order: > 1. **SF-1:** Gas detection & alarm system — `PFD₁ = 0.10` > 2. **SF-2:** Emergency Block Valve (EBV) — closes on alarm — `PFD₂ = 0.05` > 3. **SF-3:** Water Spray (Deluge) Cooling System — `PFD₃ = 0.20` > 4. **SF-4:** Emergency Response & Evacuation — `PFD₄ = 0.30` > > Construct the event tree and determine the frequency of each outcome. ### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Build the Binary Tree (4 SFs ⇒ 16 paths) We construct 2⁴ = 16 outcome paths. The path notation is written as a 4-tuple of **S** (Success) or **F** (Failure) for (SF-1, SF-2, SF-3, SF-4). ### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Define Each Outcome Consequence The outcomes are grouped into consequence bands based on **how many safety layers failed** and at what point the leakage was arrested: | Path (SF-1, SF-2, SF-3, SF-4) | Boolean Term | Consequence | |---|---|---| | S S S S | `S₁ S₂ S₃ S₄` | **Safe Shutdown** (no release) | | F S S S | `\bar{S₁} S₂ S₃ S₄` | Minor release, isolated by EBV | | S F S S | `S₁ \bar{S₂} S₃ S₄` | Small pool fire, deluge controls | | S S F S | `S₁ S₂ \bar{S₃} S₄` | Pool fire, no cooling | | S S S F | `S₁ S₂ S₃ \bar{S₄}` | Pool fire, no evacuation (casualties) | | F F S S | `\bar{S₁} \bar{S₂} S₃ S₄` | Major leak, cooled, no evac | | F S F S | `\bar{S₁} S₂ \bar{S₃} S₄` | Major leak, on fire, no evac | | F F F S | `\bar{S₁} \bar{S₂} \bar{S₃} S₄` | BLEVE risk, evacuation saves lives | | S F F F | `S₁ \bar{S₂} \bar{S₃} \bar{S₄}` | Major pool fire, all barriers fail | | F S S F | `\bar{S₁} S₂ S₃ \bar{S₄}` | Isolated leak, casualties nearby | | F F S F | `\bar{S₁} \bar{S₂} S₃ \bar{S₄}` | Major release, on-site casualties | | S F S F | `S₁ \bar{S₂} S₃ \bar{S₄}` | Small fire, casualties | | S S F F | `S₁ S₂ \bar{S₃} \bar{S₄}` | Pool fire, no mitigation, casualties | | F S F F | `\bar{S₁} S₂ \bar{S₃} \bar{S₄}` | Major pool fire, casualties | | F F F F | `\bar{S₁} \bar{S₂} \bar{S₃} \bar{S₄}` | **Catastrophic VCE / BLEVE** | | S F F S | `S₁ \bar{S₂} \bar{S₃} S₄` | Pool fire, evac works | ### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Compute Branch Probabilities Success probabilities are: $$ P_{S1} = 1 - 0.10 = 0.90 $$ $$ P_{S2} = 1 - 0.05 = 0.95 $$ $$ P_{S3} = 1 - 0.20 = 0.80 $$ $$ P_{S4} = 1 - 0.30 = 0.70 $$ ### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Apply the Product Rule to Each Path The frequency of each outcome `i` is: $$ f_i \;=\; f_{IE} \times \prod_{k=1}^{4} P_{branch,k} $$ Let us compute the *most safety-critical* outcomes — those that traverse the most F branches. **Outcome: Safe Shutdown (S S S S)** $$ f_{SSSS} = 1 \times 10^{-2} \times (0.90)(0.95)(0.80)(0.70) $$ $$ f_{SSSS} = 1 \times 10^{-2} \times 0.4788 $$ $$ f_{SSSS} = 4.788 \times 10^{-3} \;\; \text{events/yr} $$ **Outcome: Minor Release Isolated by EBV (F S S S)** $$ f_{FSSS} = 1 \times 10^{-2} \times (0.10)(0.95)(0.80)(0.70) $$ $$ f_{FSSS} = 1 \times 10^{-2} \times 0.05320 $$ $$ f_{FSSS} = 5.320 \times 10^{-4} \;\; \text{events/yr} $$ **Outcome: Major Pool Fire with All Barriers Failing (F F F S)** $$ f_{FFFS} = 1 \times 10^{-2} \times (0.10)(0.05)(0.20)(0.70) $$ $$ f_{FFFS} = 1 \times 10^{-2} \times 7.0 \times 10^{-4} $$ $$ f_{FFFS} = 7.0 \times 10^{-6} \;\; \text{events/yr} $$ **Outcome: Catastrophic VCE / BLEVE (F F F F)** $$ f_{FFFF} = 1 \times 10^{-2} \times (0.10)(0.05)(0.20)(0.30) $$ $$ f_{FFFF} = 1 \times 10^{-2} \times 3.0 \times 10^{-4} $$ $$ f_{FFFF} = 3.0 \times 10^{-6} \;\; \text{events/yr} $$ ### 3.1.5 Step 5 — Frequency Conservation Check The sum of all 16 path frequencies must equal `f_IE`. Algebraically: $$ \sum_{i=1}^{16} f_i = f_{IE} \prod_{k=1}^{4} \left[P_{Sk} + P_{Fk}\right] = f_{IE} \cdot (1)(1)(1)(1) = f_{IE} $$ Therefore: $$ \sum_{i=1}^{16} f_i = 1 \times 10^{-2} \;\; \text{events/yr} \quad \checkmark $$ ### 3.1.6 Step 6 — Aggregate into Consequence Categories For risk-evaluation purposes, similar consequences are grouped: | Consequence Category | Paths (count) | Aggregate Frequency (events/yr) | |---|---|---| | Safe Shutdown | 1 (S S S S) | `4.79 × 10⁻³` | | Minor Release, No Fire | 3 (F S S S; S F S S; S S F S with mitigants) | `≈ 2.51 × 10⁻³` | | Pool Fire — Controlled | 4 | `≈ 1.84 × 10⁻³` | | Pool Fire — Uncontrolled, On-site Casualties | 5 | `≈ 7.71 × 10⁻⁴` | | Major Release with VCE/BLEVE Potential | 3 | `≈ 9.5 × 10⁻⁶` | | **Catastrophic VCE / BLEVE** | 1 (F F F F) | `3.0 × 10⁻⁶` | > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Worst Path" is the Most Probable Worst Case.** Notice that the catastrophic F-F-F-F path has a frequency of `3 × 10⁻⁶/yr`. This is **above the typical individual fatality risk tolerability of 1 × 10⁻⁶/yr** and demands further risk reduction (e.g., installing a foam pourer or upgrading the EBV). ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Generalized Outcome Probabilities For an event tree with `n` safety functions, the **probability of an outcome path containing `m` failures** follows a combinatorial structure. If all SFs have the *same* PFD `p`, the probability of exactly `m` failures among `n` SFs is: $$ P(\text{exactly } m \text{ failures}) = \binom{n}{m} \, p^{\,m} (1-p)^{\,n-m} $$ This is the **binomial distribution** of failed safety functions. KTU examiners frequently test this identity to confirm whether the student understands that the *aggregate* frequency of all "exactly-m-failure" outcomes can be computed in one shot without enumerating every path. For the worked example with `p` average ≈ 0.15 and `n = 4`: | Number of Failures (m) | Combinations `C(4,m)` | Aggregate Probability | Frequency (events/yr) | |---|---|---|---| | 0 | 1 | `0.4788` | `4.79 × 10⁻³` | | 1 | 4 | `0.3380` | `3.38 × 10⁻³` | | 2 | 6 | `0.0894` | `8.94 × 10⁻⁴` | | 3 | 4 | `0.0105` | `1.05 × 10⁻⁴` | | 4 | 1 | `4.65 × 10⁻⁴`* | `4.65 × 10⁻⁶`* | *Approximate using representative PFD; full per-path calculation as shown in §3.1.4 is required for marks. > [!WARNING] > **Do NOT confuse the average-PFD binomial shortcut with the exact path-by-path product!** The shortcut gives an aggregate probability; the full ETA path enumeration gives the **per-consequence** frequency that the safety officer needs to design specific mitigations. KTU Part B (14 marks) questions almost always require the full enumeration. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture of ETA Construction The following Mermaid diagram shows the **process of building an event tree** in a top-down block architecture — from IE identification through to risk-decision output. This complements the actual event-tree diagram in §4.2. ```mermaid flowchart TD A0[Step 1: Define Process Scope] --> B0[Step 2: Identify Initiating Event IE] B0 --> C0[Step 3: Determine f_IE from Historical Data] C0 --> D0[Step 4: List Safety Functions SF-1 to SF-n] D0 --> E0[Step 5: Assign PFD to Each SF] E0 --> F0[Step 6: Build Binary Tree] F0 --> G0[Step 7: Apply Product Rule] G0 --> H0[Step 8: Aggregate into Consequence Bands] H0 --> I0[Step 9: Compute Individual Risk IR] H0 --> J0[Step 10: Plot F-N Curve for Societal Risk] I0 --> K0[Step 11: Compare vs Tolerability Criteria] J0 --> K0 K0 --> L0{ALARP Demonstrated?} L0 -- Yes --> M0[Accept / Proceed] L0 -- No --> N0[Add SFs or Reduce IE Frequency] ``` ## 4.2 Worked-Example Event Tree (LPG Sphere Leak) The diagram below represents the 16-path event tree for the LPG example of §3.1. Each node is labeled with an alphanumeric identifier prefixed with a letter (per the Mermaid safeguard rule). Branch labels show the outcome of the safety function (S = success, F = failure). ```mermaid flowchart TD IE[IE: 50mm LPG Leak
f_IE = 1E-2 per yr] --> N1A{SF-1: Gas Detection} N1A -- S 0.90 --> N2A{SF-2: EBV} N1A -- F 0.10 --> N2B{SF-2: EBV} N2A -- S 0.95 --> N3A{SF-3: Deluge} N2A -- F 0.05 --> N3B{SF-3: Deluge} N2B -- S 0.95 --> N3C{SF-3: Deluge} N2B -- F 0.05 --> N3D{SF-3: Deluge} N3A -- S 0.80 --> N4A{SF-4: Evacuation} N3A -- F 0.20 --> N4B{SF-4: Evacuation} N3B -- S 0.80 --> N4C{SF-4: Evacuation} N3B -- F 0.20 --> N4D{SF-4: Evacuation} N3C -- S 0.80 --> N4E{SF-4: Evacuation} N3C -- F 0.20 --> N4F{SF-4: Evacuation} N3D -- S 0.80 --> N4G{SF-4: Evacuation} N3D -- F 0.20 --> N4H{SF-4: Evacuation} N4A -- S 0.70 --> O1[O1 Safe Shutdown 4.79E-3] N4A -- F 0.30 --> O2[O2 Pool Fire Casualties 1.03E-3] N4B -- S 0.70 --> O3[O3 Pool Fire Evac OK 1.03E-3] N4B -- F 0.30 --> O4[O4 Major Fire 3.45E-4] N4C -- S 0.70 --> O5[O5 Pool Fire 2.71E-4] N4C -- F 0.30 --> O6[O6 Major Release 9.08E-5] N4D -- S 0.70 --> O7[O7 BLEVE Risk 3.60E-5] N4D -- F 0.30 --> O8[O8 BLEVE No Evac 1.54E-5] N4E -- S 0.70 --> O9[O9 Pool Fire 5.42E-4] N4E -- F 0.30 --> O10[O10 Major Fire 2.32E-4] N4F -- S 0.70 --> O11[O11 Major Release 1.08E-4] N4F -- F 0.30 --> O12[O12 BLEVE 4.62E-5] N4G -- S 0.70 --> O13[O13 BLEVE Risk 1.80E-5] N4G -- F 0.30 --> O14[O14 BLEVE 7.71E-6] N4H -- S 0.70 --> O15[O15 BLEVE 7.20E-6] N4H -- F 0.30 --> O16[O16 CATASTROPHIC VCE 3.00E-6] ``` ## 4.3 Mapping ETA to the Bow-Tie Concept ETA (inductive) and FTA (deductive) combine in the **Bow-Tie** diagram, which is widely used in KTU and ALARP demonstrations. The IE sits at the knot, ETAs fan out to the right (consequences), and FTAs fan out to the left (causes). ```mermaid flowchart LR L0[Threats / Causes] --> L1[Fault Tree side] L1 --> Knot[Initiating Event] Knot --> R1[Event Tree side] R1 --> R0[Consequences / Outcomes] Knot -- SF-1 --> S1 Knot -- SF-2 --> S2 Knot -- SF-3 --> S3 Knot -- SF-4 --> S4 S1 --> R0 S2 --> R0 S3 --> R0 S4 --> R0 ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set A]` **Distinguish clearly between Event Tree Analysis (ETA) and Fault Tree Analysis (FTA). Mention the direction of analysis and the type of logic gates used in each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Criterion | ETA | FTA | |---|---|---| | Direction | Inductive / Forward (cause → consequence) | Deductive / Backward (consequence → cause) | | Starting Point | A single Initiating Event | An undesired Top Event | | Logic Elements | Binary success/failure branch points | AND / OR / NOT gates | | Output | Frequency of each consequence scenario | Probability of the top event | | Typical Use | Consequence & barrier assessment, LOPA | Root-cause identification, system reliability | *(1 mark for direction; 1 mark for logic type; 1 mark for output and use.)* --- ### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set B]` **State the "product rule" used in Event Tree Analysis. Why is the independence of safety functions a critical assumption?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The frequency of an outcome path `i` in ETA is given by: $$ f_i = f_{IE} \times \prod_{k=1}^{n} P_{branch,k} $$ where the product is taken over all branch probabilities along that specific path. (1 mark) Independence means the success/failure of one safety function does not statistically influence the success/failure of any other. (1 mark) This assumption allows the use of the product rule (multiplication of probabilities). If two barriers share a sensor, power supply, or operator, their failures are *not* independent and the product rule underestimates risk; common-cause failure (β-factor) must be added. (1 mark) --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]` **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the step-by-step methodology of constructing an Event Tree. List **five** typical safety functions used in a refinery event tree. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A spherical LPG storage tank has a probability of `5 × 10⁻³ per year` of developing a major flange leak. Three independent safety systems are in place: - **SF-1** (Gas Detection + Alarm): `PFD = 0.10` - **SF-2** (Emergency Block Valve): `PFD = 0.05` - **SF-3** (Water Deluge Cooling): `PFD = 0.15` **(i)** Construct the event tree and compute the frequency of **every** outcome. **(4 marks)** **(ii)** Determine the cumulative frequency of outcomes leading to a **major pool fire** (any path where SF-3 fails). **(2 marks)** **(iii)** If the tolerability threshold for major pool fire is `1 × 10⁻⁴ /yr`, recommend **one** practical risk-reduction measure. **(1 mark)** --- ### **Model Solution to Question A** #### Part (a) — 7 marks **Step-by-step ETA methodology (1 mark each, total 5 marks):** 1. **Define the scope** and choose the *Initiating Event* (e.g., flange leak). Document the IE frequency `f_IE`. 2. **Identify all safety functions / barriers** in chronological order (e.g., detection → isolation → mitigation → response). 3. **Assign PFD to each SF** from LOPA tables, instrument reliability data (IEC 61511, OREDA). 4. **Draw the binary event tree**, branching at each SF into S (top branch) and F (bottom branch). 5. **Apply the product rule** to each path: `f_i = f_IE × Π P_branch`. 6. **Aggregate frequencies** of paths with similar consequences. 7. **Compare to risk criteria** (individual / societal risk). **Five typical refinery safety functions (2 marks):** - **SF-1:** Process gas detection and alarm (flammable / toxic gas detectors). - **SF-2:** Emergency Shutdown (ESD) system + Emergency Block Valve (EBV). - **SF-3:** Pressure Relief Valve (PSV) / rupture disc. - **SF-4:** Firewater deluge / foam pourer system. - **SF-5:** Emergency response and evacuation (operator, fire brigade). #### Part (b) — 7 marks **Given:** - `f_IE = 5 × 10⁻³ /yr` - `P_S1 = 0.90, P_S2 = 0.95, P_S3 = 0.85` **(i) Tree and frequency of every outcome (4 marks):** | Path (SF-1, SF-2, SF-3) | Probability Product | Frequency (yr⁻¹) | |---|---|---| | S S S | 0.90 × 0.95 × 0.85 = 0.72675 | `3.634 × 10⁻³` | | F S S | 0.10 × 0.95 × 0.85 = 0.08075 | `4.038 × 10⁻⁴` | | S F S | 0.90 × 0.05 × 0.85 = 0.03825 | `1.913 × 10⁻⁴` | | S S F | 0.90 × 0.95 × 0.15 = 0.12825 | `6.413 × 10⁻⁴` | | F F S | 0.10 × 0.05 × 0.85 = 0.00425 | `2.125 × 10⁻⁵` | | F S F | 0.10 × 0.95 × 0.15 = 0.01425 | `7.125 × 10⁻⁵` | | S F F | 0.90 × 0.05 × 0.15 = 0.00675 | `3.375 × 10⁻⁵` | | F F F | 0.10 × 0.05 × 0.15 = 0.00075 | `3.75 × 10⁻⁶` | **Conservation check:** $$ \sum f_i = (0.72675 + 0.08075 + 0.03825 + 0.12825 + 0.00425 + 0.01425 + 0.00675 + 0.00075) \times 5 \times 10^{-3} $$ $$ = 1.00000 \times 5 \times 10^{-3} = 5.0 \times 10^{-3} \;\; \text{✓} $$ **(ii) Cumulative frequency of major pool fire (SF-3 fails): (2 marks)** Major pool fire occurs whenever SF-3 fails, i.e., paths `S S F`, `F S F`, `S F F`, `F F F`: $$ f_{MPF} = 6.413 \times 10^{-4} + 7.125 \times 10^{-5} + 3.375 \times 10^{-5} + 3.75 \times 10^{-6} $$ $$ f_{MPF} = 7.500 \times 10^{-4} \;\; \text{events/yr} $$ **(iii) Recommendation (1 mark):** Since `f_MPF = 7.5 × 10⁻⁴ /yr` exceeds the threshold of `1 × 10⁻⁴/yr` by a factor of 7.5, an **automatic foam pourer system** should be added as a fourth safety function (with PFD ≈ 0.10). Adding this brings the major-pool-fire frequency down by an order of magnitude to approximately `7.5 × 10⁻⁵ /yr`, satisfying the tolerability criterion. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]` **(a)** A reactor cooling water pump trips. The IE frequency is `2 × 10⁻¹ /yr`. Construct an event tree with the following safety functions: **(7 marks)** - **SF-1:** Low-flow alarm + operator action — `PFD = 0.50` - **SF-2:** Standby pump auto-start — `PFD = 0.10` - **SF-3:** Back-up cooling from secondary loop — `PFD = 0.20` - **SF-4:** Emergency depressurization (blowdown) — `PFD = 0.05` Compute the frequency of **(i)** safe controlled cooldown, **(ii)** runaway reaction, and **(iii)** verify the conservation law. **(b)** Discuss the **limitations** of ETA. How is **common-cause failure (CCF)** handled in an event tree? **(7 marks)** --- ### **Model Solution to Question B** #### Part (a) — 7 marks **Branch probabilities:** - `P_S1 = 0.50, P_S2 = 0.90, P_S3 = 0.80, P_S4 = 0.95` The complete event tree has 2⁴ = 16 outcomes. For brevity, the two most critical aggregates are: **(i) Safe controlled cooldown** (all four SFs succeed, S S S S): $$ f_{SSS S} = 2 \times 10^{-1} \times (0.50)(0.90)(0.80)(0.95) $$ $$ f_{SSS S} = 2 \times 10^{-1} \times 0.3420 = 6.840 \times 10^{-2} \;\; \text{events/yr} $$ **(ii) Runaway reaction** (all four SFs fail, F F F F — depressurization is the last line of defense): $$ f_{FFFF} = 2 \times 10^{-1} \times (0.50)(0.10)(0.20)(0.05) $$ $$ f_{FFFF} = 2 \times 10^{-1} \times 5 \times 10^{-4} = 1.0 \times 10^{-4} \;\; \text{events/yr} $$ **(iii) Conservation check:** $$ \sum_{i=1}^{16} f_i = f_{IE} \prod_{k=1}^{4}(P_{Sk} + P_{Fk}) = 2 \times 10^{-1} \times (1)^4 = 2.0 \times 10^{-1} \;\; \text{✓} $$ #### Part (b) — 7 marks **Limitations of ETA (4 marks):** 1. **Single Initiating Event assumption** — ETA analyzes one IE at a time; multiple concurrent IEs (e.g., earthquake + leak) require separate trees or a combined bow-tie. 2. **Independence assumption** — Common-cause, common-mode, and human-error dependencies are not captured by the simple product rule. 3. **Binary branches** — Real safety functions can have *partial* success or degraded performance; ETA reduces these to all-or-nothing. 4. **Static analysis** — Time-dependent phenomena (e.g., operator response, escalation windows) are not naturally captured; dynamic event trees (DET) or DYLAM methods are needed. 5. **Human reliability** — Operator intervention must be lumped into a single PFD; HRA methods (HEART, THERP) must supply the value. 6. **No recovery actions** beyond the modelled SFs. **Common-Cause Failure handling (3 marks):** - **β-factor model** (IEC 61511): Partition each SF's PFD into an independent part and a common part. The combined PFD for two redundant barriers `i, j` is: $$ PFD_{i \cup j} = PFD_i + PFD_j - (1-\beta) PFD_i PFD_j - \beta \cdot \min(PFD_i, PFD_j) $$ - **Simplified form:** $$ PFD_{total} \approx (1-\beta)(PFD_i + PFD_j) + \beta \cdot PFD_{CCF} $$ - In practice, ETA may either add a **virtual "CCF barrier"** with its own PFD (e.g., `β × PFD_avg`) at the top of the tree, or **inflate** the PFDs of dependent SFs by a CCF factor (typical β = 0.05–0.10 for instrumented systems; up to 0.25 for human-related CCFs). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting the conservation check** — always show that `Σ f_i = f_IE`; this is a free 1 mark. > 2. **Conflating PFD and unavailability** — PFD applies to a *demand-driven* event; for continuously operating barriers, use **PFD = λ·T_proof / 2** (mean fractional downtime). > 3. **Ignoring CCF** — when two SFs share a sensor or power supply, the product rule is mathematically invalid. State this explicitly if the question hints at shared components. > 4. **Forgetting units** — final answer must be in **events/yr** (or **/yr**), not dimensionless probability. > 5. **Wrong Boolean notation** — use `\bar{S}` or `S'` for "failure" in the Boolean term; do not write "NOT S" in plain English. > 6. **Order of safety functions** — they must appear in *chronological* order of response. A wrong order (e.g., deluge before detection) is logical nonsense and is heavily penalized. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Pin This for Last-Minute Review):** - ✅ ETA is **inductive (forward)**, FTA is **deductive (backward)** — do not mix up. - ✅ An event tree with `n` safety functions has exactly **2ⁿ mutually exclusive, collectively exhaustive (MECE) outcome paths**. - ✅ The **product rule**: `f_i = f_IE × (product of branch probabilities along path i)`. - ✅ Branch **success probability** = `1 − PFD`; **failure probability** = `PFD`. - ✅ **Conservation law:** `Σ f_i = f_IE` for all 2ⁿ paths — always verify. - ✅ Safety functions in an ETA must be **statistically independent**; otherwise, CCF correction is mandatory. - ✅ Order of SFs must be **chronological** (earliest response first). - ✅ ETA is the **backbone of LOPA** (Layer of Protection Analysis) and **QRA** (Quantitative Risk Assessment). - ✅ Combine ETA (right) + FTA (left) ⇒ **Bow-Tie diagram**, used for ALARP demonstration. - ✅ Use **Boolean product terms** (S₁ · S₂ · S̄₃ · S₄) to label each path concisely. - ✅ Standard risk-metric: **Individual Risk (IR)** in **yr⁻¹**; tolerability for workers is typically `10⁻³ to 10⁻⁶ /yr`. - ✅ **Societal Risk** is plotted on the **F–N curve** (cumulative frequency vs number of fatalities). - ✅ Average-PFD shortcut: aggregate probability of exactly-m-failures = `C(n,m) · pᵐ · (1−p)ⁿ⁻ᵐ` (binomial) — useful for sanity checks. - ✅ **Common pitfall** to avoid: do not treat the *absence* of an SF as a "Success" — an unmodelled barrier is **not** an outcome, it just means the model is incomplete.

topics.title: Safety Integrity Level (SIL)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT723 - PROCESS SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Safety Integrity Level (SIL)**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (IEC 61508 / IEC 61511)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)** is a discrete, numerical safety integrity rating (ranging from **SIL 1 to SIL 4**) used to specify the required **risk reduction** for a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF). It is defined by international functional safety standards **IEC 61508** (general) and **IEC 61511** (process industries).

Mathematically, SIL quantifies the *target Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD)* of a safety function. The higher the consequence severity, the higher the SIL demanded, and the lower the allowable probability of dangerous failure.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Brake System of a Truck"

Imagine a fully loaded chemical tanker truck speeding down a hill.

- The **driver's eyes, brain, and reflexes** = the **Basic Process Control System (BPCS)** (prevents most problems under normal operation).
- The **seat belt** = an **independent protection layer (IPL)** (passive, independent, low reliability demand).
- The **anti-lock braking system (ABS)** = the **Safety Instrumented Function (SIF)** — a dedicated, independent system that *only* kicks in when something is going wrong.
- The **SIL rating** = the *quality* of the ABS. A truck carrying radioactive waste (SIL 3) needs far more reliable brakes than a truck carrying bottled water (SIL 1).

In other words: **SIL tells you "how trustworthy" your last line of automated safety defense must be.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology Recap (KTU Syllabus Highlights)**
> - **SIF** — Safety Instrumented Function (a single safety function, e.g., "close valve XV-101 if pressure > X").
> - **SIS** — Safety Instrumented System (the engineered hardware + software implementing one or more SIFs).
> - **PFD** — Probability of Failure on Demand.
> - **RRF** — Risk Reduction Factor = $1 / \text{PFD}_{\text{avg}}$.
> - **SFF** — Safe Failure Fraction.
> - **HFT** — Hardware Fault Tolerance.
> - **PFD avg** — Average probability of failure on demand over the proof test interval.

---

### 1.3 Why Does SIL Exist?

Before SIL standards, engineers chose safety systems based on experience, vendor reputation, or "rules of thumb." The Bhopal disaster (1984) and Piper Alpha (1988) exposed the cost of non-quantified safety decisions. IEC 61508 (published 1998) and its process-industry sibling **IEC 61511** introduced a **risk-based, performance-based** approach: instead of asking "Is this valve big enough?" engineers must now prove quantitatively "What is the probability this safety function will fail when demanded?" — and that probability is graded on a four-level SIL scale.

> [!NOTE]
> **Domain Scope:** IEC 61508 is a *general* (cross-sector) standard covering electrical/electronic/programmable electronic (E/E/PE) systems. IEC 61511 specializes it for the **process industries** — the direct domain of OECHT723.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Architectural Components of a SIL Study

A complete SIL determination project has three orthogonal activities:

**(A) SIL Determination (Allocation)** — *How much risk reduction is needed?*
- Consequence × Frequency × Probability-of-Not-Recovering → Required SIL.
- Done *before* the SIF is designed.

**(B) SIL Verification** — *Can the proposed SIF architecture actually achieve that SIL?*
- Sum the PFDs of all components (sensor + logic solver + final element) and confirm it falls inside the target band.
- Done *after* the SIF is selected.

**(C) SIL Management** — *How do we keep the SIL valid for the next 20 years?*
- Proof testing, functional safety audits, modification management, competence management.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Tip:** KTU questions frequently ask students to distinguish between SIL *determination* and SIL *verification*. Determination = target. Verification = achieved.

### 2.2 The Four SIL Bands — Numerical Anchors

| SIL | Target PFD avg Range | Risk Reduction Factor (RRF) | Equivalent Demand Rate | Example Industry Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **SIL 1** | $10^{-2} \leq \text{PFD} < 10^{-1}$ | **10 to 100** | ≥ 1 demand / 10 years | Boiler flame-fail shutdown, low-pressure trip |
| **SIL 2** | $10^{-3} \leq \text{PFD} < 10^{-2}$ | **100 to 1 000** | ≥ 1 demand / 100 years | Reactor over-pressure interlock, fired-heater trip |
| **SIL 3** | $10^{-4} \leq \text{PFD} < 10^{-3}$ | **1 000 to 10 000** | ≥ 1 demand / 1 000 years | Compressor anti-surge, offshore ESD |
| **SIL 4** | $10^{-5} \leq \text{PFD} < 10^{-4}$ | **10 000 to 100 000** | ≥ 1 demand / 10 000 years | Rarely used; some nuclear/aviation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic:** "S-I-L-1-0" — read the PFD exponent: SIL 1 → 10⁻¹, SIL 2 → 10⁻², SIL 3 → 10⁻³, SIL 4 → 10⁻⁴. The PFD is the *geometric mean* anchor of the band.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{PFD}_{\text{SIF}} = \text{PFD}_{\text{sensor}} + \text{PFD}_{\text{logic}} + \text{PFD}_{\text{final element}}$ | Sum of subsystem PFDs gives overall SIF PFD | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\text{RRF} = 1 \,/\, \text{PFD}_{\text{avg}}$ | Risk Reduction Factor | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\text{PFD}_{\text{sensor, avg}} = \tfrac{1}{2} \lambda_{\text{DU}} \, T_{\text{proof}}$ (for one-out-of-one, 1oo1) | Average PFD for a single non-repairing channel over proof-test interval $T$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\text{PFD}_{1\text{oo}2,\text{ avg}} = \tfrac{1}{6} \lambda_{\text{DU}}^2 \, T_{\text{proof}}^2$ | 1-out-of-2 voted architecture (more demanding) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\text{SFF} = \dfrac{\lambda_{\text{SD}} + \lambda_{\text{SU}} + \lambda_{\text{DD}}}{\lambda_{\text{total}}} = 1 - \dfrac{\lambda_{\text{DU}}}{\lambda_{\text{total}}}$ | Safe Failure Fraction | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\text{MTBF} = 1 \,/\, \lambda_{\text{total}}$ | Mean Time Between Failures | hours |
| 7 | $\text{MTTF}_{\text{spur}} = 1 \,/\, \lambda_{\text{SU}}$ | Spurious-trip rate (1/MTBF) | hr⁻¹ |
| 8 | $\text{Risk} = f \times C$ | Frequency × Consequence (used in LOPA) | events/yr × loss |

**Symbol legend**
- $\lambda_{\text{DU}}$ = dangerous-undetected failure rate (per hour)
- $\lambda_{\text{DD}}$ = dangerous-detected failure rate
- $\lambda_{\text{SU}}$ = safe-undetected (spurious) failure rate
- $\lambda_{\text{SD}}$ = safe-detected (spurious) failure rate
- $T_{\text{proof}}$ = proof test interval (hours)

> [!CAUTION]
> **Pitfall:** In a 1oo2 architecture the *dangerous* failure rate is *higher* than in 1oo1 because the voting logic itself can fail. However, 1oo2 is preferred for **HFT** and to detect faults — it is safer from a *system-level* perspective but has a higher PFD on paper. KTU frequently tests this nuance.

### 2.4 SIL Determination Methodologies

KTU syllabus prescribes three methods, each suited to a different project stage:

**Method 1 — Risk Graph (Qualitative)**
A semi-quantitative matrix using four parameters:
- **C** = Consequence severity (Ca/Cb/Cc/Cd)
- **F** = Frequency of occupancy / exposure time (Fr/Fr/Pr/Pr)
- **P** = Probability of avoiding the hazardous event
- **W** = Demand rate (W1/W2/W3)

Output: a target SIL (1, 2, 3, or "no SIL required"). Popular in early-stage FEED work.

**Method 2 — Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA, Semi-Quantitative)**
A simplified-event-tree method that allocates **one order-of-magnitude** of risk reduction per Independent Protection Layer (IPL). A SIF that must close the remaining gap to tolerable risk is assigned the required SIL.

**Method 3 — Quantitative (FTA / ETA)**
Fault Tree Analysis or Event Tree Analysis computes absolute event frequency. The required SIF PFD is back-calculated from the tolerable risk envelope:

$$
\text{PFD}_{\text{required}} = \frac{f_{\text{process hazard, no SIF}}}{f_{\text{tolerable}}}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world utility:** A refinery SIL study typically assigns ~70 % of SIFs at SIL 1, ~25 % at SIL 2, ~4 % at SIL 3, and <1 % at SIL 4. Most "SIL 4" claims in industry are actually SIL 3 — the cost of SIL 4 hardware is rarely justified by the risk reduction achieved.

### 2.5 Hardware Fault Tolerance (HFT) and Route 1H/2H

IEC 61511 prescribes a **two-route** approach for hardware architecture:

- **Route 1H** — Based on hardware fault tolerance and SFF (a "cook-book" table). If the SFF ≥ 60 % (SIL 1/2) or ≥ 90 % (SIL 3) and HFT matches, the architecture is acceptable.
- **Route 2H** — Component-based: use certified devices and prior-use data to demonstrate that $\text{PFD} \leq \text{PFD}_{\text{target}}$.

The minimum HFT required for a SIL is:

| SIL | Minimum HFT (Route 1H) |
|---|---|
| 1 | 0 |
| 2 | 1 |
| 3 | 2 |
| 4 | 2 (with special restrictions) |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Examples

### 3.1 Derivation: Average PFD of a 1oo1 Channel

We derive the analytical expression used in the formula sheet because KTU frequently asks for its derivation.

**Assumptions**
1. Failures occur as a Poisson process with constant dangerous-undetected failure rate $\lambda_{\text{DU}}$.
2. All dangerous-undetected failures are *revealed* only at the proof test.
3. The system is repaired to "as new" after each proof test (renewal).
4. The proof test interval is $T_{\text{proof}}$ (constant).

**Step 1 — Set up the failure density.**
The probability that a dangerous failure occurs in an infinitesimal interval $[t,\, t+\mathrm{d}t]$ is $\lambda_{\text{DU}}\,\mathrm{d}t$. If the failure occurs at age $t$, it will be undetected until the next proof test at $T_{\text{proof}}$. Therefore the *contribution* to PFD from a failure at time $t$ is $(T_{\text{proof}} - t)\,/\,T_{\text{proof}}$.

**Step 2 — Integrate over the cycle.**

$$
\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = \int_{0}^{T_{\text{proof}}} \frac{T_{\text{proof}} - t}{T_{\text{proof}}} \lambda_{\text{DU}} \,\mathrm{d}t
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the integral.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} &= \frac{\lambda_{\text{DU}}}{T_{\text{proof}}} \int_{0}^{T_{\text{proof}}} (T_{\text{proof}} - t) \,\mathrm{d}t \\[4pt]
&= \frac{\lambda_{\text{DU}}}{T_{\text{proof}}} \left[ T_{\text{proof}} t - \tfrac{t^{2}}{2} \right]_{0}^{T_{\text{proof}}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{\lambda_{\text{DU}}}{T_{\text{proof}}} \left( T_{\text{proof}}^{2} - \tfrac{T_{\text{proof}}^{2}}{2} \right) \\[4pt]
&= \frac{\lambda_{\text{DU}}}{T_{\text{proof}}} \cdot \frac{T_{\text{proof}}^{2}}{2} \\[4pt]
&= \tfrac{1}{2} \lambda_{\text{DU}} \, T_{\text{proof}}
\end{aligned}
$$

This recovers the textbook formula $\text{PFD}_{1\text{oo}1} = \tfrac{1}{2} \lambda_{\text{DU}} T_{\text{proof}}$.

**Step 4 — Same derivation for 1oo2 architecture.**
For two parallel sensors, both must fail dangerously for the system to fail on demand. The joint failure density is $\lambda_{\text{DU}}^{2} \,\mathrm{d}t_{1}\,\mathrm{d}t_{2}$. After integrating both failure times over the proof-test window and averaging, the result is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PFD}_{1\text{oo}2,\text{avg}} &= \int_{0}^{T}\int_{0}^{T} \frac{(T-t_1)(T-t_2)}{T^{2}}\,\lambda_{\text{DU}}^{2}\,\mathrm{d}t_{1}\,\mathrm{d}t_{2} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{\lambda_{\text{DU}}^{2}}{T^{2}} \left(\int_{0}^{T}(T-t)\,\mathrm{d}t\right)^{2} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{\lambda_{\text{DU}}^{2}}{T^{2}} \cdot \frac{T^{4}}{4} \\[4pt]
&= \tfrac{1}{6} \lambda_{\text{DU}}^{2} T^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

(The factor $\tfrac{1}{6}$ rather than $\tfrac{1}{4}$ arises because we restrict the integration to $0 \le t_1 \le t_2 \le T$ to avoid double-counting the ordering, plus the double integral factor of 2 that follows.)

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Verify a SIF PFD

A Safety Instrumented Function consists of:
- A pressure transmitter (sensor): 1oo2 voted, $\lambda_{\text{DU}} = 5 \times 10^{-7} \,\text{hr}^{-1}$, $T_{\text{proof}} = 8760 \,\text{hr}$ (1 year).
- A logic solver: single channel, $\lambda_{\text{DU}} = 1 \times 10^{-7} \,\text{hr}^{-1}$, $T_{\text{proof}} = 8760 \,\text{hr}$.
- A solenoid-operated shut-off valve (final element): 1oo1, $\lambda_{\text{DU}} = 2 \times 10^{-6} \,\text{hr}^{-1}$, $T_{\text{proof}} = 4380 \,\text{hr}$ (6 months).

**Step 1 — Compute sensor PFD (1oo2 architecture).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PFD}_{\text{sensor}} &= \tfrac{1}{6} \lambda_{\text{DU}}^{2} \, T^{2} \\
&= \tfrac{1}{6} (5 \times 10^{-7})^{2} (8760)^{2} \\
&= \tfrac{1}{6} (25 \times 10^{-14})(7.674 \times 10^{7}) \\
&= \tfrac{1}{6} (1.9185 \times 10^{-5}) \\
&= 3.20 \times 10^{-6}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute logic-solver PFD (1oo1).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PFD}_{\text{logic}} &= \tfrac{1}{2} \lambda_{\text{DU}} T \\
&= \tfrac{1}{2} (1 \times 10^{-7})(8760) \\
&= 4.38 \times 10^{-4}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute final-element PFD (1oo1, 6-month test).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PFD}_{\text{FE}} &= \tfrac{1}{2} (2 \times 10^{-6})(4380) \\
&= 4.38 \times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Sum the subsystem PFDs.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PFD}_{\text{SIF}} &= 3.20 \times 10^{-6} + 4.38 \times 10^{-4} + 4.38 \times 10^{-3} \\
&= 4.82 \times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Check against SIL target bands.**
- SIL 1 range: $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$ → $4.82 \times 10^{-3}$ is *lower* than $10^{-2}$, so it is *better* than SIL 1 minimum but **does not** fall inside SIL 2 (which requires $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$).
- However, $4.82 \times 10^{-3} \approx 4.82 \times 10^{-3}$ lies just under the SIL 1 lower bound. **The SIF effectively meets SIL 1, marginally.**

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** A common KTU answer will conclude: "The SIF achieves a PFD in the SIL 1 band, so a SIL 1 SIF is justified. If SIL 2 were required, the final element would need to be 1oo2 or tested every 2–3 months to bring PFD down by an order of magnitude."

### 3.3 Worked Example 2 — SIL Determination via LOPA

A reactor overpressure scenario has the following LOPA data:

- **Initiating event frequency** (control loop failure): $f_{\text{I}} = 0.1 \,\text{events/yr}$
- **Conditional modifier** (probability of ignition given overpressure): $0.5$
- **Independent Protection Layers present:**
  1. BPCS pressure control loop — PFD = $10^{-1}$
  2. Pressure safety valve (PSV) — PFD = $10^{-2}$
  3. Operator intervention within 30 min — PFD = $10^{-1}$
- **Tolerable frequency** for catastrophic overpressure: $f_{\text{T}} = 10^{-5} \,\text{events/yr}$

**Step 1 — Mitigated frequency before SIF.**

$$
f_{\text{mitigated, no SIF}} = f_{\text{I}} \times P_{\text{ignition}} \times \text{PFD}_{\text{BPCS}} \times \text{PFD}_{\text{PSV}} \times \text{PFD}_{\text{operator}}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{\text{mitigated, no SIF}} &= 0.1 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-1} \times 10^{-2} \times 10^{-1} \\
&= 0.1 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-4} \\
&= 5 \times 10^{-6} \,\text{events/yr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Required PFD of the SIF.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PFD}_{\text{SIF, required}} &= \frac{f_{\text{mitigated, no SIF}}}{f_{\text{T}}} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-6}}{10^{-5}} = 0.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Map to SIL band.**
A required PFD of 0.5 lies in the SIL 1 band ($10^{-2} \le \text{PFD} < 10^{-1}$)? No — 0.5 is in the **"No SIL" / SIL 0** band (PFD $\ge 10^{-1}$).

> [!WARNING]
> **Reading the band correctly:** $10^{-2} \le \text{PFD} < 10^{-1}$ means the *upper* bound of SIL 1 is 0.1, *not* 0.5. A PFD of 0.5 means the safety function is too weak — it provides only 2× risk reduction, not the 10× minimum required for SIL 1.

**Step 4 — Conclusion.** The current layers reduce the risk to $5 \times 10^{-6}$, which is already *below* the tolerable $10^{-5}$. Therefore **no additional SIF is required** — the LOPA shows residual risk is acceptable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Learning:** LOPA is a *gap-closing* tool. If existing layers already bring the risk under the tolerable limit, SIL is *not* required, even if the initiating frequency looks alarming. This is a frequent KTU exam trap.

---
