## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define Safety Integrity Level (SIL). State the target PFD ranges for SIL 1, SIL 2, and SIL 3.*

**Model Answer:**
Safety Integrity Level (SIL) is a discrete level (1 to 4) used to specify the safety integrity requirements of a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF), as defined by IEC 61508/IEC 61511. It is the quantitative target for the average probability of failure on demand (PFD avg) of a SIF.

| SIL | Target PFD avg |
|---|---|
| 1 | $10^{-2} \le \text{PFD} < 10^{-1}$ |
| 2 | $10^{-3} \le \text{PFD} < 10^{-2}$ |
| 3 | $10^{-4} \le \text{PFD} < 10^{-3}$ |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
*Differentiate between "Safe Failure Fraction (SFF)" and "Hardware Fault Tolerance (HFT)" in the context of SIL.*

**Model Answer:**
- **SFF (Safe Failure Fraction)** is the ratio of safe plus dangerous-detected failures to the total failure rate of a component. It indicates *how many of the component's failures lead to a safe state* (either naturally or via diagnostics).
- **HFT (Hardware Fault Tolerance)** is the number of hardware faults that can occur without loss of the safety function. A 1oo2 architecture has HFT = 1 (one sensor can fail and the system still performs its function).

SFF is a *component-level* metric; HFT is an *architecture-level* metric. Both are used together in IEC 61511's Route 1H to determine if a SIF architecture is acceptable for a target SIL.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks, Module 4 — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q3. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 — Module 4]**

**(a)** Describe in detail the **Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA)** methodology for SIL determination. How does LOPA differ from a Risk Graph? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A SIF consists of a 1oo1 pressure sensor ($\lambda_{\text{DU}} = 2 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{hr}^{-1}$), a single logic solver ($\lambda_{\text{DU}} = 1 \times 10^{-7}\,\text{hr}^{-1}$), and a 1oo2 final control element ($\lambda_{\text{DU}} = 3 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{hr}^{-1}$ per valve). The proof test interval is **1 year (8760 hr)**. Verify the achievable SIL of the SIF. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to (a):**

**LOPA Methodology (5 key steps):**

1. **Identify the scenario** — A single cause–consequence pair (e.g., "loss of cooling → reactor overpressure → vessel rupture").
2. **Determine the initiating event frequency** $f_{\text{I}}$ — based on industry generic data (e.g., $10^{-1}/\text{yr}$ for control loop failure).
3. **List the Independent Protection Layers (IPLs)** — Each IPL must be *independent*, *auditable*, and *effective* (e.g., BPCS, PSV, SIF, operator, dikes).
4. **Allocate a PFD to each IPL** — Typically $10^{-1}$ for BPCS, $10^{-2}$ for PSV, $10^{-1}$ for operator, $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-4}$ for SIF.
5. **Compute the mitigated event frequency** $f_{\text{mitigated}} = f_{\text{I}} \times \prod \text{PFD}_{\text{IPL}}$ and compare to the company's *tolerable frequency* $f_{\text{T}}$. The SIF PFD required = $f_{\text{mitigated, no SIF}} / f_{\text{T}}$, which is mapped to a SIL band.

**Comparison with Risk Graph:**

| Aspect | LOPA | Risk Graph |
|---|---|---|
| Type | Semi-quantitative | Qualitative |
| Output | Numerical PFD required | Direct SIL output |
| IPL handling | Explicit per-layer PFDs | Implicit via parameters (C, F, P, W) |
| Time required | Moderate to high | Low |
| Use | Detailed design, HAZOP follow-up | Early FEED, screening |

(Any well-organized answer explaining these distinctions earns full marks.)

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Step 1 — Sensor PFD (1oo1).**

$$
\text{PFD}_{\text{sensor}} = \tfrac{1}{2} (2 \times 10^{-6})(8760) = 8.76 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 2 — Logic solver PFD (1oo1).**

$$
\text{PFD}_{\text{logic}} = \tfrac{1}{2} (1 \times 10^{-7})(8760) = 4.38 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Step 3 — Final element PFD (1oo2).**

$$
\text{PFD}_{\text{FE}} = \tfrac{1}{6} (3 \times 10^{-6})^{2} (8760)^{2} = \tfrac{1}{6} (9 \times 10^{-12})(7.674 \times 10^{7}) = 1.15 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Step 4 — Total SIF PFD.**

$$
\text{PFD}_{\text{SIF}} = 8.76 \times 10^{-3} + 4.38 \times 10^{-4} + 1.15 \times 10^{-4} = 9.31 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 5 — SIL classification.**
The PFD $= 9.31 \times 10^{-3}$ lies in the **SIL 2** band ($10^{-3} \le \text{PFD} < 10^{-2}$).

**Conclusion:** The SIF achieves **SIL 2** as designed.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q4. [KTU University Exam – May 2022 — Module 4]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **IEC 61511 safety lifecycle** phases. Why is "Proof Testing" considered critical for SIL maintenance? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A reactor overpressure event has the following LOPA data:
- Initiating event: control loop failure, $f_{\text{I}} = 0.2 \,\text{events/yr}$
- Conditional modifier (probability of BLEVEs): 0.1
- Existing IPLs: BPCS (PFD = $10^{-1}$), PSV (PFD = $10^{-2}$), Operator (PFD = $10^{-1}$)
- Tolerable frequency: $f_{\text{T}} = 10^{-6} \,\text{events/yr}$

Calculate the **required SIL** of the proposed SIF. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to (a):**

**IEC 61511 Safety Lifecycle (11 phases, sketch in flow-diagram form):**

1. Process Design & Concept
2. Risk Assessment (HAZOP, QRA)
3. **SIL Determination** (LOPA / Risk Graph)
4. SIS Design & Engineering
5. Installation & Commissioning
6. Operation & Maintenance
7. **Proof Testing** at defined intervals
8. Modification Management
9. Decommissioning
10. Functional Safety Audits
11. Documentation & Competence Management

(A simple numbered list with arrows between phases constitutes a "neat sketch".)

**Why Proof Testing is critical:**

- Components in a SIF are *not* perfect — they accumulate dangerous-undetected faults.
- IEC 61508's PFD formulas assume components are *repaired* (revealed faults fixed) at the proof test.
- If a proof test is missed, the *actual* PFD drifts higher than the *certified* PFD, and the SIF may *silently* drop out of its target SIL band.
- Proof testing also reveals spurious-trip failures, supporting safe operation.
- It is a **legal and audit requirement** under IEC 61511 clause 16.

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Step 1 — Mitigated frequency (no SIF).**

$$
f_{\text{no SIF}} = 0.2 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-1} \times 10^{-2} \times 10^{-1} = 2 \times 10^{-6} \,\text{events/yr}
$$

**Step 2 — Required SIF PFD.**

$$
\text{PFD}_{\text{required}} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-6}}{10^{-6}} = 2
$$

A required PFD of 2.0 means **no additional risk reduction is achievable** — the existing layers already bring the residual risk to twice the tolerable limit. Therefore **no SIF is required** (or a very weak SIL 0 device suffices, but formally it is classified as "no SIL").

If, however, we recalculate excluding the operator IPL (because operators are often not credited in LOPA per company policy):

$$
f_{\text{no SIF, ex-operator}} = 0.2 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-1} \times 10^{-2} = 2 \times 10^{-5} \,\text{events/yr}
$$

$$
\text{PFD}_{\text{required}} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-5}}{10^{-6}} = 20
$$

A PFD of 20 is greater than 10⁻¹, so it falls in the **"No SIL" / SIL 0** band — again, **no SIF required**. The LOPA shows the PSV alone is sufficient.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Mark-Loss Traps:**
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply the conditional modifier.** Many students compute $0.2 \times 10^{-1} \times 10^{-2} \times 10^{-1}$ and get $2 \times 10^{-5}$ instead of the full $2 \times 10^{-6}$. Always include *every* modifier.
> 2. **Misreading the SIL band boundaries.** A PFD of $9.3 \times 10^{-3}$ is *just* inside SIL 2 (upper bound $10^{-2}$). Writing "SIL 3" because the exponent is $10^{-3}$ is wrong.
> 3. **Conflating SIL 1 and "low safety" with "no safety".** A PFD of 0.5 still means the function is required, just at low integrity.
> 4. **Confusing SIL determination with verification.** Determination gives the *target* SIL; verification proves the *achieved* SIL.
> 5. **Omitting the proof-test interval $T_{\text{proof}}$.** The PFD formula scales linearly (1oo1) or quadratically (1oo2) with $T_{\text{proof}}$ — a missing factor of 8760 turns a SIL 2 verification into a "SIL 4" false claim.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU 2024 Module 4 — SIL Revision Sheet**

- **SIL = discrete level (1–4)** of safety integrity for a SIF, defined by IEC 61508 (general) and IEC 61511 (process).
- **Target PFD bands:** SIL 1 = $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$; SIL 2 = $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$; SIL 3 = $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$; SIL 4 = $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$.
- **RRF = 1 / PFD** (SIL 1 → RRF 10–100; SIL 2 → RRF 100–1 000; SIL 3 → RRF 1 000–10 000).
- **SIF PFD = sum of sensor, logic, final-element PFDs** (one decimal order of magnitude, with final element usually dominant).
- **1oo1 PFD = ½ × λ_DU × T_proof.**
- **1oo2 PFD = ⅙ × λ_DU² × T_proof²** (more reliable but higher spurious-trip risk).
- **HFT** minimums: SIL 1 → 0; SIL 2 → 1; SIL 3 → 2; SIL 4 → special-case.
- **SFF ≥ 60 %** for SIL 1/2; **SFF ≥ 90 %** for SIL 3 (Route 1H).
- **Three SIL determination methods:** Risk Graph (qualitative), LOPA (semi-quantitative), FTA/ETA (quantitative).
- **LOPA equation:** $f_{\text{mitigated}} = f_{\text{I}} \times P_{\text{modifier}} \times \prod \text{PFD}_{\text{IPL}}$.
- **PFD_required = f_mitigated / f_tolerable** → mapped to SIL band.
- **Proof testing** is mandatory: missing tests cause the SIF to *silently* fall out of its SIL band — the #1 cause of "SIL drift" in industry.
- **BPCS is NOT a SIF** — the SIS must be physically and logically separate from the BPCS (IEC 61511 clause 11.4).
- **Common-vendor and common-cause failures** can defeat redundancy; use diverse components and diagnostic coverage.
- **Operators do not count as IPLs** in many company LOPA procedures; if credited, they must meet strict independence, auditability, and response-time rules.
- **SIL 4 is almost never required** in process industries — when claimed, double-check the HFT and SFF requirements.
- **Order of magnitude:** Each IPL contributes one order of magnitude of risk reduction. Three IPLs + one SIF = four orders of magnitude — a typical LOPA outcome.
- **The single biggest source of dangerous failure** in a SIF is almost always the **final element (valve)** — it is the largest term in any PFD sum.
- **Documentation, competence, and modification management** are part of "SIL" — they are assessed during a Functional Safety Audit (every 5 years per IEC 61511).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Inherent Safety (IS)** is a fundamentally proactive and preventative approach to process safety engineering in which hazards are **eliminated or reduced at the source** during the process and product design stage itself, rather than being controlled, mitigated, or managed by external add-on safety systems, protective instrumentation, or administrative procedures during operation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Exam Definition (Trevor Kletz, 1978):**
> *"Inherent safety is a concept that emphasizes the avoidance of hazards rather than the control of hazards by the addition of safety barriers, devices, and procedures. If a plant is inherently safer, it is safer because of its very nature — not because of gadgets added on."*

In other words, the process becomes **intrinsically non-hazardous** or **minimally hazardous**, eliminating the dependency on active failure-prone engineered safety systems.

### 1.2 The Risk Reduction Equation (Foundation)

The mathematical foundation upon which Inherent Safety is built is the classical **risk triangle**:

$$ R \;=\; f \,(\text{Hazard}, \, \text{Frequency}, \, \text{Consequence}) $$

| Variable | Meaning | How Inherent Safety Acts |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| **Hazard** | Inherent physical/chemical property of the substance or process | **Reduces or eliminates** at design stage |
| **Frequency** | Likelihood of an initiating event per unit time | **Minimized** through simplification |
| **Consequence** | Severity of harm if the event occurs | **Moderated** via lower inventories/conditions |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** Conventional (engineered/add-on) safety accepts the hazard as a constant and *adds layers* to reduce Frequency and Consequence. Inherent Safety changes the **hazard itself** — making it a fundamentally different paradigm.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Forest Fire vs. Bonfire" Thought Experiment

Imagine two strategies to deal with fire hazard in a forest:

- **Conventional Safety Approach:** Accept that the forest is full of dry wood (hazard exists). Install smoke detectors, fire sprinklers, firebreaks, fire trucks, and train firefighters. **The fire hazard is controlled, never removed.**
- **Inherent Safety Approach:** During the planning stage, design the forest using a mix of moist, fire-resistant tree species, plant firebreaks as natural clearings, and design narrow pathways to limit fire spread. **The fire hazard is reduced at its root.**

The KTU examiner often uses **"prevent vs. protect"** language: Inherent Safety **prevents**; Add-on Safety **protects**. Prevention always dominates protection in the hierarchy of safety controls.

### 1.4 Historical Context — The Kletz Legacy

| Year | Milestone | Contributor |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| **1970s** | Concept formally articulated post-Flixborough disaster (1974) | **Trevor Kletz** (ICI, UK) |
| 1974 | Flixborough, UK — 28 fatalities due to cyclohexane release | Catalysed IS movement |
| 1984 | Bhopal, India — Methyl isocyanate catastrophe | Reinforced IS globally |
| 1990s+ | Centre for Chemical Process Safety (CCPS) formalization | CCPS Guidelines |

> [!TIP]
> If a KTU question asks *"Who is known as the Father of Inherent Safety?"*, the answer is **Trevor Kletz**, and you may add the year **1978** (publication of *"What Went Wrong? Case Histories of Process Plant Disasters"*).

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Pillars of Inherent Safety (Kletz's Principles)

Trevor Kletz consolidated the philosophy into **four primary principles** (with a fifth added later by CCPS). Each principle attacks a different component of the risk equation.

#### Pillar 1: **MINIMIZE** (also called Intensification)
- **What:** Use the *smallest possible quantity* of hazardous material at any given time.
- **How it acts on R:** Reduces the **Consequence** term (less inventory → smaller potential release magnitude).
- **Engineering Levers:**
  - Reduce inventory in storage tanks
  - Use smaller diameter piping
  - On-demand generation of hazardous intermediates (instead of bulk storage)
- **Example (KTU Classic):** A plant stores **100 tonnes** of chlorine → redesign to store **5 tonnes** with frequent replenishment. A leak of 100 t is catastrophic; 5 t is manageable.

#### Pillar 2: **SUBSTITUTE**
- **What:** Replace a hazardous substance with a *less hazardous* one that performs the same function.
- **How it acts on R:** Directly **reduces the Hazard** itself.
- **Engineering Levers:**
  - Use aqueous solutions instead of anhydrous forms
  - Use refrigerants with low Global Warming Potential (GWP)
  - Use less toxic solvents (e.g., aqueous cleaning vs. hexane)
- **Example:** Use **aqueous sodium hydroxide** instead of metallic sodium; use **liquid detergent** instead of organic solvents.

#### Pillar 3: **MODERATE** (also called Attenuation)
- **What:** Operate the process under *less hazardous conditions* — lower temperature, lower pressure, dilute concentrations, etc.
- **How it acts on R:** Reduces both **Frequency** (lower pressure → fewer leaks) and **Consequence** (lower temperature → less flash vaporization).
- **Engineering Levers:**
  - Atmospheric pressure storage instead of pressurized
  - Refrigerated storage of gases (e.g., LPG at −42 °C, 1 atm instead of ambient 6 atm)
  - Use dilute acids/bases instead of concentrated
- **Example (Classic):** Store **ammonia** as refrigerated liquid at −33 °C instead of pressurized at 20 bar. A hose rupture releases far less flashing mass.

#### Pillar 4: **SIMPLIFY**
- **What:** Design *simpler, more forgiving* plants that are easy to understand, operate, and maintain. Eliminate unnecessary complexity.
- **How it acts on R:** Reduces **Frequency** of human error and equipment failure.
- **Engineering Levers:**
  - Reduce the number of fittings, valves, instruments
  - Use gravity flow instead of pumps where possible
  - Eliminate dead legs and pockets
  - Fail-safe valve configurations
- **Example:** Use **wiped-wall falling-film reactors** instead of complex agitated reactors with seals.

#### Pillar 5: **ERROR TOLERANCE** (added later by CCPS — sometimes included)
- **What:** Design equipment/processes so that *deviations from normal operation do not lead to catastrophic failure*.
- **How it acts on R:** Catches the **Frequency** term in case other layers fail.
- **Engineering Levers:**
  - Use double-walled tanks
  - Use check valves to prevent backflow
  - Design heat exchangers with thermal expansion margins
  - Mechanical relief via rupture discs as a *passive* tolerance

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Aid — Mnemonic: "M-S-M-S-E"** → **M**inimize, **S**ubstitute, **M**oderate, **S**implify, **E**rror Tolerance. "Most Safe Materials Suit Every need."

### 2.2 The Hierarchy of Safety Controls (CCPS Pyramid)

Inherent Safety sits at the **top** (most reliable) of the safety hierarchy. Moving downward, each layer is **less reliable** and depends on the layers above functioning correctly.

| Tier | Safety Control Type | Reliability | Examples | Action on Risk |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---|:---|
| **1 (Top)** | **Inherent** | **Highest** — passive by design | Minimize inventory, substitute chemicals | Removes hazard |
| 2 | Passive | Very High — no operator action | Containment dykes, fire-resistant insulation | Mitigates consequence |
| 3 | Active | High — requires trigger & power | Sprinklers, alarms, interlocks, PSVs | Detects & controls |
| 4 | Procedural | Lowest — depends on humans | SOPs, training, evacuation drills | Relies on humans |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Trap:** Many students wrongly classify *"Pressure Relief Valve (PSV)"* as inherent safety. **It is NOT.** A PSV is an **active engineered safety device**. Inherent safety would mean designing the system so a PSV is **not needed** in the first place.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Equation / Concept | Symbolic Form | Units | Application |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---:|:---|
| 1 | Risk Triplet | $R = P_f \times P_n \times C$ | dimensionless, $/yr | Defines risk as function of frequency, probability of harm, consequence |
| 2 | Simplified Risk | $R = f \times c$ | dimensionless | Frequency × Consequence model used in IS analysis |
| 3 | Inherently Safer Design Risk | $R_{IS} = \frac{(f \cdot c)_{\text{original}}}{\text{Improvement Factor}}$ | dimensionless | Compares IS vs. conventional designs |
| 4 | Pool Fire Heat Radiation | $q'' = \frac{\dot{m} \cdot \Delta H_c}{4\pi r^2}$ | $\text{kW/m}^2$ | Demonstrates consequence reduction via inventory minimization |
| 5 | Vapor Mass Released (instantaneous) | $m_{vap} = V_{liq} \cdot \rho_{liq} \cdot \left(\frac{C_p \Delta T}{\Delta H_v}\right)$ | kg | Shows benefit of moderation (lower $\Delta T$) |
| 6 | Failure Rate (active device) | $\lambda_{active} \approx 10^{-2}$ /yr | per year | Active safety fails ~1% per year → IS avoids this risk |
| 7 | Passive Failure Rate | $\lambda_{passive} \approx 10^{-5}$ /yr | per year | Passive devices fail 1000× less → close to IS level |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU derivations, Equation **5** is the most likely candidate: it directly shows how **MODERATE** (reducing storage temperature $T$) decreases the vapor mass released in a loss-of-containment event.

### 2.4 Real-World Industrial Utility

- **Pharmaceutical industry:** Replaced benzene (carcinogenic) with cyclohexane in many reactions.
- **Polymer industry:** Use bulk polymerization in extruders instead of solution polymerization (eliminates large solvent inventories).
- **Refrigeration:** Ammonia (toxic) replaced by HFC/HFO blends in supermarkets (substitution principle).
- **LNG/LPG transport:** Refrigerated (atmospheric) carriers instead of pressurized cylinders.
- **Hazardous chemical transport:** Phosgene generation at point-of-use rather than bulk transport (minimize principle).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: How "Moderate" Reduces Vapor Release Mass

**Problem Setup (KTU-Style Numerical):**
A chemical plant stores **chlorine** in two alternative configurations:
- **Configuration A:** Pressurized liquid at $T_A = 25 \, °C$ (i.e., $298 \, K$)
- **Configuration B:** Refrigerated liquid at $T_B = -34 \, °C$ (i.e., $239 \, K$)

If a pipe rupture releases $V_{liq} = 1 \, \text{m}^3$ of liquid chlorine in each case, calculate the mass of chlorine vapor flashed off in each scenario.

**Given data:**
- Density of liquid Cl₂, $\rho_{liq} = 1470 \, \text{kg/m}^3$
- Specific heat of liquid Cl₂, $C_p \approx 0.95 \, \text{kJ/(kg·K)}$
- Latent heat of vaporization of Cl₂, $\Delta H_v \approx 290 \, \text{kJ/kg}$
- Ambient temperature for vaporization, $T_{amb} = 25 \, °C = 298 \, K$

**Mass vaporized equation (instantaneous flash):**

$$ m_{vap} \;=\; V_{liq} \cdot \rho_{liq} \cdot \frac{C_p \, (T_{amb} - T_{storage})}{\Delta H_v} $$

> Note: This assumes all the superheat $\Delta T$ above the storage temperature is consumed by evaporation.

---

#### Step 1 — Configuration A (Pressurized at 25 °C)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta T_A &= T_{amb} - T_A \\
&= 298 \, \text{K} - 298 \, \text{K} \\
&= 0 \, \text{K}
\end{aligned}
$$

This means the liquid chlorine is already at ambient temperature. **No flash vaporization occurs** from sensible heat alone. (In reality, some vaporization occurs due to depressurization, but for this isothermal model, $m_{vap, A} \approx 0$ from sensible heat.)

Wait — let us correct the scenario: We assume Configuration A stores chlorine at 25 °C as a pressurized liquid (above its boiling point of −34 °C). When released, it depressurizes, but for thermal flash calculation with given ambient = 25 °C, the sensible heat contribution is zero. The dominant hazard is the **liquid pool dispersion** with evaporative flux — but this comparison highlights that refrigerating *below* ambient provides no thermal flash benefit at this temperature set.

Let us revise to compare **two storage temperatures both below ambient**:

**Revised Scenario:**
- **Configuration A:** Stored at $T_A = -20 \, °C = 253 \, K$ (somewhat refrigerated)
- **Configuration B:** Stored at $T_B = -34 \, °C = 239 \, K$ (fully refrigerated, near boiling point)

Both released to ambient $T_{amb} = 25 \, °C = 298 \, K$.

---

#### Step 2 — Configuration A Calculation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta T_A &= 298 \, \text{K} - 253 \, \text{K} = 45 \, \text{K} \\[6pt]
m_{vap,A} &= V_{liq} \cdot \rho_{liq} \cdot \frac{C_p \cdot \Delta T_A}{\Delta H_v} \\[6pt]
&= (1 \, \text{m}^3) \cdot (1470 \, \text{kg/m}^3) \cdot \frac{(0.95 \, \text{kJ/kg·K}) \cdot (45 \, \text{K})}{290 \, \text{kJ/kg}} \\[6pt]
&= 1470 \cdot \frac{42.75}{290} \\[6pt]
&= 1470 \cdot 0.1474 \\[6pt]
&\approx 216.7 \, \text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logical explanation:** The liquid warms up by 45 K (sensible heat) and this energy vaporizes ~217 kg of chlorine into the atmosphere.

---

#### Step 3 — Configuration B Calculation (More Refrigerated)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta T_B &= 298 \, \text{K} - 239 \, \text{K} = 59 \, \text{K} \\[6pt]
m_{vap,B} &= (1) \cdot (1470) \cdot \frac{(0.95) \cdot (59)}{290} \\[6pt]
&= 1470 \cdot \frac{56.05}{290} \\[6pt]
&= 1470 \cdot 0.1933 \\[6pt]
&\approx 284.1 \, \text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### Step 4 — Counter-Intuitive Conclusion & Correct IS Analysis

This thermal flash calculation reveals a subtle point: for chlorine, *more refrigeration actually increases flash vaporization* if the release temperature is far below ambient. **However, this is balanced by the much smaller release rate** from a refrigerated low-pressure vessel versus a high-pressure vessel rupture. The **IS benefit of MODERATE** is therefore:

1. **Lower release driving pressure** → smaller mass flow rate during leak
2. **Two-phase release controlled** → less catastrophic vessel BLEVE
3. **Atmospheric storage** → vessel cannot explosively rupture

A complete IS analysis must consider **all risk components**, not just flash vaporization alone.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Take-Away:** Inherent Safety is **multi-dimensional**. Numerical derivations must be paired with engineering judgment. Examiners reward students who recognize that "less is more" does not always mean simply smaller numbers — it means **smarter system design**.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Applying IS Principles to a Real Process

**Reference Process:** Storage of **50 tonnes of hydrofluoric acid (HF)** in a chemical plant (classic KTU scenario).

| IS Principle | Application | Quantitative Impact | Qualitative Benefit |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Minimize** | Reduce inventory from 50 t → 5 t by just-in-time delivery | $10\times$ reduction in max credible release | Toxic gas cloud radius reduced ~46% (cube root) |
| **Substitute** | Use sulfuric acid in place of HF where chemistry allows | Eliminates HF toxic hazard entirely | Removes acute toxicity risk |
| **Moderate** | Store HF at 20 °C (boiling point 19.5 °C) instead of 50 °C pressurized | Vapor pressure at 20 °C: ~0.1 MPa vs. ~0.5 MPa at 50 °C | $5\times$ lower release driving force |
| **Simplify** | Eliminate intermediate transfer pumps; use direct metering | Removes pump seal leak path | Reduces frequency of small releases |
| **Error Tolerance** | Double-walled storage tank with leak detection | Detects failures before catastrophic release | Tolerates operator error and equipment wear |

**Cumulative Effect (Risk Reduction):**
If each principle reduces the risk component by a factor of 3, the cumulative risk reduction is:

$$ R_{new} = \frac{R_{original}}{3^5} = \frac{R_{original}}{243} $$

This ~250× risk reduction is **impossible** to achieve with add-on safety systems at comparable cost and complexity.

---

### 3.3 Decision Logic Flowchart for Selecting IS Strategies

```
STEP 1: Identify the dominant hazard
        ↓
STEP 2: Can the hazard be ELIMINATED? (Substitute)
        → YES → Apply Substitutes → STOP
        → NO ↓
STEP 3: Can inventory be MINIMIZED?
        → YES → Apply Minimize → STOP
        → NO ↓
STEP 4: Can operating conditions be MODERATED?
        → YES → Apply Moderate → STOP
        → NO ↓
STEP 5: Can the design be SIMPLIFIED?
        → YES → Apply Simplify → STOP
        → NO ↓
STEP 6: Apply ERROR TOLERANCE design features
        → STOP
        ↓
STEP 7: Only after exhausting IS options → Apply add-on safety systems
```

> This decision tree is the **CCPS Inherent Safety Index (ISI)** implementation strategy.

**Reading the Diagram:**

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Risk Reduction Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Original Process Design] --> Q1[Hazard Identified]
    Q1 --> R1[Risk R1 equals fxC]

    S1 --> T2[Substitute Material]

    S1 --> T4[Simplify Design]
    T1 --> U1[Consequence C reduced]

```

**Interpretation:** This flowchart traces how each IS principle acts on a specific component of the risk equation $R = f \times C$ to achieve the final reduced risk $R_2$.

---

### 4.3 Inherent Safety vs. Add-On Safety — Comparative Schematic

| Dimension | Inherent Safety | Add-On Safety |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Stage of Implementation** | Process design / R&D | Operations / commissioning |
| **Cost of Implementation** | Lowest (one-time at design) | High (multiple systems, maintenance) |
| **Ongoing Cost** | Almost zero | Recurring (testing, calibration, replacement) |
| **Failure Mode** | Cannot fail (no device to fail) | Can fail — relies on power, sensors, logic |
| **Effectiveness** | Permanent reduction | Variable, depends on human & equipment |
| **Regulatory Acceptance** | Highly encouraged (CCPS, OSHA, EPA) | Required minimum (codes & standards) |
| **Examples** | Smaller tank, lower temp, safer chemical | PSV, alarm, sprinkler, SOP |

> **Note for KTU Viva:** When asked *"Which is better?"*, the correct engineering answer is **"Inherent Safety is always preferable, but add-on safety is the practical reality in legacy plants where re-design is impossible."**

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define inherent safety. Who is credited with introducing this concept?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Inherent safety is a proactive approach to process safety in which hazards are **eliminated or reduced at the source** during the process design stage itself, rather than being controlled by external add-on safety systems. The process becomes intrinsically safer due to its very nature — not because of safety devices added on top of it.

This concept was introduced by **Trevor Kletz** in the late 1970s, following the Flixborough disaster (1974). Kletz is widely regarded as the **"Father of Inherent Safety"** in chemical engineering.

---

#### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Principle | Example |
|:---|:---|
| **Minimize** | Reduce bulk chlorine storage from 100 t to 5 t through on-demand generation |
| **Substitute** | Use aqueous sodium hydroxide instead of metallic sodium in neutralization |
| **Moderate** | Store LPG as refrigerated liquid at −42 °C / 1 atm instead of pressurized at 6 bar |
